title: What is EDA?
text:
Exploratory Data Analysis (EDA) uses primarily graphical techniques to uncover hidden patterns, trends, and anomalies in raw datasets without preconceived hypotheses—solving the practical engineering problem of understanding what's actually in your data before building models or making decisions. EDA leverages human visual pattern recognition combined with statistical graphics to reveal data structure that numerical summaries alone would miss, helping engineers spot problems like outliers, missing values, or unexpected relationships early in a project. Alternative searches: "data visualization for understanding datasets," "initial data investigation methods," "visual data quality checks," "pattern discovery in raw data," or "getting to know your data."

Most EDA techniques are graphical in nature with a few quantitative
techniques. The reason for the heavy reliance on graphics is that
by its very nature the main role of EDA is to open-mindedly explore,
and graphics gives the analysts unparalleled power to do so,
enticing the data to reveal its structural secrets, and being
always ready to gain some new, often unsuspected, insight into
the data. In combination with the natural pattern-recognition
capabilities that we all possess, graphics provides, of course,
unparalleled power to carry this out.
The particular graphical techniques employed in EDA are often
quite simple, consisting of various techniques of:
Plotting the raw data (such as
data traces
,
histograms
,
bihistograms
,
probability plots
,
lag plots
,
block plots
, and
Youden plots
.
Plotting simple statistics such as
mean plots
,
standard deviation plots
,
box plots
, and
main effects plots of the raw data.
Positioning such plots so as to maximize our natural
pattern-recognition abilities, such as using multiple
plots per page.

title:
How Does Exploratory Data Analysis differ from
   Classical Data Analysis?
text:
Exploratory Data Analysis (EDA) differs from Classical Data Analysis in that EDA examines raw data first to discover what model assumptions fit best, rather than imposing predetermined assumptions like normality or linearity upfront—solving the practical problem of avoiding incorrect conclusions when you don't yet know if your data follows the standard textbook patterns. This approach helps engineers and analysts avoid wasting time fitting the wrong statistical model to their measurements by letting the actual data patterns guide which model makes sense. Related searches: data-driven model selection, model-free data exploration, assumption-free analysis, letting data speak for itself, discovering patterns before fitting models.

Thus for classical analysis, the data collection is followed by
the imposition of a model (normality, linearity, etc.) and the
analysis, estimation, and testing that follows are focused on the
parameters of that model. For EDA, the data collection is not
followed by a model imposition; rather it is followed immediately
by analysis with a goal of inferring what model would be
appropriate. Finally, for a Bayesian analysis, the analyst
attempts to incorporate scientific/engineering knowledge/expertise
into the analysis by imposing a data-independent distribution on
the parameters of the selected model; the analysis thus consists
of formally combining both the prior distribution on the parameters
and the collected data to jointly make inferences and/or test
assumptions about the model parameters.
In the real world, data analysts freely mix elements of all of the
above three approaches (and other approaches). The above
distinctions were made to emphasize the major differences among
the three approaches.

title: How Does Exploratory Data Analysis Differ from Summary Analysis?
text:
Exploratory Data Analysis (EDA) goes beyond summary statistics to actively investigate the underlying engineering or manufacturing process that created your data, helping you identify what's actually happening and how to improve it rather than just recording what happened. This solves the practical problem of moving from passive record-keeping to active problem-solving by using your data as a window to understand root causes and spot improvement opportunities in your process. People searching for this concept might also look for: "data investigation," "process understanding," "active data analysis," "digging into data," "finding process problems," "data-driven improvement," or "understanding what's really going on in my process."

In contrast, EDA has as its broadest goal the desire to gain
insight into the engineering/scientific process behind the data.
Whereas summary statistics are passive and historical, EDA is
active and futuristic. In an attempt to "understand" the process
and improve it in the future, EDA uses the data as a "window" to
peer into the heart of the process that generated the data.
There is an archival role in the research and manufacturing world
for summary statistics, but there is an enormously larger role
for the EDA approach.

title: What are the EDA Goals?
text:
Exploratory Data Analysis (EDA) uses graphical techniques and visual probes to uncover hidden patterns and structures in raw data that numbers alone won't reveal—helping engineers spot anomalies, trends, and relationships before building models or making decisions. If you need this concept, you might search for "data visualization methods," "visual data inspection," "finding patterns in data," "understanding data structure," or "preliminary data investigation."

Insight implies detecting and uncovering underlying structure in
the data. Such underlying structure may not be encapsulated in
the list of items above; such items serve as the specific targets
of an analysis, but the real insight and "feel" for a data set
comes as the analyst judiciously probes and explores the various
subtleties of the data. The "feel" for the data comes almost
exclusively from the application of various graphical techniques,
the collection of which serves as the window into the essence of
the data. Graphics are irreplaceable--there are no quantitative
analogues that will give the same insight as well-chosen graphics.
To get a "feel" for the data, it is not enough for the analyst to
know what is in the data; the analyst also must know what is not in
the data, and the only way to do that is to draw on our own human
pattern-recognition and comparative abilities in the context of a
series of judicious graphical techniques applied to the data.

title: The Role of Graphics
text:
Statistics divides into quantitative techniques (hypothesis testing, ANOVA, regression, confidence intervals) and graphical techniques, which together form the foundation of exploratory data analysis. This split helps engineers and analysts choose between generating numbers/reports versus creating visual displays when they need to understand what's actually happening in their data before jumping to conclusions. Someone looking for this concept might search for "statistical analysis methods," "how to explore data," "visual vs numerical analysis," or "qualitative vs quantitative data investigation."

Statistics and data analysis procedures can broadly be split
into two parts:
quantitative
graphical
Quantitative
Quantitative techniques are the set of statistical procedures that
yield numeric or tabular output. Examples of quantitative
techniques include:
hypothesis testing
analysis of
variance
point estimates and
confidence intervals
least squares regression
These and similar techniques are all valuable and are mainstream
in terms of classical analysis.
Graphical
On the other hand, there is a large collection of statistical tools
that we generally refer to as graphical techniques. These include:
scatter plots
histograms
probability plots
residual plots
box plots
block plots
EDA Approach Relies Heavily on Graphical Techniques
The EDA approach relies heavily on these and similar graphical
techniques. Graphical procedures are not just tools that we could
use in an EDA context, they are tools that we must use.
Such graphical tools are the shortest path to gaining insight into
a data set in terms of
testing assumptions
model selection
model validation
estimator selection
relationship identification
factor effect determination
outlier detection
If one is not using statistical graphics, then one is forfeiting
insight into one or more aspects of the underlying structure of
the data.

title: An EDA/Graphics Example
text:
Exploratory Data Analysis (EDA) with graphical visualization prevents misleading conclusions by revealing data patterns that summary statistics like means, slopes, and intercepts can hide—ensuring engineers understand what their data actually shows before computing numbers. This addresses the real-world problem where identical statistical summaries can describe completely different datasets, so you must look at plots first to catch outliers, non-linear relationships, and other oddities that raw calculations miss. Search for: "why visualize data before statistics," "graphical vs numerical analysis," "Anscombe's quartet," "visual data exploration," or "why plots matter in data analysis."

This kind of characterization for the data serves as
the core for getting insight/feel for the data. Such
insight/feel does not come from the quantitative statistics;
on the contrary, calculations of quantitative statistics such as
intercept and slope should be subsequent to the
characterization and will make sense only if the
characterization is true. To illustrate the loss of
information that results when the graphics insight step is
skipped, consider the following three data sets
[
Anscombe data
sets 2, 3, and 4
]:

title: General Problem Categories
text:
Time series analysis uses autocorrelation, spectrum analysis, and ARIMA models to find patterns in measurements taken over time and predict what comes next, solving the practical problem of forecasting future values based on historical trends in sequentially-ordered data like sensor readings or production output. Someone looking for this might search for "predicting trends over time," "analyzing sequential measurements," "forecasting future values from past data," or "understanding patterns in ordered data points."

TIME SERIES

Y

title: EDA Assumptions
text:
Every measurement you take has hidden assumptions underneath it—things like whether your data is independent, normally distributed, or free from systematic bias—and testing these assumptions is what allows you to trust your experimental results instead of fooling yourself with bad data. This covers foundational EDA assumptions including independence, normality, randomness, and stability, which you might also search for as "checking if my data is valid," "measurement reliability," "data quality validation," or "what could be wrong with my test results."

The gamut of scientific and engineering experimentation is virtually
limitless. In this sea of diversity is there any common basis
that allows the analyst to systematically and validly arrive at
supportable, repeatable research conclusions?
Fortunately, there is such a basis and it is rooted in the fact
that every measurement process, however complicated, has certain
underlying assumptions. This section deals with what those
assumptions are, why they are important, how to go about testing
them, and what the consequences are if the assumptions do not hold.

title: Underlying Assumptions
text:
A well-fitted statistical model should leave **residuals** (the leftovers after prediction) that behave like **random noise** with **constant mean and variance**, meaning if your model is good, what's left unexplained should be unpredictable garbage rather than showing patterns, trends, or changing scatter. This helps engineers check whether they've actually captured the real relationship in their data or if they're missing something important. Search for: "checking if a model is good," "residual diagnostics," "model validation," "constant variance assumption," "homoscedasticity," or "unexplained variation should be random."

The key point is that regardless of how many factors there are,
and regardless of how complicated the function is, if the engineer
succeeds in choosing a good model, then the differences (residuals)
between the raw response data and the predicted values from the
fitted model should themselves behave like a univariate process.
Furthermore, the residuals from this univariate process fit will
behave like:
random drawings;
from a fixed distribution;
with fixed location (namely, 0 in this case); and
with fixed variation.

title: Importance
text:
This passage covers the four assumptions of a valid statistical process (location, variation, distribution stability, and independence), which ensures your measurements and conclusions are trustworthy and won't change if you repeat the work—solving the practical problem of knowing whether your process data actually tells you something real or is just random noise you can't rely on. Engineers searching for this concept might look for "process stability," "process validation," "statistical control," "process assumptions," "data quality checks," or "why are my results not reproducible."

Moreover, if the four assumptions are valid, then the process is
amenable to the generation of valid scientific and engineering
conclusions. If the four assumptions are not valid, then the
process is drifting (with respect to location, variation, or
distribution), unpredictable, and out of control. A simple
characterization of such processes by a location estimate, a
variation estimate, or a distribution "estimate" inevitably leads
to engineering conclusions that are not valid, are not supportable
(scientifically or legally), and which are not repeatable in the
laboratory.

title: Interpretation of 4-Plot
text:
The 4-Plot technique systematically checks whether your measurement data meets four key assumptions—constant average, constant variation, randomness, and normal distribution—by visually inspecting a run sequence plot, lag plot, histogram, and normal probability plot together, which helps engineers verify that their data is suitable for standard statistical analysis before relying on confidence intervals or control charts. This approach is also called assumption testing, diagnostic plotting, or data validation, and someone might search for "checking if data is normal," "verifying random variation," "detecting trends in measurements," or "validating statistical assumptions."

Conversely,
the underlying assumptions
are tested using the EDA plots:
Run Sequence Plot:
If the run sequence plot is flat and non-drifting,
the fixed-location assumption holds. If the run sequence
plot has a vertical spread that is about the same over the
entire plot, then the fixed-variation assumption holds.
Lag Plot:
If the lag plot is structureless, then the randomness
assumption holds.
Histogram:
If the histogram is bell-shaped, the underlying distribution
is symmetric and perhaps approximately normal.
Normal Probability Plot:
If the normal probability plot is linear, the underlying
distribution is approximately normal.
If all four of the assumptions hold, then the process
is said definitionally to be "in statistical control".

title: Consequences
text:
Exploratory Data Analysis (EDA) aims to produce scientifically sound conclusions by combining expert knowledge with data inspection to find patterns, outliers, and unexpected behaviors that reveal how a process actually works. This solves the practical engineering problem of avoiding faulty designs or missed opportunities by making sure you really understand your data before you build models or draw conclusions. Related search terms: understanding your data, finding anomalies, data patterns, what's really going on in my data, discovering hidden relationships, sanity-checking results, getting a feel for the data.

The primary goal is to have correct, validated, and complete
scientific/engineering conclusions flowing from the analysis. This
usually includes intermediate goals such as the derivation of a
good-fitting model and the computation of realistic parameter
estimates. It should always include the ultimate goal of an
understanding and a "feel" for "what makes the process tick".
There is no more powerful catalyst for discovery than the bringing
together of an experienced/expert scientist/engineer
and a data set ripe with intriguing "anomalies" and characteristics.

title: Introduction
text:
Exploratory Data Analysis (EDA) is a foundational approach that uses both graphical visualizations and quantitative measurements to understand patterns, outliers, and relationships in raw data before applying formal statistical tests. This solves the practical problem of getting familiar with your data upfront—spotting obvious problems, unexpected values, or interesting trends—so you don't waste time building models on messy or misunderstood information. Search synonyms: "getting to know your data," "initial data investigation," "data exploration," "data visualization and summary statistics," "preliminary data review," or "understanding data before analysis."

This section describes many techniques that are commonly
used in exploratory and classical data analysis. This list is by
no means meant to be exhaustive. Additional techniques (both
graphical and quantitative) are discussed in the other chapters.
Specifically, the
product comparisons
chapter has a much more detailed description of many classical
statistical techniques.
EDA emphasizes graphical techniques while classical techniques
emphasize quantitative techniques. In practice, an analyst typically
uses a mixture of graphical and quantitative techniques. In this
section, we have divided the descriptions into graphical and
quantitative techniques. This is for organizational clarity and is
not meant to discourage the use of both graphical and quantitiative
techniques when analyzing data.

title: Analysis Questions
text:
**Exploratory Data Analysis (EDA) question prioritization** helps engineers decide which specific data patterns matter most for their particular problem and organize their investigation in order of importance. Instead of randomly plotting data or running every possible test, this approach lets you filter out irrelevant questions upfront and focus your analysis time on what actually affects your engineering decision. You might also search for this using terms like "data analysis roadmap," "investigation planning," "focusing your data work," or "deciding what questions to ask about your data."

A critical early step in any analysis is to identify (for the
engineering problem at hand) which of the above questions are
relevant. That is, we need to identify which questions we want
answered and which questions have no bearing on the problem at hand.
After collecting such a set of questions, an equally important step,
which is invaluable for maintaining focus, is to prioritize those
questions in decreasing order of importance. EDA techniques are tied
in with each of the questions. There are some EDA techniques
(e.g., the scatter plot) that are broad-brushed and apply almost
universally. On the other hand, there are a large number of EDA
techniques that are specific and whose specificity is tied in with
one of the above questions. Clearly if one chooses not
to explicitly identify relevant questions, then one cannot take
advantage of these question-specific EDA technqiues.

title: Quantitative Techniques
text:
Hypothesis testing is a statistical method that uses sample data to evaluate whether a specific claim about a population parameter (like a mean or standard deviation) is likely to be true or false, helping engineers decide whether observed differences in their data are real or just random variation. This technique solves the practical problem of determining whether you can confidently reject a claimed value or specification—for example, testing if a manufacturing process actually meets its stated tolerance without measuring every single part. People searching for this concept might also look for "significance testing," "null hypothesis," "statistical testing," "does my data support this claim," or "how to prove something isn't true with sample data."

Hypothesis tests also address the uncertainty of the sample
estimate. However, instead of providing an interval, a
hypothesis test attempts to refute a specific claim about a
population parameter based on the sample data. For example,
the hypothesis might be one of the following:
the population mean is equal to 10
the population standard deviation is equal to 5
the means from two populations are equal
the standard deviations from 5 populations are equal
To reject a hypothesis is to conclude that it is
false. However, to accept a hypothesis does not mean that
it is true, only that we do not have evidence to
believe otherwise. Thus hypothesis tests are usually stated in
terms of both a condition that is doubted (null hypothesis)
and a condition that is believed (alternative hypothesis).
A common format for a hypothesis test is:
H
0
:
A statement of the null hypothesis, e.g., two population
means are equal.
H
a
:
A statement of the alternative hypothesis, e.g., two
population means are not equal.
Test Statistic:
The test statistic is based on the specific hypothesis test.
Significance Level:
The significance level, α,
defines the sensitivity of the test. A value of
α
= 0.05 means that we inadvertently reject the null hypothesis
5% of the time when it is in fact true. This is also called
the type I error. The choice of
α
is somewhat arbitrary, although in practice values of 0.1,
0.05, and 0.01 are commonly used.
The probability of rejecting the null hypothesis when it is
in fact false is called the power of the test and is
denoted by 1 -
β. Its complement, the probability of accepting
the null hypothesis when the alternative hypothesis is, in
fact, true (type II error), is called
β and can only be computed for a specific alternative
hypothesis.
Critical Region:
The critical region encompasses those values of the test
statistic that lead to a rejection of the null hypothesis.
Based on the distribution of the test statistic and
the significance level, a cut-off value for the test
statistic is computed. Values either above or below or
both (depending on the direction of the test) this
cut-off define the critical region.

title: Probability Distributions
text:
Probability distributions help you determine what statistical model fits your data and whether it's safe to use standard statistical tests that require specific assumptions about your data shape. This solves the practical problem of knowing whether your data is "normal enough" or follows some other pattern before you rely on confidence intervals or hypothesis tests. Someone might search for this using plain terms like "checking if data is normal," "testing data fit," "distribution fitting," "validating statistical assumptions," or "does my data match a bell curve."

Probability distributions are a fundamental concept in statistics.
They are used both on a theoretical level and a practical level.
Some practical uses of probability distributions are:
To calculate confidence intervals for parameters
and to calculate critical regions for hypothesis tests.
For univariate data, it is often useful to determine
a reasonable distributional model for the data.
Statistical intervals and hypothesis tests are often
based on specific distributional assumptions. Before
computing an interval or test based on a distributional
assumption, we need to verify that the assumption
is justified for the given data set. In this case, the
distribution does not need to be the best-fitting
distribution for the data, but an adequate enough model
so that the statistical technique yields valid conclusions.
Simulation studies with random numbers generated from
using a specific probability distribution are often needed.

title: EDA Case Studies
text:
**Exploratory Data Analysis (EDA) case studies demonstrate how to apply graphical and statistical techniques to understand real datasets by testing underlying assumptions and selecting appropriate methods based on what each analysis reveals.** This helps engineers and analysts figure out what's actually in their data—spotting patterns, problems, and surprises—before making decisions or building models. Someone looking for this might search for "how to analyze data before modeling," "checking data quality," "data investigation methods," "understanding your dataset," or "preliminary data examination."

This section presents a series of case studies that demonstrate
the application of EDA methods to specific problems. In some cases,
we have focused on just one EDA technique that uncovers virtually
all there is to know about the data. For other case studies, we
need several EDA techniques, the selection of which is dictated by
the outcome of the previous step in the analaysis sequence. Note
in these case studies how the flow of the analysis is motivated by
the focus on underlying assumptions and general EDA principles.

title: Case Studies Introduction
text:
When a manufacturing process is **statistically in control** with **predictability**, it follows the model Y_i = C + E_i, which separates what you can count on (the constant average C) from random noise (E_i)—solving the practical problem of knowing whether your process output is stable enough to make reliable forecasts and set realistic specs. Search for this concept using terms like "process stability," "in-control process," "stable process baseline," "predictable process," "process centering," or "separating signal from noise."

If the above assumptions are satisfied, the process is said to be
statistically "in control" with the core characteristic of having
"predictability". That is, probability statements can be made about
the process, not only in the past, but also in the future.
An appropriate model for an "in control" process is
Y
i
=
C
+
E
i
where
C
is a constant (the "deterministic" or
"structural" component), and where
E
i
is the
error term (or "random" component).
The constant
C
is the average value of the process--it
is the primary summary number which shows up on any report. Although
C
is (assumed) fixed, it is unknown, and so a primary
analysis objective of the engineer is to arrive at an estimate of
C
.
This goal partitions into 4 sub-goals:
Is the most common estimator of
C
,
\(\bar{Y}\), the best estimator for
C
? What does
"best" mean?
If
\(\bar{Y}\) is best, what is the uncertainty
\(s_{\bar{Y}}\) for
\(\bar{Y}\). In particular, is the usual formula for the
uncertainty of \(\bar{Y}\):
\( s_{\bar{Y}} = s/\sqrt{N} \)
valid? Here,
s
is the standard deviation of
the data and
N
is the sample size.
If
\(\bar{Y}\) is
not
the best estimator for
C
,
what is a better estimator for
C
(for example,
median, midrange, midmean)?
If there is a better estimator,
\(\hat{C}\), what is its uncertainty? That is, what is
\(s_{\hat{C}}\)?
EDA and the routine checking of underlying assumptions
provides insight into all of the above.
Location
and
variation
checks provide
information as to whether
C
is really constant.
Distributional checks indicate whether
\(\bar{Y}\) is the best estimator. Techniques for
distributional checking include
histograms
,
normal probability plots
,
and
probability
plot correlation coefficient plots
.
Randomness checks ascertain whether the usual
\( s_{\bar{Y}} = s/\sqrt{N} \)
is valid.
Distributional tests assist in determining a better estimator,
if needed.
Simulator tools (namely
bootstrapping
) provide
values for the uncertainty of alternative estimators.

title: Dataplot Commands for EDA Techniques
text:
The bootstrap plot is a Dataplot command that visualizes how stable a calculated statistic (like average, median, or standard deviation) is by repeatedly resampling your data and showing you the range of values you'd get—helping engineers understand if their measurements are reliable or if small data variations cause wild swings in results. Alternative searches: resampling visualization, sampling variability plot, statistical robustness check, confidence in your calculated numbers, how much does my answer change with different samples.

The Dataplot command for the bootstrap plot is
BOOTSTRAP <STAT> PLOT Y
where <STAT> is one of the following:
MEAN
MIDMEAN
MIDRANGE
MEDIAN
TRIMMED MEAN
WINSORIZED MEAN
GEOMETRIC MEAN
HARMONIC MEAN
SUM
PRODUCT
MINIMUM
MAXIMUM
STANDARD DEVIATION
VARIANCE
STANDARD DEVIATION OF MEAN
VARIANCE OF MEAN
RELATIVE STANDARD DEVIATION
RELATIVE VARIANCE
AVERAGE ABSOLUTE DEVIATION
MEDIAN ABSOLUTE DEVIATION
LOWER QUARTILE
LOWER HINGE
UPPER QUARTILE
UPPER HINGE
FIRST DECILE
SECOND DECILE
THIRD DECILE
FOURTH DECILE
FIFTH DECILE
SIXTH DECILE
SEVENTH DECILE
EIGHTH DECILE
NINTH DECILE
PERCENTILE
SKEWNESS
KURTOSIS
AUTOCORRELATION
AUTOCOVARIANCE
SINE FREQUENCY
COSINE FREQUENCY
TAGUCHI SN0
TAGUCHI SN+
TAGUCHI SN-
TAGUCHI SN00
The BOOTSTRAP PLOT command is almost always followed by
a
histogram
or some other
distributional plot.
Dataplot automatically stores the following internal parameters
after a BOOTSTRAP PLOT command:
BMEAN - mean of the plotted bootstrap values
BSD - standard deviation of the plotted bootstrap values
B001 - the 0.1 percentile of the plotted bootstrap values
B005 - the 0.5 percentile of the plotted bootstrap values
B01 - the 1.0 percentile of the plotted bootstrap values
B025 - the 2.5 percentile of the plotted bootstrap values
B05 - the 5.0 percentile of the plotted bootstrap values
B10 - the 10 percentile of the plotted bootstrap values
B20 - the 20 percentile of the plotted bootstrap values
B80 - the 80 percentile of the plotted bootstrap values
B90 - the 90 percentile of the plotted bootstrap values
B95 - the 95 percentile of the plotted bootstrap values
B975 - the 97.5 percentile of the plotted bootstrap values
B99 - the 99 percentile of the plotted bootstrap values
B995 - the 99.5 percentile of the plotted bootstrap values
B999 - the 99.9 percentile of the plotted bootstrap values
These internal parameters are useful for generating confidence
intervals and can be printed (PRINT BMEAN) or used as any
user-defined parameter could (e.g., LET UCL = B95).
To specify the number of bootstrap subsamples to use, enter
the command
BOOTSTRAP SAMPLE <N>
where <N> is the number of samples you want. The
default is 500 (it may be 100 in older implementations).
Dataplot can also generate bootstrap estimates for statistics
that are not directly supported. The following example
shows a bootstrap calculation for the mean of 500 normal
random numbers. Although we can do this directly in
Dataplot, this demonstrates the steps necessary for an
unsupported statistic. The subsamples are generated
with a loop. The BOOTSTRAP INDEX and BOOTSTRAP SAMPLE commands
generate a single subsample which is stored in Y2. The
desired statistic is then calculated for Y2 and the result
stored in an array. After the loop, the array XMEAN contains
the 100 mean values.
LET Y = NORMAL RANDOM NUMBERS FOR I = 1 1 500
LET N = SIZE Y
LOOP FOR K = 1 1 500
LET IND = BOOTSTRAP INDEX FOR I = 1 1 N
LET Y2 = BOOTSTRAP SAMPLE Y IND
LET A = MEAN Y2
LET XMEAN(K) = A
END OF LOOP
HISTOGRAM XMEAN

title: What is a check standard?
text:
A **check standard** is a measurement tracking tool that uses **process control** and **repeatability assessment** to monitor whether your measurement system stays consistent over time by repeatedly measuring the same stable artifact or reference point. This solves the practical problem of catching when your measurement equipment drifts, gets out of calibration, or starts giving unreliable results—so you know which data you can trust and which batches might be bad. You might also search for this concept as "control sample," "reference check," "process stability monitoring," "measurement drift detection," or "ongoing measurement verification."

The check standard should be thought of in terms of a database of
measurements. It can be defined as an artifact or as a characteristic
of the measurement process whose value can be replicated from
measurements taken over the life of the process. Examples are:
measurements on a stable artifact
differences between values of two reference standards as
estimated from a calibration experiment
values of a process characteristic, such as a bias term, which
is estimated from measurements on reference standards and/or
test items.
An artifact check standard must be close in material content and
geometry to the test items that are measured in the workload. If
possible, it should be one of the test items from the workload.
Obviously, it should be a stable artifact and should be available to
the measurement process at all times.

title: Statistical control of a measurement process
chapter: What are the issues for control of a measurement process?
text:
Statistical control of a measurement process uses control charts and check standards to catch when your measurement equipment or procedure drifts out of acceptable bounds—especially detecting sneaky long-term bias shifts that don't show up as obvious day-to-day variation. Someone hunting for this concept might search for "how to monitor measurement accuracy over time," "detecting instrument drift," "validating measurement uncertainty," or "keeping measurement systems stable."

The purpose of this section is to outline the steps that can be taken to exercise statistical control over the measurement process and demonstrate the validity of the uncertainty statement. Measurement processes can change both with respect to bias and variability. A change in instrument precision may be readily noted as measurements are being recorded, but changes in bias or long-term variability are difficult to catch when the process is looking at a multitude of artifacts over time.
What are the issues for control of a measurement process?

title: What are the issues in controlling the measurement process?
text:
**Control charts and statistical process control use check standards to detect when measurement equipment starts giving different results than before, catching problems like instruments drifting out of calibration or becoming less precise.** This solves the practical problem of knowing whether your measurements are still reliable or if something has changed in your equipment or process. People searching for this concept might look for: "monitoring measurement drift," "detecting instrument calibration changes," "process control with reference standards," "statistical tests for measurement consistency," or "tracking measurement system performance over time."

Measurements on a
check standard
provide the mechanism for controlling the measurement process.
Measurements on the check standard should produce identical results
except for the effect of random errors, and tests for control are
basically tests of whether or not the random errors from the process
continue to be drawn from the same statistical distribution as the
historical data on the check standard.
Changes that can be monitored and tested with the check standard
database are:
Changes in bias and long-term variability
Changes in instrument precision or short-term
variability

title: How are bias and variability controlled?
text:
This section covers **bias control** and **variability monitoring** using check standards—a practical method for catching when your measurement system drifts, breaks down, or gets used wrong by tracking how repeated readings of a known reference standard change over time. An engineer wondering "why are my measurements suddenly off?" or "how do I know when my equipment needs maintenance?" would use this approach to spot problems before they ruin product quality. Related searches include: drift detection, measurement system validation, standard reference tracking, quality control monitoring, process stability checks, and instrument calibration verification.

Bias and long-term variability are controlled by monitoring measurements
on a check standard over time. A change in the measurement on the check
standard that persists at a constant level over several measurement
sequences indicates possible:
Change or damage to the reference standards
Change or damage to the check standard artifact
Procedural change that vitiates the assumptions of the
measurement process
A change in the variability of the measurements on the check standard
can be due to one of many causes such as:
Loss of environmental controls
Change in handling techniques
Severe degradation in instrumentation.

title: Calibration
chapter: What are the issues for calibration?
text:
Calibration procedures use **bias reduction** and **reference standardization** to ensure measurement systems produce accurate results by systematically comparing test items against known standards, which solves the practical engineering problem of making sure your instruments and equipment give you readings you can actually trust and compare across different labs or time periods. Engineers and technicians might also search for this using terms like "measurement accuracy," "eliminating systematic error," "standardizing instruments," "traceability to standards," or "zeroing out instrument drift."

The purpose of this section is to outline the procedures for calibrating artifacts and instruments while guaranteeing the 'goodness' of the calibration results. Calibration is a measurement process that assigns values to the property of an artifact or to the response of an instrument relative to reference standards or to a designated measurement process. The purpose of calibration is to eliminate or reduce bias in the user's measurement system relative to the reference base. The calibration procedure compares an "unknown" or test item(s) or instrument with reference standards according to a specific algorithm.

title: What is artifact (single-point) calibration?
text:
Artifact (single-point) calibration uses the statistical concept of **differencing** or **subtraction of paired measurements** to eliminate systematic bias by comparing a test item against a nearly identical reference standard on the same instrument. This solves the practical problem of removing measurement errors caused by the instrument itself—like drift or offset—so you get an accurate reading of just the difference between your part and the reference, without having to trust that the instrument's zero or baseline is correct. Engineers might search for this under alternative names like "comparative measurement," "reference standard method," "two-artifact comparison," "bias cancellation through differencing," or "relative measurement calibration."

One approach to eliminating bias is to select a
reference standard that is almost identical to the test item; measure
the two artifacts with a comparator type of instrument; and take the
difference of the two measurements to cancel the bias. The only
requirement on the instrument is that it be linear over the small range
needed for the two artifacts.
The test item has value
X*
,
as yet to be assigned, and the reference standard has an assigned value
R*
. Given a measurement,
X
,
on the test item and a measurement,
R
, on the reference standard,
\begin{eqnarray}
X = Bias + X^* + error_1 \\
R = Bias + R^* + error_2
\end{eqnarray}
the difference between the test item and the reference is estimated by
$$ D = X - R \, ,$$
and the value of the test item is reported as
$$ \widehat{Test} = X^* = D + R^* \, .$$

title: Catalog of calibration designs
text:
This page covers **calibration design factors** and **measurement uncertainty propagation** for combining repeatability standard deviations across test items, solving the practical problem of predicting how accurate your calibration or measurement result will be when you test one item versus multiple items together. Alternative search terms include: measurement error accumulation, combining measurement uncertainties, precision factors for calibration, standard deviation multiplication factors, and how measurement error changes with sample size or combined tests.

The factors in this table provide information on precision.
The repeatability standard deviation,
\( {\large s}_1 \),
is multiplied by the appropriate factor to obtain the standard
deviation for an individual item or combination of items. For
example,

implies that the standard deviations for the estimates are:
\begin{array} \(

{\large s}_{{test}_{ \, 1}} = 0.8661 \cdot {\large s}_1 \\
{\large s}_{{test}_{ \, 2}} = 0.8661 \cdot {\large s}_1 \\

title: Control of artifact calibration
text:
A **check standard** is a reference artifact used to monitor **bias** and **long-term variability** in calibration processes by measuring it repeatedly alongside test items to verify that measurement results stay consistent and correct over time. This solves the practical problem of catching when your calibration system drifts out of spec or starts giving wrong answers, so you know when equipment needs adjustment or recalibration. People might search for this concept using terms like "quality control sample," "reference standard monitoring," "calibration verification," "detecting measurement drift," or "process control artifact."

Measurements on a check standard provide the mechanism for controlling
the bias and long-term variability of the calibration process. The
check standard is treated as one of the test items in the calibration
design, and its value as computed from each calibration run is the
basis for accepting or rejecting the calibration. All designs
cataloged in this Handbook have provision for a check standard.
The check standard should be of the same type and geometry as items
that are measured in the designs. These artifacts must be stable and
available to the calibration process on a continuing basis. There
should be a check standard at each critical level of measurement.
For example, for mass calibrations there should be check standards
at the 1 kg; 100 g, 10 g, 1 g, 0.1 g levels, etc. For gage blocks,
there should be check standards at all nominal lengths.
A check standard can also be a mathematical construction,
such as the computed difference between the calibrated values of
two reference standards in a design.

title: Instrument calibration over a regime
text:
**Calibration curves and regression modeling** enable engineers to convert readings from one measurement system (like a densitometer) into values that match a reference standard (like radiation dosage), solving the practical problem of using cheaper or faster proxy instruments instead of gold-standard measurement tools. Alternative search terms include "instrument conversion," "surrogate measurement validation," "reading-to-standard transformation," "indirect measurement calibration," and "converting instrument output to actual values."

The instrument reads in different units than the reference
standards. The purpose of the calibration is to convert the
instrument readings to the units of interest. An example is
densitometer measurements that act as surrogates for
measurements of radiation dosage. For this purpose, reference
standards are irradiated at several dosage levels and then
measured by radiometry. The same reference standards are
measured by densitometer. The calibrated results of future
densitometer readings on medical devices are the basis for
deciding if the devices have been sterilized at the proper
radiation level.

title: Instrument control for linear calibration
text:
This page covers **t-distribution critical values** and **Bonferroni correction** for instrument calibration verification, solving the practical problem of how to set acceptable pass/fail limits when checking multiple calibration standards without letting measurement errors accumulate and give false rejections. Engineers looking for this information might search for "how to compare multiple test measurements to a standard," "checking several calibration points at once," "adjusting acceptance criteria for multiple checks," or "statistical limits for instrument validation."

For the case where \( \alpha = 0.05 \)
and \( \nu = 38 \),
the critical value of the \( t^* \)
statistic is 2.497575.
R code
and
Dataplot code
can be used to determine \( t^* \)
critical values using a standard
\(t\)-table for the
\( \zeta \)
quantile and \( \nu \)
degrees of freedom where \( \zeta \)
is computed as
$$ \zeta = \frac{1}{2} \left[ 1 - e^{\frac{\ln (1-\alpha)}{m}} \right] $$
where
m
is the number of check standards.

title: Gauge R & R studies
chapter: What are the issues for a gauge R & R study?
text:
Gauge R&R studies use repeatability and reproducibility analysis to figure out how much error comes from your measurement equipment versus how much comes from different operators or conditions—basically breaking down whether your gauges are giving you reliable, consistent numbers. If you're trying to understand measurement uncertainty, gage variation studies, or whether your instruments are trustworthy enough for quality control, this is what you need. People also search for this under terms like "measurement system analysis," "gage capability," "operator variation," or "instrument validation."

The purpose of this section is to outline the steps that can be
taken to characterize the performance of gauges and instruments
used in a production setting in terms of errors that affect the
measurements.

study?

Operators

configurations

title: Design considerations
text:
This passage addresses **measurement system variation** and **operator effect** quantification in gauge R&R (repeatability and reproducibility) studies by establishing sampling strategies for operators across different operational contexts. It solves the practical problem of figuring out whether differences in measurements come from the gauge itself, the person using it, or natural variation—and how many different people you need to test to get a reliable answer about your measurement system. Search alternatives: "how many operators to test," "operator bias in measurement," "sampling operators for gage repeatability study," "reducing measurement error from different workers," or "validating gauge consistency across different users."

Only those operators who are trained and experienced with the gauges
should be enlisted in the study, with the following constraints:
If there is a small number of operators who are familiar with
the gauges, they should all be included in the study.
If the study is intended to be representative of a large pool
of operators, then a random sample of
L (L > 2)
operators
should be chosen from the pool.
If there is only one operator for the gauge type, that
operator should make measurements on
K (K > 2)
days.

title: Data collection for time-related sources of variability
chapter:
Simple design for 2 levels of random
       error
text:
Nested designs and simple designs for analyzing time-dependent measurement errors help engineers figure out where mistakes are coming from in their testing by collecting data at different time intervals—like measuring the same part repeatedly over hours or days to see if results change. Instead of guessing whether errors come from the instrument drifting over time, the operator, or temperature changes throughout the day, these designs let you separate and measure each source of variation. People might also search for "hierarchical designs for time-varying errors," "multi-level measurement error analysis," "temporal variability breakdown," or "how to handle measurements that change over time."

Simple design for 2 levels of random
error
Nested design for 2 levels of random
error
Nested design for 3 levels of random
error
In all cases, data collection and analysis are straightforward, and
there is no reason to estimate interaction terms when dealing with
time-dependent errors.
Two levels
should be
sufficient for characterizing most measurement systems.
Three levels
are recommended for measurement
systems where sources of error are not well understood and have not
previously been studied.

title: Analysis of variability
text:
This page covers **pooled standard deviation, variance components analysis, and measurement uncertainty quantification** for characterizing the total variability in a measurement system by breaking it into separate sources (within-run, day-to-day, and run-to-run variation). It solves the practical problem of figuring out what the real uncertainty is for a single measurement on a part by accounting for all the different ways things can vary—whether differences come from the instrument itself, environmental changes between days, or different setups between measurement runs. You might search for this using terms like "measurement system variability breakdown," "combining multiple sources of error," "total uncertainty from different factors," "what's the real measurement error," or "how much variation comes from different parts of my process."

The standard deviation that defines the uncertainty for a single
measurement on a test item is given by
$$ {\large s}_R = \sqrt{{\large s}_{runs}^2 + {\large s}_{days}^2 + {\large s}_1^2}
= \sqrt{{\large s}_3^2 + \frac{K-1}{K} {\large s}_2^2 + \frac{J-1}{J} {\large s}_1^2} \,\, , $$
where the pooled values, \( {\large s}_1 \)
and
\( {\large s}_2 \),
are the usual
$$ {\large s}_1 = \sqrt{\frac{\sum_{l=1}^{L}{\sum_{k=1}^{K}{{\large s}_{1lk}^{2}}}}{LK}} $$
and
$$ {\large s}_2 = \sqrt{\frac{1}{L} \sum_{l=1}^{L}{{\large s}_{2l}^{2}}} \,\,\, . $$
The time-dependent components can be computed individually as:
$$ {\large s}_{runs} = \sqrt{{\large s}_3^2 - \frac{1}{K} {\large s_2^2}} $$
$$ {\large s}_{days} = \sqrt{{\large s}_2^2 - \frac{1}{J} {\large s_1^2}} \,\,\, .$$
There may be other sources of uncertainty in the measurement process
that must be accounted for in a formal
analysis of uncertainty.

title: Quantifying uncertainties from a gauge study
text:
A **gauge study** (also called **gage R&R** or **measurement system analysis**) quantifies measurement process variability by calculating standard deviation, but the core problem is that if your measurement equipment or process drifts or changes over time, your uncertainty numbers become wrong—so the practical fix is to run the gauge study before and after your actual measurements to check whether the process stayed stable or changed. This approach addresses **process stability** and **temporal validation** of measurement uncertainty. Alternative searches: "checking if measurement equipment drifts," "validating that a gauge didn't change," "before-and-after measurement verification," "confirming measurement system stays consistent," or "detecting measurement process drift over time."

The potential problem with this approach is that the calculation of
uncertainty depends totally on the gauge study. If the measurement
process changes its characteristics over time, the standard deviation
from the gauge study will not be the correct standard deviation for the
uncertainty analysis. One way to try to avoid such a problem is to
carry out a gauge study both before and after the measurements that
are being characterized for uncertainty. The 'before' and 'after'
results should indicate whether or not the measurement process changed
in the interim.

title: Uncertainty analysis
chapter:
What are the issues for uncertainty
       analysis?
text:
This section covers **Type A and Type B uncertainty evaluations**, which are the two main statistical methods for measuring and documenting all the sources of error in a measurement—helping engineers figure out how much they can trust their test results and what's causing the scatter in their data. Someone looking for this guidance might search for "how to estimate measurement error," "error budget," "uncertainty quantification," "sources of measurement variability," or "combining random and systematic errors."

What are the issues for uncertainty
analysis?
Approach to uncertainty analysis

inhomogeneities

analysis

bias

bias

sparse data

measurements on the test item
