title: Issues
text:
This passage addresses **prediction intervals versus confidence intervals** and the challenges of using **pooled standard deviation** across multiple laboratories for measurement uncertainty estimation. In practical terms, it solves the problem of how to estimate what range a single future measurement will fall into when you're combining data from multiple labs with different levels of quality and consistency—but the current method can unfairly penalize good labs or reward bad ones. Someone looking for this concept might search for terms like "inter-laboratory variability," "combining measurement uncertainty across labs," "prediction interval for future measurements," or "pooled versus individual lab performance in uncertainty budgets."

The standard deviation computed in this manner describes a future
single measurement made at a laboratory randomly drawn from the group
and leads to a prediction interval
(Hahn & Meeker)
rather than a
confidence interval. It is not an ideal solution and may produce
either an unrealistically small or unacceptably large uncertainty for
a particular laboratory. The procedure can reward laboratories with
poor performance or those that do not follow the test procedures to the
letter and punish laboratories with good performance. Further, the
procedure does not take into account sources of uncertainty other than
those captured in the interlaboratory test. Because the interlaboratory
test is a snapshot at one point in time, characteristics of the
measurement process over time cannot be accurately evaluated.
Therefore, it is a strategy to be used only where there is no
possibility of conducting a realistic uncertainty investigation.

title: Type A evaluations
text:
This page explains **random effects versus fixed effects** (or **between-unit variation versus within-unit variation**) in measurement uncertainty: it helps you figure out whether instrument differences should be treated as unpredictable variation that applies to any random tool you pick, or as a consistent offset tied to one specific machine you're using. Engineers trying to decide whether their uncertainty budget should account for "any instrument like this one" versus "this exact instrument" need to understand this distinction to report the right margin of error. Synonyms include: instrument-to-instrument variation, batch uncertainty, specific versus typical instrument analysis, or whether to pool data across multiple tools.

How the source of error affects the reported value and the context
for the uncertainty determines whether an analysis of random error
or bias is appropriate.
Consider a laboratory with several instruments that can reasonably be
assumed to be representative of all similar instruments. Then the
differences among these instruments can be considered to be a random
effect if the uncertainty statement is intended to apply to the result
of any instrument, selected at random, from this batch.
If, on the other hand, the uncertainty statement is intended to apply
to one specific instrument, then the bias of this instrument relative
to the group is the component of interest.

title: Type B evaluations
text:
Type B uncertainty evaluations use uniform, triangular, and normal distributions to convert worst-case limits into standard deviations for measurement sources that can't be repeatedly tested—solving the practical problem of quantifying error contributions from things like instrument calibration drift, environmental effects, or limited historical data when you only know the maximum possible error, not its actual spread. Someone looking for this concept might search for "how to estimate uncertainty without repeated measurements," "converting error limits to standard deviation," "non-statistical uncertainty analysis," or "estimating measurement error from experience and judgment."

Sources of uncertainty that are local to the measurement
process but which cannot be adequately sampled to allow a statistical
analysis require type B evaluations. One technique, which is widely
used, is to estimate the worst-case effect,
a,
for the source of interest, from
experience
scientific judgment
scant data
A standard deviation, assuming that the effect is two-sided, can then be
computed based on a
uniform
,
triangular
, or
normal distribution
of possible effects.
Following the
Guide to the
Expression of Uncertainty of Measurement (GUM)
, the convention
is to assign
infinite degrees of freedom
to
standard deviations derived in this manner.

title: Propagation of error considerations
text:
This section covers **variance of a product** and **error propagation for multiplicative measurements**, solving the practical problem of predicting how measurement uncertainties in two dimensions (like width and length) combine and affect the final uncertainty when you multiply them together to get area or volume. Engineers might search for this using terms like "uncertainty in products," "how errors multiply," "combined measurement error for area calculations," "error propagation through multiplication," or "variance of product of two variables."

Goodman (1960)
derived
an exact formula for the variance between two products. Given
two random variables, \(x\) and \(y\) (correspond to width
and length in the above approximate formula), the exact formula for
the variance is:
$$ V(\bar{x} \bar{y}) = \frac{1}{n} \left[
X^2 V(y) + Y^2 V(x) + 2XYE_{11} + 2X\frac{E_{12}}{n} + 2Y\frac{E_{21}}{n}
+ \frac{V(x) V(y)}{n} + \frac{Cov((\Delta x)^2, (\Delta y)^2) -E_{11}^2 }{n^2}
\right] $$
with
\(X = E(x)\) and \(Y = E(y)\) (corresponds to width and length,
respectively, in the approximate formula)
\(V(x)\) is the variance of \(x\) and \(V(y)\)
is the variance \(y\)
(corresponds to \(s^2\) for width and length,
respectively, in the approximate formula)
\( E_{ij} = {(\Delta x)^i, (\Delta y)^j}\) where \( \Delta x = x - X \)
and \( \Delta y = y - Y \)
\( Cov((\Delta x)^2, (\Delta y)^2) = E_{22} - V(x) V(y) \)
To obtain the standard deviation, simply take the square root of the
above formula. Also, an estimate of the statistic is obtained by
substituting sample estimates for the corresponding population
values on the right hand side of the equation.

title: Uncertainty budgets and sensitivity coefficients
text:
This page explains sensitivity coefficients—the multipliers that show how much each source of measurement variation (repeatability and reproducibility) affects your final result—which lets you identify and prioritize which parts of your measurement process are causing the most uncertainty in your answer. Engineers looking to reduce measurement error can search for "how much does equipment variation vs. operator skill vs. environmental conditions affect my measurement," "which source of error matters most," or "weighting factors for different types of measurement uncertainty."

This section defines sensitivity coefficients that are appropriate
for type A components estimated from repeated measurements. The
pages on
type A evaluations
, particularly the
pages related to
estimation of repeatability and
reproducibility components
, should be reviewed before continuing on
this page. The convention for the notation for sensitivity
coefficients for this section is that:
\(a_1\)
refers to the
sensitivity coefficient for the repeatability standard deviation,
\(s_1\)
\(a_2\)
refers to the
sensitivity coefficient for the reproducibility standard deviation,
\(s_2\)
\(a_3\)
refers to the
sensitivity coefficient for the stability standard deviation,
\(s_3\)
with some of the coefficients possibly equal to zero.

title: Standard and expanded uncertainties
text:
Expanded uncertainty (U = k·u) solves the practical problem of scaling up your measurement uncertainty to guarantee you're capturing the true value most of the time—like saying "my measurement is 10.5 ± 0.3 mm, and I'm 95% confident the real value falls in that range" instead of just reporting the raw measurement scatter. The t-distribution critical value (k factor) does the heavy lifting by accounting for how many tests you ran and how confident you want to be, so you're not over-promising or under-protecting your results. People looking for this concept might search for coverage factor, confidence interval for measurements, scaling measurement uncertainty, 95% confidence bounds, or how to convert standard deviation to a safe tolerance range.

If the purpose of the uncertainty statement is to provide coverage
with a high level of confidence, an expanded uncertainty is
computed as
$$ U = k \cdot u $$
where \(k\) is chosen to be the
\( t_{1-\alpha/2, \,\nu}\)
critical value
from the
t
-table
with
\(\nu\)
degrees of freedom
. For large
degrees of freedom, \(k = 2\) approximates 95 % coverage.

title: Treatment of uncorrected bias
text:
This guidance covers **uncertainty quantification with uncorrected bias** and **coverage probability alignment**, addressing the practical problem of how to safely report measurement uncertainty when you know a systematic error exists but choose not to correct it—ensuring your stated uncertainty range will actually contain the true value as often as you claim it does. Alternative searches: "how to handle known systematic errors in measurements," "uncertainty when not correcting for bias," "conservative uncertainty statements," "bias adjustment versus uncertainty padding," or "what uncertainty do I report when I know something's wrong but can't fix it."

A method needs to be developed which assures that the resulting
uncertainty has the following properties
(
Phillips and Eberhardt
):
The final uncertainty must be greater than or equal to the
uncertainty that would be quoted if the bias were corrected.
The final uncertainty must reduce to the same uncertainty
given that the bias correction is applied.
The level of coverage that is achieved by the final
uncertainty statement should be at least the level obtained
for the case of corrected bias.
The method should be transferable so that both the
uncertainty and the bias can be used as components of
uncertainty in another uncertainty statement.
The method should be easy to implement.

title: Case studies
text:
This section covers **measurement process characterization, Type A and Type B uncertainty analysis, and error propagation** — practical methods that help engineers figure out how accurate their lab equipment and measurements really are and what's causing the variation they see in their data. Someone looking to solve this problem might search for "how to calculate measurement uncertainty," "estimating equipment error," "gauge repeatability study," "check standard validation," or "combining different sources of measurement error."

The purpose of this section is to illustrate the planning,
procedures, and analyses outlined in the various sections of
this chapter with data taken from measurement processes at
the National Institute of Standards and Technology. A
secondary goal is to give the reader an opportunity to run
the analyses in real-time using the software package, Dataplot.
Gauge study of resistivity
probes
Check standard study for resistivity
measurements
Type A uncertainty analysis
Type B uncertainty analysis and
propagation of error

title: Evaluation of type B uncertainty and propagation of error
text:
This passage covers **Type B uncertainty evaluation** and **error propagation**, which allow engineers to quantify total measurement uncertainty when you have bounds on multiple independent error sources (like instrument resolution and calibration errors) rather than repeated measurements. It solves the practical problem of combining different kinds of measurement errors—some from equipment limitations, some from known calibration mistakes—into a single uncertainty budget that tells you how much your final answer might be off. Engineers searching for this might look for "combining measurement errors," "uncertainty from bounds," "non-statistical uncertainty estimation," or "how to add up different sources of error in my measurements."

There are two basic sources of uncertainty for the electrical
measurements. The first is the least-count of the digital volt meter
in the measurement of
X
with a maximum bound of
\( a = 0.0000534 \,\, ohm \)
which is assumed to be the half-width of a uniform distribution. The
second is the uncertainty of the electrical scale factor. This has two
sources of uncertainty:
error in the solution of the transcendental equation for
determining the factor
errors in measured voltages
The maximum bounds to these errors are assumed to be half-widths of
\( a = 0.0001 \,\, ohm.cm \) and \( a = 0.00038 \,\, ohm.cm \)
respectively, from
uniform distributions.
The corresponding standard deviations are shown below.
\( s_X = 0.0000534 / \sqrt{3} = 0.0000308 \,\, ohmc.cm \)
\( \displaystyle
s_{Ka} = \sqrt{\frac{0.0001^2}{3} + \frac{0.00038^2}{3}} = 0.000227 \,\, ohm.cm
\)

title: What is PPC?
text:
Production Process Characterization (PPC) uses screening experiments and response surface methodology to identify which process inputs actually matter and how they affect your product quality, solving the common manufacturing problem of figuring out which knobs to turn when you have too many variables to control. If you're trying to understand your factory process without getting buried in data, you might search for "design of experiments," "DOE," "factor screening," "which process variables matter most," or "how to map what inputs affect my output."

This activity is typically a three-step process.
The Screening Step
In this phase we identify all possible significant process inputs and outputs
and conduct a series of screening experiments in order to reduce that list
to the key inputs and outputs. These experiments will also allow us
to develop initial models of the relationships between those inputs
and outputs.
The Mapping Step
In this step we map the behavior of the key outputs over their expected
operating ranges. We do this through a series of more detailed experiments
called Response Surface experiments.
The Passive Step
In this step we allow the process to run at nominal conditions and estimate
the process stability and capability.

title: PPC Steps
text:
Production Process Characterization (PPC) planning establishes a structured approach to efficiently gather manufacturing data by defining clear objectives, mapping process inputs and outputs, and assigning data collection responsibilities—solving the practical problem of avoiding wasted time collecting the wrong measurements or duplicating effort on a factory floor. This concept is also known as experimental design, process study planning, or data collection strategy, and helps engineers figure out what to actually measure and how to measure it before they start running expensive production trials.

The most important step by far is the planning step.
By faithfully executing this step, we will ensure that we only collect
data in the most efficient manner possible and still support the goals
of the PPC. Planning should generate the following:
a statement of the goals
a descriptive process model (a list of process inputs and
outputs)
a description of the sampling plan (including a description of
the procedure and settings to be used to run the process during
the study with clear assignments for each person involved)
a description of the method of data collection, tasks and
responsibilities, formatting, and storage
an outline of the data analysis
All decisions that affect how the characterization will be conducted should
be made during the planning phase. The process characterization should
be conducted according to this plan, with all exceptions noted.

title: Assumptions / Prerequisites
text:
Production Process Characterization (PPC) uses statistical modeling and factor analysis to identify which process inputs cause unwanted variation in product quality or performance. This helps engineers figure out what's actually making their process inconsistent so they can fix the real problems instead of guessing. People looking for this might search for "what causes process variation," "process troubleshooting," "finding root causes of defects," "process optimization," or "identifying critical process parameters."

The primary goal of PPC is to identify and quantify
sources of variation. Only by doing this will we be able to define an effective
plan for variation reduction and process improvement. Sometimes, in order
to achieve this goal, we must first build mathematical/statistical models
of our processes. In these models we will identify influential factors
and the responses on which they have an effect. We will use these models
to understand how the sources of variation are influenced by the important
factors. This subsection will review many of the modeling tools we have
at our disposal to accomplish these tasks. In particular, the models covered
in this section are linear models, Analysis of Variance (ANOVA) models
and discrete models.

title: General Assumptions
text:
This page covers **decomposition of process variation into systematic and random components**, which solves the practical problem of separating what your process naturally does (the predictable pattern) from what it does by chance (the noise and measurement error). You might search for this concept using terms like "breaking down process behavior," "signal versus noise in manufacturing," "separating real trends from random fluctuation," or "modeling process consistency."

In order to employ the modeling techniques described in this section,
there are a few assumptions about the process under study that must be
made. First, we must assume that the process can adequately be modeled
as the sum of a systematic component and a random component. The systematic
component is the mathematical model part and the random component is the
error or noise present in the system. We also assume that the systematic
component is fixed over the range of operating conditions and that the
random component has a constant location, spread and distributional form.

title: Continuous Linear Model
text:
This page covers **Continuous Linear Models (CLM)** and **Shewhart Control Charts**, which help engineers predict and monitor product quality by modeling measurements as a constant value plus random variation. The practical problem it solves is detecting when a manufacturing process (like a diffusion furnace) has drifted out of control or identifying which operating conditions (temperature, location, time) are affecting the final product specification. Engineers looking for this concept might search for "process monitoring models," "quality control baselines," "how to set control limits," or "relating process settings to product outcomes."

Shewhart Control Chart
- The simplest example of
a very common usage of the CLM is the underlying model used for Shewhart
control charts. This model assumes that the process parameter being
measured is a constant with additive Gaussian noise and is given by:
\( y = a_{0} + \epsilon \)
Diffusion Furnace - Suppose we want to model the average wafer sheet
resistance as a function of the location or zone in a furnace tube, the
temperature, and the anneal time. In this case, let there be 3 distinct
zones (front, center, back) and temperature and time are continuous explanatory
variables.  This model is given by the CLM:
\[ y = a_{0} + \left\{
\begin{array}{ll}
a_{1} & \mbox{if front} \\
a_{2} + a_{4}\mbox{temp} + a_{5} \mbox{time} + \epsilon &
\mbox{if center} \\
a_{3} & \mbox{if back}
\end{array}
\right.
\]
Diffusion Furnace (cont.) - Usually, the fitted line for the average
wafer sheet resistance is not straight but has some curvature to it. This
can be accommodated by adding a quadratic term for the time parameter as
follows:
\[ y = a_{0} + \left\{
\begin{array}{ll}
a_{1} & \mbox{if front} \\
a_{2} + a_{4}\mbox{temp} + a_{5} \mbox{time} +
a_{6} \mbox{time}^2 + \epsilon & \mbox{if center} \\
a_{3} & \mbox{if back}
\end{array}
\right.
\]

title: First Steps
text:
The scatter plot and histogram are foundational exploratory data analysis tools used in production process characterization to catch mistakes like typos in data entry, spot weird numbers that don't make sense, and find missing values before analysis begins. An engineer trying to verify their measurement data is clean and trustworthy would use these plots to visually inspect whether the numbers look reasonable and whether all expected data points are present. You might also search for this under "data quality checks," "data validation," or "identifying bad data points."

The next step is to perform a quality check on the data. Here we are
typically looking for data entry problems, unusual data values, missing
data, etc. The two most useful tools for this step are the
scatter plot
and the
histogram
.
By constructing scatter plots of all of the response variables,
any data entry problems will be easily identified.  Histograms of
response variables are also quite useful for identifying data entry problems.
Histograms of explanatory variables help identify problems with the execution
of the sampling plan. If the counts for each level of the explanatory
variables are not the same as called for in the sampling plan, you know
you may have an execution problem. Running numerical summary statistics
on all of the variables (both response and explanatory) also helps to identify
data problems.

title: Exploring Relationships
text:
This page covers **pairwise graphing, main effects analysis, and interaction detection** — practical techniques for figuring out which process inputs actually matter and whether certain input combinations work together in unexpected ways rather than independently. An engineer troubleshooting a production process would use this three-step visual approach to move from "what variables are related?" to "which knobs do I actually need to turn?" to "does changing one knob differently depending on another knob's setting?" Alternative searches: detecting factor interactions, finding main effects, conditional plotting, visualizing process dependencies, understanding variable relationships, nested boxplot analysis, or block plot interpretation.

The order that generally proves most effective for data analysis is
to first graph all of the responses against each other
in a pairwise fashion. Then we graph responses against the
explanatory variables. This will give an indication of the main
factors that have an effect on response variables. Finally, we
graph response variables, conditioned on the levels of explanatory
factors. This is what reveals interactions between explanatory
variables. We will use nested
boxplots
and
block plots
to visualize
interactions.

title: Analyzing Variance Structure
text:
This page teaches **variance structure analysis** and **nested/hierarchical sampling design**, which helps engineers figure out which parts of a furnace (or any equipment with multiple zones) are causing inconsistent results in their product quality. Someone looking for this concept might search for "where is variation coming from in my process," "how to identify problem areas in equipment," "comparing consistency across different sections," or "understanding layer thickness variation by location."

The example is a furnace operation in semiconductor manufacture where
we are growing an oxide layer on a wafer. Each lot of wafers is
placed on quartz containers (boats) and then placed in a long
tube-furnace. They are then raised to a certain temperature and held
for a period of time in a gas flow. We want to understand the
important factors in this operation. The furnace is broken down into
four sections (zones) and two wafers from each lot in each zone are
measured for the thickness of the oxide layer.

title: Assessing Process Stability
text:
To assess whether a manufacturing process is running consistently without unexpected shifts or drift, engineers use **scatter plots with control limits** to visualize process stability by plotting sample measurements over time and checking that data points fluctuate randomly around a constant average. This technique solves the practical problem of detecting when a process has gone out of control—such as when the average shifts, variation increases, or a systematic trend appears—before it produces defective parts. Someone looking for this concept might search for "process control charts," "time series stability analysis," "detecting process drift," or "checking if my process is behaving normally."

The graphical tool we use to assess process stability is the
scatter plot
. We
collect a sufficient number of independent samples (greater than 100)
from our process over a sufficiently long period of time (this can
be specified in days, hours of processing time or number of parts
processed) and plot them on a scatter plot with sample order on the
x-axis and the sample value on the y-axis. The plot should look like
constant random variation about a constant mean. Sometimes it is
helpful to calculate
control
limits
and plot them on the scatter plot along with the data. The
two plots in the
controlled
variation example
are good illustrations of stable and unstable
processes.

title: Checking Assumptions
text:
When analyzing production data, checking for **normality assumptions** and **multimodal distributions** helps identify whether your measurements are coming from a single consistent process or getting mixed up from different sources or unstable conditions—essentially, it prevents you from drawing wrong conclusions by catching situations where your data is contaminated or your process is drifting. This concept is also known as **distribution analysis**, **process stability assessment**, or checking for **multiple data sources**, and engineers might search for terms like "why is my data not normal," "detecting mixed batches in production," or "identifying unstable manufacturing processes."

There are several things that could cause the data
to appear non-normal, such as:
The data come from two or more different sources. This type of
data will often have a multi-modal distribution. This can be
solved by identifying the reason for the multiple sets of data
and analyzing the data separately.
The data come from an unstable process. This type of data is
nearly impossible to analyze because the results of the
analysis will have no credibility due to the changing nature
of the process.
The data were generated by a stable, yet fundamentally
non-normal mechanism. For example, particle counts are
non-normal by the very nature of the particle generation
process. Data of this type can be handled using
transformations.

title: References
text:
This collection covers **exploratory data analysis (EDA), experimental design, and analysis of variance (ANOVA)** — practical techniques for understanding raw data patterns, planning fair tests, and breaking down what factors actually affect your process output. If you're trying to figure out which variables matter most in your manufacturing or testing process, spot hidden trends in messy data, or design an experiment that won't waste time and materials, these methods help you see the signal before diving into complicated statistics. You might also search for: "data visualization for engineers," "how to plan an experiment," "finding what affects my process," or "comparing multiple test conditions."

Box, G.E.P., Hunter, W.G., and Hunter, J.S. (1978),
Statistics for
Experimenters
, John Wiley and Sons, New York.
Cleveland, W.S. (1993),
Visualizing
Data
, Hobart Press, New Jersey.
Hoaglin, D.C.,
Mosteller, F., and Tukey, J.W. (1985),
Exploring Data Tables,
Trends, and Shapes
, John Wiley and Sons, New York.
Hoaglin, D.C.,
Mosteller, F., and Tukey, J.W. (1991),
Fundamentals of Exploratory
Analysis of Variance
, John Wiley and Sons, New York.

title: Introduction to Process Modeling
text:
Function-based process modeling uses statistical methods to build mathematical representations of how your manufacturing or engineering process behaves, letting you predict outputs based on inputs and identify which factors actually matter for quality or performance. If you're trying to figure out why your product varies, what settings to adjust to get better results, or how different process conditions affect your final output, this is what you need. People also search for this using terms like "process optimization," "input-output relationships," "design of experiments," "response modeling," or "understanding what affects my process."

The goal for this section is to give the big picture of function-based
process modeling. This includes a discussion of what process modeling is,
the goals of process modeling, and a comparison of the different statistical
methods used for model building. Detailed information on how to collect data,
construct appropriate models, interpret output, and use process models is
covered in the following sections. The final section of the chapter contains
case studies that illustrate the general information presented in the
first five sections using data from a variety of scientific and engineering
applications.

title: What is process modeling?
text:
Process modeling separates the predictable relationship between variables (like pressure and temperature) from random noise, using regression analysis and error decomposition to show what your data can reliably tell you versus what's just measurement scatter. This solves the practical problem of figuring out which patterns in your data are real and repeatable versus which are just random fluctuations you should ignore. People might search for this as "signal and noise separation," "fitting a line to messy data," "explaining variation in measurements," or "breaking down data into trends and randomness."

The top left plot in the figure shows pressure data that vary
deterministically with temperature except for a small amount of random error.
The relationship between pressure and temperature is a straight line,
but not a perfect straight line. The top row plots on the right-hand side of
the equals sign show a partitioning of the data into a perfect straight
line and the remaining "unexplained" random variation in the data (note
the different vertical scales of these plots). The plots in the middle row of
the figure show the deterministic structure in the data again and a
histogram
of the random variation. The histogram shows the relative frequencies of
observing different-sized random errors. The bottom row of the figure shows
how the relative frequencies of the random errors can be summarized by a
(normal) probability distribution.

title: What terminology do statisticians use to describe process models?
text:
This page explains the components of process models by distinguishing between **predictor variables** and **parameters** — the inputs you measure versus the unknown coefficients you need to figure out. An engineer building a prediction model needs to know which numbers are "knowns" (things you observe, like temperature or pressure) versus "unknowns" (the multipliers or coefficients you solve for through fitting), and this page clarifies that terminology. You might also search for this using phrases like "input variables vs. coefficients," "independent variables and regression coefficients," "model factors and estimates," or "what are X variables and beta values."

The mathematical function consists of two parts. These parts are
the predictor variables, \(x_1, \, x_2, \, \ldots \, \),
and the parameters, \(\beta_0, \, \beta_1, \, \ldots \, \).
The predictor variables are observed along with the response variable.
They are the quantities described on the previous page as inputs to the
mathematical function, \(f(\vec{x};\vec{\beta})\)
The collection of all of the predictor variables is denoted by
\(\vec{x}\)
for short.
$$ \vec{x} \equiv (x_1, \, x_2, \, \ldots) $$
The parameters are the quantities that will be estimated during the
modeling process. Their true values are unknown and unknowable, except
in simulation experiments. As for the predictor variables, the
collection of all of the parameters is denoted by
\(\vec{\beta}\)
for short.
$$ \vec{\beta} \equiv (\beta_0, \, \beta_1, \, \ldots) $$

title: What are process models used for?
text:
Regression function estimation determines predicted output values for any combination of input variables, allowing engineers to forecast system performance at untested conditions without running new experiments. This solves the practical problem of filling in missing data points and predicting behavior beyond the range of measurements you've actually collected. People searching for this concept might use terms like "prediction," "forecasting," "interpolation," "extrapolation," "curve fitting," or "response surface modeling."

The goal of estimation is to determine
the value of the
regression function
(i.e., the average value of the response variable),
for a particular combination of the values of the predictor variables.
Regression function values can be estimated for any combination of
predictor variable values, including values for which no data have been
measured or observed. Function values estimated for points
within the observed space of predictor variable values are sometimes called
interpolations. Estimation of regression function values for points outside
the observed space of predictor variable values, called extrapolations,
are sometimes necessary, but require caution.

title: What are some of the different statistical methods for model building?
text:
Model building involves selecting from multiple competing statistical techniques and general approaches to find the most effective method for a given application, solving the practical problem of deciding which analytical tool will give you the best results without wasting time testing every possible option. This concept addresses the core challenge of method selection and technique comparison in statistical modeling. Someone might search for this using terms like "choosing statistical methods," "comparing modeling approaches," "picking the right analysis tool," "statistical method selection," or "which statistical technique should I use."

Model building, however, is different from most other areas of statistics
with regard to method selection. There are more general approaches and more
competing techniques available for model building than for most other types of
problems. There is often more than one statistical tool that can be effectively
applied to a given modeling application. The large menu of methods applicable
to modeling problems means that there is both more opportunity for effective and
efficient solutions and more potential to spend time doing different analyses,
comparing different solutions and mastering the use of different tools. The
remainder of this section will introduce and briefly discuss some of the
most popular and well-established statistical techniques that are useful for
different model building situations.

title: Underlying Assumptions for Process Modeling
text:
This NIST guidance addresses **assumption validation** and **model verification** in process modeling, explaining the practical challenge that you can't always tell right away whether your process model is actually working correctly or giving you trustworthy answers. An engineer might search for this concept using terms like "how to check if my model is right," "validating process assumptions," "delayed feedback in modeling," or "long-term model verification."

If the implicit assumptions that underlie a particular action are not true,
then that action is not likely to meet expectations either. Sometimes it
is abundantly clear when a goal has been met, but unfortunately that is not
always the case. In particular, it is usually not possible to obtain immediate
feedback on the attainment of goals in most process modeling applications.
The goals of process modeling, sucha as answering a scientific or engineering question,
depend on the correctness of a process model, which can often only be directly
and absolutely determined over time. In lieu of immediate, direct feedback,
however, indirect information on the effectiveness of a process modeling
analysis can be obtained by checking the validity of the underlying
assumptions. Confirming that the underlying assumptions are valid helps
ensure that the methods of analysis were appropriate and that the results will
be consistent with the goals.

title: What are the typical underlying assumptions in process modeling?
text:
Process modeling relies on six major underlying assumptions about how systems behave and can be represented mathematically, which determine whether the chosen modeling method will actually work for your real-world situation. This solves the practical problem of knowing upfront what conditions your process needs to meet before you spend time building a model—if your situation violates one of these basic assumptions, your model predictions will be unreliable. Related searches: "process model requirements," "when should I use process modeling," "process modeling limitations," "does my process fit this model type," or "model assumptions checklist."

This section lists the typical assumptions underlying most process modeling
methods. On each of the following pages, one of the six major assumptions is
described individually; the reasons for it's importance are also briefly
discussed; and any methods that are not subject to that particular assumption
are noted. As discussed on the
previous page
, these
are implicit assumptions based on properties inherent to the process modeling
methods themselves. Successful use of these methods in any particular
application hinges on the validity of the underlying assumptions, whether
their existence is acknowledged or not.
Section 4.4.4
discusses methods for
checking the validity of these assumptions.

title: Data Collection for Process Modeling
text:
Experimental design—the practice of planning data collection procedures before running tests—helps engineers get reliable process models while spending less money and time on experiments. Instead of collecting data randomly and hoping it works out, analysts can strategically decide what measurements to take and how to take them so the resulting model actually captures how the process behaves. People looking for this concept might search for "how to set up an experiment," "test planning," "data collection strategy," "designing experiments to reduce waste," or "getting good results from fewer tests."

This section lays out some general principles for collecting data for
construction of process models. Using well-planned data collection
procedures is often the difference between successful and unsuccessful
experiments. In addition, well-designed experiments are often less
expensive than those that are less well thought-out, regardless of
overall success or failure.
Specifically, this section will answer the question:
What can the analyst do even prior to collecting the data
(that is, at the experimental design stage) that would allow
the analyst to do an optimal job of modeling the process?

title: Why is experimental design important for process modeling?
text:
Experimental design affects the precision of fitted model parameters and prediction accuracy—meaning that how you choose where to collect measurements directly impacts whether your process model gives you reliable results and tight confidence in your predictions. Someone trying to figure out which measurement points to test first, how many samples to run, or whether spreading tests across the operating range matters more than clustering them should search for terms like "optimal sampling strategy," "data collection planning for modeling," "parameter estimation variance reduction," or "design of experiments for regression."

But are the fitted lines, i.e., the fitted process models, better
for some designs than for others? Are the coefficient estimator
variances smaller for some designs than for others? For given
estimates, are the resulting predicted values better (that is,
closer to the observed \(y\)
values)
than for other designs? The
answer to all of the above is YES. It DOES make a difference.
The most popular answer to the above question about which
design to use for linear modeling is design #1 with ten
equi-spaced points. It can be shown, however, that the
variance of the estimated slope parameter depends on the
design according to the relationship
$$ \mbox{Var}(\hat{\beta}_1) \propto \frac{1}{\sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_i-\bar{x})} $$
Therefore to obtain minimum variance estimators, one
maximizes the denominator on the right. To maximize the
denominator, it is (for an arbitrarily fixed \(\bar{x}\)),
best to position the \(x\)'s
as far away from \(\bar{x}\)
as possible. This is done by positioning half of the \(x\)'s
at the lower extreme and the other half at the upper extreme.
This is design #3 above, and this "dumbbell" design (half low and
half high) is in fact the best possible design for fitting a line.
Upon reflection, this is intuitively arrived at by the adage that
"2 points define a line", and so it makes the most sense to determine
those 2 points as far apart as possible (at the extremes) and as
well as possible (having half the data at each extreme).
Hence the design of experiment solution to model processing
when the model is a line is the "dumbbell" design--half the
\(x\)'s at each extreme.

title: What are some general design principles for process modeling?
text:
The core concept is **heteroscedasticity** (non-constant variance) in **non-linear regression modeling**, which addresses the practical problem that measurement errors in your input values create bigger swings in your output predictions in steep regions of the curve than in flat regions. Engineers dealing with exponential models or other curved fits often need to account for this uneven noise pattern when deciding how much weight to give different data points—otherwise your fit will be pulled too hard toward the noisy steep sections. Related terms you might search for: "unequal variance," "weighted least squares," "noise amplification in steep curves," or "input error propagation."

A common occurence for non-linear models is for some regions of the
curve to be steeper than others. For example, in fitting an
exponential model (small \(x\)
corresponding to large \(y\),
and large \(y\)
corresponding to small \(x\))
it is often the case that the \(y\)
data in the steep region are intrinsically noisier than the \(y\)
data in the relatively flat regions. The reason for
this is that commonly the \(x\)
values themselves have a bit of noise and this \(x\)-noise
gets translated into larger \(y\)-noise
in the steep sections than in the
shallow sections. In such cases, when we know the shape of the
response curve well enough to identify steep-versus-shallow
regions, it is often a good idea to sample more heavily in the steep
regions than in the shallow regions. A practical rule-of-thumb for where
to position the \(x\)
values in such situations is to
sketch out your best guess for what the resulting curve
will be;
partition the vertical (that is the \(y\))
axis into \(n\)
equi-spaced points (with \(n\)
denoting the total number of data points that you can afford);
draw horizontal lines from each vertical axis point to where
it hits the sketched-in curve.
drop a vertical projection line from the curve intersection
point to the horizontal axis.
These will be the recommended \(x\)
values to use in the design.
The above rough procedure for an exponentially decreasing curve
would thus yield a logarithmic preponderance of points in the steep
region of the curve and relatively few points in the flatter part
of the curve.

title: I've heard some people refer to "optimal" designs, shouldn't I use those?
text:
When classical experimental designs like factorial or fractional factorial designs don't fit your real-world constraints—such as a tight budget that only allows 13 test runs instead of the standard 16 or 32—optimal designs use computer algorithms to create a custom experiment plan that still gives you reliable results within your limitations. This solves the practical problem of running experiments when you can't use off-the-shelf design templates because of budget, time, or physical impossibilities in your process. You might also search for this using terms like "custom designs," "algorithmic designs," "computer-generated designs," "designs for constrained situations," or "designs when standard designs won't work."

Cases do arise, however, for which the tabulated classical
designs do not cover a particular practical situation.
That is, user constraints
preclude the use of tabulated classical designs
because such classical designs do not
accommodate user constraints. Such constraints include:
Limited maximum number of runs:
User constraints in budget and time may dictate a
maximum allowable number of runs that is too small
or too "irregular" (e.g., "13") to be accommodated by
classical designs--even fractional factorial designs.
Impossible factor combinations:
The user may have some factor combinations that are
impossible to run. Such combinations may at times be
specified (to maintain balance and orthogonality) as
part of a recommeded classical design. If the user
simply omits this impossible run from the design, the
net effect may be a reduction in the
quality and optimaltiy of the classical design.
Too many levels:
The number of factors and/or the number of levels of some factors
intended for use may not be included in tabulations of classical designs.
Complicated underlying model:
The user may be assuming an underlying model that is
too complicated (or too non-linear), so that
classical designs would be inappropriate.

title: How can I tell if a particular experimental design is good for my application?
text:
To check if an experimental design works well across multiple factors, create scatter plots of every pair of input variables (called **2-way balance** or **pairwise balance**) and look for a symmetric, evenly-spread pattern—this helps you catch designs that look good overall but actually have weak spots in certain factor combinations. A non-statistician engineer might think of this as "making sure my test points aren't clustered in one corner" or "verifying my experiment covers all the combinations evenly." Related searches: factor combinations, design space coverage, design uniformity, point distribution in experiments, two-factor interactions, design quality check, or experimental uniformity.

If you have a design that is purported to be globally good in \(k\)
factors, then generally that design should be locally good
in all pairs of the individual \(k\)
factors. Graphically check for such 2-way balance by generating
plots for all pairs of factors, where the horizontal axis of a
given plot is \(x_i\)
and the vertical axis is \(x_j\).
The response variable \(y\)
does NOT come into play in these plots. We are only interested in
characteristics of the design, and so only the \(x\)
variables are involved. The 2-way plots of most good designs
have a certain symmetric and balanced look about them--all
combination points should be covered and each combination point
should have about the same number of points.

title: Data Analysis for Process Modeling
chapter: What are the basic steps for developing an effective process model?
text:
This section covers **least squares estimation, weighted least squares, and functional form selection** for fitting mathematical models to experimental data. It solves the practical problem of figuring out what equation best describes your process and how to find the numbers that make that equation match your actual measurements. Someone looking for this might search for "how to fit a curve to my data," "finding the best-fit equation," "parameter estimation," "model fitting," or "does my model match my test results."

What are the basic steps for developing an effective process model?
How do I select a function to describe my process?
Incorporating Scientific Knowledge into Function Selection
Using the Data to Select an Appropriate Function
Using Methods that Do Not Require Function Specification
How are estimates of the unknown parameters obtained?
Least Squares
Weighted Least Squares
How can I tell if a model fits my data?
How can I assess the sufficiency of the functional part of the model?
How can I detect non-constant variation across the data?
How can I tell if there was drift in the measurement process?
How can I assess whether the random errors are independent from one to the next?
How can I test whether or not the random errors are normally distributed?
How can I test whether any significant terms are missing or misspecified in the functional part of the model?
How can I test whether all of the terms in the functional part of the model are necessary?
If my current model does not fit the data well, how can I improve it?
Updating the Function Based on Residual Plots
Accounting for Non-Constant Variation Across the Data
Accounting for Errors with a Non-Normal Distribution

title: What are the basic steps for developing an effective process model?
text:
The three core steps of process modeling—**model selection, model fitting, and model validation**—provide a systematic approach to building equations that describe how your process actually works based on real data. This solves the practical problem of figuring out what math formula best captures your process behavior so you can predict outputs, spot problems, or set up controls. People searching for this concept might also look for "how to build a mathematical model," "fitting equations to data," "process characterization," or "finding the right equation for my process."

The basic steps of the model-building process are:
model selection
model fitting, and
model validation.
These three basic steps are used iteratively until an appropriate model for
the data has been developed. In the model selection step, plots of the data,
process knowledge and assumptions about the process are used to determine the
form of the model to be fit to the data. Then, using the selected model and
possibly information about the data, an appropriate model-fitting method
is used to estimate the unknown parameters in the model. When the parameter
estimates have been made, the model is then carefully assessed to see if the
underlying assumptions of the analysis appear plausible. If the assumptions
seem valid, the model can be used to answer the scientific or engineering questions
that prompted the modeling effort. If the model validation identifies problems
with the current model, however, then the modeling process is repeated using
information from the model validation step to select and/or fit an improved
model.

title: How do I select a function to describe my process?
text:
When selecting a statistical model to describe your process, you must validate that your chosen function actually captures all the patterns in your data—a practice called **model adequacy checking** or **goodness-of-fit assessment**—which solves the practical problem of catching when your initial model is missing important behavior and needs to be refined before you rely on it for decisions. Alternative ways engineers search for this concept include "model validation," "checking if my model fits the data," "finding hidden patterns a simple model misses," "detecting lack of fit," or "why my model isn't working even though it seems reasonable."

Sometimes the different sources of information that need to be integrated to
find an effective model will be contradictory. An open mind and a willingness
to think about what the data are saying is important. Maintaining balance and
looking for alternate sources for unusual effects found in the data are also
important. For example, in the
load cell
calibration case study
the statistical analysis pointed out that the model
initially thought to be appropriate did not account for all of the structure in
the data. A refined model was developed, but the appearance of an unexpected
result brings up the question of whether the original understanding of the
problem was inaccurate, or whether the need for an alternate model was due
to experimental artifacts. In the load cell problem it was easy to accept that
the refined model was closer to the truth, but in a more complicated case
additional experiments might have been needed to resolve the issue.

title: How are estimates of the unknown parameters obtained?
text:
**Least squares estimation and maximum likelihood estimation** are two methods for finding the best values for unknown parameters in mathematical models. When you have measurement data with normal (bell-curve) errors, both methods give you identical answers, so engineers can use the simpler least squares approach instead of more complicated maximum likelihood software to fit curves and find optimal model coefficients. If someone is searching for how to "fit a curve to data," "find best-fit parameters," "optimize model coefficients," or "solve for unknown constants in equations," they're looking for these parameter estimation techniques.

Although robust techniques are valuable, they are not as well developed as the
more traditional methods and often require specialized software that is not
readily available. Maximum likelihood also requires specialized algorithms in
general, although there are important special cases that do not have such a
requirement. For example, for data with normally distributed random errors,
the least squares and maximum likelihood parameter estimators are identical.
As a result of these software and developmental issues, and the coincidence of
maximum likelihood and least squares in many applications, this section
currently focuses on parameter estimation only by least squares methods. The
remainder of this section offers some intuition into how least squares works
and illustrates the effectiveness of this method.

title: How can I tell if a model fits my data?
If my current model does not fit the data well, how can I improve it?
text:
When a regression model doesn't match your data well, **residual analysis**, **model validation**, and **diagnostic plots** help you spot exactly what's wrong and suggest specific fixes—so you don't have to start over from scratch. Instead of just knowing your model failed, these graphical tools and statistical tests pinpoint whether the problem is wrong equation form, outliers, unequal variance, or non-random patterns, and often show you directly how to fix it. Someone hunting for this concept might search for "how to improve a bad model," "fixing regression problems," "model diagnostics," "what to do when model doesn't fit," or "interpreting residual plots for model improvement."

Validating a model using residual plots, formal hypothesis tests and descriptive
statistics would be quite frustrating if discovery of a problem meant restarting
the modeling process back at square one. Fortunately, however, there are also
techniques and tools to remedy many of the problems uncovered using residual
analysis. In some cases the model validation methods themselves suggest
appropriate changes to a model at the same time problems are uncovered.
This is especially true of the graphical tools for model validation, though
tests on the parameters in the regression function also
offer insight into model refinement. Treatments for the various model
deficiencies that were diagnosed in
Section 4.4.4.
are
demonstrated and discussed in the subsections listed below.

title: What types of predictions can I make using the model?
text:
This section covers **estimation and prediction methods** using process models, which help you figure out what will happen next in your manufacturing or system process based on data you already have. If you're trying to forecast future outputs, estimate unknown values, or simulate what-if scenarios without running expensive experiments, these computational formulas and algorithms show you how to do it. You might also search for this using terms like "forecasting," "projecting future results," "making guesses based on your model," "what happens if," or "filling in missing values."

This section details some of the different types of predictions that can be
made using the various process models whose development is discussed in
Section 4.1
through
Section 4.4
.
Computational formulas or algorithms are given for each different type of
estimation or prediction, along with simulation examples showing its probabilisitic
interpretation. An introduction to the different types of estimation and prediction
can be found in
Section 4.1.3.1
. A brief
description of estimation and prediction versus the other uses of process models
is given in
Section 4.1.3
.

title: How can I use my process model for calibration?
text:
This section covers calibration methods and computational algorithms for process models, showing engineers how to use their measurement system models to adjust equipment settings and correct measurement errors. Someone looking for this might search for "using models to fix equipment," "adjusting instrument readings," "calibration procedures," "tuning measurement systems," or "applying process models to correct measurements."

This section details some of the different types of calibrations that can be
made using the various process models whose development was discussed in
previous sections. Computational formulas or algorithms are given for each
different type of calibration, along with simulation examples showing its
probabilistic interpretation. An introduction to calibration can be found
in
Section 4.1.3.2
. A brief comparison
of calibration versus the other uses of process models is given in
Section 4.1.3
. Additional information on
calibration is available in
Section 3
of
Chapter 2: Measurement Process Characterization
.

title: How can I optimize my process using the process model?
text:
Response surface designs (RSDs) and process models enable engineers to systematically find the best input settings that deliver desired output results, whether that means hitting a specific target value or maximizing/minimizing performance. This approach solves the practical problem of tuning process parameters efficiently instead of through trial-and-error testing. Someone might search for this using terms like "optimizing process settings," "finding the best operating conditions," "fitting models to experimental data," "response surface methodology (RSM)," or "design of experiments for optimization."

Process optimization using models fit to data collected using
response surface designs
is
primarily covered in
Section 5.5.3
of
Chapter 5: Process Improvement
. In
that section detailed information is given on how to determine the
correct process inputs to hit a target output value or to maximize or minimize
process output. Some background on the use of process models for optimization
can be found in
Section 4.1.3.3
of
this chapter, however, and information on the basic analysis of data from
optimization experiments is covered along with that of other types of models
in
Section 4.1
through
Section 4.4
of this chapter.

title: Case Studies in Process Modeling
text:
This handbook teaches **model fitting, residual analysis, and variance stabilization techniques** to help engineers build accurate prediction equations from measurement data and fix problems when a simple straight-line relationship doesn't work well. Engineers use these methods when they need to calibrate instruments or sensors—situations where you're measuring something physical (like load or distance) but your measurements aren't perfectly accurate or consistent, so you need to figure out the right mathematical formula to convert raw readings into true values. If you're searching for how to fix a poor-fitting equation, improve prediction accuracy, handle unequal measurement noise, or account for equipment batch differences, these case studies show the step-by-step process.

title: References For Chapter 4: Process Modeling
text:
This reference collection covers **regression analysis, weighted regression, transformation methods, and robust locally weighted regression (LOWESS)** — techniques that help you fit mathematical equations to experimental or measured data when your measurements have unequal reliability, vary in quality across different ranges, or contain outliers that would throw off a standard fit. If you're an engineer trying to build a predictive model from noisy test data, find the best-fit curve through scattered measurements, or account for the fact that some data points are more trustworthy than others, these are the methods you need. You might also search for: "curve fitting for unreliable data," "handling bad measurements in data fitting," "non-uniform data quality in regression," or "outlier-resistant curve fitting."

title: What is experimental design?
text:
Linear models with interaction effects allow engineers to predict how a product or process output (Y) responds to multiple input factors (X₁, X₂, X₃) while capturing situations where two factors work together to produce results different from their individual effects—solving the practical problem of understanding whether changing factor combinations have unexpected synergistic or counteracting impacts. This concept is also called "factorial design," "two-way interactions," "factor interactions," "combined effects modeling," or "response surface analysis," and someone might search for "how do I test if changing two things together matters more than changing them separately" or "modeling when variables affect each other."

A linear model with two factors,
X
1
and
X
2
, can be written as
\( Y = \beta_{0} + \beta_{1}X_{1} + \beta_{2}X_{2} +
\beta_{12}X_{1}X_{2} + \mbox{experimental error} \)
Here,
Y
is the response for given levels of the
main effects
X
1
and
X
2
and the
X
1
X
2
term is included to account for a possible
interaction
effect between
X
1
and
X
2
. The constant
\( \beta_{0} \)
is the response of
Y
when both main effects are 0.
For a more complicated example, a linear model with three factors
X
1
,
X
2
,
X
3
and
one response,
Y
, would look like (if all possible terms were
included in the model)
\( Y = \beta_{0} + \beta_{1}X_{1} + \beta_{2}X_{2} + \beta_{3}X_{3} +
\beta_{12}X_{1}X_{2} + \\
\beta_{13}X_{1}X_{3} + \beta_{23}X_{2}X_{3} +
\beta_{123}X_{1}X_{2}X_{3} + \\
\mbox{experimental error} \)
The three terms with single "
X
's" are the
main
effects
terms. There are
k
(
k
-1)/2 = 3*2/2 = 3
two-way
interaction
terms and 1
three-way
interaction term (which is often omitted, for
simplicity). When the experimental data are analyzed, all the unknown
"\( \beta \)"
parameters are estimated and the coefficients of the "
X
" terms
are tested to see which ones are significantly different from 0.

title: What are the uses of DOE?
text:
Design of Experiments (DOE) and response surface modeling enable engineers to systematically adjust machine settings and process parameters to consistently hit a target output, rather than relying on trial-and-error tweaking. This approach solves the practical problem of tuning equipment after maintenance or setup by using data from a small planned experiment to build a predictive model that shows exactly what adjustments are needed. People searching for this concept might look for terms like "process optimization," "finding the right settings," "tuning equipment," "hitting target specifications," or "systematic parameter adjustment."

This is a frequently encountered goal for an experiment.
One might try out different settings until the desired target is `hit'
consistently. For example, a machine tool that has been recently
overhauled may require some setup `tweaking' before it runs on target.
Such action is a small and common form of experimentation. However,
rather than experimenting in an ad hoc manner until we happen to find
a setup that hits the target, one can fit a model estimated from a small
experiment and use this model to determine the necessary adjustments to
hit the target.
More complex forms of experimentation, such as the determination of
the correct chemical mix of a coating that will yield a desired
refractive index for the dried coat (and simultaneously achieve
specifications for other attributes), may involve many ingredients and
be very sensitive to small changes in the percentages in the mix.
Fitting suitable models, based on sequentially planned experiments, may
be the only way to efficiently achieve this goal of hitting targets for
multiple responses simultaneously.

title: What are the steps of DOE?
text:
Design of Experiments (DOE) practical implementation guidelines address the real-world challenges of running reliable tests, such as measurement errors skewing results, process inconsistencies ruining data quality, and unexpected disruptions invalidating your findings. Engineers looking for help with experiment execution might search for "how to run reliable tests," "avoiding mistakes in manufacturing trials," "controlling variables during production runs," or "collecting good experimental data."

Important practical considerations in
planning and running experiments are
Check performance of gauges/measurement devices first.
Keep the experiment as simple as possible.
Check that all planned runs are feasible.
Watch out for process drifts and shifts during the run.
Avoid unplanned changes (e.g., swap operators at halfway
point).
Allow some time (and back-up material) for unexpected
events.
Obtain buy-in from all parties involved.
Maintain effective ownership of each step in the experimental
plan.
Preserve all the raw data--do not keep only summary averages!
Record everything that happens.
Reset equipment to its original state after the experiment.

title: Is the process stable?
text:
**Core concept: Process stability assessment and control runs; drift/shift detection in experimental design.**

This guidance solves the practical problem of figuring out whether changes you observe in an experiment actually came from your test changes or just from the process itself acting up and changing on its own during the experiment. **Alternative search terms:** detecting process drift during testing, baseline control measurements, checking if process is behaving consistently, accounting for time-dependent process changes, experimental validity when process conditions vary, monitoring for process shifts mid-experiment.

Experimental runs should have control runs that are made at the
`standard' process setpoints, or at least at some standard operating
recipe. The experiment should start and end with such runs. A plot of
the outcomes of these control runs will indicate if the underlying
process itself has drifted or shifted during the experiment.
It is desirable to experiment on a stable process. However, if this
cannot be achieved, then the process instability must be accounted for
in the analysis of the experiment. For example, if the mean is
shifting with time (or experimental trial run), then it will be
necessary to include a trend term in the experimental model (i.e.,
include a time variable or a run number variable).

title: Are the model residuals well-behaved?
text:
Heteroscedasticity and model specification are addressed through residual analysis—specifically, checking whether prediction errors stay consistently sized across different operating conditions or whether they balloon up at certain settings, which signals you either need to add higher-order terms (like squared variables) or transform your inputs (log, square root). An engineer troubleshooting why a regression model fits poorly in some regions but not others, or why error bars should be bigger at high temperatures than low ones, would search for "non-constant variance," "changing error size," "funnel-shaped residual plots," or "my model works great here but terrible there." Outlier detection is also covered—identifying individual suspicious data points that don't fit the overall pattern even though they're within the normal operating range you tested.

Figure 2.8 shows that the size of the residuals changed as a function
of a predictor's settings. A graph like this suggests that the model
needs a higher-order term in that predictor or that one should
transform the predictor using a logarithm or square root, for example.
Figure 2.9 shows the residuals for the same response after adding a
quadratic term. Notice the single point widely separated from the other
residuals in Figure 2.9. This point is an "outlier." That is, its
position is well within the range of values used for this predictor in
the investigation, but its result was somewhat lower than the model
predicted. A signal that curvature is present is a trace resembling a
"frown" or a "smile" in these graphs.

title: Choosing an experimental design
text:
This page covers experimental design strategies—including factorial designs, blocking methods, and fractional factorial approaches—that help engineers figure out which process settings actually matter and how to change them to get better results while testing fewer combinations than trying everything. If you're looking for ways to plan tests efficiently, reduce the number of experiments needed, or understand which knobs to turn on your process, you might search for: "how to design an experiment," "testing strategy," "process variables," "reducing test combinations," "screening which factors matter," or "efficient testing plan."

title: What are the objectives?
text:
Experimental design objectives—specifically comparative designs and screening designs—help engineers figure out which product or process changes actually matter and whether one option is genuinely better than another, rather than guessing based on intuition or incomplete tests. Someone looking for this concept might search for "how to test if changes work," "comparing design alternatives," "which factors affect my product," "factorial experiments," or "structured testing methods."

Examples of goals were given earlier in
Section 5.1.2
, in which we
described four broad categories of experimental designs, with various
objectives for each. These were:
Comparative designs
to:
choose between alternatives, with narrow scope, suitable
for an initial comparison (see
Section 5.3.3.1
)
choose between alternatives, with broad scope, suitable
for a confirmatory comparison (see Section
5.3.3.2
)
Screening designs
to identify which factors/effects are
important
when you have 2 - 4 factors and can perform a full
factorial (
Section 5.3.3.3
)
when you have more than 3 factors and want to begin with
as small a design as possible (Section
5.3.3.4
and
5.3.3.5
)
when you have some qualitative factors, or you have some
quantitative factors that are known to have a non-monotonic
effect (Section
3.3.3.10
)
Note that some authors prefer to restrict the term screening
design to the case where you are trying to extract the
most important factors from a large (say > 5) list of initial
factors (usually a fractional factorial design). We include
the case with a smaller number of factors, usually a full
factorial design, since the basic purpose and analysis is
similar.
Response Surface
modeling to achieve one or more of the
following objectives:
hit a target
maximize or minimize a response
reduce variation by locating a region where the process
is easier to manage
make a process robust (note: this objective may often
be accomplished with screening designs rather than with
response surface designs - see
Section 5.5.6
)
Regression modeling
to estimate a precise model, quantifying the dependence
of response variable(s) on process inputs.

title: How do you select and scale the process variables?
text:
This page explains **2-level factorial design** (also called **full factorial experimentation**), which solves the practical problem of systematically testing how different process settings affect your output without having to run every possible combination—you use coded values (+1 and -1) to represent high and low settings for each factor. Someone might search for this using terms like "factorial experiment," "design of experiments (DOE)," "factor testing," "high-low settings," or "running controlled trials to find what matters."

The standard layout for a 2-level design uses +1 and -1 notation to
denote the "high level" and the "low level" respectively, for each
factor. For example, the matrix below
Factor 1 (X1)
Factor 2 (X2)
Trial 1
-1
-1
Trial 2
+1
-1
Trial 3
-1
+1
Trial 4
+1
+1
describes an experiment in which 4 trials (or runs) were conducted with
each factor set to high or low during a run according to whether the
matrix had a +1 or -1 set for the factor during that trial. If the
experiment had more than 2 factors, there would be an additional column
in the matrix for each additional factor.
Note
: Some authors shorten the matrix notation for a two-level
design by just recording the plus and minus signs, leaving out the "1's".

title: How do you select an experimental design?
text:
A **comparative design** tests whether changes in one key factor actually cause measurable differences in your output, while accounting for other factors that might also affect results. This solves the practical problem of figuring out if a process change, material choice, or equipment adjustment really makes a difference or if any improvement is just random variation. You might also search for this as "factor significance testing," "does this change matter," "one-factor experiment," or "isolating the effect of one variable."

Types of designs are listed here according to the experimental
objective they meet.
Comparative objective
: If you have one or several factors
under investigation, but the primary goal of your experiment is
to make a conclusion about one a-priori important factor, (in
the presence of, and/or in spite of the existence of the other
factors), and the question of interest is whether or not that
factor is "significant", (i.e., whether or not there is a
significant change in the response for different levels of that
factor), then you have a
comparative problem
and you
need a
comparative design
solution.
Screening objective
: The primary
purpose of the experiment is to select or
screen out
the
few important main effects from the many less important ones.
These
screening designs
are also termed main effects
designs.
Response Surface
(method) objective
: The experiment is designed to allow us
to estimate interaction and even quadratic effects, and therefore
give us an idea of the (local) shape of the response surface we
are investigating. For this reason, they are termed
response
surface method (RSM) designs
. RSM designs are used to:
Find improved or optimal process settings
Troubleshoot process problems and weak points
Make a product or process more
robust
against
external and non-controllable influences. "Robust" means
relatively insensitive to these influences.
Optimizing responses when factors are proportions of a
mixture objective
: If you have factors that are proportions
of a mixture and you want to know what the "best" proportions of
the factors are so as to maximize (or minimize) a response, then
you need a
mixture design
.
Optimal fitting of a regression model objective
: If you
want to model a response as a mathematical function (either
known or empirical) of a few continuous factors and you desire
"good" model parameter estimates (i.e., unbiased and minimum
variance), then you need a
regression design.

title: Completely randomized designs
text:
Completely randomized designs (CRD) solve the problem of fairly testing whether different settings or conditions actually affect your results by randomly ordering your test runs so that external factors don't accidentally favor one setting over another. Instead of running all tests at one condition together, you mix them up randomly so that time-of-day effects, equipment drift, or operator fatigue won't bias your conclusions. Someone might search for this using terms like "randomized experiment," "random test order," "one-factor experiment," "unbiased comparison of conditions," or "how to test if a setting really matters."

Here we consider completely randomized designs that have one primary
factor. The experiment compares the values of a response variable
based on the different levels of that primary factor.
For completely randomized designs, the levels of the primary factor
are randomly assigned to the experimental units. By
randomization
,
we mean that the run sequence of the
experimental units
is
determined randomly. For example, if there are 3 levels of the primary
factor with each level to be run 2 times, then there are
6 factorial possible run sequences (or 6! ways to order the experimental
trials). Because of the replication, the number of unique orderings
is 90 (since 90 = 6!/(2!*2!*2!)). An example of an unrandomized design
would be to always run 2 replications for the first level, then 2 for the
second level, and finally 2 for the third level. To randomize the
runs, one way would be to put 6 slips of paper in a box with 2 having
level 1, 2 having level 2, and 2 having level 3. Before each run, one
of the slips would be drawn blindly from the box and the level selected
would be used for the next run of the experiment.

title: Randomized block designs
text:
**Randomized block designs** reduce measurement noise by grouping similar test conditions together, so you can see the real effects of your changes instead of having them buried under noise from uncontrollable variations like furnace run differences. Instead of running each test separately and letting furnace variability mess up your results, you run multiple tests in the same furnace batch so the batch conditions stay constant for that group. People also call this "blocking," "grouping similar tests together," "controlling for nuisance variables," or "running replicates under the same conditions."

A non-blocked way to run this experiment would be to run each of the
twelve experimental wafers, in random order, one per furnace run. That
would increase the experimental error of each resistivity measurement by
the run-to-run furnace variability and make it more difficult to study
the effects of the different dosages. The blocked way to run this
experiment, assuming you can convince manufacturing to let you put four
experimental wafers in a furnace run, would be to put four wafers with
different dosages in each of three furnace runs. The only randomization
would be choosing which of the three wafers with dosage 1 would go into
furnace run 1, and similarly for the wafers with dosages 2, 3 and 4.

title: Full factorial designs
text:
A **full factorial design** is a systematic method where you test every possible combination of high and low settings across all your input factors, requiring 2^k test runs for k factors, which helps engineers quickly understand how each factor and their interactions affect product performance without guessing. People looking for this concept might search for "testing all combinations," "complete experimental design," "two-level factorial experiments," "factor interaction testing," or "how many tests do I need to check all possibilities."

A common experimental design is one with all input factors set at two
levels each. These levels are called `high' and `low' or `+1' and `-1',
respectively. A design with all possible high/low combinations of all the
input factors is called a full factorial design in two levels.
If there are k factors, each at 2 levels, a full factorial design
has 2
k
runs.

Number of Factors
