For \(U = 40.0\)
and \(\mbox{E }(U) = 0.5 \, n_1 \, n_2 = 60.5\),
the test statistic is
$$ z = \frac{U - \mbox{E }(U)}{\sigma} = \frac{40.0 - 60.5}{15.23} = -1.346 \, , $$
where
$$ \sigma = \sqrt{\frac{n_1 \, n_2 \, (n_1 + n_2 + 1)}{12}} = \sqrt{\frac{11(11)(11+11+1)}{12}} =15.23 \, . $$
For a two-sided test with significance level \(\alpha\)
= 0.05, the critical value is \(z_{1-\alpha/2}\)
= 1.96. Since \(|z|\)
is less than the critical value, we do not reject the null hypothesis and
conclude that there is not enough evidence to claim that
two groups have different central tendencies.

title: How can we compare several populations with unknown distributions (the Kruskal-Wallis test)?
text:
The Kruskal-Wallis test is a non-parametric method for comparing whether multiple groups or batches come from the same distribution when you don't know their underlying shape. This solves the practical problem of determining if several process outputs, measurement sets, or product batches are performing differently without having to assume they follow a normal distribution. People might search for this using terms like "comparing multiple groups without normal distribution," "testing if several samples are the same," "non-parametric ANOVA," or "rank-based test for multiple groups."

It can be shown that if the \(k\)
samples come from the same population, that is, if the null hypothesis is true,
then the test statistic, \(H\),
used in the KW procedure is distributed approximately as a chi-square statistic with
df
= \(k-1\),
provided that the sample sizes of the \(k\)
samples are not too small (say, \(n_i > 4\),
for all \(i\)).
\(H\)
is defined as follows:
$$ H = \frac{12}{N(N+1)} \, \sum_{i=1}^k \frac{R_i^2}{n_i} - 3(N+1) \, , $$
where
\(k\)
= number of samples (groups)
\(n_i\)
= number of observations for the \(i\)-th
sample or group
\(N\)
= total number of observations (sum of all the \(n_i\))
\(R_i\)
= sum of ranks for group \(i\)

title:
Assuming the observations are normal, do the processes
       have the same variance?
text:
Levene's test is a statistical method for checking whether different groups have the same spread or variability in their data, and it works better than Bartlett's test when you can't be sure your measurements follow a normal bell curve. Engineers use this test to compare consistency across different batches or processes—for example, checking if Product A from Supplier 1 varies as much as Product B from Supplier 2—without having to worry as much about whether their data looks perfectly normal. You might also search for this under terms like "testing for equal variances," "homogeneity of variance," "comparing consistency between groups," or "robust equality of variances test."

Levene's test offers a more robust alternative to Bartlett's
procedure. That means it will be less likely to reject a true
hypothesis of equality of variances just because the distributions
of the sampled populations are not normal. When non-normality is
suspected, Levene's procedure is a better choice than Bartlett's.
Levene's test is described
in
Chapter 1
.
This description also includes an example where the test is
applied to the gear data. Levene's test does not reject the
assumption of equality of batch variances for these data. This
differs from the conclusion drawn from Bartlett's test and is
a better answer if, indeed, the batch population distributions
are non-normal.

title: Are the means equal?
text:
ANOVA (analysis of variance) tests whether the average results differ across different settings or groups—for example, checking if product quality is actually different when you change a manufacturing parameter, or if it just looks different by chance. If you're wondering "do these groups have different average values," "is there a real difference between my test conditions," or "does changing this factor actually matter," you're looking for a one-way or two-way ANOVA.

First consider the one-way ANOVA. The null hypothesis is: there
is no difference in the population means of the different levels of
factor \(A\) (the only factor).
The alternative hypothesis is: the means are not the same.
For the two-way ANOVA, the possible null hypotheses are:
There is no difference in the means of factor \(A\)
There is no difference in means of factor \(B\)
There is no interaction between factors \(A\) and \(B\)
The alternative hypothesis for cases 1 and 2 is: the means are not
equal.
The alternative hypothesis for case 3 is: there is an interaction
between \(A\) and \(B\).
For the three-way ANOVA, the main effects are factors \(A\), \(B\), and \(C\), and
the two-factor interactions are \(AB\), \(AC\), and \(BC\). There is also a
three-factor interaction, \(ABC\).
For each of the seven cases the null hypothesis is the same:
there is no difference in means, and the alternative hypothesis is
the means are not equal.

title: What are variance components?
text:
This page explains **variance components** and **Expected Mean Squares (EMS)** in a **random effects ANOVA model**, which helps you figure out how much variation in your product or process comes from different sources—like whether batch-to-batch differences are real or just random noise. Engineers use this to decide if they should focus improvement efforts on controlling batch conditions or if the variation is just natural sampling fluctuation. You might also search for "sources of variation analysis," "random effects model," "estimating batch effects," or "breaking down where variation comes from."

The computations that produce the
SS
are the same for both the fixed
and the random effects models. For the random effects model, however, the
batch sum of squares, 147.73,
is an estimate of \(\{\sigma_\epsilon^2 + 3 \sigma_\tau^2\}\).
This is shown in the
EMS
column of the ANOVA table.
The statistic used to test the hypothesis that there are no batch
effects, that is \(\sigma_\tau^2\) = 0, is
F
= 36.933 / 1.800 = 20.52. The
p
-value is the probability of
observing an
F
statistic this large or larger owing to the vagaries
of sampling alone, when \(\sigma_\tau^2\) = 0.
Since the
p
-value is very small, we conclude that
\(\sigma_\tau^2\) > 0, hence that there are significant batch
effects. The validity of this conclusion rests on several
assumptions: (i) the \(\{\tau_{i}\}\) are a sample from a Gaussian
distribution with mean 0 and standard deviation \(\sigma_\tau\); (ii)
the \(\{\epsilon_{ij}\}\) are a sample from a Gaussian distribution with
mean 0 and standard deviation \(\sigma_{\epsilon}\); and (iii) the
\(\{\tau_{i}\}\) and the \(\{\epsilon_{ij}\}\) are mutually independent.
To find out how much of the variance in the results of the experiment
may be attributed to batch differences and how much to random error,
we need to estimate the
variance components
,
\(\sigma_{\tau}^{2}\) and \(\sigma_{\epsilon}^{2}\).

title:
How can we compare the results of classifying according
       to several categories?
text:
This passage describes **chi-square test of independence**, which tests whether two categorical classifications are related to each other or occur completely separately. An engineer uses this test to determine whether defects of different types happen randomly across different shifts, or whether certain defect types are actually more likely in particular shifts—answering whether two categories are connected or just coincidental. Related search terms include: contingency table analysis, testing for association between categories, independence test for categorical data, or checking if two grouping methods affect each other.

For example, the probability that a particular defect will occur in
shift 1
and
is of type \(A\) is \((p_1)(p_A)\).
While the numerical
values of the cell probabilities are unspecified, the null
hypothesis states that each cell probability will equal the product
of its respective row and column probabilities. This condition
implies independence of the two classifications. The alternative
hypothesis is that this equality does not hold for at least one
cell.
In other words, we state the null hypothesis as \(\mbox{H}_0\):
the two classifications are independent, while the alternative
hypothesis is \(\mbox{H}_1\):
the classifications are dependent.
To obtain the observed column probability, divide the column total
by the grand total, \(n\).
Denoting the total of column \(j\) as \(c_j\),
we get
$$ \begin{eqnarray}
\hat{p}_A = \frac{c_1}{n} = \frac{74}{309} & \,\,\,\,\, & \hat{p}_C = \frac{c_3}{n} = \frac{128}{309} \\
& & \\
\hat{p}_B = \frac{c_2}{n} = \frac{69}{309} & \,\,\,\,\, & \hat{p}_D = \frac{c_4}{n} = \frac{38}{309} \, .\\
\end{eqnarray} $$
Similarly, the row probabilities \(p_1, \, p_2,\) and \(p_3\)
are estimated by dividing the row totals \(r_1, \, r_2,\) and \(r_3\)
by the grand total \(n\),
respectively.
$$ \begin{eqnarray}
\hat{p_1} & = & \frac{r_1}{n} = \frac{94}{309} \\
& & \\
\hat{p_2} & = & \frac{r_2}{n} = \frac{96}{309} \\
& & \\
\hat{p_3} & = & \frac{r_3}{n} = \frac{119}{309} \\
\end{eqnarray} $$

title: Do all the processes have the sameproportion of defectives?
text:
This page covers **contingency table analysis** and **chi-square test for homogeneity of proportions**, which lets you check whether different production lots, suppliers, or batches have the same defect rate or quality level. Instead of assuming all your sources produce equally good parts, this test tells you whether the differences you're seeing in defect rates are real or just random noise. Someone might also search for "comparing defect rates across suppliers," "testing if quality is consistent between batches," or "chi-square test for proportions."

When we have samples from \(n\)
populations (i.e., lots, vendors,
production runs, etc.), we can test whether there are significant
differences in the proportion defectives for these populations using
a contingency table approach. The contingency table we construct
has two rows and \(n\)
columns.
To test the null hypothesis of no difference in the proportions
among the \(n\)
populations
\( \mbox{H}_0: \,\,\, p_1 = p_2 = \cdots = p_n \)
against the alternative that not all \(n\)
population proportions are equal
\( \mbox{H}_a: \,\,\, \mbox{Not all } p_i \mbox{ are equal } (i = 1, \, 2, \, \ldots, \, n) \, , \)

title: How can we make multiple comparisons?
text:
**Orthogonal contrasts** and **planned comparisons** address the problem of how to fairly compare multiple treatment groups without inflating your error rates or losing statistical confidence when you run several tests in one experiment. An engineer might search for this as "how to compare multiple groups without losing accuracy," "controlling error when testing several combinations," or "valid ways to test multiple hypotheses from one experiment."

These types of investigations should be done on combinations of
factors that were determined in advance of observing the
experimental results, or else the confidence levels are not as
specified by the procedure. Also, doing several comparisons might
change the overall confidence level (see
note
above). This can be avoided by carefully selecting contrasts to
investigate in advance and making sure that:
the number of such contrasts does not exceed the number of
degrees of freedom between the treatments
only
orthogonal contrasts
are chosen.
However, there are also several powerful multiple comparison
procedures we can use after observing the experimental results.

title: References
text:
This references section covers binomial proportion interval estimation, Kolmogorov-Smirnov goodness-of-fit testing, and experimental design methods—tools that help engineers determine whether measurements fall within acceptable ranges, whether observed data matches expected patterns, and how to set up fair comparison tests between products or processes. Instead of searching for "interval estimation for a binomial proportion," an engineer might ask "how do I calculate confidence limits for pass/fail data?" or "what's the margin of error for my defect rate?" For the Kolmogorov test, they might search "how do I check if my data follows a normal distribution?" or "goodness-of-fit test." The experimental design references address the practical problem of figuring out which factors matter most and how many test samples you actually need to run before deciding if one product is genuinely better than another.

If you're looking for ways to plan fair product tests, figure out which factors matter most in a process, or set up inspection procedures that catch defects efficiently, search for terms like "design of experiments," "process optimization," "quality sampling plans," "statistical comparison methods," or "how to test if products are different."

An engineer might use these techniques to predict how a system behaves under different conditions, decide how many parts to inspect to catch defects, figure out the margin of error on a critical measurement, or set up a test that doesn't waste time and materials. A factory engineer trying to set up acceptance sampling, determine if a process change really worked, or estimate how reliable a product actually is would use these methods. Related searches might include: "how many parts do I need to test," "comparing two production methods," "pass/fail inspection plans," "confidence limits for defect rates," or "checking if process improvement is real."

title: References
text:
This references section covers **sampling inspection, statistical quality control, design of experiments, nonparametric methods, and tolerance limits** — practical tools for checking whether manufactured products meet specifications, planning efficient tests, and understanding how much variation is acceptable in a production process. An engineer might search for these concepts using everyday terms like "how to inspect batches without testing everything," "setting acceptable ranges for product measurements," "planning reliable experiments with limited data," or "detecting when something's wrong with production without assuming normal distribution."

title: Introduction
text:
This handbook section covers **reliability functions, hazard rates, censoring, and lifetime distribution models** — the statistical tools engineers use to predict how long products will work before failing and make decisions about warranties, maintenance schedules, and product design improvements. Someone looking for this content might search for "how to analyze product failure data," "predicting equipment lifespan," "dealing with incomplete test data," or "understanding failure patterns over time."

important?

title: Why is the assessment and control of product reliability important?
text:
Product reliability assessment uses failure rate analysis and survival statistics to predict how long equipment will function before breaking down, helping engineers design systems that won't suddenly quit on customers and cause safety hazards or expensive recalls. If you're looking for this concept, you might search for terms like "how to measure product lifetime," "predicting when equipment fails," "ensuring devices work consistently," or "controlling defect rates in manufacturing."

In today's technological world
nearly everyone depends upon the continued functioning of a wide array
of complex machinery and equipment for their everyday health, safety, mobility
and economic welfare. We expect our cars, computers, electrical appliances,
lights, televisions, etc. to function whenever we need them - day after
day, year after year. When they fail the results can be catastrophic: injury,
loss of life and/or costly lawsuits can occur. More often, repeated failure
leads to annoyance, inconvenience and a lasting customer dissatisfaction
that can play havoc with the responsible company's marketplace position.

title:
What are some common difficulties with reliability data
and how are they overcome?
text:
Censoring and lack of failures are two interconnected challenges in reliability testing where some equipment keeps running without breaking down during the test period, limiting the actual failure data available to analyze product lifespan. This practical problem occurs because reliability tests often end before all units fail, leaving engineers with incomplete information about how long products really last. Engineers searching for solutions might look for terms like "incomplete test data," "surviving units in reliability tests," "testing without full failures," or "how to analyze reliability when nothing breaks."

There are two closely related problems that are typical with reliability
data and not common with most other forms of statistical data. These are:
Censoring
(when the observation period ends, not
all units have failed - some are survivors)
Lack of Failures
(if there is too much censoring,
even though a large number of units may be under observation, the information
in the data is limited due to the lack of actual failures)
These problems cause considerable practical difficulty when planning reliability
assessment tests and analyzing failure data. Some solutions are discussed
in the next two sections. Typically, the solutions involve making additional
assumptions and using complicated models.

title: What is "physical acceleration" and how do we model it?
text:
**Accelerated life testing (ALT) and acceleration factors** address the problem of different failure types degrading at different rates under stress, so engineers must analyze each failure mode separately rather than using one catch-all acceleration factor for all failures. Someone looking for this concept might search for "failure mode acceleration," "stress-dependent degradation analysis," "separate failure mechanisms in reliability testing," or "how to apply different acceleration factors to different failures."

Note
: Acceleration requires that there be
a stress dependent physical process causing change or degradation that
leads to failure. In general, different
failure modes will be affected differently by stress and have different
acceleration factors. Therefore, it is unlikely that a single acceleration
factor will apply to more than one failure mechanism. In general, different
failure modes will be affected differently by stress and have different
acceleration factors. Separate out different types of failure when analyzing
failure data.
Also, a consequence of the linear acceleration relationships shown above
(which follows directly from "true acceleration") is the following:
The Shape Parameter for the key life distribution models (Weibull,
Lognormal) does not change for units operating under different stresses.
Probability plots of data from different stress cells will line
up roughly parallel.
These
distributions
and
probability
plotting
will be discussed in later sections.

title: What are some common acceleration models?
text:
This page covers **acceleration models** and **acceleration factors**, which solve the practical problem of predicting how long products will last in normal use by testing them under harsher conditions and then converting those results back to real-world timeframes. Instead of waiting months or years for failures at normal operating conditions, engineers can stress-test equipment at higher temperatures, voltages, or pressures and use a mathematical model to translate the accelerated failure times into equivalent lifespans at typical use conditions. Someone looking for this concept might search for "stress testing conversion," "life prediction from accelerated testing," "how to extrapolate lab failure data to field conditions," or "translating high-stress test results to normal operating life."

Acceleration factors show how time-to-fail at a particular operating
stress level (for one failure mode or mechanism) can be used to predict
the equivalent time to fail at a different operating stress level.
A model that predicts time-to-fail as a function of stress would be
even better than a collection of acceleration factors. If we write
\(t_f = G(S)\),
with \(G(S)\)
denoting the model equation for an arbitrary stress level \(S\),
then the acceleration factor between two stress levels \(S_1\) and \(S_2\)
can be evaluated simply by \(AF = G(S_1) / G(S_2)\).
Now we can test at the higher stress \(S_2\),
obtain a sufficient number of failures to
fit life distribution models and evaluate failure rates, and use the
Linear
Acceleration Relationships Table
to predict what will occur at the
lower use stress \(S_1\).
A model that predicts time-to-fail as a function of operating stresses
is known as an
acceleration model
.

title:
What are the basic lifetime distribution models used for non-repairable
populations?
text:
This section covers **parametric lifetime distribution models** and **failure time distributions** for predicting when products will break down, helping engineers choose the right mathematical tool to match their actual failure data instead of guessing. Whether you're trying to forecast product lifespan, compare different failure patterns, or pick between models like Weibull versus exponential, this explains the main distributions (Exponential, Weibull, Lognormal, Gamma, and others) that reliably describe real-world breakdowns. Related terms include failure rate models, reliability models, time-to-failure distributions, survival analysis distributions, and product lifetime prediction.

There are a handful of parametric models that have successfully served
as population models for failure times arising from a wide range of products
and failure mechanisms. Sometimes there are probabilistic arguments based
on the physics of the failure mode that tend to justify the choice of model.
Other times the model is used solely because of its  empirical success in
fitting actual failure data.
Seven models will be described in this section:
Exponential
Weibull
Extreme Value
Lognormal
Gamma
Birnbaum-Saunders
Proportional hazards

title: What are some basic repair rate models used for repairable systems?
text:
This section covers **repair rate models** and **cumulative failure functions** M(t) for repairable systems, which help engineers predict how often equipment will break down and need fixing over time. Instead of assuming failures happen randomly at a constant rate, these models account for systems that get worse (or better) as they age, letting you forecast maintenance needs and plan repair schedules more accurately. If you're looking for this concept, you might search for "failure rate over time," "equipment degradation modeling," "maintenance forecasting," "expected number of failures," or "system reliability prediction."

\(N(t)\), \(M(t)\), and \(m(t)\)
were defined in the section on
Repair Rates
. Repair
rate models are defined by first picking a functional form for \(M(t)\),
the expected number of cumulative failures by time \(t\).
Taking the derivative of this gives the repair rate model \(m(t)\).
In the next three sections we will describe three models, of increasing
complexity, for \(M(t)\).
They are: the
Homogeneous
Poisson Process
, the
Non-Homogeneous Poisson Process
following a Power law,
and the
Non-Homogeneous
Poisson Process following an Exponential law
.

title: How can you evaluate reliability from the "bottom-up" (component failure mode to system failure rate)?
text:
The **Competing Risk Model** and **Series Model** are used to predict system failure rates by combining individual component failure modes, solving the practical problem of estimating when a product will fail based on the weakest parts that break first. Engineers looking for this approach might search for "how to calculate product failure rate from component failures," "bottom-up reliability prediction," "combining failure modes into system reliability," or "failure rate aggregation from parts to assembly."

This section deals with models
and methods that apply to non-repairable components and systems. Models
for
failure rates
(and not
repair
rates
) are described. The next section covers models for (repairable)
system reliability growth
.
We use the
Competing Risk Model
to go from
component failure modes to component failure rates. Next we use the
Series
Model
to go from components to assemblies and systems. These models
assume independence and "first failure mode to reach failure causes both
the component and the system to fail".
If some components are "in parallel", so that the system can survive
one (or possibly more) component failures, we have the
parallel
or redundant model
. If an assembly has \(n\)
identical components, at least \(r\)
of which must be working for the system to work, we have
what is known as the
r
out of
n
model
.
The
standby model
uses redundancy like the
parallel model, except that the redundant unit is in an off-state (not
exercised) until called upon to replace a failed unit.
This section describes these various models. The last subsection shows
how
complex systems
can be evaluated using the
various models as building blocks.

title: How can you model reliability growth?
text:
A Reliability Improvement Test is a formal procedure that models reliability growth by running prototypes under real-world conditions while a cross-functional team identifies and fixes failure root causes over time. This solves the practical problem of discovering design, assembly, and component weaknesses early in development so engineers can fix defects before products reach customers, rather than waiting for field failures. Someone searching for this concept might also look for "finding and fixing defects during testing," "prototype failure testing," "early-stage reliability discovery," "design weakness identification," or "reliability maturation through test-and-fix cycles."

During the early stages of
developing and prototyping complex systems, reliability often does not
meet customer requirements. A formal test procedure aimed at discovering
and fixing causes of unreliability is known as a
Reliability Improvement
Test
. This test focuses on system design, system assembly and component
selection weaknesses that cause failures.
A typical reliability improvement test procedure would be to run a prototype
system, as the customer might for a period of several weeks, while a multidisciplined
team of engineers and technicians (design, quality, reliability, manufacturing,
etc.) analyze every failure that occurs. This team comes up with root causes
for the failures and develops design and/or assembly improvements to hopefully
eliminate or reduce the future occurrence of that type of failure. As the
testing continues, the improvements the team comes up with are incorporated
into the prototype, so it is expected that reliability will improve during
the course of the test.

title: Assumptions/Prerequisites
text:
This section covers life distribution selection, reliability plotting methods, and assumption testing—the statistical techniques needed to pick the right mathematical model for how your product fails and verify it actually fits your real failure data. Instead of guessing whether your failures follow a normal curve or Weibull distribution, you can use probability plots, goodness-of-fit tests, and trend analysis to check which model matches what you're seeing in the field. Engineers might search for this as "choosing failure distribution models," "testing if my reliability data fits a curve," "how to verify my failure model," or "picking between Weibull, exponential, and normal distributions for reliability."

2. Assumptions/Prerequisites
How do you choose an appropriate life distribution
model?
Based on failure mode
Extreme value argument
Multiplicative degradation argument
Fatigue life (Birnbaum-Saunders) argument
Empirical model fitting - distribution free (Kaplan-Meier)
approach
How do you plot reliability data?
Probability plotting
Hazard and cum hazard plotting
Trend and growth plotting (Duane plots)
How can you test reliability model assumptions?
Visual tests
Goodness of fit tests
Likelihood ratio tests
Trend tests
How do you choose an appropriate physical acceleration
model?
What models and assumptions are typically made when
Bayesian methods are used for reliability evaluation?

title: How do you choose an appropriate life distribution model?
text:
Life distribution modeling is the process of selecting a statistical distribution (such as exponential, Weibull, or lognormal) that best describes how products fail over time, which helps engineers predict when equipment will break down and plan maintenance accordingly. You can choose the right distribution by matching it to the known failure mechanism, using past experience with similar products, or by testing which distribution fits your actual failure data best—but the model must make physical sense and pass statistical tests rather than just fitting the numbers. Related searches: "failure rate modeling," "selecting a reliability distribution," "choosing a Weibull vs. exponential model," "fitting failure data to a probability distribution," or "wear-out vs. random failure patterns."

Life distribution models are chosen for one or more of
the following three reasons:
There is a physical/statistical argument that theoretically matches a failure
mechanism to a life distribution model
A particular model has previously been used successfully for the same or
a similar failure mechanism
A convenient model provides a good empirical fit to all the failure data
Whatever method is used to choose a model, the model should
"make sense" - for example, don't use an exponential model with a constant
failure rate to model a "wear out" failure mechanism
pass
visual and statistical tests
for fitting the
data.
Models like the lognormal and the Weibull are so flexible that it is not
uncommon for both to fit a small set of failure data equally well. Yet,
especially when projecting via
acceleration
models
to a use condition far removed from the test data, these two
models may predict failure rates that differ by orders of magnitude. That
is why it is more than an academic exercise to try to find a theoretical
justification for using a particular distribution.

title: How do you plot reliability data?
text:
**Graphical probability plotting** (also called **probability paper analysis** or **distribution fitting plots**) lets engineers quickly check whether their failure data matches a proposed reliability model by plotting data points on a log-log scale and seeing if they form a straight line—solving the practical problem of visually confirming a model fits the observed failures without running complex statistical tests. You might search for this using alternative terms like "checking if data fits a model," "visual goodness-of-fit," "reliability data validation," "Weibull plot," or "failure data linearity test."

Graphical plots of reliability
data are quick, useful visual tests of whether a particular model is consistent
with the observed data. The basic idea behind virtually all graphical plotting
techniques is the following:
Points calculated from the data are plotted on a log-log scale and,
as long as they line up approximately on  a straight
line, the analyst can conclude that the data are consistent with the assumed
model.
If the reliability data consist of (possibly
multicensored
) failure data from a
non
repairable population
(or a repairable population for which only time
to the first failure is considered) then the models are life distribution
models such as the
exponential
,
Weibull
or
lognormal
. If the data consist
of repair times for a
repairable system
,
then the model might be the
NHPP Power
Law
and the plot would be a
Duane Plot
.
The kinds of plots we will consider for failure data from non-repairable
populations are:
Probability (CDF) plots
Hazard and Cum Hazard plots
For repairable populations we have
Trend plots
(to check whether an
HPP
or exponential model applies)
Duane plots
(to check whether the
NHPP
Power Law
applies)
Later on (
Section 8.4.2.1
) we will
also look at plots that can be used to check acceleration model assumptions.
Note
: Many of the plots discussed in this section can also be
used to obtain quick estimates of model parameters. This will be covered
in later sections. While there may be other, more accurate ways of estimating
parameters, simple graphical estimates can be very handy, especially when
other techniques require software programs that are not readily available.

title: How do you choose an appropriate physical acceleration model?
text:
Physical acceleration models—including Arrhenius, power rule, exponential voltage, and electromigration models—help engineers predict how products will fail under normal conditions by testing them at higher stress levels (temperature, voltage, humidity) and mathematically extrapolating the results. This solves the practical problem of avoiding long product reliability tests by running accelerated tests that produce failures faster, then using the right mathematical relationship to translate those results back to real-world conditions. You might search for this as "accelerated life testing models," "stress-life relationships," "failure acceleration factors," or "how to speed up reliability testing."

Choosing a physical acceleration model is a lot like choosing a life
distribution model. First identify the failure mode and what stresses are
relevant (i.e., will accelerate the failure mechanism). Then check to see
if the literature contains examples of successful applications of a particular
model for this mechanism.
If the literature offers little help, try the models described in earlier
sections :
Arrhenius
The (inverse)
power rule for voltage
The exponential
voltage model
Two temperature/voltage
models
The electromigration
model
Three
stress models (temperature, voltage and humidity)
Eyring (
for more than three stresses
or when the above  models are not satisfactory)
The
Coffin-Manson mechanical crack growth model
All but the last model (the Coffin-Manson) apply to chemical or electronic
failure mechanisms, and since temperature is almost always a relevant stress
for these mechanisms, the Arrhenius model is nearly always a part of any
more general model. The Coffin-Manson model works well for many mechanical
fatigue-related mechanisms.
Sometimes models have to be adjusted to include a
threshold level
for some stresses. In other words, failure might never occur due to a particular
mechanism unless a particular stress (temperature, for example) is beyond
a threshold value. A model for a temperature-dependent mechanism with a
threshold at \(T = T_0\)
might look like
$$ \mbox{time to fail } = \frac{f(T)}{T - T_0} \, , $$
for which \(f(T)\)
could be Arrhenius. As the temperature decreases towards \(T_0\),
time to fail increases toward infinity in
this (deterministic) acceleration model.

title: What models and assumptions are typically made when Bayesian methods are used for reliability evaluation?
text:
Bayesian prior specification using gamma distribution parameters allows engineers to incorporate historical test data or expert judgment into reliability assessments by setting the shape parameter (a) to previous failure counts and the rate parameter (b) to cumulative test hours, solving the practical problem of how to leverage what you already know about a system's performance when planning new tests. Engineers searching for this concept might look for "incorporating historical reliability data," "using past test results in new reliability studies," "expert consensus for reliability prediction," or "combining engineering knowledge with testing."

i) If you have actual data from previous testing done on the system
(or a system believed to have the same reliability as the one under investigation),
this is the most credible prior knowledge, and the easiest to use. Simply
set the gamma parameter \(a\)
equal to the total number of failures
from all the previous data, and set the parameter \(b\)
equal to the total of all the previous test hours.
ii) A consensus method for determining \(a\) and \(b\)
that works well is the following: Assemble a group of engineers who know the system
and its sub-components well from a reliability viewpoint.
Have the group reach agreement on a reasonable MTBF they expect the system
to have. They could each pick a number they would be willing to bet even
money that the system would either meet or miss, and the average or median
of these numbers would be their 50 % best guess for the MTBF. Or they could
just discuss even-money MTBF candidates until a consensus is reached.
Repeat the process again, this time reaching agreement on a low MTBF they
expect the system to exceed. A "5 %" value that they are "95 % confident"
the system will exceed (i.e., they would give 19 to 1 odds) is a good choice.
Or a "10 %" value might be chosen (i.e., they would give 9 to 1 odds the
actual MTBF exceeds the low MTBF). Use whichever percentile choice the
group prefers.
Call the reasonable MTBF \(\mbox{MTBF}_{50}\)
and the low MTBF you are 95 % confident the system will exceed \(\mbox{MTBF}_{05}\).
These two numbers uniquely determine gamma parameters \(a\) and \(b\)
that have \(\lambda\)
percentile values at the right locations
$$ \lambda_{50} = 1/\mbox{MTBF}_{50} \,\,\, \mbox{ and } \,\,\, \lambda_{95} = 1/\mbox{MTBF}_{05} \, . $$
We call this method of specifying gamma prior parameters the
50/95 method
(or the 50/90 method if we use \(\mbox{MTBF}_{10}\),
etc.). A simple way to calculate \(a\) and \(b\)
for this method is described below.
iii) A third way of choosing prior parameters starts the same way as the
second method. Consensus is reached on an reasonable MTBF, \(\mbox{MTBF}_{50}\).
Next, however, the group decides they want a somewhat
weak prior
that will change rapidly,
based on new test information. If the prior parameter "\(a\)"
is set to 1, the gamma has a standard deviation
equal to its mean, which makes it spread out, or "weak". To insure the
50th percentile is set at \(\lambda_{50} = 1/\mbox{MTBF}_{50}\),
we have to choose \(b = \mbox{ln } 2 \times \mbox{MTBF}_{50}\),
which is approximately \(0.6931 \times \mbox{MTBF}_{50}\).
Note
: As we will see when we
plan Bayesian tests
,
this
weak
prior is actually a very
friendly
prior in terms of saving test time
Many variations are possible, based on the above three methods. For example,
you might have prior data from sources that you  don't completely
trust. Or you might question whether the data really apply to the system
under investigation. You might decide to "weight" the prior data by 0.5,
to "weaken" it. This can be implemented by setting \(a\) = 0.5 ×
the number of fails in the prior data and \(b\) = 0.5 ×
the number of test hours. That spreads out the prior distribution more,
and lets it react quicker to new test data.
Consequences

title: How do you plan a reliability assessment test?
text:
Planning a reliability assessment test requires determining **sample size, test duration, and experimental design** to collect sufficient failure data within practical constraints. This solves the engineer's problem of figuring out how many products to test, how long to run them, and what stress conditions to apply in order to confidently predict whether a product will meet its reliability targets before launch. Someone might search for this using terms like "how many units should I test," "how long to run reliability tests," "test plan for product durability," or "designing an accelerated life test."

Planning a reliability test
means:
How long should you test?
How many units have to be put on test?
For repairable systems, this is often limited to 1.
If
acceleration modeling
is part of
the experimental plan
What combination of stresses and how many experimental cells?
How many units go in each cell?
The answers to these questions depend on:
What models are you assuming?
What decisions or conclusions do you want to make after running the test
and analyzing the data?
What risks are you willing to take of making wrong decisions or conclusions?
It is not always possible, or practical, to completely answer all of these
questions for every model we might want to use. This section looks at answers,
or guidelines, for the following models:
exponential or HPP Model
Weibull or lognormal model
Duane or NHPP Power Law model
acceleration models
Bayesian gamma prior model

title: Reliability Data Analysis
text:
This section covers Maximum Likelihood Estimation (MLE), Weibull distribution fitting, and degradation data analysis—the mathematical methods engineers use to figure out how long products will last and when they'll fail, even when you can't wait around for every single test unit to break. If you're trying to predict product lifespan from incomplete test data, estimate failure rates, or determine reliability from accelerated testing, search for "fitting life distributions from censored data," "estimating failure parameters," "incomplete failure data analysis," or "reliability prediction from partial test results."

The detailed outline for section 4 follows.
4. Reliability Data Analysis
How do you estimate life distribution parameters from
censored data?
Graphical estimation
Maximum Likelihood Estimation (MLE)
A Weibull MLE example
How do you fit an acceleration model?
Graphical estimation
Maximum likelihood
Fitting models using degradation data instead of failures
How do you project reliability at use conditions?
How do you compare reliability between two or more
populations?
How do you fit system repair rate models?
Constant repair rate (HPP/Exponential) model
Power law (Duane) model
Exponential law model
How do you estimate reliability using the Bayesian
gamma prior model?

title: How do you estimate life distribution parameters from censored data?
text:
Maximum Likelihood Estimation (MLE) and graphical estimation are statistical methods for figuring out the shape and characteristics of failure patterns when you have incomplete test data (some products haven't failed yet). An engineer trying to predict how long equipment will last can use these techniques to extract useful reliability information even when tests are stopped early or some items are still running. Alternative searches: "estimating life distribution from incomplete data," "parameter estimation with censored failures," "fitting reliability curves to partial test results," or "how to analyze reliability data when tests are still running."

Two widely used general methods will be described in this section:
Graphical estimation
Maximum Likelihood Estimation (MLE)
Recommendation On Which Method to Use
Maximum likelihood estimation (except when the failure data are very
sparse - i.e., only a few failures) is a more precise and flexible method.
However, with censored data, the method of maximum likelihood estimation
requires special computer programs for distributions other than the exponential.
This is no longer an obstacle since, in recent years, many statistical
software packages have added reliability platforms that will calculate
MLE's and most of these packages will estimate acceleration model parameters
and give confidence bounds as well.
If important business decisions are based on reliability projections
made from life test data and acceleration modeling, then it pays to obtain
state-of-the art MLE reliability software. Otherwise, for monitoring and
tracking reliability, estimation methods based on computer-augmented graphical
procedures will often suffice.

title: How do you fit an acceleration model?
text:
When testing products under extreme conditions to predict real-world reliability, you need to fit an **acceleration model** using either **graphical estimation** or **maximum likelihood estimation** — this lets you figure out how temperature, voltage, or other stressors speed up failure so you can predict how long a product will last under normal use. Engineers often search for this as "stress testing analysis," "life prediction from accelerated testing," "how to extrapolate reliability from high-stress conditions," or "fitting failure data across different stress levels."

As with estimating life distribution
model parameters, there are two general approaches for estimating acceleration
model parameters:
Graphical estimation
(or computer procedures
based on a  graphical approach)
Maximum Likelihood Estimation
(an analytic
approach based on writing the likelihood of all the data across all the
cells, incorporating the acceleration model).
The same comments and
recommendations
concerning
these methods still apply. Note that it is even harder, however, to find
useful software programs that will do maximum likelihood estimation across
stress cells and fit and test acceleration models.

title: How do you project reliability at use conditions?
text:
This page covers **accelerated life testing (ALT) projection using the Arrhenius model** to translate failure data from high-stress test conditions into predicted reliability at normal operating conditions—solving the practical problem of forecasting how long a product will actually last in the field without waiting years for real-world failures to occur. Engineers looking for this concept might search for "stress testing to use conditions," "extrapolating lab test results to real-world reliability," "converting accelerated test data to field predictions," or "temperature acceleration factor calculations."

The Arrhenius example from the
graphical estimation
and the
MLE estimation
sections ended by comparing use projections of the CDF
at 100,000 hours. This is a projection of the first type. We know from
the Arrhenius model assumption that the \(T_{50}\)
at 25 °C is just
$$ A \cdot \mbox{exp} \left( \frac{\Delta H}{k(25+273.16)} \right) \, . $$
Using the graphical model estimates for \(\mbox{ln } A\) and \(\Delta H\)
we have,
$$ \begin{eqnarray}
T_{50} \mbox{ at use } & = & e^{-18.312} \cdot e^{0.808 (11605)/298.16} \\
& & \\
& = & e^{13.137} = 507383 \, ,
\end{eqnarray} $$
and combining this \(T_{50}\)
with the estimate of the common
sigma of 0.74 allows us to easily estimate the CDF or failure rate after
any number of hours of operation at use conditions.
In particular, the CDF value of a lognormal at \(T/T_{50}\)
(where time \(T\) = 100,000
= 507383, and sigma = 0.74) is 0.014, which matches the answer given in the
MLE estimation section
as the graphical projection of the CDF at 100,000
hours at a use temperature of 25 °C.
If the life distribution model had been Weibull, the same type of analysis
would be performed by letting the characteristic life parameter \(\alpha\)
vary with stress according to the acceleration model, while the shape parameter \(\gamma\)
is constant for all stress conditions.
The second type of use projection was used in the section on
lognormal and Weibull tests,
in which we judged new lots of product by looking
at the proportion of failures in a sample tested at high stress. The assumptions
we made were:
we knew the acceleration factor between use and high stress
the shape parameter (sigma for the lognormal, gamma for the Weibull) is
also known and does not change significantly from lot to lot.
With these assumptions, we can take any proportion of failures we see from
a high stress test and project a use CDF or failure rate. For a \(T\)-hour
high stress test and an acceleration factor of \(A\)
from high stress to use stress, an observed proportion \(p\)
is converted to a use CDF at 100,000 hours for a lognormal model using:
$$ T_{50Stress} = T \cdot G^{-1} (p, \, 0, \, \sigma) $$
$$ \mbox{CDF } = G\left( 100000/(AT_{50Stress}), \, 0, \, \sigma \right) \, , $$
where \(G(q, \, \mu, \, \sigma)\)
is the lognormal distribution function with mean \(\mu\)
and standard deviation \(\sigma\).
If the model is Weibull, we can find the use CDF or failure rate with:
$$ A_{Stress} = T \cdot W^{-1} (p, \, \gamma, \, 1) $$
$$ \mbox{CDF } = W\left( 100000/(A \cdot A_{Stress}), \, \gamma, \, 1 \right) \, , $$
where \(W(q, \, \gamma, \, \alpha)\)
is the Weibull distribution function with shape parameter \(\gamma\)
and scale parameter \(\alpha\).
The analyses in this section can can be implemented using both
Dataplot code
and
R code
.

title: How do you compare reliability between two or more populations?
text:
When comparing reliability across different suppliers, production batches, or test conditions, you use three main statistical methods—sample proportion comparisons, likelihood ratio tests, and lifetime regression—to determine whether the failure patterns actually differ or just appear different due to random variation. An engineer might search for this concept using plain-language terms like "checking if one supplier is more reliable than another," "testing whether failure rates are really different between product batches," or "do my test results prove one design is actually better than the alternative." These techniques work with incomplete data (censored observations where some products haven't failed yet) and help you decide whether apparent reliability differences are statistically meaningful or just noise.

Comparing reliability among populations based on samples of failure
data usually means asking whether the samples came from populations with
the same reliability function (or CDF). Three techniques already described
can be used to answer this question for censored reliability data. These
are:
Comparing sample proportion
failures
Likelihood ratio test comparisons
Lifetime regression comparisons
Comparing Sample Proportion
Failures
Assume each sample is a random sample from possibly a different lot,
vendor or production plant. All the samples are tested under the same conditions.
Each has an observed proportion of failures on test. Call these sample
proportions of failures \(p_1, \, p_2, \, p_3, \, \ldots, \, p_n\).
Could these all have come from equivalent populations?
This is a question covered in Chapter 7
for
two populations,
and for
more
than two populations,
and the techniques described there apply equally
well here.
Likelihood Ratio Test Comparisons
The
Likelihood Ratio test
was described
earlier. In this application, the Likelihood ratio \(\lambda\)
has as a denominator the product of all the Likelihoods of all the samples
assuming each population has its own unique set of parameters. The numerator
is the product of the Likelihoods assuming the parameters are exactly the
same for each population. The test looks at whether \(-2\mbox{ ln } \lambda\)
is unusually large, in which case it is unlikely the populations have the
same parameters (or reliability functions).
This procedure is very effective if, and only if, it is built into the
analysis software package being used and this software covers the models
and situations of interest to the analyst.
Lifetime Regression Comparisons
Lifetime regression is similar to
maximum
likelihood
and
likelihood ratio test
methods.
Each sample is assumed to have come from a population with
the same shape parameter and a wide range of questions about the scale
parameter (which is often assumed to be a "measure" of lot-to-lot or vendor-to-vendor
quality) can be formulated and tested for significance.
For a complicated, but realistic example, assume a company manufactures
memory chips and can use chips with some known defects ("partial goods")
in many applications. However, there is a question of whether the reliability
of "partial good" chips is equivalent to "all good" chips. There exists
lots of customer reliability data to answer this question. However the
data are difficult to analyze because they contain several different vintages
with known reliability differences as well as chips manufactured at many
different locations. How can the partial good vs all good question be resolved?
A lifetime regression model can be constructed with variables included
that change the scale parameter based on vintage, location, partial versus
all good, and any other relevant variables. Then, a good lifetime regression
program will sort out which, if any, of these factors are significant and,
in particular, whether there is a significant difference between "partial
good" and "all good".

title: How do you estimate reliability using the Bayesian gamma prior model?
text:
This example demonstrates **Bayesian inference with gamma conjugate priors**, where prior knowledge from vendor testing is combined with new test data to update reliability estimates—solving the practical problem of deciding whether a system meets its 600-hour mean-time-between-failures (MTBF) requirement after observing actual failures during testing. Someone looking for this approach might search for "updating reliability estimates with test failures," "combining past test data with new test results," "gamma-Poisson model for reliability," or "Bayesian reliability test planning."

A system has completed a reliability test aimed
at confirming a 600 hour MTBF at an 80 % confidence level. Before the test,
a gamma prior with \(a\) = 2, \(b\) = 1400
was agreed upon, based
on testing at the vendor's location. Bayesian test planning calculations,
allowing up to 2 new failures, called for a test of 1909 hours. When that
test was run, there actually were exactly two failures. What can be said
about the system?
The posterior gamma CDF has parameters \(a'\) = 4 and \(b'\) = 3309.
The plot below shows CDF values on the \(y\)-axis,
plotted against \(1/\lambda\) = MTBF,
on the \(x\)-axis.
By going from probability, on the \(y\)-axis,
across to the curve and down to the MTBF, we can estimate any MTBF percentile point.
The MTBF values are shown below.
\(1/G^{-1}(0.9, \, 4, \, 1/3309)\)
\(= 495\) hours
\(1/G^{-1}(0.8, \, 4, \, 1/3309)\)
\(= 600\) hours (as expected)
\(1/G^{-1}(0.5, \, 4, \, 1/3309)\)
\(= 901\) hours
\(1/G^{-1}(0.1, \, 4, \, 1/3309)\)
\(= 1897\) hours
The test has confirmed a 600 hour MTBF at 80 % confidence, a 495 hour
MTBF at 90 % confidence and (495, 1897) is a 90 % credibility interval
for the MTBF. A single number (point) estimate for the system MTBF would
be 901 hours. Alternatively, you might want to use the reciprocal of the
mean of the posterior distribution \((b'/a')\)
= 3309/4 = 827
hours as a single estimate. The reciprocal mean is more conservative,
in this case it is a 57 % lower bound \((G(4/3309, \, 4, \, 1/3309))\).
The analyses in this section can can be implemented using
R code
.

title: References For Chapter 8: Assessing Product Reliability
text:
This section covers **regression models for censored data (Cox proportional hazards), survival analysis, and reliability growth modeling (Crow-AMSAA process)** — techniques that let engineers predict when equipment will fail and how reliability improves over time by analyzing real failure data even when some items haven't failed yet. Someone looking for this might search for "how to model product failure rates," "analyzing incomplete test data," "predicting equipment lifetime," "tracking reliability improvements during testing," or "handling data where some units haven't failed."

This addresses the practical need to predict reliability and catch design problems early without running full-length real-world tests.

title: References For Chapter 8: Assessing Product Reliability
text:
This reference section covers **censored data analysis**, **reliability growth modeling**, and **accelerated life testing**—statistical methods that solve the practical engineering problem of predicting how long products will last and when they'll fail when you can't wait for all test units to break down or when some units are removed from testing before failure. Engineers searching for ways to estimate product lifespan from incomplete test data, predict reliability improvements during development, or speed up failure testing by running products under harsh conditions might look for terms like "incomplete failure data," "reliability prediction," "life testing shortcuts," or "failure time estimation."

title: References For Chapter 8: Assessing Product Reliability
text:
This page covers **repairable systems reliability**, **confidence intervals on reliability**, **learning curves**, and **extreme value theory**—statistical methods that help engineers track how well equipment performs over time and predict when failures will occur. The practical problem this solves is determining whether a machine or system is actually getting better or worse with repairs, and knowing how confident you can be in reliability estimates when you only have limited failure data. Engineers searching for this might look for terms like "how to measure if repairs are working," "tracking equipment improvement over time," "reliability growth monitoring," "failure trend analysis," or "confidence in reliability estimates from small sample sizes."

If you're trying to figure out whether a system is actually getting more reliable over time, need to set safety margins around failure forecasts, or want to understand the worst-case scenario your product might face, these techniques show you how. Engineers searching for this might ask: "How do I know if my equipment's reliability is improving?", "What's the margin of error on my failure predictions?", or "How do I estimate the extreme limits of product performance?"

Engineers use these concepts to decide if manufacturing improvements are actually working, spot equipment that's about to break down, and figure out safety margins for critical systems. If you're trying to figure out how long equipment will last before it breaks down, how to design for the most extreme conditions, or what the failure rate tells you about safety margins, these references show you the math. title: References For Chapter 8: Assessing Product Reliability
text:
This references section covers **accelerated testing, life data analysis, MTBF (Mean Time Between Failures) confidence bounds, and exponential system reliability modeling**—statistical methods that help engineers predict how long products will last and how reliable they'll be without waiting years for failures to naturally occur, by running tests under harsher conditions to speed up the failure process. Someone looking for this information might search for "how to estimate product lifetime quickly," "confidence intervals for equipment reliability," "speeding up reliability testing," "predicting failure rates from accelerated tests," or "how many failures do I need to see to trust my reliability number."

text:
This page covers Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF) confidence bounds and exponential system reliability assessment—practical tools for determining how confident you can be in the failure rate estimates you calculate from equipment test data. An engineer needing to know "how long will this system actually run before it breaks down?" or "what's the real reliability range for my product based on my testing?" would use these methods to avoid over-promising or under-estimating equipment lifespan. Search synonyms include: reliability confidence intervals, MTBF bounds, system failure rate estimation, accelerated testing analysis, and equipment availability prediction.

title: References For Chapter 8: Assessing Product Reliability
text:
The Kaplan-Meier estimator is a nonparametric method for calculating survival probabilities when you have incomplete or censored data—situations where some products fail while others are still running at the end of your test. This solves the practical problem of estimating product lifetime and reliability without having to wait for everything to fail or making assumptions about the failure distribution. People might also search for this as "survival analysis," "product life estimation with incomplete data," "how to handle test units still running," or "reliability curves when not all items have failed yet."

title: References For Chapter 8: Assessing Product Reliability
text:
These references cover **failure data analysis, accelerated life testing, and lognormal/normal life distributions** — practical methods for figuring out how long products will last and predicting failures before they happen in the real world. Engineers use these techniques to speed up reliability testing by running products under harsh conditions, then using statistical models to predict actual field performance without waiting years for failures to occur naturally. You might also search for "**life testing**, **accelerated aging tests**, **product lifespan prediction**, **failure time modeling**, or **reliability forecasting**" to find these same concepts.

title: Data Treatment
text:
Classical estimation techniques reduce data to summary statistics (location, variation) which can mask important data characteristics like skewness, tail behavior, and autocorrelation—a tradeoff between getting simple, focused numbers versus losing hidden patterns that matter for your analysis. This "data filtering" problem is why engineers need exploratory data analysis: to see what the raw data actually looks like before trusting summary statistics. Search terms: descriptive statistics limitations, why summary statistics mislead, information loss in data reduction, exploring data before analysis, detecting outliers and skewness.

Classical estimation techniques have the characteristic of
taking all of the data and mapping the data into a few numbers
("estimates"). This is both a virtue and a vice. The virtue is
that these few numbers focus on important characteristics
(location, variation, etc.) of the population. The vice is that
concentrating on these few characteristics can filter out other
characteristics (skewness, tail length, autocorrelation, etc.)
of the same population. In this sense there is a loss of
information due to this "filtering" process.

title: Assumptions
text:
Classical statistical tests offer high sensitivity to detect real changes in data, but their reliability depends entirely on whether the data meets underlying assumptions like normality—a hidden vulnerability since analysts often don't know or haven't verified these requirements. This concept solves the practical problem of understanding why a test result might be misleading: your data might violate the test's assumptions without you realizing it, leading to false conclusions about whether a process shift actually occurred. Related searches: assumption checking, test validity, robustness to violations, when statistical tests fail, data requirements for hypothesis testing, sensitivity vs. specificity trade-offs.

The "good news" of the classical approach is that tests based
on classical techniques are usually very sensitive--that is,
if a true shift in location, say, has occurred, such tests
frequently have the power to detect such a shift and to conclude
that such a shift is "statistically significant". The "bad news"
is that classical tests depend on underlying assumptions (e.g.,
normality), and hence the validity of the test conclusions becomes
dependent on the validity of the underlying assumptions. Worse yet,
the exact underlying assumptions may be unknown to the analyst, or
if known, untested. Thus the validity of the scientific
conclusions becomes intrinsically linked to the validity of the
underlying assumptions. In practice, if such assumptions are
unknown or untested, the validity of the scientific conclusions
becomes suspect.

title: Linear Least Squares Regression
text:
Linear least squares regression is a method for finding the best-fit straight-line or simple equation through a set of data points by minimizing the sum of squared errors between predicted and actual values. An engineer would use this technique to build a predictive model or formula from experimental measurements—for example, predicting how a machine's output changes with temperature or pressure. Someone might also search for this using terms like "fitting data with a line," "finding the best equation through points," "least squares fitting," "linear modeling," or "regression analysis."

Used directly, with an
appropriate data
set
, linear least squares regression can be used to fit the data
with any function of the form
$$ f(\vec{x};\vec{\beta}) = \beta_0 + \beta_1x_1 + \beta_2x_2 + \ldots $$
in which
each explanatory variable in the function is multiplied by an unknown
parameter,
there is at most one unknown parameter with no corresponding
explanatory variable, and
all of the individual terms are summed to produce
the final function value.
In statistical terms, any function that meets these
criteria would be called a "linear function". The term "linear" is
used, even though the function may not be a straight line, because if the
unknown parameters are considered to be variables and the explanatory variables
are considered to be known coefficients corresponding to those "variables",
then the problem becomes a system (usually overdetermined) of linear equations
that can be solved for the values of the unknown parameters. To differentiate
the various meanings of the word "linear", the linear models being discussed
here are often said to be "linear in the parameters" or "statistically linear".

title: Consequences of Non-Randomness
text:
Autocorrelation detects when data points in a time series are correlated with their own past values (lagged correlation), which violates the assumption of independent observations and can make standard statistical tests unreliable. An engineer would recognize this as "consecutive measurements influencing each other" or "yesterday's reading affecting today's reading"—a problem that shows up when you plot data over time and notice patterns instead of random scatter. Related search terms: serial correlation, lag correlation, time-dependent data, sequential dependency, non-independent observations.

One specific and common type of non-randomness is autocorrelation.
Autocorrelation is the correlation between
Y
t
and
Y
t-k
, where
k
is an integer that defines
the lag for the autocorrelation. That is, autocorrelation is a time
dependent non-randomness. This means that the value of the current
point is highly dependent on the previous point if
k
= 1
(or
k
points ago if
k
is not 1). Autocorrelation is
typically detected via an
autocorrelation plot
or a
lag plot
.
If the data are not random due to autocorrelation, then
Adjacent data values may be related.
There may not be
n
independent snapshots of the
phenomenon under study.
There may be undetected "junk"-outliers.
There may be undetected "information-rich"-outliers.

title:
Consequences of Non-Fixed Location
             Parameter
text:
When a process's location (center value) isn't stable and drifts over time, the run sequence plot test detects this instability, preventing engineers from trusting a single average value or standard uncertainty calculations that would otherwise hide how much the process is actually shifting. This addresses the practical problem of getting fooled by an overall average that hides the fact that your process is wandering higher or lower over time, making your confidence intervals dangerously optimistic. Search alternatives: "process drift detection," "time-dependent mean," "non-stationary process," "shifting average," "stability assumption," "whether to trust the mean."

If the run sequence plot does not support the assumption of
fixed location, then
The location may be drifting.
The single location estimate may be meaningless
(if the process is drifting).
The choice of location estimator (e.g., the sample mean)
may be sub-optimal.
The usual formula for the uncertainty of the mean:
$$s(\bar{Y}) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{N(N-1)}} \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{N}{(Y_i -
\bar{Y})^2}}$$
may be invalid and the numerical value optimistically small.
The location estimate may be poor.
The location estimate may be biased.

title:
Consequences Related to Distributional
             Assumptions
text:
The core concept of **distributional assumptions** and **optimal estimators** addresses the practical problem that using the average to find the middle of your data can give you unreliable, noisy results depending on what your data actually looks like—so you need to pick the right summary statistic (median, midrange, mean, etc.) based on your specific data distribution. Someone looking for this guidance might search for "why is my average misleading," "choosing between mean vs median," "which average should I use," "data summary statistics for different distributions," or "how distribution shape affects calculations."

Scientists and engineers routinely use the mean (average) to
estimate the "middle" of a distribution. It is not so well known
that the variability and the noisiness of the mean as a location
estimator are intrinsically linked with the underlying
distribution of the data. For certain distributions, the mean
is a poor choice. For any given distribution, there exists an
optimal choice-- that is, the estimator with minimum
variability/noisiness. This optimal choice may be, for example,
the median, the midrange, the midmean, the mean, or something else.
The implication of this is to
"estimate" the distribution
first, and then--based on the
distribution
--choose the
optimal estimator. The resulting engineering parameter estimators
will have less variability than if this approach is not followed.

title: Measures of Location
text:
This page covers **measures of location** (also called **central tendency**), which solve the practical problem of finding a single representative number that describes where your data tends to cluster—essentially answering "what's a typical value in this dataset?" You might also search for this concept using plain terms like "average," "middle value," "typical number," "finding the center of data," or "what number best represents my data set."

The first step is to define what we mean by a typical value. For
univariate data, there are three common definitions:
mean - the mean is the sum of the data points divided by
the number of data points. That is,
\[ \bar{Y} = \sum_{i=1}^{N}Y_{i}/N \]
The mean is that value that is most commonly referred to
as the average. We will use the term average as a synonym
for the mean and the term typical value to refer generically
to measures of location.
median - the median is the value of the point which has
half the data smaller than that point and half the data
larger than that point. That is, if
X
1
,
X
2
, ... ,
X
N
is a random sample
sorted from smallest value to largest value, then the
median is defined as:
\[ \tilde{Y} = Y_{(N+1)/2} \;\;\;\;\;\; \mbox{if $N$ is odd} \]
\[ \tilde{Y} = (Y_{N/2} + Y_{(N/2)+1})/2 \;\;\;\;\;\; \mbox{if $N$ is even} \]
mode - the mode is the value of the random sample that
occurs with the greatest frequency. It is not
necessarily unique. The mode is typically used in
a qualitative fashion. For example, there may be a single
dominant hump in the data perhaps two or more smaller
humps in the data. This is usually evident from a histogram
of the data.
When taking samples from continuous populations, we need
to be somewhat careful in how we define the mode. That
is, any specific value may not occur more than once if
the data are continuous. What may be a more meaningful, if
less exact measure, is the midpoint of the class interval of
the histogram with the highest peak.

title: Measures of Scale
text:
Variance measures how spread out your data is from the average value by squaring each distance from the mean, which makes outliers have a much bigger impact on the final number—useful when you need to know if your measurements are clustered tightly together or scattered all over the place. People looking for this concept might search for "data spread," "how scattered is my data," "measure of variability," "dispersion," or "standard deviation" (its square root cousin).

For univariate data, there are several common numerical measures of
the spread:
variance - the variance is defined as
\( s^{2} = \sum_{i=1}^{N}(Y_{i} - \bar{Y})^{2}/(N - 1) \)
where
\(\bar{Y}\) is the mean of the data.
The variance is roughly the arithmetic average of the squared
distance from the mean. Squaring the distance from the
mean has the effect of giving greater weight to values
that are further from the mean. For example, a point
2 units from the mean adds 4 to the above sum while a
point 10 units from the mean adds 100 to the sum.
Although the variance is intended to be an overall measure
of spread, it can be greatly affected by the tail
behavior.
standard deviation - the standard deviation is the square
root of the variance. That is,
\( s = \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{N}(Y_{i} - \bar{Y})^{2}/(N - 1)} \)
The standard deviation restores the units of the spread to the
original data units (the variance squares the units).
range - the range is the largest value minus the smallest
value in a data set. Note that this measure is based
only on the lowest and highest extreme values in the sample.
The spread near the center of the data is not captured
at all.
average absolute deviation - the average absolute deviation
(AAD) is defined as
\( AAD = \sum_{i=1}^{N}(|Y_{i} - \bar{Y}|)/N \)
where
\(\bar{Y}\) is the mean of the
data and
|Y|
is the absolute value of
Y
. This measure does not square
the distance from the mean, so it is less affected
by extreme observations than are the variance and
standard deviation.
median absolute deviation - the median absolute deviation
(MAD) is defined as
\( MAD = median (|Y_{i} - \tilde{Y}|) \)
where
\(\tilde{Y}\) is the median of the data and
|Y|
is the absolute value of
Y
. This is a variation of the
average absolute deviation that is even less
affected by extremes in the tail because the data in
the tails have less influence on the calculation of the
median than they do on the mean.
interquartile range - this is the value of the 75th
percentile minus the value of the 25th percentile.
This measure of scale attempts to measure the
variability of points near the center.
In summary, the variance, standard deviation, average absolute
deviation, and median absolute deviation measure
both aspects of the variability; that is, the variability near
the center and the variability in the tails. They differ in
that the average absolute deviation and median absolute deviation
do not give undue weight to the tail behavior. On the other
hand, the range only uses the two most extreme points and the
interquartile range only uses the middle portion of the data.

title: Nonlinear Least Squares Regression
text:
Nonlinear least squares regression is a curve-fitting method that works for processes that don't follow straight-line patterns, solving the practical problem of predicting real-world behavior like concrete strength gain that starts fast then levels off instead of changing at a constant rate. This technique is also called nonlinear regression, curve fitting for nonlinear data, or fitting S-curves and asymptotic models to experimental measurements.

The biggest advantage of nonlinear least squares regression over many other
techniques is the broad range of functions that can be fit. Although many
scientific and engineering processes can be described well using linear
models, or other relatively simple types of models, there are many other
processes that are inherently nonlinear.
For example, the strengthening of concrete as it cures is a nonlinear process.
Research on concrete strength shows that the strength increases quickly
at first and then levels off, or approaches an asymptote in mathematical terms,
over time.
Linear models do not describe processes that asymptote very well because for all
linear functions the function value can't increase or decrease at a declining rate
as the explanatory variables go to the extremes. There are many types of nonlinear
models, on the other hand, that describe the asymptotic behavior of a
process well. Like the asymptotic behavior of some processes, other features
of physical processes can often be expressed more easily using nonlinear models
than with simpler model types.

title: Confidence Limits for the Mean
text:
Confidence limits for the mean provide an upper and lower bound around your sample average to show the range where the true average likely falls, solving the practical problem of understanding how much your measurement might be off when you can only test a small batch instead of everything. This concept is also called confidence intervals, margin of error bounds, or uncertainty ranges around an average, and engineers often search for it when they need to report measurement precision, establish acceptable tolerance bands, or understand how reliable their test results are.

Confidence limits for the mean
(
Snedecor and
Cochran, 1989
)
are an interval estimate for the
mean. Interval estimates are often desirable because the
estimate of the mean varies from sample to sample. Instead of
a single estimate for the mean, a confidence interval generates
a lower and upper limit for the mean. The interval
estimate gives an indication of how much uncertainty there is
in our estimate of the true mean. The narrower the interval,
the more precise is our estimate.
Confidence limits are expressed in terms of a confidence
coefficient. Although the choice of confidence coefficient
is somewhat arbitrary, in practice 90 %, 95 %, and 99 % intervals
are often used, with 95 % being the most commonly used.
As a technical note, a 95 % confidence interval does
not
mean that there is a 95 % probability that the interval
contains the true mean. The interval computed from a given
sample either contains the true mean or it does not. Instead,
the level of confidence is associated with the method of
calculating the interval. The confidence coefficient is
simply the proportion of samples of a given size that may
be expected to contain the true mean. That is, for a 95 %
confidence interval, if many samples are collected and the
confidence interval computed, in the long run about 95 % of these
intervals would contain the true mean.

title: Two-Samplet-Test for Equal Means
text:
The **two-sample t-test for equal means** (also called independent samples t-test) lets you determine whether two groups have genuinely different average values or if observed differences are just random variation—in this case, comparing whether U.S. and Japanese cars truly differ in miles per gallon. Someone looking for this concept might search for "comparing two groups," "testing if averages are different," "are these two sets of data really different," or "independent samples comparison."

The following two-sample
t

AUTO83B.DAT
data set. The data set
contains miles per gallon for U.S. cars (sample 1) and for
Japanese cars (sample 2); the summary statistics for each
sample are shown below.

SAMPLE 2:

STANDARD DEVIATION = 6.10771

for the two samples. We assume that the variances for the two
samples are equal.

title: One-Factor ANOVA
text:
One-way ANOVA (analysis of variance) tests whether different batches of gears have genuinely different diameters or if the differences are just random measurement noise—helping engineers figure out if a manufacturing problem exists across production runs. You might also search for this using terms like "comparing multiple groups," "batch-to-batch variation," "testing if groups are different," or "F-test for multiple samples."

A one-way analysis of variance was generated for the
GEAR.DAT
data set. The data set
contains 10 measurements of gear diameter for ten
different batches for a total of 100 measurements.
DEGREES OF SUM OF MEAN
SOURCE FREEDOM SQUARES SQUARE
F
STATISTIC
---------------- ---------- -------- -------- -----------
BATCH 9 0.000729 0.000081 2.2969
RESIDUAL 90 0.003174 0.000035
TOTAL (CORRECTED) 99 0.003903 0.000039
RESIDUAL STANDARD DEVIATION = 0.00594
BATCH N MEAN SD(MEAN)
---------------------------------
1 10 0.99800 0.00188
