title: Seasonal Subseries Plot
section: Process or Product Monitoring and Control
text:
A seasonal subseries plot is a time series visualization technique that groups data points by repeating seasonal periods (such as months or quarters) to reveal consistent patterns within each season while filtering out long-term trends—solving the practical problem of spotting whether the same time-of-year issues keep happening month after month or quarter after quarter. This method helps engineers and operators quickly diagnose recurring seasonal problems like "does January always run hot?" or "does Q4 always show higher variability?" Alternative names or search terms include seasonal pattern analysis, by-season comparison chart, intra-seasonal trend plot, or grouped seasonal visualization.

Seasonal subseries plots are formed by
Vertical axis:
Response variable
Horizontal axis:
Time ordered by season. For example, with monthly data,
all the January values are plotted (in chronological
order), then all the February values, and so on.
In addition, a reference line is drawn at the group means.
The user must specify the length of the seasonal pattern before
generating this plot. In most cases, the analyst will know this
from the context of the problem and data collection. Sometimes
the series will need to be detrended before generating the plot,
as was the case for the CO
2
data.

title: Quantitative Output and Interpretation
text:
This page covers **confidence intervals, hypothesis testing for normality (PPCC and Anderson-Darling tests), autocorrelation analysis, and drift detection** — practical tools that help engineers verify whether their measurement data is reliable, stable over time, and suitable for standard statistical methods before making decisions based on it. Someone might search for this using terms like "how to check if my data is good," "testing if measurements are random," "detecting trends in data," "data quality assessment," or "is my sample size enough."

It is sometimes useful and convenient to summarize the above results
in a report.
Analysis of 500 normal random numbers
1: Sample Size = 500
2: Location
Mean = -0.00294
Standard Deviation of Mean = 0.045663
95% Confidence Interval for Mean = (-0.09266,0.086779)
Drift with respect to location? = NO
3: Variation
Standard Deviation = 1.021042
95% Confidence Interval for SD = (0.961437,1.088585)
Drift with respect to variation?
(based on Bartletts test on quarters
of the data) = NO
4: Data are Normal?
(as tested by Normal PPCC) = YES
(as tested by Anderson-Darling) = NO
5: Randomness
Autocorrelation = 0.045059
Data are Random?
(as measured by autocorrelation) = YES
6: Statistical Control
(i.e., no drift in location or scale,
data are random, distribution is
fixed, here we are testing only for
fixed normal)
Data Set is in Statistical Control? = YES
7: Outliers?
(as determined by Grubbs' test) = NO

title: t Distribution
text:
The **t distribution** and its **probability density function (PDF)** provide a way to model and test measurements from small samples where you don't know the true variation in your population—solving the practical problem of making reliable predictions and checking whether results are real or just random noise when you have limited data. Engineers and analysts often search for this concept using simpler terms like "small sample statistics," "Student's t-test," "degrees of freedom," "confidence intervals for small samples," or "how to analyze data when sample size is low."

The formula for the
probability density
function
of the
t
distribution is
\( f(x) = \frac{(1 + \frac{x^2}{\nu})^{\frac{-(\nu + 1)} {2}}}
{B(0.5,0.5\nu)\sqrt{\nu}} \)
where
B
is the beta function and
ν
is a positive
integer shape parameter. The formula for the beta function is
\( B(\alpha,\beta) = \int_{0}^{1} {t^{\alpha-1}(1-t)^{\beta-1}dt} \)
In a testing context, the
t
distribution is treated as a
"standardized distribution" (i.e., no location or scale parameters).
However, in a distributional modeling context (as with other
probability distributions), the
t
distribution itself can be
transformed with a
location parameter
,
μ
, and a
scale parameter
,
σ
.
The following is the plot of the
t
probability density function
for 4 different values of the shape parameter.
These plots all have a similar shape. The difference is in the heaviness
of the tails. In fact, the
t
distribution with ν equal to 1 is
a
Cauchy
distribution. The
t
distribution approaches a
normal
distribution
as
ν
becomes large. The approximation is quite good for values
of
ν
> 30.

title: Sample sizes required
text:
This excerpt covers **sample size determination for proportion testing** (also called **hypothesis testing for binomial proportions** or **one-sample proportion test design**). It solves the practical problem of figuring out how many units a manager needs to inspect to reliably catch when their defect rate has gotten worse—answering "how big does my sample need to be to spot a real problem before it costs us money?" Alternative search terms include: detecting changes in defect rate, sample size for quality control, detecting shifts in percentage defective, binomial sample size calculation, or how many items to check to find a quality problem.

Suppose that a department manager needs to be able to detect any
change above 0.10 in the current proportion defective of his product
line, which is running at approximately 10 % defective. He is interested
in a one-sided test and does not want to stop the line except
when the process has clearly degraded and, therefore, he chooses a
significance level for the test of 5 %. Suppose, also, that he is
willing to take a risk of 10 % of failing to detect a change of this
magnitude. With these criteria:
\(z_{0.95} = 1.645 , \,\, z_{0.90} = 1.282\)
\(\delta = 0.10 \,\, (p_1 = 0.20)\)
\(p_0 = 0.10\)
and the minimum sample size for a
one-sided test
procedure
is
$$\begin{eqnarray}
N & \ge & \left( \frac{z_{1-\alpha} \, \sqrt{p_0 (1-p_0)} + z_{1-\beta} \, \sqrt{p_1 (1-p_1)}}{\delta} \, \right)^2 \\
& & \\
& = & \left( \frac{1.645 \, \sqrt{0.1 \times 0.9} + 1.282 \, \sqrt{0.2 \times 0.8}}{0.1} \, \right)^2 \\
& & \\
& \approx & 102 \, .
\end{eqnarray} $$
With the continuity correction, the minimum sample size becomes 112.

title: Uniform Distribution
text:
This page explains **method of moments estimators** and **maximum likelihood estimators** for a **uniform distribution**, which tells you how to calculate the lower and upper boundaries of a range when your data is evenly spread between two limits. An engineer might need this to determine the minimum and maximum operating specs of a component or process when measurements cluster uniformly within bounds. Search alternatives: "how to find min/max limits from data," "estimating uniform distribution parameters," "finding range boundaries from samples," or "calculating endpoints of uniform data."

The method of moments estimators for A and B are
\( \hat{A} = \bar{x} - \sqrt{3}s \)
\( \hat{B} = \bar{x} + \sqrt{3}s \)
The maximum likelihood estimators are usually given in terms
of the parameters
a
and
h
where
A
=
a
-
h
B
=
a
+
h
The maximum likelihood estimators for
a
and
h
are
\( \hat{a} = \mbox{midrange}(Y_{1}, Y_{2}, ... , Y_{n}) \)
\( \hat{h} = 0.5[\mbox{range}(Y_{1}, Y_{2}, ... , Y_{n})] \)
This gives the following maximum likelihood estimators for A and B
\( \hat{A} = \mbox{midrange}(Y_{1}, Y_{2}, ... , Y_{n}) -
0.5[\mbox{range}(Y_{1}, Y_{2}, ... , Y_{n})] = Y_{1} \)
\( \hat{B} = \mbox{midrange}(Y_{1}, Y_{2}, ... , Y_{n}) +
0.5[\mbox{range}(Y_{1}, Y_{2}, ... , Y_{n})] = Y_{n} \)

title: Weibull Distribution
text:
The **Weibull distribution** is a flexible probability model used to predict when equipment fails or how long products last under real conditions, accounting for how failure rates change over time. Engineers use this distribution to analyze reliability data, estimate maintenance schedules, and determine product warranties by fitting failure times to a curve with adjustable shape, scale, and starting parameters. Related search terms include: failure rate modeling, time-to-failure analysis, reliability analysis, lifetime prediction, wear-out analysis, and bathtub curve modeling.

The formula for the
probability density
function
of the general Weibull distribution is
\( f(x) = \frac{\gamma} {\alpha} (\frac{x-\mu}
{\alpha})^{(\gamma - 1)}\exp{(-((x-\mu)/\alpha)^{\gamma})}
\hspace{.3in} x \ge \mu; \gamma, \alpha > 0 \)
where
γ
is the
shape parameter
,
μ
is the
location parameter
and
α
is the
scale parameter
. The case
where
μ
= 0 and
α
= 1 is called the
standard
Weibull distribution
. The case where
μ
= 0 is called the
2-parameter Weibull distribution. The equation for the standard Weibull
distribution reduces to
\( f(x) = \gamma x^{(\gamma - 1)}\exp(-(x^{\gamma})) \hspace{.3in}
x \ge 0; \gamma > 0 \)
Since the general form of probability functions can be
expressed in terms of the standard
distribution
, all subsequent formulas in this section are
given for the standard form of the function.
The following is the plot of the Weibull probability density function.

title: Anderson-Darling and Shapiro-Wilk tests
text:
The **Shapiro-Wilk test** is a statistical method that checks whether your data follows a bell curve (normal distribution) by calculating a W statistic, helping engineers determine if their measurements or experimental results are normally distributed before applying standard analysis techniques. You might search for this using terms like "test if data is normally distributed," "normality test," "checking for bell curve fit," "validating Gaussian distribution," or "goodness-of-fit test for normal data."

The Shapiro-Wilk Test For Normality
The Shapiro-Wilk test, proposed in
1965
,
calculates a \(W\)
statistic that tests whether a random sample, \(x_1, \, x_2, \, \ldots, \, x_n\)
comes from (specifically) a normal
distribution . Small values of \(W\)
are evidence of departure from
normality and percentage points for the \(W\)
statistic, obtained via Monte Carlo simulations, were reproduced by
Pearson
and Hartley (1972, Table 16)
. This test has done very well in comparison
studies with other goodness of fit tests.
The \(W\)
statistic is calculated as follows:
$$ W = \frac{\left( \sum_{i=1}^n a_i x_{(i)} \right)^2}
{\sum_{i=1}^n (x_i - \bar{x})^2} \, , $$
where the \(x_{(i)}\)
are the ordered sample values (\(x_{(1)}\)
is the smallest) and the \(a_i\)
are constants generated from
the means, variances and covariances of the order statistics of a sample
of size \(n\)
from a normal distribution
(see
Pearson
and Hartley (1972, Table 15)
.
For more information about the Shapiro-Wilk test the reader is referred
to the original
Shapiro and Wilk (1965)
paper and the tables in
Pearson and Hartley (1972)
.

title: Bias and Accuracy
text:
This section covers **bias** and **measurement process characterization**, explaining how to detect when your instruments or standards are giving consistently wrong results by comparing them against known reference standards, control charts, and blind samples. If you're trying to figure out why your measurements are off or whether your equipment needs recalibration, these methods help you catch systematic errors before they ruin your work. You might also search for this using terms like "systematic error detection," "instrument calibration verification," "measurement validity," or "checking if my equipment is accurate."

Bias in a measurement process can be identified by:
Calibration
of standards
and/or instruments by a reference laboratory,
where a value is assigned to the client's standard
based on comparisons with the reference laboratory's
standards.
Check standards
, where violations
of the control limits on a
control chart for the check standard suggest that
re-calibration of standards or instruments is needed.
Measurement assurance programs, where artifacts from a
reference laboratory or other qualified agency are
sent to a client and measured in the client's environment
as a 'blind' sample.
Interlaboratory comparisons,
where reference standards or materials are circulated
among several laboratories.

title: Variability
text:
This page covers **standard deviation at three hierarchical levels** (Level-1, Level-2, and Level-3) to quantify how much your measurement results bounce around over different time scales—from one reading to the next, from day to day, and over months or years—so you can figure out whether variations in your measurements are just normal noise or signs of real problems. Search for this concept using terms like "measurement repeatability and reproducibility," "sources of variation in testing," "short-term vs. long-term measurement drift," "gauge R&R," or "measurement system stability over time."

We adopt precise definitions and provide data collection and analysis
techniques in the sections on
check standards
and
measurement control
for
estimating:
Level-1 standard deviation for short-term
variability
Level-2 standard deviation for day-to-day
variability
In the section on
gauge studies
,
the concept of variability is extended to include very long-term
measurement variability:
Level-1 standard deviation
for short-term variability
Level-2 standard deviation
for day-to-day variability
Level-3 standard deviation
for very long-term variability
We refer to the standard deviations associated with these three kinds
of uncertainty as "Level 1, 2, and 3 standard deviations", respectively.

title: Assumptions
text:
A **histogram** and **normal distribution** check validates that measurement data follows a single bell-shaped pattern, helping engineers catch when their measurement system is mixing data from different sources, has bad readings, or isn't working right. If your measurements show two bumps instead of one, have extreme outliers, or look flat, your system has a problem that needs fixing before you can trust your results. People also search for this using terms like "checking if data is normally distributed," "bell curve test," "spotting measurement system problems," or "verifying single population data."

An easy method for checking the assumption of a single
normal distribution is to construct a histogram of the check
standard data. The
histogram
should follow a
bell-shaped pattern with a single hump. Types of anomalies that indicate
a problem with the measurement system are:
a double hump indicating that errors are being drawn
from two or more distributions;
long tails indicating outliers in the process;
flat pattern or one with humps at either end
indicating that the measurement process in not in
control or not properly specified.

title: Data collection
text:
This guidance covers **check standard measurement, short-term standard deviation, and degrees of freedom** — practical tools for tracking how consistent your measuring instrument is over time by recording repeated measurements of a known reference standard. Instead of guessing whether your scale, gauge, or testing machine is drifting or becoming less reliable, you systematically capture data that shows you exactly when and how much your readings vary day-to-day. You might search for this as "instrument calibration verification," "measurement system repeatability," "process capability baseline," or "monitoring instrument drift over time."

The values for the check standard should be recorded along
with pertinent environmental readings and identifications for
all other significant factors. The best way to record this
information is in one file with one line or row (on a
spreadsheet) of information in fixed fields for each check
standard measurement. A list of typical entries follows.
Identification for check standard
Date
Identification for the measurement design (if applicable)
Identification for the instrument
Check standard value
Short-term standard deviation from
J
repetitions
Degrees of freedom
Operator identification
Environmental readings (if pertinent)

title: Analysis
text:
This section covers **short-term standard deviation** and **time-dependent error quantification** in measurement systems, which solves the practical problem of figuring out how much variation in your measurements comes from day-to-day drift versus the normal "noise" you get when you repeat the same measurement multiple times. Someone looking for this concept might search for "measurement repeatability," "within-day variation," "short-term precision," "detecting measurement drift over time," or "separating random error from systematic error in a calibration process."

An analysis of the check standard data is the basis for quantifying
random errors in the measurement process -- particularly
time-dependent errors.
Given that we have a database of check standard measurements as
described in
data collection
where
$$Y_{kj}(k=1, \,\ldots, \, K, \,\, j=1, \,\ldots, \, J)$$
represents the
j
th repetition on the
k
th day, the mean
for the
k
th day is
$$\overline{Y}_{k \, \small{\bullet}} = \frac{1}{J}\sum_{j=1}^{J} Y_{kj}$$
and the short-term (level-1) standard deviation with
v = J
- 1
degrees of freedom is
$${\large s}_k = \sqrt{\frac{1}{J-1} \sum_{j=1}^{J} ( Y_{kj} - \overline{Y}_{k \, \small{\bullet}} ) ^2} \,\,\,\, . $$

title: Shewhart control chart
text:
A **Shewhart control chart for individual observations** monitors one measurement at a time rather than groups, letting you track how your instrument's actual output drifts over days or weeks instead of just checking its repeatability in a single test session. This solves the problem of catching when a measurement device slowly goes out of calibration or when your process conditions change—problems that wouldn't show up if you only looked at short-term precision. You might also search for this as an "individuals chart," "X-chart," "monitoring individual measurements over time," or "long-term process variation tracking."

This procedure is an
individual observations
control chart. The
previously described control charts depended on
rational subsets
,
which use the standard deviations computed from the rational subsets to
calculate the control limits. For a measurement process, the subgroups
would consist of short-term repetitions which can characterize the
precision of the instrument but not the long-term variability of the
process. In measurement science, the interest is in assessing
individual measurements (or averages of short-term repetitions). Thus,
the standard deviation over time is the appropriate measure of
variability.

title: EWMA control chart
text:
The EWMA (Exponentially Weighted Moving Average) control chart is a statistical process control method that detects small shifts in a measurement process by computing weighted averages that give more importance to recent data points—solving the practical problem of catching gradual drift or creep in your process before it produces bad parts, which simpler control charts might miss. Alternative search terms include: exponential smoothing control chart, weighted moving average monitoring, trend detection in measurements, process drift detection, or continuous process monitoring with memory.

The implementation of the EWMA control chart is the same as for any
other type of control procedure. The procedure is built on the
assumption that the "good" historical data are representative of the
in-control process, with future data from the same process tested for
agreement with the historical data. To start the procedure, a target
(average) and process standard deviation are computed from historical
check standard data. Then the procedure enters the
monitoring stage
with the EWMA statistics
computed and tested against the control limits. The EWMA statistics
are weighted averages, and thus their standard deviations are smaller
than the standard deviations of the raw data and the corresponding
control limits are narrower than the control limits for the Shewhart
individual observations chart.

title: Data collection
text:
This section covers **control charting, measurement process characterization, and sources of variation** in metrology, which solves the practical problem of detecting when your measurement equipment or process has drifted out of acceptable performance so you catch bad results before they cause problems downstream. Alternative ways people search for this concept: "how to monitor if my measuring instrument is still accurate," "checking measurement consistency," "quality control for test equipment," "drift detection in calibration," and "validating measurement reliability over time."

A schedule should be set up for making measurements on the artifact
(check standard) chosen for control purposes. The measurements are
structured to sample all environmental conditions in the laboratory and
all other sources of influence on the measurement result, such as
operators and instruments.
For high-precision processes where the uncertainty of the result must
be guaranteed, a measurement on the check standard should be included
with every measurement sequence, if possible, and at least once a day.
For each occasion,
J
measurements are made on the check standard.
If there is no interest in controlling the short-term variability or
precision of the instrument, then one measurement is sufficient.
However, a dual purpose is served by making two or three measurements
that track both the bias and the short-term variability of the process
with the same database.

title: Monitoring bias and long-term variability
chapter:
re-analysis
of the kilogram check standard data
text:
Control charts (specifically Shewhart and EWMA charts) detect when a measurement process has drifted or shifted out of its normal operating range, helping engineers catch gradual changes or sudden jumps in their production or calibration standards that wouldn't be obvious from individual measurements alone. This concept is also called process monitoring, drift detection, shift detection, out-of-control detection, or tracking process changes over time.

The control chart should be viewed in its entirety on a regular basis]
to identify drift or shift in the process. In the Shewhart control
chart shown above, only a few points exceed the control limits.
The small, but significant, shift in the process that occurred after
1985 can only be identified by examining the plot of control
measurements over time. A
re-analysis
of the kilogram check standard data
shows that the control limits
for the Shewhart control chart should be updated based on the the data
after 1985. In the EWMA control chart, multiple violations of the
control limits occur after 1986. In the calibration environment, the
incidence of several violations should alert the control engineer that
a shift in the process has occurred, possibly because of damage or
change in the value of a reference standard, and the process requires
review.

title: Remedial actions
text:
This page covers **out-of-control signals** and **remedial actions** in statistical process control, teaching engineers how to figure out whether a measurement problem is a one-time fluke that should just be thrown out versus a real shift in the tool or environment that needs fixing. Someone looking for this content might search for "troubleshooting bad measurements," "why is my instrument drifting," "detecting measurement system problems," or "when to recalibrate equipment versus when to discard data."

There are many possible causes of out-of-control signals.
A. Causes that do
not
warrant corrective action for the
process (but which do require that the current measurement be
discarded) are:
Chance failure where the process is actually in-control
Glitch in setting up or operating the measurement process
Error in recording of data
B. Changes in bias can be due to:
Damage to artifacts
Degradation in artifacts (wear or build-up of dirt and mineral
deposits)
C. Changes in long-term variability can be due to:
Degradation in the instrumentation
Changes in environmental conditions
Effect of a new or inexperienced operator

title: Control chart for standard deviations
text:
This page explains how to set up an **F-distribution-based control limit for standard deviation monitoring**, which solves the practical problem of detecting when the variability or spread of your measurements has unexpectedly increased compared to your historical baseline data. Alternative ways someone might search for this: "control chart for variation," "monitoring process consistency," "detecting changes in measurement scatter," "standard deviation chart with F-test," or "comparing current variability to historical baseline."

The control limit is
$$UCL = {\large s}_1 \sqrt{F_{\alpha, \, J-1, \, K(J-1)}}$$
where the quantity under the radical is the upper
α
critical value from the
F
table
with degrees of
freedom (
J
- 1) and
K
(
J
- 1). The numerator degrees of
freedom,
v
1
= (
J
-1), are associated with the standard deviation computed
from the current measurements, and the denominator degrees of freedom,
v
2
=
K
(
J
- 1), correspond to the pooled standard deviation of the
historical data. The probability
α
is chosen to be small, say 0.05.
The justification for this control limit, as opposed to the more
conventional standard deviation control limit, is that we are
essentially performing the following hypothesis test:
$$H_0: \,\, \sigma_1 = \sigma_2$$
$$H_a: \,\, \sigma_1 < \sigma_2$$
where
σ
1
is the population value for the
s
1
defined above and
σ
2
is the value against which the standard deviation of the current value is being
tested.
Generally,
s
1
is based on sufficient
historical data that it is reasonable to make the assumption that
σ
1
is a "known" value.
The upper control limit above is then derived based on the standard
F
test for equal standard deviations. Justification and details of this
derivation are given in
Cameron and
Hailes (1974)
.

title: Data collection
text:
This section explains **repeatability standard deviation** and **within-occasion variation**, which tells you how much your measurements bounce around when you repeat the same test multiple times on the same day or setup—helping engineers figure out if their measurement system itself is consistent or if the variation comes from real differences in what they're testing. If you're looking for how to calculate measurement noise, short-term precision, or day-to-day consistency of a single gauge or instrument, this is where you'll find it.

The measurements are denoted
$$Y_{kj}(k=1, \,\ldots, \, K, \,\, j=1, \,\ldots, \, J)$$
where there are
J
measurements on each of
K
occasions.
The average for the
k
th occasion is:
$$\overline{Y}_{k \, \small{\bullet}} = \frac{1}{J}\sum_{j=1}^{J} Y_{kj}$$
The short-term (repeatability) standard deviation for the
k
th occasion is:
$${\large s}_k = \sqrt{\frac{1}{J-1} \sum_{j=1}^{J} ( Y_{kj} - \overline{Y}_{k \, \small{\bullet}} ) ^2} \,\,\,\, . $$
with (
J
- 1) degrees of freedom.

title: Monitoring short-term precision
text:
This section covers **F-test for variance comparison** and **control limits with variable sample sizes**, which solves the practical problem of checking whether a measuring instrument's precision has gotten worse when you take a different number of measurements than you used historically—so you don't have to redraw your control chart every time your sample size changes. Someone looking for this concept might search for "comparing measurement variability with different sample sizes," "adjusting control limits for new sample size," or "testing if instrument precision stayed the same."

There is no requirement that future standard deviations be based on
J
, the number of measurements in the historical database.
However, a change in the number of measurements leads to a change in the
test for control, and it may not be convenient to draw a control chart
where the control limits are changing with each new measurement sequence.
For a new standard deviation based on
J'
measurements, the
precision of the instrument is in control if
$${\large s}_{new} < {\large s}_1 \sqrt{F_{\alpha, \, J'-1, \, K(J-1)}}$$
Notice that the numerator degrees of freedom,
v
1
=
J'
- 1, changes
but the denominator degrees of freedom,
v
2
=
K
(
J
- 1), remains
the same.

title: Example of EWMA control chart for mass calibrations
text:
EWMA (Exponentially Weighted Moving Average) control charts use weighted historical data to detect when a measurement process drifts out of specification, making small shifts visible earlier than raw data alone would reveal them. Engineers use this technique to catch gradual problems in calibration or production before they cause bad parts, by smoothing out random noise while staying sensitive to real trends. You might also search for this concept as "exponential smoothing for process control," "weighted moving average monitoring," or "drift detection in measurement systems."

The target (average) and process standard deviation are computed from
the check standard data taken prior to 1985. The computation of the
EWMA statistic begins with the data taken at the start of 1985. In the
control chart below, the control data after 1985 are shown in green, and
the EWMA statistics are shown as black dots superimposed on the raw
data. The control limits are calculated according to the equation above
where the process standard deviation,
s
= 0.03065 mg and
k
= 3. The EWMA statistics, and not the raw data, are of interest
in looking for out-of-control signals. Because the EWMA statistic
is a weighted average, it has a smaller standard deviation than a single
control measurement, and, therefore, the EWMA control limits are
narrower than the limits for a Shewhart control chart.
The EWMA control chart for mass calibrations can be
generated using both
Dataplot code
and
R code
. The reader can download
the
data as a text file
.

title: Reference base
text:
A **reference base** (also called a **measurement standard** or **calibration reference**) solves the practical problem of ensuring that measurements made in different labs or at different times can be reliably compared—if your pressure gauge reads the same as mine, it's because we both calibrated against the same official standard maintained by NIST or similar organizations. This concept is essential for manufacturing, quality control, and any field where you need confidence that your instruments are accurate and traceable to internationally recognized benchmarks. You might search for this under terms like "measurement traceability," "calibration standards," "reference standards," "standardized units," or "measurement uncertainty and validation."

The base units of measurement in the Le Systeme International
d'Unites (SI) are (
Taylor
):
kilogram - mass
meter - length
second - time
ampere - electric current
kelvin - thermodynamic temperature
mole - amount of substance
candela - luminous intensity
These units are maintained by the Bureau International des Poids
et Mesures in Paris. Local reference bases for these units and SI
derived units such as:
pascal - pressure
newton - force
hertz - frequency
ohm - resistance
degrees Celsius - Celsius temperature, etc.
are maintained by national and regional standards laboratories.

title: Reference standards
text:
This section covers **reference standards** and **traceability** — the practical approach to ensuring that your measurements can be reliably compared to a known baseline so you know your instruments and processes are actually measuring what you think they're measuring. Engineers looking for this concept might search for "calibration standards," "measurement traceability," "standard artifacts," or "how to verify instrument accuracy."

A reference standard for a unit of measurement is an artifact that
embodies the quantity of interest in a way that ties its value to the
reference base.
At the highest level, a primary reference standard is assigned a value
by direct comparison with the reference base. Mass is the only unit of
measurement that is defined by an artifact. The kilogram is defined as
the mass of a platinum-iridium kilogram that is maintained by the
Bureau International des Poids et Mesures in Sevres, France.
Primary reference standards for other units come from realizations of
the units embodied in artifact standards. For example, the reference
base for length is the meter which is defined as the length of the path
by light in vacuum during a time interval of 1/299,792,458 of a second.

title: Solutions to calibration designs
text:
This section covers **standard deviation estimation, calibration design factors, and measurement uncertainty propagation** for test items across multiple measurement days using check standards as reference points. It solves the practical problem of figuring out how much variation to expect in your measurements when you're calibrating instruments or standards over time, by breaking down the different sources of error (day-to-day changes, instrument drift, and repeatability) and combining them using design-specific multipliers. Someone looking for this might search for "how to calculate measurement uncertainty from repeated calibrations," "combining repeatability and check standard variation," "calibration design factors," or "accounting for day-to-day variation in measurement systems."

The steps in computing the standard deviation for a test item are:
Compute the
repeatability standard
deviation
from the design or historical data.
Compute the
standard deviation of
the check standard
from historical data.
Locate the factors, \( K_1 \)
and
\( K_2 \)
for the
check standard
; for the 1,1,1 design the factors
are 0.8165 and 1.4142, respectively, where the check standard
entries are last in the tables.
Apply the unifying equation to the check standard to estimate
the standard deviation for days. Notice that the standard
deviation of the check standard is the same as the
level-2 standard deviation
,
\( {\large s}_2 \),
that is
referred to on some pages. The equation
for the between-days standard deviation from the unifying
equation is
$$ {\large s}_{days} = \frac{1}{K_2} \sqrt{{\large s}_2^2 - K_1^2 {\large s}_1^2} $$
Thus, for the example above
$$ {\large s}_{days} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \sqrt{{\large s}_2^2 - \frac{2}{3} {\large s}_1^2} $$
This is the number that is entered into the
NIST mass calibration
software
as the between-time standard deviation. If you are
using this software, this is the only computation that you need
to make because the standard deviations for the test items are
computed automatically by the software.
If the computation under the radical sign gives a negative
number, set \( {\large s}_{days} =0 \).
(This is possible and
indicates that there is no contribution to uncertainty from
day-to-day effects.)
For completeness, the computations of the
standard deviations for the test item and for the sum of the
test and the check standard using the appropriate factors are
shown below.
$$ {\large s}_{test} = \sqrt{\frac{2}{3} {\large s}_1^2 + 2 {\large s}_{days}^2}
= \sqrt{\frac{2}{3} {\large s}_1^2 + \frac{5}{2} \left( {\large s}_2^2 - \frac{2}{3} {\large s}_1^2 \right) }
= {\large s}_2 $$
$$ {\large s}_{test+chk} = \sqrt{2 {\large s}_1^2 + 6 {\large s}_{days}^2}
= \sqrt{2 {\large s}_1^2 + \frac{13}{2} \left( {\large s}_2^2 - \frac{2}{3} {\large s}_1^2 \right)}
= \sqrt{3} \, {\large s}_2 $$

title: Uncertainties of calibrated values
chapter:
classifying and
combining components of uncertainty
text:
This section covers **Type A and Type B uncertainty evaluations** for combining random errors and systematic uncertainties in calibration processes. A practical engineer needs this when they've calibrated a measurement instrument and must figure out the total uncertainty in the final readings—accounting for both repeated measurement scatter and one-time sources of error like the reference standard's own uncertainty. People typically search for this using phrases like "combining calibration uncertainties," "uncertainty budget for measurements," "reference standard error propagation," or "how to calculate total measurement uncertainty from a calibration."

This section discusses the calculation of uncertainties of calibrated
values from calibration designs. The discussion follows the guidelines
in the section on
classifying and
combining components of uncertainty
. Two types of evaluations are
covered.
type A evaluations
of
time-dependent sources of random error
type B evaluations
of other
sources of error
The latter includes, but is not limited to, uncertainties from
sources that are not replicated in the calibration design such as
uncertainties of values
assigned to reference standards.

title: Mass weights
text:
This page explains **comparative weighing design** and **constraint-based calibration**, which solves the practical problem of assigning accurate mass values to multiple test weights when you only have a couple of reference standards to work from—by strategically comparing weights against each other and using known reference values as anchors. Someone looking for this concept might search for "weight calibration method," "balance design for mass standards," "how to calibrate multiple weights with limited references," or "constraint-based mass comparison."

The calibrations start with a comparison of the one kilogram test
weight with the reference kilograms (see the graphic above). The
1,1,1,1 design
requires two kilogram
reference standards with known values, \( R_1^* \) and \( R_2^* \).
The fourth kilogram in this design is actually a summation of the
500, 300, 200 g weights which becomes the restraint in the next series.
The restraint for the first series is the known average mass of the
reference kilograms,
$$ R^* = \frac{R_1^* + R_2^*}{2} \, .$$
The design assigns values to all weights including the individual
reference standards. For this design, the check standard is not an
artifact standard but is defined as the difference
between the values assigned to the reference kilograms by the design;
namely,
$$ C = \left( \widehat{R_1^*} - \widehat{R_2^*} \right) \, . $$

title: Drift-elimination designs for gauge blocks
chapter:
Doiron design for 3 gauge blocks - 6
      measurements
text:
This page covers **drift-elimination design** and **measurement standards**, which solve the practical problem of detecting and removing errors that slowly change over time during gauge block calibration, ensuring that reference measurements stay accurate and consistent. Someone looking for this concept might search for "how to account for instrument drift in measurements," "removing systematic errors from calibration," "drift correction in precision measurement," or "gauge block comparison methods that eliminate drift."

At the National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST), the
first two blocks in the design are NIST masters which are designated
R
1
and
R
2
, respectively. The
R
1
block is a steel
block, and the
R
2
block is a chrome-carbide block. If the test
blocks are steel, the reference is
R
1
; if the test blocks are
chrome-carbide, the reference is
R
2
. The check standard is
always the difference between
R
1
and
R
2
as estimated from
the design and is independent of
R
1
and
R
2
. The designs
are listed in this section of the catalog as:
Doiron design for 3 gauge blocks - 6
measurements
Doiron design for 3 gauge blocks - 9
measurements
Doiron design for 4 gauge blocks - 8
measurements
Doiron design for 4 gauge blocks - 12
measurements
Doiron design for 5 gauge blocks - 10
measurements
Doiron design for 6 gauge blocks - 12
measurements
Doiron design for 7 gauge blocks - 14
measurements
Doiron design for 8 gauge blocks - 16
measurements
Doiron design for 9 gauge blocks - 18
measurements
Doiron design for 10 gauge blocks - 20
measurements
Doiron design for 11 gauge blocks - 22
measurements

title: Designs for electrical quantities
text:
This page covers **balanced experimental designs** and **constraint-based estimation** for comparing standard cells, solving the practical problem of fairly measuring how standard reference cells differ from each other without letting one cell's value bias the whole comparison. Engineers looking for this concept might search for "symmetric measurement designs," "how to compare reference standards fairly," "removing bias from cell comparisons," or "designs that don't favor one measurement over another."

Left-right balanced
designs
for comparing standard cells among themselves where the
restraint is over all reference cells are listed below. These designs
are
not
appropriate for assigning values to test cells.
Estimates for individual standard cells and the bias term,
P
,
are shown under the heading, 'SOLUTION MATRIX'. These designs also
have the advantage of requiring a change of connections to only one
cell at a time.
Design for 3 standard cells
Design for 4 standard cells
Design for 5 standard cells
Design for 6 standard cells

title: Designs for angle blocks
text:
This NIST handbook page covers **calibration design solutions and repeatability standard deviation estimation** for angle block measurement systems, which solves the practical problem of figuring out how to arrange reference standards, check standards, and test blocks in a measurement sequence so you can reliably estimate the true angles and understand how much variation your measurement process introduces. Engineers looking for this guidance might search for terms like "angle gauge calibration procedure," "measurement uncertainty from repeated tests," "how to set up a calibration experiment for precision instruments," or "standard deviation factors for measurement repeatability."

Solutions to the angle block designs are shown on the following pages.
The solution matrix and factors for the repeatability standard deviation
are to be interpreted as explained in
solutions to
calibration designs
. As an example, the solution for the
design for
n
=4 angle blocks
is as follows:
The solution for the reference standard is shown under the first column
of the solution matrix; for the check standard under the second
column; for the first test block under the third column; and for the
second test block under the fourth column. Notice that the estimate for
the reference block is guaranteed to be
R*
, regardless of the
measurement results, because of the restraint that
is imposed on the design. Specifically,
$$ \widehat{Reference} = 0 + R^* $$
$$ \widehat{Check} = \frac{1}{24} \left( \begin{array}{l}
\,\,\,\,\, 2.272 Y_{11} + 9.352 Y_{12} + 2.272 Y_{13} \\
-5.052 Y_{21} + 7.324 Y_{22} - 1.221 Y_{23}\\
-1.221 Y_{31} + 7.324 Y_{32} - 5.052 Y_{33}
\end{array}
\right) + R^* $$
$$ \widehat{Test}_1 = \frac{1}{24} \left( \begin{array}{l}
-5.052 Y_{11} + 7.324 Y_{12} - 1.221 Y_{13} \\
-1.221 Y_{21} + 7.324 Y_{22} - 5.052 Y_{23}\\
\,\,\,\,\, 2.272 Y_{31} + 9.352 Y_{32} + 2.272 Y_{33}
\end{array}
\right) + R^* $$
$$ \widehat{Test}_2 = \frac{1}{24} \left( \begin{array}{l}
-1.221 Y_{11} + 7.324 Y_{12} - 5.052 Y_{13} \\
\,\,\,\,\, 2.272 Y_{21} + 9.352 Y_{22} + 2.272 Y_{23}\\
-5.052 Y_{31} + 7.324 Y_{32} - 1.221 Y_{33}
\end{array}
\right) + R^* $$
Solutions are correct only for the restraint as shown.

title: Thermometers in a bath
text:
This procedure uses **repeated measurements** and **bracketing design** (or sandwich design) to correct for **systematic drift** — a practical solution for removing the gradual temperature creep that occurs during a long calibration run so that readings aren't skewed by the bath slowly warming up over time. Engineers might search for this under terms like "accounting for equipment drift," "temperature compensation during testing," "time-ordered measurement correction," or "calibration with baseline checks."

Calibration of liquid in glass thermometers is usually carried out in a
controlled bath where the temperature in the bath is increased steadily
over time to calibrate the thermometers over their entire range. One
way of accounting for the temperature drift is to measure the
temperature of the bath with a standard resistance thermometer at the
beginning, middle and end of each run of
K
test thermometers.
The test thermometers themselves are measured twice during the run in
the following time sequence:
$$ R_1, \, T_1, \, T_2, \, \ldots, \, T_K, \, R_2, \, T_K', \, \ldots, \, T_2', \, T_1', \, R_3 $$
where \( R_1, \, R_2, \, R_3 \)
represent the measurements on the standard resistance thermometer and
\( T_1, \, T_2, \, \ldots, \, T_K \)
and
\( T_1', \, T_2', \, \ldots, \, T_K' \)
represent the pair of measurements on the
K
test thermometers.

title: Control of precision
text:
This section covers **control charting for measurement system precision** and **pooled standard deviation estimation**, which solves the practical problem of detecting when a measuring instrument's repeatability has gotten worse so you can catch drift or degradation before it ruins your parts. Engineers looking for this concept might also search for "measurement equipment monitoring," "gage control limits," "instrument drift detection," or "short-term variation tracking."

A modified control chart procedure is used for controlling instrument
precision. The procedure is designed to be implemented in real time
after a baseline and control limit for the instrument of interest have
been established from the database of short-term standard deviations.
A separate control chart is required for each instrument -- except
where instruments are of the same type with the same basic precision,
in which case they can be treated as one.
The baseline is the process standard deviation that is pooled from
\( k = 1, \, \ldots, \, K \)
individual repeatability standard deviations,
\( {\large s}_k \),
in the database, each having
\( \nu_k \)
degrees of freedom. The pooled repeatability standard deviation is
$$ {\large s}_1 = \sqrt{ \frac{1}{\nu} \sum_{k=1}^K \nu_k \, {\large s}_k^2 } $$
with degrees of freedom
$$ \nu = \sum_{k=1}^K \nu_k \,\, .$$

title: Control of bias and long-term variability
text:
Control charts, specifically Shewhart control charts with symmetric upper and lower control limits, are used to detect when a measurement instrument is drifting out of its expected range or producing biased results over time. This helps engineers catch when their measuring equipment needs recalibration before bad parts slip through or test results become unreliable. You might also search for this using terms like "process monitoring," "drift detection," "baseline tracking," "out-of-control detection," or "real-time quality checks."

A control chart procedure is used for controlling bias and long-term
variability. The procedure is designed to be implemented in real time
after a baseline and control limits for the check standard of interest
have been established from the database of check standard values. A
separate control chart is required for each check standard. The control
procedure outlined here is based on a Shewhart control chart with upper
and lower control limits that are symmetric about the average. The
EWMA control procedure
that is sensitive to
small changes in the process is discussed on another page.

title: Models for instrument calibration
text:
Non-linear calibration models (including exponential and rational functions) help engineers fit instrument response curves when the relationship between input and output doesn't follow a straight line, making it possible to create accurate measurement standards for complex instruments. Someone searching for this concept might look for terms like "curved calibration curves," "fitting bent responses," "non-straight-line instrument fitting," or "handling curved sensor behavior."

Instruments whose responses are not linear in the coefficients can
sometimes be described by non-linear models. In some cases, there are
theoretical foundations for the models; in other cases, the models are
developed by trial and error. Two classes of non-linear functions that
have been shown to have practical value as calibration functions are:
Exponential
Rational
Non-linear models are an important class of calibration models, but
they have several significant limitations.
The model itself may be difficult to ascertain and verify.
There can be severe computational difficulties in estimating
the coefficients.
Correction for bias cannot be applied algebraically and can only
be approximated by interpolation.
Uncertainty analysis is very difficult.

title: Data collection
text:
The NIST handbook describes **calibration curve design** and **optimal spacing of reference standards**, which solves the practical problem of figuring out how many measurement points you need and where to place them along your instrument's range to get an accurate relationship between what you're measuring and what the instrument reads. Engineers looking for guidance on this topic might search for "how many calibration points do I need," "reference standard spacing," "calibration sample selection," or "experimental design for instrument calibration."

A minimum of
five
reference standards is required for a
linear calibration curve
, and
ten
reference standards should be adequate for more complicated
calibration models.
The optimal strategy in selecting the reference standards is to space
the reference standards at points corresponding to equal increments on
the
y
-axis, covering the range of the instrument. Frequently,
this strategy is not realistic because the person producing the
reference materials is often not the same as the person who is creating
the calibration curve. Spacing the reference standards at equal
intervals on the
x
-axis is a good alternative.

title: What can go wrong with the calibration procedure
text:
This section addresses **operator bias** and **inter-operator variability** in measurement systems, which helps engineers detect when different people using the same instrument get systematically different results that aren't just normal measurement noise. The practical problem it solves is figuring out whether your measurement differences between operators are just random error you have to live with, or whether someone needs retraining because they're consistently reading the instrument wrong. You might search for this concept using terms like "operator effect," "personnel variation," "measurement consistency between operators," "operator-induced bias," or "who-did-the-measurement bias."

It is possible for different operators to produce measurements with
biases that differ in sign and magnitude. This is not usually a
problem for automated instrumentation, but for instruments that depend
on line of sight, results may differ significantly by operator. To
diagnose this problem, measurements by different operators on the same
artifacts are plotted and compared. Small differences among operators
can be accepted as part of the imprecision of the measurement process,
but large systematic differences among operators require resolution.
Possible solutions are to retrain the operators or maintain separate
calibration curves by operator.

title: Data analysis and model validation
text:
This page demonstrates **quadratic regression modeling** and **residual analysis** for calibrating measurement instruments by fitting a curved equation (y = a + bx + cx²) to load cell data, which allows engineers to accurately predict sensor output across a range of weights and understand whether the simple linear relationship works or if a curve provides better predictions. Someone looking for this concept might search for "calibration curve fitting," "sensor accuracy validation," "nonlinear instrument calibration," "polynomial regression for measurement systems," or "how to check if my sensor reading formula is correct."

Load cell measurements
are
modeled as a quadratic function of known loads as shown below.
There are three repetitions at each load level for a total of 33
measurements.
Parameter estimates for model y = a + b*x + c*x*x + e:
Parameter Estimate Std. Error t-value Pr(>|t|)
a -1.840e-05 2.451e-05 -0.751 0.459
b 1.001e-01 4.839e-06 20687.891 <2e-16
c 7.032e-06 2.014e-07 34.922 <2e-16
Residual standard error = 3.764e-05 (30 degrees of freedom)
Multiple R-squared = 1
Adjusted R-squared = 1
Analysis of variance table:
Source of Degrees of Sum of Mean
Variation Freedom Squares Square F-Ratio Pr(>F)
Model 2 12.695 6.3475 4.48e+09 <2.2e-16
Residual 30 4.2504e-08 1.4170e-09
(Lack of fit) 8 4.7700e-09 5.9625e-10 0.3477 0.9368
(Pure error) 22 3.7733e-08 1.7151e-09
Total 32 12.695
The analyses shown above can be reproduced using
Dataplot code
and
R code
. The reader can download the
data as a text file
.
Note: Dataplot reports a probability associated with the F-ratio
(for example, 6.334 % for the lack-of-fit test), where a probability greater
than 95 % indicates an F-ratio that is
significant at the 5 % level. R reports a
p
-value that
corresponds to the probability greater than the F-ratio, so a value less
than 0.05 would indicate significance at the 5 % level. Other software
may report in other ways; therefore, it is necessary to check the
interpretation for each package.
The F-ratio is used to test the goodness of the
fit to the data
The F-ratio provides information on the model as a good descriptor
of the data. The F-ratio is compared with a critical value from the
F-table
. An F-ratio smaller
than the critical value indicates that all significant structure has been
captured by the model.
F-ratio < 1 always indicates a good fit
For the load cell analysis, a
plot of the
data
suggests a linear fit. However, the linear fit gives a very
large F-ratio. For the quadratic fit, the F-ratio is 0.3477 with
v
1
= 8 and
v
2
= 22 degrees of freedom. The critical value
of F(0.05, 8, 20) = 2.45 indicates that the quadratic function is sufficient
for describing the data. A fact to keep in mind is that an F-ratio < 1
does not need to be checked against a critical value; it always
indicates a good fit to the data.
The t-values are used to test the significance of individual
coefficients
The
t
-values can be compared with critical values
from a
t
-table
.
However, for a test at the 5 % significance level, a
t
-value < 2
is a good indicator of non-significance. The
t
-value for the
intercept term,
a
, is < 2 indicating that
the intercept term is not significantly different from zero. The
t
-values for the linear and quadratic terms are significant
indicating that these coefficients are needed in the model. If the
intercept is dropped from the model, the analysis is repeated to obtain
new estimates for the coefficients,
b
and
c
.
Residual standard deviation
The residual standard deviation estimates the standard deviation of
a single measurement with the load cell.
Further considerations and tests of assumptions
The residuals (differences between the measurements and their fitted
values) from the fit should also be examined for
outliers
and
structure
that might
invalidate the calibration curve. They are also a good indicator of
whether
basic assumptions
of
normality and equal precision for all measurements are valid.
If the initial model proves inappropriate for the data,
a
strategy for
improving the model
is followed.

title: Calibration of future measurements
text:
This page covers **hypothesis testing of regression parameters** (specifically testing intercept and slope coefficients against null values) to determine whether a measurement instrument needs calibration correction before use. In practical terms, it solves the problem of deciding whether your measurement device is already giving you accurate readings or whether you need to apply a mathematical correction formula based on reference standards. Someone looking for this concept might search for: "when to calibrate equipment," "checking if calibration is needed," "regression coefficient significance testing," "intercept and slope hypothesis tests," or "determining measurement system bias and linearity."

Before correcting for the calibration line by the equation above, the
intercept and slope should be tested for \( a=0 \), and \(b=1\).
If both
$$ \left|\frac{\hat{a}}{s_a}\right| < t_{1-\alpha/2, \nu} \,\,\, \mbox{ and } \,\,\,
\left|\frac{\hat{b}-1}{s_b}\right| < t_{1-\alpha/2, \nu} $$
there is no need for calibration. If, on the other hand only the test
for \( a=0 \)
fails,
the error is constant; if only the test for \( b=1 \)
fails
, the errors are related to the size of the reference standards.

title: Uncertainties of calibrated values
text:
This section covers **Type A uncertainties** and **propagation of error** for calibration curves, which solve the practical problem of figuring out how much you can trust the values you get when you use a calibration curve to measure something—basically, how to calculate the margin of error in your final measurement results. Someone looking for this information might search for "calibration uncertainty," "error in calibrated measurements," "how accurate is my calibration curve," "uncertainty from calibration," or "confidence in measurement values from a curve."

General procedures for computing an uncertainty based on ISO
principles of uncertainty analysis are given in the
chapter on modeling.
Type A uncertainties
for
calibrated values from calibration curves can be derived from
check standard values
propagation of error
An example of
type A uncertainties of calibrated
values from a linear calibration curve
are analyzed from
measurements on linewidth check standards.
Comparison of the uncertainties from check
standards and propagation of error
for the linewidth calibration
data are also illustrated.
An example of the derivation of propagation of error
type A uncertainties for calibrated values
from a quadratic calibration curve
for loadcells is discussed on
the next page.

title: Uncertainty for quadratic calibration using propagation of error
text:
This page explains **propagation of error** (also called **error propagation** or **uncertainty propagation**) for a **quadratic calibration model**, which solves the practical problem of figuring out how measurement errors from your instrument reading, your fitted curve coefficients, and your calibration data all add up to create total uncertainty in your final result. Someone looking for this might search for "how to calculate uncertainty in calibration," "combining errors from curve fit," "total error budget for quadratic fit," or "uncertainty analysis for polynomial calibration."

The variance of \(X'\)
is defined from
propagation of error
as follows:
$$ u^{2} = \left( \frac{\partial{X'}}{\partial{Y'}}\right) ^{2} (s_{Y'})^{2} +
\left( \frac{\partial{X'}}{\partial{\hat{a}}}\right) ^{2} (s_{\hat{a}})^{2} +
\left( \frac{\partial{X'}}{\partial{\hat{b}}}\right) ^{2} (s_{\hat{b}})^{2} +
\left( \frac{\partial{X'}}{\partial{\hat{c}}}\right )^{2} (s_{\hat{c}})^{2} $$
The values of the coefficients and their respective standard deviations
from the quadratic fit to the calibration curve are substituted in the
equation. The standard deviation of the measurement, \(Y\),
may not be the same as the standard deviation from the fit to the calibration
data if the measurements to be corrected are taken with a different
system; here we assume that the instrument to be calibrated has a
standard deviation that is essentially the same as the instrument used
for collecting the calibration data and the residual standard
deviation from the quadratic fit is the appropriate estimate.
a = -0.1850148e-04
sa = 0.2081e-04
b = 0.100102
sb = 0.3681e-05
c = 0.7030346e-05
sc = 0.1460e-06
sy = 0.0000340856

title: Uncertainty for linear calibration using check standards
text:
This page explains **inverse regression** (also called **reverse calibration** or **calibration uncertainty estimation**) — a method for checking whether your measurement equipment is still accurately calibrated by comparing predictions from a calibration curve back to the actual standard values you measured. If you need to verify that a calibrated instrument hasn't drifted or changed accuracy over time, or you want to quantify how much measurement error comes from the calibration process itself, you might search for terms like "check standard validation," "calibration verification," "measurement system drift detection," or "uncertainty from calibration curve."

For the linewidth data, the regression equation from the calibration
experiment is
$$ Y = a + bX + \epsilon $$
and the estimated regression coefficients are the following.
$$ \hat{a} = 0.2357 $$
$$ \hat{b} = 0.9870 $$
Next, we calculate the difference between the "predicted"
\(X\)
from the regression fit and the observed \(X\).
$$ W_i = \frac{(Y_i - \hat{a})}{\hat{b}} - X_i $$
Finally, we find the calibration standard deviation by
calculating the standard deviation of the computed differences.
$$ S = \sqrt{\frac{\sum \left( W_i - \overline{W} \right)^2}{n-1}} $$
The calibration standard deviation for the linewidth data is
0.119
μ
m.
The calculations in this section can be completed using
Dataplot code
and
R code
. The reader can download the
data as a text file
.

title: Comparison of check standard analysis and propagation of error
text:
This page covers **propagation of error** (also called **error propagation** or **uncertainty propagation**) and **partial derivatives** applied to a linear calibration equation, showing how measurement uncertainties from a test reading (Y'), calibration intercept (â), and calibration slope (b̂) combine to determine the total uncertainty in a final calibrated result (X'). It solves the practical problem of figuring out how accurate your final measurement will be when you're using a calibration curve and each ingredient of that curve has its own measurement error. Engineers and technicians searching for this might look for "how to calculate total measurement uncertainty," "combining errors from calibration," "uncertainty budget for calibrated instruments," or "error stackup in measurement systems."

The propagation of error is performed for the equation
$$ X' = \frac{Y'- \hat{a}}{\hat{b}} $$
so that the squared uncertainty of a calibrated value, \(X'\),
is
$$ \hspace{-.25in}u^2 =
\left[ \frac{\partial{X'}}{\partial{Y'}} \right]^2 s_{Y'}^2 \, + \,
\left[ \frac{\partial{X'}}{\partial{\hat{a}}} \right]^2 s_{\hat{a}}^2 \, + \,
\left[ \frac{\partial{X'}}{\partial{\hat{b}}} \right]^2 s_{\hat{b}}^2 \, + \,
2 \left[ \frac{\partial{X'}}{\partial{\hat{a}}} \right]
\left[ \frac{\partial{X'}}{\partial{\hat{b}}} \right] s_{\hat{a}\hat{b}} $$
where
$$ \frac{\partial{X'}}{\partial{Y'}} = \frac{1}{\hat{b}} $$
$$ \frac{\partial{X'}}{\partial{\hat{a}}} = \frac{-1}{\hat{b}} $$
$$ \frac{\partial{X'}}{\partial{\hat{b}}} = \frac{-(Y'-\hat{a})}{\hat{b}^{2}} $$
The uncertainty of the calibrated value, \(X'\),
$$ \hspace{-.25in}u^2 =
\left( \frac{1}{\hat{b}} \right)^2 s_{Y'}^2 \, + \,
\left( \frac{-1}{\hat{b}} \right)^2 s_{\hat{a}}^2 \, + \,
\left( \frac{-(Y'-\hat{a})}{\hat{b}^{2}} \right)^2 s_{\hat{b}}^2 \, + \,
2 \left( \frac{-1}{\hat{b}} \right)
\left( \frac{-(Y'-\hat{a})}{\hat{b}^{2}} \right) s_{\hat{a}\hat{b}} $$
is dependent on the value of the instrument reponse \(Y'\).

title: Control chart for a linear calibration line
text:
This page covers **control charts and linear regression calibration**, which helps engineers track whether their measurement instruments are staying accurate and consistent over time by plotting repeated test measurements against known standard values. If you're looking for ways to monitor if your gauges or sensors are drifting out of spec, or need to verify that your measurement system is still reliable day-to-day, you might search for terms like "calibration verification," "measurement system monitoring," "repeatability checks," or "instrument drift detection."

The Information Technology Laboratory (ITL) is one of NIST’s six research laboratories. ITL focuses on IT measurements, testing, and standards, and is a globally recognized and trusted source of high-quality, independent, and unbiased research and data. ITL’s mission, to cultivate trust in information technology (IT) and metrology, is accomplished using its world-class measurement and testing facilities and encompassing a wide range of areas of computer science, mathematics, statistics, and systems engineering.
This non-regulatory role, along with ITL’s deep technical expertise in all fields of information technology, helps increase trust in IT worldwide.

title: General matrix solutions to calibration designs

This page covers **design matrices, least squares estimation, and the general linear model** for solving calibration problems where you need to figure out unknown measurement values from a set of comparison tests. It shows how to set up your measurement experiments and calculations so you can accurately determine what an unknown weight is worth by comparing it against known reference standards. Someone looking for this might search for "how to calibrate unknown measurements using reference standards," "comparing test samples to known standards," or "extracting unknown values from comparison measurements."

title: Example of Shewhart control chart for mass calibrations
text:
Shewhart control charts monitor measurement process stability by tracking whether calibration results stay within calculated control limits and checking if data points distribute evenly around the center line. This helps engineers catch when their measurement equipment or standards have drifted out of spec before bad data ruins their work—similar to checking that a scale keeps giving consistent readings over time. You might also search for "process control limits," "statistical process control for calibration," "detecting measurement drift," or "monitoring instrument variability."

The control chart shows only two violations of the control limits. For
those occasions, the calibrations were discarded and repeated. The
configuration of points is unacceptable if many points are close to a
control limit and there is an unequal distribution of data points on
the two sides of the control chart -- indicating a change in either:
process average which may be related to a change in the
reference standards
or
variability which may be caused by a change in the instrument
precision or may be the result of other factors on the
measurement process.

You might also search for this using terms like: hypothesis testing threshold values, calibration verification limits, statistical significance cutoff for linear regression, or t-test decision boundaries.

title: Analysis of repeatability
text:
This page covers **repeatability analysis** and **nested experimental design** to separate measurement variation into its sources—specifically, it shows how to quantify how much of your measurement scatter comes from taking multiple readings on the same day versus differences between days or operators, so you know whether your measurement system is consistent or if something like temperature or operator technique is throwing off your results. Someone looking for this concept might search for "measurement system variation," "how much does my instrument drift," "breaking down sources of error," or "short-term versus long-term measurement variation."

To illustrate the calculations, a
subset of data
collected in a
nested design for one check standard (#140) and one probe (#2362) are
shown below. The measurements are resistivity (ohm.cm) readings with six
repetitions per day. The individual level-1 standard deviations from

title: Analysis of reproducibility
text:
This page explains **pooled standard deviation** and **variance pooling** across multiple measurement runs, which solves the practical problem of combining variability measurements from different days or repeated tests into a single reliable estimate of measurement uncertainty. Engineers looking for ways to combine measurement noise from multiple runs, average out inconsistencies across repeated experiments, or consolidate day-to-day variations into one number would search for terms like "combining standard deviations," "weighted average of variances," "pooling measurement variation," or "overall measurement repeatability."

The table below gives the mechanism for pooling level-2 standard
deviations over runs. The pooled value is an average of weighted
variances and is the last entry in the right-hand column of the table.
The pooling can be extended in the same manner to cover check standards,
if appropriate.

subset of data

standard (#140) with probe (#2362) over six days. The data are analyzed
for between-day effects. The level-2 standard deviations and pooled
level-2 standard deviations over runs 1 and 2 are:

title: Analysis of stability
text:
This page explains **pooled standard deviation** and **sum of squares pooling**, statistical techniques that combine measurement variability data from multiple check standards into a single overall stability estimate for a gauge. In practical terms, this lets engineers combine five separate measurements of gauge drift into one reliable number that tells them how much their instrument is drifting over time, rather than having five separate and potentially confusing drift values. Related searches would include "combining standard deviations," "pooling variance across groups," "weighted average of standard deviations," or "aggregate measurement uncertainty from multiple sources."

single gauge (probe #2362) are pooled over check standards. The

title: Drift
text:
This section covers **drift detection and characterization** using time-series measurement data from check standards to identify systematic changes in instrument performance. It solves the practical problem of figuring out how much your instrument's readings will shift over time so you know when to recalibrate and how to account for measurement errors in your results. Someone looking for this might search for terms like "instrument stability monitoring," "detecting measurement bias over time," "check standard trending," "when to recalibrate equipment," or "how instrument accuracy changes with use."

For other instruments, measurements can be made on a daily basis on
two or more check standards over a preset time period, say, one month.
These measurements are plotted on a time scale to determine the extent
and nature of any drift. Drift rarely continues unabated at the same
rate and in the same direction for a long time period.
Thus, the expectation from such an experiment is to document the
maximum change that is likely to occur during a set time period and
plan adjustments to the instrument accordingly. A further impact of
the findings is that uncorrected drift is treated as a
type A component
in the uncertainty
analysis.

title: Differences among gauges
chapter:
standard deviation of 
       the correction as a type A uncertainty.
text:
This page covers **gauge bias correction** and **measurement uncertainty from instrument variation**, addressing the practical problem of how to handle the fact that different measuring instruments give slightly different readings on the same part—you can either adjust each measurement to account for your particular gauge's known errors, or you can accept the readings as-is and add extra uncertainty to account for gauge-to-gauge differences. Alternative searches: "correcting for instrument bias," "accounting for gage variation in measurements," "measurement system differences," "how much to trust readings from different gauges," or "gauge repeatability and reproducibility."

Given that the gauges are a random sample of like-kind gauges, the
best estimate in any situation is an average over all gauges. In
the usual production or metrology setting, however, it may only be
feasible to make the measurements on a particular piece with one gauge.
Then, there are two methods of dealing with the differences among
gauges.
Correct each measurement made with a particular gauge for the
bias of that gauge and report the
standard deviation of
the correction as a type A uncertainty.
Report each measurement as it occurs and assess a
type A uncertainty for
the differences among the gauges.

title: Geometry/configuration differences
chapter:
Measurements were made on six
wafers
text:
This page demonstrates **repeatability and reproducibility analysis** (also called **gauge R&R** or **measurement system variation**) by comparing paired measurements taken under different conditions to quantify how much of the variation in your data comes from the measurement setup itself rather than real differences in what you're measuring. Engineers use this approach to determine whether switching probe configurations, instruments, or operators is introducing unwanted measurement error that could mask or fake product quality differences. Related searches include: "equipment variation," "instrument calibration drift," "setup-induced error," "measurement consistency between configurations," or "how much does my measurement method affect results."

Measurements were made on six
wafers
over six days (except for 5 measurements on wafer 39) with
probe #2062 wired in two configurations. This sequence of measurements
was repeated after about a month resulting in two runs. Differences
between measurements in the two configurations on the same day are
