title: Simple design
text:
This page explains **reproducibility standard deviation** and **time-dependent variation components**, which help engineers figure out how much a single measurement result will bounce around due to day-to-day changes versus immediate instrument variability. Someone looking for this might search for "measurement uncertainty breakdown," "how to separate short-term and long-term measurement errors," "reproducibility vs repeatability," or "accounting for variation across different measurement days."

The standard deviation that defines the uncertainty for a single
measurement on a test item, often referred to as the reproducibility
standard deviation (
ASTM
), is
given by
$$ {\large s}_R = \sqrt{{\large s}_{days}^2 + {\large s}_1^2} = \frac{{\large s}_2}{\sqrt{2}} \,\, . $$
The time-dependent component is
$$ {\large s}_{days} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{2} {\large s}_2^2 - {\large s}_1^2} \,\, . $$
There may be other sources of uncertainty in the measurement process
that must be accounted for in a formal
analysis of uncertainty.

title: 2-level nested design
text:
A **2-level nested design** is a structured measurement approach where repeated observations of a check standard are collected across two hierarchical time periods to separate different sources of measurement error and variability. This method helps engineers figure out which errors in their measurement system come from short-term drift versus long-term changes, without having to collect expensive three-level data when the main sources of problems are already known. Someone might also search for this using terms like "nested measurement design," "two-stage sampling for measurement systems," "hierarchical measurement study," or "time-stratified repeatability and reproducibility analysis."

Measurements on a
check standard
are recommended for studying the effect of sources of variability that
manifest themselves over time. Data collection and analysis are
straightforward, and there is no reason to estimate interaction terms
when dealing with time-dependent errors. The measurements can be made
at one of two levels. Two levels should be sufficient for
characterizing most measurement systems.
Three
levels
are recommended for measurement systems for which sources
of error are not well understood and have not previously been studied.

title: 3-level nested design
text:
This page covers **nested experimental design** (specifically 3-level nesting) and **operator variability assessment** in measurement systems, solving the practical problem of figuring out whether different people taking measurements will get different results and how much that difference matters when you're trying to validate your measurement process. Someone looking for this might search for "how much does the operator affect my measurements," "do different people get different results," "operator repeatability study," "measurement variation by operator," or "checking if my measurement system is operator-dependent."

The measurements should be made with
ONE
operator.
Operator is not usually a consideration with automated systems.
However, systems that require decisions regarding line edge or other
feature delineations may be operator dependent. If there is reason to
believe that results might differ significantly by operator,
'operators' can be substituted for 'runs' in the design. Choose
L (L > 2)
operators at random from the pool of operators who are
capable of making measurements at the same level of precision. (Conduct
a small experiment with operators making repeatability measurements, if
necessary, to verify comparability of precision among operators.) Then
complete the data collection and analysis as outlined. In this case,
the
level-3 standard deviation
estimates
operator effect.

title: Analysis and interpretation
text:
This section covers **measurement uncertainty propagation** and **error component aggregation** for certified values, solving the practical problem of figuring out the total measurement error when you combine results across different test runs and days—accounting for how much variation comes from each source even when you didn't repeat measurements in all conditions. Engineers might also search for "combining measurement errors," "total gauge uncertainty," "time-dependent measurement variation," or "pooled standard deviation from multiple error sources."

The certified value for each wafer is the average of
N
= 6
repeatability measurements at the center of the wafer on
M
= 1
days and over
P
= 1 runs. Notice that
N
,
M
and
P
are not necessarily the same as the number of measurements in
the gauge study per wafer; namely,
J
,
K
and
L
.
The standard deviation of a certified value (for time-dependent sources
of error), is
\( \displaystyle \large{
s = \sqrt{s_{runs}^2 + s_{days}^2 - \frac{1}{6} s_1^2}
} \)
Standard deviations for days and runs are included in this calculation,
even though there were no replications over days or runs for the
certification measurements. These factors contribute to the overall
uncertainty of the measurement process even though they are not sampled
for the particular measurements of interest.

title: Standard deviations from assumed distributions
text:
This content covers **Type B uncertainty evaluation** and **conversion of bias to standard deviation**, which solves the practical problem of combining worst-case error limits (like instrument tolerances or calibration uncertainties) with random measurement variations into a single uncertainty budget that engineers can work with. Someone needing this concept might search for "how to convert tolerance to uncertainty," "turning maximum error into standard deviation," "combining fixed errors with random errors," or "ISO uncertainty from assumed distributions."

The ISO guidelines do not allow worst-case estimates of bias to be
added to the other components, but require they in some way be
converted to equivalent standard deviations. The approach is to
consider that any error or bias, for the situation at hand, is a random
draw from a known statistical distribution. Then the standard deviation
is calculated from known (or assumed) characteristics of the
distribution. Distributions that can be considered are:
Uniform
Triangular
Normal (Gaussian)

title: Steps
text:
This section covers **error propagation** (also called **uncertainty propagation**) and addresses the practical problem of figuring out how mistakes or variations in individual measurements add up to affect your final answer when you're calculating a result from multiple input values. Engineers often search for this concept using plain-language terms like "how do measurement errors combine," "calculating total uncertainty from multiple sources," "compounding errors in formulas," or "combining uncertainties when your answer depends on several measurements."

B. -
Reported value involves more than one quantity.
Write down the equation showing the relationship between
the quantities.
Write-out the
propagation of error
equation
and do a preliminary evaluation, if possible,
based on propagation of error.
If the measurement result
can be replicated directly
,
regardless of the number of secondary quantities in the
individual repetitions, treat the uncertainty evaluation as
in (A.1) to (A.5) above, being sure to evaluate all sources of
random error in the process.
If the measurement result
cannot be replicated directly
,
treat
each measurement quantity
as in (A.1) and (A.2) and:
Compute a standard deviation for each measurement
quantity.
Combine the standard deviations for the individual
quantities into a standard deviation for the reported
result via
propagation of error.
Compute a standard deviation for each
type
B component of uncertainty.
Combine type A and type B standard deviations into a
standard uncertainty
for the reported
result.
Compute an
expanded uncertainty.
Compare the uncerainty derived by propagation of error with the
uncertainty derived by data analysis techniques.

title: Type A evaluations of random components
text:
This section covers **Type A uncertainty evaluation** and **homogeneity assessment** in measurement systems, addressing the practical problem of deciding whether you need to account for variation within a test specimen or batch—for instance, when a chemical product differs from bottle to bottle or a material varies across its surface, and you can't measure at the exact same spot every time. Someone looking for this concept might search for "handling measurement uncertainty from material variation," "accounting for batch-to-batch differences in testing," "inhomogeneity in measurement uncertainty," or "when product inconsistency affects test results."

Artifacts, electrical devices, and chemical substances, etc. can be
inhomogeneous relative to the quantity that is being characterized
by the measurement process. If this fact is known beforehand, it
may be possible to measure the artifact very carefully at a
specific site and then direct the user to also measure at this site.
In this case, there is no contribution to measurement uncertainty
from inhomogeneity.
However, this is not always possible, and measurements may be
destructive. As an example, compositions of chemical compounds may
vary from bottle to bottle. If the reported value for the lot is
established from measurements on a few bottles drawn at random from
the lot, this variability must be taken into account in the uncertainty
statement.
Methods for testing for
inhomogeneity
and
assessing the appropriate uncertainty are discussed on another page.

title: Type A evaluations of time-dependent effects
text:
A **nested design** (also called hierarchical or multi-level design) breaks down measurement variability into separate short-term, day-to-day, and long-term components so you can identify which time period is causing the biggest swings in your results. This solves the practical problem of figuring out whether your equipment drifts slowly over months, changes between different days, or just bounces around within a single session. If you're looking for this approach, you might also search for "sources of variation analysis," "time-dependent error estimation," "layered experimental design," or "separating short-term from long-term drift."

A less-efficient method for estimating time-dependent sources of
uncertainty is a designed experiment. Measurements can be made
specifically for estimating two or three levels of errors. There are
many ways to do this, but the easiest method is a
nested design
where
J
short-term measurements are replicated on
K
days and the entire
operation is then replicated over
L
runs (months, etc.).
The analysis of these data leads to:
s
1
=
standard deviation with
(J -1)
degrees of freedom for
short-term errors
s
2
= standard
deviation with
(K -1)
degrees of freedom for day-to-day errors
s
3
= standard
deviation with
(L -1)
degrees of freedom for very long-term
errors

title: Measurement configuration within the laboratory

text:
This page covers **pooled standard deviation** and **Type A uncertainty evaluation**, which solve the practical problem of combining measurement variability from multiple instruments or probes into a single reliability estimate so engineers know how much their measurement equipment typically varies across different test conditions. Someone looking for this concept might search for "combining measurement repeatability across instruments," "overall instrument uncertainty from repeated measurements," "standard deviation across multiple gauges or probes," or "how to estimate measurement equipment error."

A two-way table of resistivity

title: Material inhomogeneity
text:
This excerpt describes **variance components analysis** and **stratified sampling**, which solves the practical problem of separating measurement noise from real differences between items in a batch so you can report accurate results without needing every single item to be perfectly measured. Engineers might also search for this concept using terms like "breaking down sources of variation," "between-group vs. within-group variability," "two-level sampling strategy," or "separating material differences from measurement error."

The best strategy is to draw a sample of bottles from the lot for the
purpose of
identifying and quantifying
between-bottle variability
. These measurements can be made with
a method that lacks the accuracy required to certify isotopic ratios,
but is precise enough to allow between-bottle comparisons. A second
sample is drawn from the lot and measured with an accurate method for
determining isotopic ratios, and the reported value for the lot is
taken to be the average of these determinations. There are therefore
two components of uncertainty assessed:
component that quantifies the imprecision of the average
component that quantifies how much an individual bottle can
deviate from the average.

title: Data collection and analysis
text:
This section covers **batch homogeneity assessment** and **uncertainty propagation from sampling and measurement repeatability**, addressing the practical problem of confidently certifying an entire batch of material based on testing only a few samples rather than everything. Engineers and quality managers searching for guidance on this topic might look for "how to estimate batch uncertainty from sample testing," "combining sampling variability with measurement repeatability," "assigning one certified value to a whole batch," or "uncertainty when you only test a few bottles from many."

The purpose of assessing inhomogeneity is to be able to
assign a value to the entire batch based on the average of a few bottles,
and the determination of inhomogeneity is usually made by a less accurate
method than the certification method. The reported value for the batch
would be the
average of
N
repetitions on
Q
bottles
using the certification method.
The uncertainty calculation is summarized below for the case where the
only contribution to uncertainty from the measurement method itself is
the repeatability standard deviation,
s
1
associated with the certification method. For more complicated
scenarios, see the pages on
uncertainty budgets.
If
\( \large{
s_{inh}^2 \le 0 \Rightarrow s_{reported \, value} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{QN}} s_1
} \)
If
\( \large{ s_{inh}^2 > 0 } \)
,
we need to distinguish two cases and their interpretations:
The standard deviation
\( \displaystyle \large{ s_{reported \, value} =
\sqrt{ \frac{Q+1}{Q} s_{inh}^2 + \frac{1}{QN} s_1^2}
} \)
leads to an interval that covers the difference between the
reported value and the average for a bottle selected at random
from the batch.
The standard deviation
\( \displaystyle \large{ s_{reported \, value} =
\sqrt{ \frac{Q+1}{Q} s_{inh}^2 + \frac{QN+1}{QN} s_1^2}
} \)
allows one to test the instrument using a single measurement.
The prediction interval for the difference between the reported
value and a single measurement, made with the same precision as
the certification measurements, on a bottle selected at random
from the batch. This is appropriate when the instrument under
test is similar to the certification instrument. If the
difference is not within the interval, the user's instrument is
in need of calibration.

title: Type A evaluations of bias
text:
Type A evaluation of bias addresses the practical problem of deciding whether differences between measurement devices (like multiple instruments in a lab) should be treated as random variation affecting all results or as systematic offsets specific to each instrument that need correction. When you only care about measurements from one particular instrument, you can reduce your uncertainty estimate by correcting that instrument's readings to match the lab average, then calculating a Type A uncertainty for just that correction factor. Engineers searching for how to handle instrument calibration differences, multiple instruments giving different readings, or reducing measurement uncertainty through calibration adjustments would find this concept under terms like "instrument bias correction," "calibration offset adjustment," "reducing measurement uncertainty by correcting systematic instrument differences," or "treating instrument variation as correctable bias rather than random error."

This problem was treated on the foregoing page as an
analysis of random error
for the case where
the uncertainty was intended to apply to all measurements for all
configurations. If measurements for only
one configuration
are
of interest, such as measurements made with a specific instrument, or
if a smaller uncertainty is required, the differences among, say,
instruments are treated as biases. The
best strategy
in this
situation is to correct all measurements made with a specific
instrument to the average for the instruments in the laboratory and
compute a type A uncertainty for the correction. This strategy, of
course, relies on the assumption that the instruments in the laboratory
represent a random sample of all instruments of a specific type.

title: Inconsistent bias
text:
Inconsistent bias occurs when a measurement instrument produces systematic errors that change direction or magnitude across repeated measurements over time, requiring engineers to detect and account for measurement drift rather than applying a single correction factor. This concept helps identify when a probe or instrument is becoming unreliable because its errors aren't stable—in this case, probe #283 shows negative bias in Run 1 but positive bias in Run 2, signaling that a constant correction won't work. Alternative searches: measurement drift, time-dependent bias, unstable systematic error, instrument calibration drift, or non-constant measurement error.

The results of resistivity measurements with five
probes on five silicon wafers are shown below for probe #283, which
is the probe of interest at this level with the artifacts being
1 ohm.cm wafers. The bias for probe #283 is negative for run 1 and
positive for run 2 with the runs separated by a two-month time period.
The correction is taken to be zero.
Table of biases (ohm.cm) for probe 283
Wafer Probe Run 1 Run 2

A conservative assumption is that the bias could fall somewhere within
the limits ± a, with a = maximum bias or 0.0000652 ohm.cm. The
standard deviation of the correction is included as a type A systematic
component of the uncertainty.
\( \displaystyle \large{
s_{correction} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}} MaxBias = 0.000038 \, \mbox{ohm.cm}
} \)

title: Consistent bias
text:
This section explains **consistent bias** and **type A uncertainty estimation** in measurement systems by showing how to identify and quantify systematic errors that a single instrument (probe) introduces across multiple test items (wafers). Engineers use this method when they need to figure out if one measuring device is consistently reading too high or too low compared to others, and how much confidence they can have in correcting those measurements. Related searches: instrument calibration error, systematic measurement offset, probe correction factor, removing consistent instrument drift, type A standard uncertainty, repeatability analysis with bias correction.

The table below comes from the
table of resistivity measurements
from a type A analysis of random effects with the average for each wafer
subtracted from each measurement. The differences, as shown, represent
the biases for each probe with respect to the other probes. Probe #2362
has an average bias, over the five wafers, of -0.02724 ohm.cm. If
measurements made with this probe are corrected for this bias, the
standard deviation of the correction is a type A uncertainty.

title: Bias with sparse data
text:
This section covers the **t-test for paired differences** (also called **paired t-test** or **dependent samples t-test**), which lets engineers figure out whether two different ways of measuring or setting something up are actually giving different results or if the differences are just random noise. Someone looking for this might search for "comparing two measurement methods," "testing if two setups give different results," "paired comparison test," "before-and-after comparison," or "are two configurations statistically different."

A t-statistic is used as an approximate test where we are assuming the
differences are approximately normal. The average difference and
standard deviation of the difference are required for this test. If
\( \displaystyle \large{
t = \left| \frac{\sqrt{N}}{s_{diff}} Avg_{diff} \right| > 2
} \)
the difference between the two configurations is statistically
significant.
The average and standard deviation computed from the
N = 29
differences in each run from the table above are shown along with
corresponding t-values which confirm that the differences are
significant, but in opposite directions, for both runs.
Average differences between wiring configurations
Run Probe Average Std dev N t

title: Formulas for functions of two variables
chapter:
exact formula
for the standard deviation of a product
text:
This formula applies **propagation of uncertainty** (also called error propagation or uncertainty analysis) to estimate how measurement errors in two input variables combine to affect the uncertainty of a calculated result. If you measure two things (like voltage and resistance) and use them to calculate something else (like power), this tells you how big the error bars on your final answer should be based on the error bars of your measurements. You might also search for this using terms like "uncertainty budget," "combining measurement errors," "how errors add together," or "standard deviation of a ratio or product."

\( \large{ \frac{Y}{\sqrt{N}}
\sqrt{ a^2 \frac{s_x^2}{\bar{X}^2} + b^2 \frac{s_z^2}{\bar{Z}^2} +
2ab \frac{s_{xz}}{\bar{X} \bar{Z}} } } \)
Note: this is an approximation. The exact result could be
obtained starting from the
exact formula
for the standard deviation of a product
derived by
Goodman (1960)
.

title: Propagation of error for many variables
text:
This page covers **propagation of uncertainty** (also called **error propagation** or **uncertainty propagation**), which shows engineers how to calculate the overall measurement error in a final result when multiple measured inputs each have their own measurement errors—solving the practical problem of figuring out "how wrong is my answer?" when it's calculated from several imperfect measurements. People also search for this using plain-English terms like "how errors add up," "combining measurement errors," "finding total error from multiple sources," or "uncertainty in calculated results."

Computing uncertainty for measurands based on more complicated functions can be done using basic propagation of errors principles. For example, suppose we want to compute the uncertainty of the discharge coefficient for fluid flow
(Whetstone et al.)
. The measurement equation is
$$ C_d = \frac{\dot{m} \sqrt{1-\left( \frac{d}{D} \right) ^4}}{K d^2 F \sqrt{\rho} \sqrt{\Delta P}} $$
where
$$ \begin{eqnarray*}
C_d &=& \mbox{discharge coefficient} \\
\dot{m} &=& \mbox{mass flow rate} \\
d &=& \mbox{orifice diameter} \\
D &=& \mbox{pipe diameter} \\
\rho &=& \mbox{fluid density} \\
\Delta P &=& \mbox{differential pressure} \\
K &=& \mbox{constant} \\
F &=& \mbox{thermal expansion factor (constant)} \\
\end{eqnarray*} $$
Assuming the variables in the equation are uncorrelated, the squared uncertainty of the discharge coefficient is
$$ s^2_{Cd} = \left[ \frac{\partial C_d}{\partial \dot{m}} \right]^2 s^2_{\dot m} +
\left[ \frac{\partial C_d}{\partial d} \right]^2 s^2_d +
\left[ \frac{\partial C_d}{\partial D} \right]^2 s^2_D +
\left[ \frac{\partial C_d}{\partial \rho} \right]^2 s^2_{\rho} +
\left[ \frac{\partial C_d}{\partial \Delta P} \right]^2 s^2_{\Delta P} $$
and the partial derivatives are the following.
$$ \frac{\partial C_d}{\partial \dot{m}} =
\frac{\sqrt{1-\left( \frac{d}{D} \right) ^4}}{K d^2 F \sqrt{\rho} \sqrt{\Delta P}} $$
$$ \frac{\partial C_d}{\partial d} =
\frac{-2\dot{m} d}{K F D^4 \sqrt{\rho} \sqrt{\Delta P} \sqrt{1-\left( \frac{d}{D} \right) ^4}} -
\frac{2 \dot{m} \sqrt{1-\left( \frac{d}{D} \right) ^4}}{K d^3 F \sqrt{\rho} \sqrt{\Delta P}} $$
$$ \frac{\partial C_d}{\partial D} =
\frac{2 \dot{m} d^2}{K F D^5 \sqrt{\rho} \sqrt{\Delta P} \sqrt{1-\left( \frac{d}{D} \right) ^4}} $$
$$ \frac{\partial C_d}{\partial \rho} =
\frac{- \dot{m} \sqrt{1-\left( \frac{d}{D} \right) ^4}}{2 K d^2 F \rho^{\frac{3}{2}} \sqrt{\Delta P}} $$
$$ \frac{\partial C_d}{\partial \Delta P} =
\frac{- \dot{m} \sqrt{1-\left( \frac{d}{D} \right) ^4}}{2 K d^2 F \sqrt{\rho} (\Delta P)^{\frac{3}{2}}} $$

title: Sensitivity coefficients for measurements on the test item
text:
This section covers **pooled standard deviation**, **between-group variance**, and **temporal variability estimation** to solve the practical problem of figuring out how much measurement results vary from day to day when you repeat tests on the same item, so you know how stable your answer really is. Engineers looking for this guidance might search for terms like "how to combine daily measurements," "day-to-day variation in testing," "standard deviation across repeated days," or "averaging test results over time to reduce error."

If possible, the measurements on the test item should be repeated over
\(M\) days and averaged to estimate the reported value. The standard
deviation for the reported value is computed from the daily averages,
and the standard deviation for the temporal component is:
$$ s_{reported value} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{M}}
\sqrt{ \frac{1}{M-1} \sum_{m=1}^M \left( \bar{Y}_{m \, \scriptsize{\bullet}} -
\bar{Y}_{\scriptsize{\bullet \bullet}} \right)^2
} $$
with degrees of freedom \( \nu_2 = M-1 \)
where \( \bar{Y}_{m \, \scriptsize{\bullet}} \)
are the daily averages and \( \bar{Y}_{\scriptsize{\bullet \bullet}} \)
is the grand average.
The sensitivity coefficients are:
\( a_1 = 0 \); \( a_2 = \sqrt{\frac{1}{M}} \).

title: Sensitivity coefficients for measurements on a check standard
text:
This page covers **standard deviation estimation using check standards and temporal variation components**, solving the practical problem of figuring out how much your measurement results will bounce around from day to day when you're testing similar items repeatedly—rather than assuming all variation comes from a single test run, it lets you use past check standard data to predict real-world measurement scatter. Someone looking for this concept might also search for "propagating uncertainty across multiple measurement days," "accounting for day-to-day measurement drift," "estimating repeatability and reproducibility from control standards," or "scaling measurement uncertainty by number of test repetitions."

If the temporal component of the measurement
process is evaluated from
measurements on a check
standard
and there are \(M\) days (\(M = 1\) is
permissible) of measurements on the test item that are
structured in the same manner as the measurements on the check
standard
, the standard deviation for the reported value is
$$ s_{reported \, value} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{M}} s_2 $$
with degrees of freedom \( \nu_2 = K - 1\)
from the \(K\)
entries in the check standard database.

title: Sensitivity coefficients for measurements from a 2-level design
text:
This page covers **nested design variance components** and **measurement uncertainty propagation**, which solves the practical problem of figuring out how much your final test result will vary when you take multiple measurements spread across different days—accounting separately for day-to-day drift versus measurement noise in the same day. Someone might search for this using terms like "combining short-term and long-term measurement variation," "how to estimate total uncertainty from repeated tests," "day-to-day variability," or "nested ANOVA for measurement systems."

If the temporal components are estimated from
a
2-level nested design
,
and the reported value for a test item is an average over
\(N\) short-term repetitions
\(M\) (\(M = 1\) is permissible) days
of measurements on the test item, the standard deviation for the
reported value is:
$$ s_{reported \, value} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{M}s_{days}^2 + \frac{1}{MN} s_1^2} $$
See the relationships in the section on
2-level nested design
for
definitions of the standard deviations and their respective degrees
of freedom.

title: Sensitivity coefficients for measurements from a 3-level design
text:
This page explains **nested design variance decomposition** and **sensitivity coefficients** for combining multiple sources of measurement variation (short-term repeatability, day-to-day drift, and run-to-run changes) into a single uncertainty estimate for a final reported value. A practitioner needs this when they've collected measurements over multiple days and runs and want to know what the overall uncertainty is in their final answer, accounting for how many times they repeated each test. Search terms like "combining measurement uncertainty from multiple sources," "nested ANOVA variance components," "hierarchical measurement variability," or "accounting for repeatability, reproducibility, and intermediate precision" all describe the same concept.

If the temporal components are estimated from a
3-level nested design
and the
reported value is an average over
\(N\) short-term repetitions
\(M\) days
\(P\) runs
of measurements on the test item, the standard deviation for the
reported value is:
$$ s_{reported \, value} = \sqrt{
\frac{1}{P} s_{runs}^2 + \frac{1}{PM} s_{days}^2 + \frac{1}{PMN} s_1^2
} $$
See the section on
analysis of
variability
for definitions and relationships among the
standard deviations shown in the equation above.

title: Example of uncertainty budget
text:
This page explains **nested design** and **uncertainty budget** methodology for breaking down total measurement error into separate sources (short-term variation vs. day-to-day drift). An engineer testing a part needs to know whether inconsistent results come from the measurement instrument itself, differences between days, or other factors—this method isolates each contributor so you know where to focus quality improvements. People might search for this as "sources of variation analysis," "measurement system breakdown," "component-based error analysis," or "how to separate repeatable errors from random drift."

An uncertainty budget that illustrates several principles of
uncertainty analysis is shown below. The reported value for a
test item is the average of \(N\) short-term measurements where
the temporal components of uncertainty were estimated from a
3-level nested design
with
\(J\) short-term repetitions over \(K\) days.
The number of measurements made on the test item is the same
as the number of short-term measurements in the design; i.e.,
\(N = J\). Because there were no repetitions over days or runs
on the test item, \(M = 1; \, P = 1\). The
sensitivity coefficients for this
design
are shown on the foregoing page.

title: Degrees of freedom
text:
Degrees of freedom quantify how many independent data points contribute to each uncertainty estimate, which tells you whether your measurement result's confidence is based on solid repeated testing or educated guesses. Engineers use this to decide which uncertainty sources deserve more investigation—Type A uncertainties get degrees of freedom from how many times you repeated a test, while Type B sources are assigned infinite degrees of freedom when you're estimating error limits from limited information, published specs, or worst-case scenarios. Alternative searches: "sample size for uncertainty," "how many measurements do I need," "confidence in my error estimate," "statistical reliability of measurement," or "uncertainty from one-time vs. repeated tests."

Degrees of freedom for type A uncertainties are
the degrees of freedom for the respective standard deviations.
Degrees of freedom for Type B evaluations may be available from
published reports or calibration certificates. Special cases
where the standard deviation must be estimated from fragmentary
data or scientific judgment are assumed to have infinite degrees of
freedom; for example,
Worst-case estimate based on a robustness study or other
evidence
Estimate based on an assumed distribution of possible errors
Type B uncertainty component for which degrees of freedom
are not documented

title: Computation of revised uncertainty
text:
This passage covers **bias correction** and **uncertainty interval bounds** (or **revised uncertainty limits**) in measurement systems, solving the practical problem of preventing negative tolerance ranges when a measurement instrument's systematic error is large enough to make the lower confidence bound fall below zero. Engineers searching for this concept might also look for "non-negative uncertainty constraints," "handling large bias in measurements," "preventing negative error bounds," or "uncertainty adjustment when bias exceeds tolerance."

The definition above can lead to a negative uncertainty limit; e.g.,
if the bias is positive and greater than \(U\), the upper endpoint
becomes negative. The requirement that the uncertainty limits be
greater than or equal to zero for all values of the bias guarantees
non-negative uncertainty limits and is accepted at the cost of
somewhat wider uncertainty intervals. This leads to the following
set of restrictions on the uncertainty limits:
$$ U_- = \left\{ \begin{array}{ll}
U + \delta & \mbox{if } \,\, U + \delta > 0 \\
0 & \mbox{if } \,\, U + \delta \le 0 \\
\end{array} \right. $$
$$ U_+ = \left\{ \begin{array}{ll}
U - \delta & \mbox{if } \,\, U - \delta > 0 \\
0 & \mbox{if } \,\, U - \delta \le 0
\end{array} \right. $$

Non-statisticians might search for this using terms like "how much uncertainty in my small sample," "confidence limits for small datasets," "t-test thresholds," or "sample size correction factors for confidence intervals."

title: Background and data
text:
This page covers **measurement process characterization** and **variance components analysis** (also called sources of variation decomposition), which helps engineers figure out where measurement errors are actually coming from—whether problems are caused by the gauge itself being imprecise, or by environmental changes, operator differences, or equipment drift over time. Instead of just knowing "our measurements are off by this much," this approach lets you break down the total error into separate pieces so you can fix the biggest problems first. You might also search for this under **repeatability and reproducibility studies, time-dependent variation analysis, gauge capability studies,** or **measurement system analysis (MSA)**.

Measurements of resistivity on 100 ohm.cm wafers were made according
to an ASTM Standard Test Method
(ASTM F84)
to assess the sources of uncertainty in the
measurement system. Resistivity measurements have been studied over
the years, and it is clear from those data that there are sources of
variability affecting the process beyond the basic imprecision of
the gauges. Changes in measurement results have been noted over
days and over months and the data in this study are structured to
quantify these time-dependent changes in the measurement process.

title: Analysis and interpretation
text:
This page demonstrates **bias analysis** and **probe effect characterization** by showing how individual measurement probes deviate from the average across multiple wafers and repeated runs, helping engineers identify which probes are consistently reading too high or too low so they can correct measurements or flag problematic equipment. You might also search for "systematic error by instrument," "gauge bias," "measurement tool comparison," or "repeatability and reproducibility (R&R) by device."

Biases by probe are shown in the following table.
The reader can download the following
data as a text file
.
Differences from the mean for each wafer
Wafer Probe Run 1 Run 2

138. 281. 0.0108 0.0323
138. 283. 0.0193 -0.0258

title: Effects of days and long-term stability
text:
This section covers **long-term stability analysis** and **measurement system drift detection**, which helps engineers spot whether their equipment or process is gradually changing over time by comparing measurements taken weeks or months apart. Instead of assuming your measurements stay consistent, this approach systematically checks if there's a slow upward or downward shift creeping into your results—something you'd also call "temporal drift," "instrument drift," "systematic change," or "measurement bias over time."

The data points that are plotted in the five graphs shown below are
averages of resistivity measurements at the center of each wafer for
wafers #138, 139, 140, 141, 142. Data for each of two runs are shown
on each graph. The six days of measurements for each run are separated
by approximately one month and show, with the exception of wafer #139,
that there is a very slight shift upwards between run 1 and run 2. The
size of the effect is estimated as a level-3 standard deviation in the
analysis of the data.

title: Run gauge study example using Dataplot�
text:
This page demonstrates **repeatability and reproducibility (R&R) analysis** and **measurement system variation** assessment, which helps engineers figure out whether differences they're seeing in their measurements come from real product variation or just from inconsistent measurement tools and techniques. Someone looking for this concept might search for "gage repeatability," "measurement error breakdown," "probe consistency check," "variation source identification," or "measurement system capability."

1. and 2.
Interpretation:
The plots verify that, for both runs, the repeatability of probe #2362
is not dependent on wafers or days, although the standard deviations on
days D, E, and F of run 2 are larger in some instances than for the
other days.
3. and 4.
Interpretation:
Probe #2362
appears as #5 in the plots
which show that, for both runs, the
precision of this probe is better than for the other probes.
5.
Interpretation:
There is a separate plot
for each wafer. The points on the left side of each plot are averages
at the wafer center plotted over 5 days; the points on the right are the
same measurements repeated after one month to check on the stability of
the measurement process. The plots show day-to-day variability as well
as slight variability from run-to-run.

title: Dataplot macros
text:
This macro demonstrates **measurement process characterization** and **time-series plotting** for tracking how semiconductor wafer measurements change over time, helping engineers visually detect whether measurement readings are stable or drifting across different wafers and days. Someone looking for this technique might search for "tracking measurement stability over time," "monitoring sensor consistency across batches," "plotting repeated measurements to find trends," or "visual comparison of measurement repeatability."

reset data
reset plot control
reset i/o
label size 3
dimension 50 columns
skip 25
read mcp61b.dat wafer probe mo1 day1 y1 mo2 day2 y2 diff
skip 0
let t = mo1+(day1-1)/31.
let t2= mo2+(day2-1)/31.
x3label WAFER 138
multiplot 3 2
plot y1 t subset wafer 138 and
plot y2 t2 subset wafer 138
x3label wafer 139
plot y1 t subset wafer 139 and
plot y2 t2 subset wafer 139
x3label WAFER 140
plot y1 t subset wafer 140 and
plot y2 t2 subset wafer 140
x3label WAFER 140
plot y1 t subset wafer 141 and
plot y2 t2 subset wafer 141
x3label WAFER 142
plot y1 t subset wafer 142 and
plot y2 t2 subset wafer 142

title: Background and data
text:
This page covers **repeated measures design** and **measurement system validation** through replicated observations over time, which solves the practical problem of checking whether your measuring instrument stays accurate and consistent throughout a long certification project by taking the same reading multiple times each day over several weeks. Someone looking for this concept might search for "checking measurement consistency over time," "monitoring instrument drift," "validating measurement repeatability," "quality control for test equipment," or "tracking measurement stability during a study."

The measurements were carried out according to an ASTM Test Method
(F84)
with NIST probe #2362.
The measurements on the check standard duplicate certification
measurements that were being made, during the same time period, on
individual wafers from crystal #51939. For the check standard there
were:
J = 6
repetitions at the center of the wafer on each day
K = 25
days
The
K = 25
days cover the time during which the individual
wafers were being certified at the National Institute of Standards and
Technology.

title: Analysis and interpretation
text:
This excerpt covers **control charts for standard deviations** and **F-test hypothesis testing** for monitoring measurement system performance, specifically detecting when a probe's measurement precision gets worse over time by comparing the variability of current measurements against a baseline. Engineers looking to catch when their measurement equipment starts producing scattered or unreliable results can search for "detecting measurement drift," "monitoring probe degradation," "upper control limits for variation," or "statistical process control for precision."

The control chart for monitoring the precision of
probe #2362 is constructed as discussed in the section on
control charts for standard
deviations
. The upper control limit (UCL) for testing for
degradation of the probe is computed using the critical value from the
F table
with numerator
degrees of freedom
J - 1 = 5
and denominator degrees of freedom
K(J - 1) = 125
. For a 0.05 significance level,
\( F_{0.05, 5, 125} = 2.29 \)
\( UCL = s_1 \sqrt{F_{0.05, 5, 125}} = 0.09238 \,\, \mbox{ohm.cm} \)

title: Run check standard example yourself
text:
This page teaches **run checks** and **measurement process characterization**, which help you verify that your measurement system is working consistently and that your data doesn't have hidden patterns or drift that would make your results unreliable. An engineer would call this "checking if my measurements are stable" or "making sure my test equipment isn't drifting over time." Someone looking for this concept might search for "process control charts," "detecting measurement drift," "validating test stability," or "confirming measurement system consistency."

This page allows you to repeat the analysis outlined in the case study
description on the previous page using
Dataplot
. It is required that you
have already
downloaded and installed
Dataplot and
configured your browser
to run
Dataplot. Output from each analysis step below will be displayed in
one or more of the Dataplot windows. The four main windows are the
Output Window, the Graphics window, the Command History window, and
the data sheet window. Across the top of the main windows there are
menus for executing Dataplot commands. Across the bottom is a command
entry window where commands can be typed in.

title: Background and data
text:
A **3-level nested design** is used to separate and measure different sources of measurement variation that occur at different time scales—capturing day-to-day changes and long-term drift—which certification measurements alone cannot detect. This solves the practical problem of figuring out how much of your measurement uncertainty comes from short-term inconsistencies versus gradual equipment degradation, so you know whether your process is actually stable or slowly drifting out of spec. People might search for this using terms like "hierarchical experimental design," "variation by time period," "sources of measurement error," "repeatability and reproducibility study," or "nested ANOVA."

The certification measurements themselves are
not the primary source for estimating uncertainty components because
they do not yield information on day-to-day effects and long-term
effects. The standard deviations for the three time-dependent sources
of uncertainty are estimated from a
3-level nested design
. The design
was replicated on each of
Q = 5
wafers which were chosen at
random, for this purpose, from the lot of wafers. The certification
measurements were made between the two runs in order to check on the
long-term stability of the process. The data consist of
repeatability standard deviations
(with
J - 1 = 5
degrees of freedom each) from measurements at
the wafer center.

title: Run the type A uncertainty analysis using Dataplot
text:
Type A uncertainty analysis quantifies measurement variability by analyzing repeated observations of the same item under identical conditions, helping engineers determine how much their measurement system's repeatability contributes to overall measurement error. Someone looking for this might search for "how to calculate measurement repeatability," "analyzing variation in repeated measurements," "quantifying measurement consistency," or "assessing measurement system precision from test data."

This page allows you to repeat the analysis outlined in the
case study description on the previous page using
Dataplot
. It is required that you have already
downloaded and installed
Dataplot and
configured your browser
. to run Dataplot. Output from each analysis
step below will be displayed in one or more of the Dataplot windows.
The four main windows are the Output Window, the Graphics window, the
Command History window, and the data sheet window. Across the top of
the main windows there are menus for executing Dataplot commands.
Across the bottom is a command entry window where commands can be typed
in.

title: Distribution (Location, Spread and Shape)
text:
Understanding a process's distribution means knowing three things about your output: where it centers (location/mean), how much it bounces around (spread/standard deviation/variation), and whether that variation is balanced or lopsided (shape/skewness/symmetry)—which helps you predict what parts will actually come out of your process and catch when something's gone wrong. Someone looking for this might search for "process centering and variation," "mean and range analysis," "why are my parts spread out," or "is my process stable and centered."

Location
The location is the expected value of the output being measured. For a
stable process, this is the value around which the process has stabilized.
Spread
The spread is the expected amount of variation associated with the output. This tells us the range of possible values that we would expect
to see.
Shape
The shape shows how the variation is distributed about the location. This
tells us if our variation is symmetric about the mean or if it is skewed
or possibly multimodal.

title: Process Variability
text:
The **Empirical Rule** (also called the **68-95-99.7 Rule** or **three-sigma rule**) uses **standard deviation** to predict how much of your process data will fall within certain ranges around the average, letting you quickly figure out if your process is behaving normally or if something's gone wrong. Instead of doing complicated calculations, this rule gives you simple percentages: roughly 68% of measurements stay within one standard deviation of the mean, 95% within two, and 99.7% within three—which helps engineers set realistic tolerance bands and spot when a process is drifting out of control. People searching for this concept might also look for "data spread prediction," "process centering," "normal distribution ranges," or "how to interpret standard deviation in manufacturing."

The standard deviation (square root of the variance)
gives insight into the spread of the data through the use of what is known
as the
Empirical Rule
. This rule (shown in the graph below) is:
Approximately 60-78% of the data are within a distance of one standard
deviation from the average
\( (\bar{x} - s, \bar{x} + s) \).
Approximately 90-98% of the data are within a distance of two standard
deviations from the average
\( (\bar{x} - 2s, \bar{x} + 2s) \).
More than 99% of the data are within a distance of three standard
deviations from the average
\( (\bar{x} - 3s, \bar{x} + 3s) \).

title: Propagating Error
text:
This page covers **variance propagation** (also called error propagation or uncertainty propagation) using **linear combinations of random variables** and **covariance**, which solves the practical problem of predicting how measurement errors or process variation from different sources add up to affect your final product output. A non-statistician engineer might search for "how do I combine errors from multiple sources," "what's my total uncertainty when things vary independently," or "how much does variation in inputs affect my output"—this technique gives you the math to calculate total variability when you know the variability of individual contributing factors.

Usually we can model the contribution of the various sources
of error to the total error through a simple linear relationship. If we
have a simple linear relationship between two variables, say,
\( y = \mu + \alpha y_{1} + \beta y_{2} + \epsilon \)
then the variance associated with,
y
, is given by
\( \mbox{Var}(y) = \alpha^{2}\mbox{Var}(y_{1}) +
\beta^{2}\mbox{Var}(y_{2}) + 2 \alpha \beta \mbox{Cov}(y_{1},y_{2}) \)
If the variables are not correlated, then there is no covariance and
the last term in the above equation drops off.  A good example of
this is the case in which we have both process error and measurement
error. Since these are usually independent of each other, the
total observed variance is just the sum of the variances for process and
measurement. Remember to never add standard deviations, we must add
variances.

title: Populations and Sampling
text:
This passage covers **survey sampling** and **statistical inference** — practical methods for figuring out what a large group of people thinks without having to ask every single person, which would be too expensive, time-consuming, or impossible to do in reality. Instead of surveying everyone in your target population, you collect opinions from a smaller sample and use those results to make educated guesses about the whole group's opinions. Someone looking for this concept might search for "sampling methods," "representative surveys," "polling techniques," "how to survey without asking everyone," or "using a small group to represent the whole population."

In survey sampling, if you want to know what everyone thinks about
a particular topic, you can just ask everyone and record their
answers. Depending on how you define the term,
everyone
(all the
adults in a town, all the males in the USA, etc.), it may be impossible
or impractical to survey everyone.  The other option is to survey
a small group (Sample) of the people whose opinions you are interested
in (Target Population), record their opinions and use that information
to make inferences about what everyone thinks. Opinion pollsters have developed
a whole body of tools for doing just that and many of those tools apply
to manufacturing as well. We can use these sampling techniques to
take a few measurements from a process and make statements about the
behavior of that process.

title: Process Models
text:
The **black-box model** is a foundational framework in **process characterization** that helps you map which inputs (both things you can control like settings and things you can't like humidity) affect your measurable outputs, so you can figure out what's actually driving your product quality or process performance without needing to understand the internal mechanics. Engineers looking for this concept might search for "input-output relationship," "cause and effect diagram," "what factors affect my process results," or "how to connect machine settings to product quality."

We can use the simple
black-box
model, shown below,
to describe most of the tools and processes we will encounter in PPC.
The process will be stimulated by inputs. These inputs can either be controlled
(such as recipe or machine settings) or uncontrolled (such as humidity,
operators, power fluctuations, etc.). These inputs interact with our process
and produce outputs. These outputs are usually some characteristic
of our process that we can measure. The measurable inputs and outputs can
be sampled in order to observe and understand how they behave and relate
to each other.

title: Experiments and Experimental Design
text:
This section covers **correlation and causality**, teaching engineers how to distinguish between variables that move together versus variables where one actually causes changes in the other—a critical distinction because two things can look related without one actually driving the other. Someone looking for this concept might search for "does this variable actually affect that one" or "how to tell if a relationship is real or just coincidence" or "correlation vs causation in manufacturing" or "what's really causing the problem versus what just happens at the same time."

There are generally two types of relationships that we
are interested in for purposes of PPC.  They are:
Correlation
Two variables are said to be correlated if an observed change in the level
of one variable is accompanied by a change in the level of another variable.
The change may be in the same direction (positive correlation) or in
the opposite direction (negative correlation).
Causality
There is a causal relationship between two variables if a change in the
level of one variable causes a change in the other variable.
Note that correlation does not imply causality.  It is possible for
two variables to be associated with each other without one of them causing
the observed behavior in the other.  When this is the case it is usually
because there is a third (possibly unknown) causal factor.

title: One-Way ANOVA
text:
One-way ANOVA uses Constrained Linear Model (CLM) techniques with a sum-to-zero constraint on level effects to produce a single, usable answer from what would otherwise be an unsolvable equation. This solves the practical problem of getting clear numerical estimates of how each production process level differs from the overall average, without getting stuck with multiple equally valid but contradictory solutions. You might also search for "factor level estimation," "effect coding," "identifying process differences," or "comparing multiple process settings."

The second way to estimate effects is through the use of
CLM
techniques.
If you look at the model above you will notice that it is in the form of
a CLM. The only problem is that the model is saturated
and no unique solution exists. We overcome this problem by applying a constraint
to the model. Since the level effects are just deviations from the grand
mean, they must sum to zero. By applying the constraint that the level
effects must sum to zero, we can now obtain a unique solution to the CLM equations.
Most analysis programs will handle this for you automatically. See the
chapter on
Process Modeling
for a more complete discussion on estimating the coefficients for
these models.

title: Two-Way Crossed ANOVA
text:
Two-way ANOVA (analysis of variance) with crossed factors lets you figure out how two different process inputs affect your output and whether those inputs work together in unexpected ways. An engineer would use this when they need to separate out which problems come from Factor A, which from Factor B, which from A and B together, and which is just random noise. People might also search for "factorial design analysis," "two-factor experiment," "main effects and interaction effects," or "how to test two variables at once."

For the two-way ANOVA, we display the data in a two-dimensional
table with the levels of Factor A in columns and the levels of Factor B
in rows. The replicate observations fill each cell. We can sweep out the
common value, the row effects, the column effects, the interaction effects
and the residuals using
value-splitting
techniques. Sums of squares can be calculated and summarized
in an ANOVA table as shown below.
Source
Sum of Squares
DoF
Mean Square
F
0
Rows
\( SSR = JK\sum{(\bar{y}_{i..} - \bar{y}_{...})^2} \)
I - 1
MSR = SSR/(I - 1)
MSR/MSE
Columns
\( SSC = IK\sum{(\bar{y}_{.j.} - \bar{y}_{...})^2} \)
J - 1
MSC = SSC/(J - 1)
MSC/MSE
Interaction
\( SSI = K\sum\sum{(\bar{y}_{ij.} - \bar{y}_{i..} -
\bar{y}_{.j.} + \bar{y}_{...})^2} \)
(I - 1)(J - 1)
MSI = SSC/((I - 1)(J - 1))
MSI/MSE
Residuals
\( SSE = \sum{\sum{\sum{(y_{ijk} - \bar{y}_{ij.})^2}}} \)
IJ(K - 1)
MSE = SSE/(IJ(K - 1))
Corr. Total
\( SST = \sum{\sum{\sum{(y_{ijk} - \bar{y}_{...})^2}}} \)
IJK - 1
\( \bar{y}_{i..} = \frac{1}{JK} \sum_{j=1}^{J}{\sum_{k=1}^{K}{y_{ijk}}} \)
\( \bar{y}_{.j.} = \frac{1}{IK} \sum_{i=1}^{I}{\sum_{k=1}^{K}{y_{ijk}}} \)
\( \bar{y}_{ij.} = \frac{1}{K} \sum_{k=1}^{K}{y_{ijk}} \)
\( \bar{y}_{...} = \frac{1}{IJK}
\sum_{i=1}^{I}{\sum_{j=1}^{J}{\sum_{k=1}^{K}{y_{ijk}}}}
\)
The row labeled, "Corr. Total", in the ANOVA table contains the
corrected total sum of squares and the associated degrees of freedom (DoF).
We can use
CLM
techniques to do the estimation.
We still have the problem that the model is
saturated
and no unique solution exists. We overcome this problem by applying the
constraints to the model that the two main effects and interaction effects
each sum to zero.

title: Two-Way Nested ANOVA
text:
This page covers **nested ANOVA (analysis of variance)** and **hierarchical experimental design**, which helps engineers figure out how much variation in a product or process comes from different sources when you have groups within groups—like testing multiple batches from different production lines, where you care about both line-to-line differences and batch-to-batch differences within each line. People looking for this same technique might search for "nested factors," "hierarchical variance analysis," "dependent groups ANOVA," "mixed-level testing," or "multi-stage process variation breakdown."

Source
Sum of Squares
DoF
Mean Square
F
0
A
\( SSA = JK\sum{(\bar{y}_{i..} - \bar{y}_{...})^2} \)
I - 1
MSA = SSA/(I - 1)
MSA/MSB
B within A
\( SSB = K\sum\sum{(\bar{y}_{ij.} - \bar{y}_{i..})^2} \)
I(J - 1)
MSB = SSB/(I(J - 1))
MSB/MSE
Residuals
\( SSE = \sum\sum\sum{(y_{ijk} - \bar{y}_{ij.})^2} \)

Corr. Total
\( SST = \sum\sum\sum{(y_{ijk} - \bar{y}_{...})^2} \)
IJK - 1
\( \bar{y}_{i..} = \frac{1}{JK} \sum_{j=1}^{J}{\sum_{k=1}^{K}{y_{ijk}}} \)
\( \bar{y}_{ij.} = \frac{1}{K} \sum_{k=1}^{K}{y_{ijk}} \)
\( \bar{y}_{...} = \frac{1}{IJK}
\sum_{i=1}^{I}{\sum_{j=1}^{J}{\sum_{k=1}^{K}{y_{ijk}}}} \)
The row labeled, "Corr. Total", in the ANOVA table contains the
corrected total sum of squares and the associated degrees of freedom (DoF).

title: Discrete Models
text:
When you have categorical data (like pass/fail results, defect types, or operator shifts) and need to understand relationships between them, Contingency Table Analysis organizes this information into a cross-tabulation matrix to reveal patterns and dependencies. This is also called crosstab analysis, cross-tabulation, or categorical data analysis, and engineers often use it to check whether one discrete factor (like machine type) influences another discrete outcome (like defect category). You might also search for "chi-square test," "association between categories," or "frequency table analysis" if you're trying to determine whether two categorical variables are independent or related to each other.

There are two primary methods available for the analysis of discrete
response data. The first one applies to situations in which
we have discrete explanatory variables and discrete responses and is known
as Contingency Table Analysis. The model for this is covered in detail in this
section. The second model applies when we have both discrete
and continuous explanatory variables and is referred to as a Log-Linear
Model. That model is beyond the scope of this Handbook, but interested readers
should refer to the
reference section
of
this chapter for a list of useful books on the topic.

title: Identifying Parameters, Ranges and Resolution
text:
The 1/10 tolerance rule establishes the required measurement resolution by defining the relationship between product tolerance and measurement accuracy—a core principle of measurement system design and metrology planning. This helps engineers pick the right measuring tools (like calipers or gauges) so their instruments are precise enough to actually catch parts that are out of spec, without buying equipment that's unnecessarily expensive. People searching for this concept might look for: "measurement accuracy to tolerance ratio," "how precise do my measurements need to be," "choosing measurement equipment for tolerances," "gauge capability relative to tolerance," or "measurement resolution guidelines."

Finally, the required resolution for the measurements should
be specified. This specification will help guide the choice of metrology
equipment and help define the measurement procedures. As a rule of thumb,
we would like our measurement resolution to be at least 1/10 of our tolerance.
For the oxide growth example, this means that we want to measure with an
accuracy of 2 Angstroms. Similarly, for the turning operation we would need
to measure the diameter within .001". This means that vernier calipers
would be adequate as the measurement device for this application.

title: Choosing a Sampling Scheme
text:
**Confounding variables** (also called **confounded factors** or **aliased effects**) occur when you can't separate the influence of one process change from other simultaneous changes—like in Example 1 where switching coolants at the same time as a tool change makes it impossible to know which one actually affected surface finish. Engineers searching for solutions to this problem might look for "how to isolate the effect of one factor," "separating cause and effect in experiments," "avoiding mixed-up results from multiple changes," or "factorial experiment design."

Example 1:
We want to compare the effect of
two different coolants on the resulting surface finish from a turning operation.
It is decided to run one lot, change the coolant and then run another lot.
With this sampling scheme, there is no way to distinguish the coolant effect
from the lot effect or from tool wear considerations. There is systematic
sampling error in this sampling scheme.
Example 2:
We wish to examine the effect
of two pre-clean procedures on the uniformity of an oxide growth
process. We clean one cassette of wafers with one method and another
cassette with the other method. We load one cassette in the front of
the furnace tube and
the other cassette in the middle. To complete the run, we fill the rest
of the tube with other lots. With this sampling scheme, there is no way
to distinguish between the effect of the different pre-clean methods and
the cassette effect or the tube location effect. Again, we have
systematic sampling errors.

title: Selecting Sample Sizes
text:
Sample size estimation determines how many measurements or test runs you need to collect to get a reliable answer, using probability equations that connect your desired accuracy to the number of samples required. An engineer might search for this under alternative terms like "how many samples do I need," "determining sample quantity," "sample sufficiency calculation," or "defining sample requirements for testing."

In summary, the steps involved in estimating a sample size are:
There must be a statement about what is expected of the sample.
We must determine what is it we are trying to estimate, how
precise we want the estimate to be, and what are we going to
do with the estimate once we have it. This should
easily be derived from the goals.
We must find some equation that connects the desired precision
of the estimate with the sample size. This is a probability
statement. A couple are given below; see your statistician if
these are not appropriate for your situation.
This equation may contain unknown properties of the population
such as the mean or variance. This is where prior information
can help.
If you are stratifying the population in order to reduce
variation, sample size determination must be performed for
each stratum.
The final sample size should be scrutinized for practicality.
If it is unacceptable, the only way to reduce it is to accept
less precision in the sample estimate.

title: Data Storage and Retrieval
text:
Data integrity and chain-of-custody management address the practical problem of preventing measurement errors, accidental alterations, and data corruption as information passes through multiple automatic systems and storage points—essentially keeping your numbers trustworthy from the moment they're captured until you use them. This concept also covers data validation, error detection, and quality assurance protocols that catch problems in automated data acquisition and retrieval systems. People searching for this might look for terms like "data quality control," "measurement traceability," "preventing data loss in automated systems," "data verification procedures," or "ensuring accuracy through the entire measurement pipeline."

In most larger facilities, however, the people handling the material
usually have nothing to do with the design. Quite often the
measurements are taken automatically and may not even be made in
the same country where the material was produced. Your data go
through a long chain of automatic acquisition, storage, reformatting,
and retrieval before you are ever able to see it. All
of these steps are fraught with peril and should be examined closely to
ensure that valuable data are not lost or accidentally altered.

title: Assign Roles and Responsibilities
text:
This section addresses **role assignment and responsibility allocation** in production process characterization by defining who owns which tools, processes, and measurements to prevent confusion and ensure accountability during experiments. A non-statistician engineer would recognize this as "figuring out who does what" so that experimental work runs smoothly without duplicate effort, missing tasks, or unclear decision-making authority. Someone looking for this concept might search for: "who owns the process," "job responsibilities in manufacturing experiments," "assigning accountability for tools and measurements," "defining roles in process validation," or "organizing the team structure for production testing."

A partial list of these individuals along with their roles and potential
responsibilities is given in the table below. There may be multiple
occurrences of each of these individuals across shifts or process
steps, so be sure to include everyone.
Tool Owner
Controls Tool Operations
S
chedules tool time
Ensures tool state
Advises on experimental design
Process Owner
Controls Process Recipe
Advises on experimental design
Controls recipe settings
Tool Operator
Executes Experimental Plan
Executes experimental runs
May take measurements
Metrology
Own Measurement Tools
Maintains metrology equipment
Conducts gauge studies
May take measurements
CIM
Owns Enterprise Information System
Maintains data collection system
Maintains equipment interfaces and data formatters
Maintains databases and information access
Statistician
Consultant
Consults on experimental design
Consults on data analysis
Quality Control
Controls Material
Ensures quality of incoming material
Must approve shipment of outgoing material (especially for recipe changes)

title: Background and Data
text:
This dataset demonstrates **factorial design** and **analysis of variance (ANOVA)** techniques for evaluating how multiple process factors—in this case production runs and wafer zones—simultaneously affect a critical quality measurement (wafer thickness), allowing engineers to identify which conditions or combinations cause unwanted variation rather than testing factors one at a time. Engineers searching for ways to troubleshoot why their output varies, figure out which machine settings or process steps matter most, or compare performance across different production batches and locations would use these methods. Related searches might include: "how to test multiple factors at once," "comparing results across groups and conditions," "screening which process variables affect my output," or "designed experiments for manufacturing."

title: Response Correlations
text:
A **scatterplot matrix** (also called a **pairwise correlation plot** or **scatter matrix**) visualizes how multiple output measurements relate to each other, helping process engineers spot which product characteristics move together and which work against each other—so you can figure out what's actually driving your yield problems instead of guessing. This technique is also referred to as a **correlation matrix visualization**, **multivariate scatterplot**, or **pairplot**, and engineers often search for it when they need to understand "which outputs are linked together" or "how do my measurements interact with each other."

An example of a scatterplot matrix is given below. In this
semiconductor manufacturing example, three responses, yield (Bin1),
N-channel Id effective (NIDEFF), and P-channel Id effective (PIDEFF)
are plotted against each other in a scatterplot matrix. We can see
that Bin1 is positively correlated with NIDEFF and negatively
correlated with PIDEFF. Also, as expected, NIDEFF is negatively
correlated with PIDEFF. This kind of information will prove to be
useful when we build models for yield improvement.

title: Exploring Main Effects
text:
Main effects analysis is a graphical method for studying how individual process factors (temperature, cassette slot, load lock) independently influence an output variable (particle contamination) by plotting response data separately against each explanatory variable. This helps manufacturing engineers quickly spot which process settings or equipment components are actually causing quality problems like particle defects on wafers, without needing complex statistics. You might also search for this under "factor analysis," "one-way comparison," "response plots," or "investigating which process variable affects quality."

In the example below, we have collected data on the particles added
to a wafer during a particular processing step. We ran a number
of cassettes through the process and sampled wafers from certain
slots in the cassette. We also kept track of which load lock the wafers
passed through. This was done for two different process temperatures. We
measured both small particles (< 2 microns) and large particles
(> 2 microns). We plot the responses (particle counts) against each
of the explanatory variables.

title: Exploring First Order Interactions
text:
This page teaches **first-order interactions** and **conditional main effects** — the core concept that when two factors work together, the impact of changing one factor depends on what level the other factor is at, which means you can't just look at average effects and expect them to tell you how to optimize your process. Engineers solving this problem might search for "why does my factor effect change depending on conditions," "when does changing one variable matter more or less," or "how to interpret results when factors affect each other" — because the practical issue is that a solution working great at one temperature might barely work at another, making simple before-and-after comparisons misleading.

To continue the previous example, given below are nested box plots
of the small and large particles. The load lock is nested within
the two temperature values. There is some evidence of possible
interaction between these two factors. The effect of load lock is
stronger at the lower temperature than at the higher one. This effect
is stronger for the smaller particles than for the larger ones. As
this example illustrates, when you have significant interactions the
main effects must be interpreted conditionally. That is, the main
effects do not tell the whole story by themselves.

title: Fitting Polynomial Models
text:
Polynomial regression models, specifically quadratic and higher-order polynomial fitting, let you map how a product or process output changes across different input conditions—capturing both straight-line trends and curved relationships—which is essential when you need to find the sweet spot for quality or performance. If you're trying to understand which knobs to turn and how much each one matters, or you need to predict what happens between your test points without running endless experiments, you'd search for: response surface modeling, curve fitting, design of experiments analysis, or quadratic regression.

We use polynomial models to estimate and predict the shape of
response values over a range of input parameter values. Polynomial
models are a great tool for determining which input factors drive
responses and in what direction. These are also the most common
models used for analysis of designed experiments. A quadratic
(second-order) polynomial model for two explanatory variables has
the form of the equation below. The single x-terms are called the
main effects. The squared terms are called the quadratic effects
and are used to model curvature in the response surface. The
cross-product terms are used to model interactions between the
explanatory variables.

title: Initial Analysis of Response Variable
text:
This page covers **process capability analysis** and **defect rate estimation using the normal distribution**, which helps manufacturers predict how many parts will fail inspection by comparing actual production data against specification limits. Engineers use this method to verify that a manufacturing process is producing acceptable quality—answering "Are we making good parts or bad parts?"—by calculating the percentage of items that fall outside the acceptable range. Synonyms and related searches include: process capability index (Cpk), specification limit analysis, defect percentage prediction, conformance to specification, out-of-spec rate calculation, and quality control limits.

We summarize the percent defective (i.e., the number of items
outside the specification limits) in the following table.

title: Analysis of Variance
text:
This page covers **variance components analysis** (also called **nested analysis of variance** or **hierarchical ANOVA**), which breaks down where measurement variation comes from in a manufacturing process by separating the effects of different levels like production runs, equipment locations, and random measurement error. If you're trying to figure out whether your process inconsistency is caused by different batches, different positions in your furnace, or just natural measurement noise, this method tells you exactly how much each factor contributes to your total variation. Search terms include: "sources of variation analysis," "mixed effects models," "repeatability and reproducibility," "variance decomposition," or "where does my variation come from."

From the above analysis of variance table, we can compute the
components of variance. Recall that for this data set we have
2 wafers measured at 4 furnace locations for 21 runs. This leads
to the following set of equations.
3072.11 = (4*2)*Var(Run) + 2*Var(Furnace Location) + Var(Within)
571.659 = 2*Var(Furnace Location) + Var(Within)
120.893 = Var(Within)

title: Work This Example Yourself
text:
Production process characterization involves systematically examining and documenting how a manufacturing process actually performs—measuring its consistency, capability, and behavior so engineers can identify bottlenecks, verify the process meets specifications, and make improvements based on data rather than guesswork. Someone searching for this might look for "process capability analysis," "process performance study," "manufacturing process evaluation," "production line assessment," or "process validation and verification."

This page allows you to repeat the analysis outlined in the case study
description on the previous page using
Dataplot
,

. Output from each analysis step below will be displayed

Output window, the Graphics window, the Command History window and the

for executing Dataplot commands. Across the bottom is a command entry
window where commands can be typed in.

title: Analysis of Variance
text:
Analysis of variance (ANOVA) tests whether differences in measurements across multiple factors—like different machines, days, times, and samples—are real or just random variation, helping production engineers figure out which equipment or conditions actually affect their product quality. Someone looking for this might search for "how to tell if machine differences matter," "comparing multiple production conditions," "testing if factor changes actually affect output," or "which variable is causing variation in my process."

We can confirm our interpretation of the box plots by running
an analysis of variance when all four factors are included.

title: Final Conclusions
text:
This excerpt demonstrates **location and scale parameter analysis** (comparing means and variability across groups), which helps manufacturing engineers identify why machines produce different quality outputs and throughput rates by separating problems with centering versus consistency. Someone looking for this concept might search for "comparing machine performance," "why machines give different results," "machine capability analysis," "process centering vs. consistency," or "detecting shifts in production settings."

The analysis shows that machines 1 and 2 had about the same
variablity but significantly different locations. The throughput
for machine 2 was also higher with greater variability than for
machine 1. An interview with the operator revealed that he realized
the second machine was not set correctly. However, he did not want
to change the settings because he knew a study was being conducted
and was afraid he might impact the results by making changes.
Machine 3 had significantly more variation and lower throughput.
The operator indicated that the machine had to be taken down
several times for minor repairs. Given the preceeding analysis
results, the team recommended replacing machine 3.

title: Work This Example Yourself
text:
Production Process Characterization uses descriptive statistics and exploratory data analysis to help engineers understand how their manufacturing processes actually perform by examining real output data across multiple analysis steps. This solves the practical problem of figuring out whether your process is running consistently, where problems are occurring, and how well you're meeting specifications—without needing to understand complex statistical theory. Someone might search for this using terms like "process analysis," "manufacturing performance evaluation," "understanding process variation," or "how to check if my process is working right."

This page allows you to repeat the analysis outlined in the case study
description on the previous page using
Dataplot
,
if you have
downloaded and
installed it
. Output from each analysis step below will be displayed
in one or more of the Dataplot windows. The four main windows are the
Output window, the Graphics window, the Command History window and the
Data Sheet window. Across the top of the main windows there are menus
for executing Dataplot commands. Across the bottom is a command entry
