title: Check for Batch Effect
text:
This page covers **linear regression diagnostics** (correlation coefficient, intercept, slope, and residual standard deviation) applied across multiple batches to detect whether measurement differences between batches are real or just noise in your process. It solves the practical problem of figuring out whether your lab and field measurements are giving you consistent, reliable results across different production runs—or if some batches are systematically off. You might search for this using terms like "batch consistency check," "comparing results across production runs," "detecting measurement drift between batches," or "validation across multiple lots."

We can follow up the conditional plot with
a
linear
correlation plot
,
a
linear
intercept plot
,
a
linear
slope plot
, and
a
linear
residual standard deviation plot
.
These four plots show the correlation, the intercept and
slope from a linear fit, and the residual standard deviation
for linear fits applied to each batch. These plots show
how a linear fit performs across the six batches.
The linear correlation plot (upper left), which shows the
correlation between field and lab defect sizes versus the
batch, indicates that batch six has a somewhat stronger
linear relationship between the measurements than the
other batches do. This is also reflected in the significantly
lower residual standard deviation for batch six shown in the
residual standard deviation plot (lower right), which shows the
residual standard deviation versus batch. The slopes all lie
within a range of 0.6 to 0.9 in the linear slope plot (lower left)
and the intercepts all lie between 2 and 8 in the linear
intercept plot (upper right).

title: Initial Linear Fit
text:
This section covers **homogeneity of variances** (also called **homoscedasticity**) in regression diagnostics, addressing the practical problem that prediction accuracy gets worse at certain operating conditions because measurement scatter isn't consistent across your range of lab values. Engineers looking for solutions might search for "unequal error spread," "non-constant variance," "changing scatter across the range," or "why my model predicts better at some conditions than others."

When there is a single independent variable, the
6-plot
provides a convenient method for initial model validation.
The basic assumptions for regression models are that
the errors are random observations from a normal
distribution with mean of zero and constant standard
deviation (or variance).
The plots on the first row show that the residuals have
increasing variance as the value of the independent
variable (lab) increases in value. This indicates that
the assumption of constant standard deviation, or
homogeneity of variances, is violated.
In order to see this more clearly, we will generate full-
size plots of the predicted values with the data and
the residuals against the independent variable.

title: Transformations to Improve Fit and Equalize Variances
text:
The **Box-Cox linearity plot** is a tool for finding the best power transformation (like log or square root) to straighten out curved relationships in your data so you can use simpler linear models instead of complex nonlinear ones. Engineers often use common transformations like natural log or square root for simplicity rather than the mathematically optimal value, and the Box-Cox plot helps verify that your practical choice is still reasonable. People searching for this might look for "transformation to linearize data," "fixing curved data relationships," "power transformation methods," or "how to make nonlinear data linear."

The previous step can be approached more formally
by the use of the
Box-Cox
linearity
plot. The
α
value on the
x
axis corresponding to
the maximum correlation value on the
y
axis indicates the
power transformation that yields the most linear
fit.
This plot indicates that a value of -0.1 achieves the
most linear fit.
In practice, for ease of interpretation, we often prefer to
use a common transformation, such as the ln or square root,
rather than the value that yields the mathematical maximum.
However, the Box-Cox linearity plot still indicates whether
our choice is a reasonable one. That is, we might sacrifice a
small amount of linearity in the fit to have a simpler model.
In this case, a value of 0.0 would indicate a ln
transformation. Although the optimal value from the plot is
-0.1, the plot indicates that any value between -0.2 and
0.2 will yield fairly similar results. For that reason,
we choose to stick with the common ln transformation.

title: Weighting to Improve Fit
text:
This excerpt demonstrates **weighted least squares regression** and **variance stabilization** using a **power function model** to handle unequal variances (heteroscedasticity) across data groups. The practical problem is fixing situations where some measurements are naturally more noisy or variable than others, so you can weight the reliable data points more heavily and less-reliable points less heavily to get better predictions. Someone looking for this concept might search for "how to fix unequal variance in regression," "making data more consistent for curve fitting," "stabilizing variance across different measurement ranges," or "dealing with data that gets noisier at higher values."

For the pipeline data, we chose
approximate replicate groups
so that each group has four observations (the last
group only has three). This was done by first
sorting the data by the predictor variable and then
taking four points in succession to form each
replicate group.
Using the
power function model
with the data for estimating the weights,
the following results for the fit
of ln(variances) against ln(means) for the replicate
groups were generated.

against the replicate means shows that a linear
fit provides a reasonable fit with an estimated

We used an estimate of 1.5 for the exponent in the
weighting function.

title: Compare the Fits
text:
This section covers **model transformation and weighted regression** techniques for improving fit quality across different ranges of data values. When you have measurements that don't follow a straight line or some predictions are way off while others are close, you can either transform your data mathematically or adjust how much importance each data point gets in the calculation to make your model work better across the whole range. People also search for this as "fixing bad predictions," "balancing model accuracy," "handling non-linear data," "curve fitting improvements," or "adjusting for outliers."

This plot shows that, compared to the original fit, the
transformed and weighted fits generate smaller predicted
values for low values of lab defect size and larger predicted
values for high values of lab defect size. The three fits
match fairly closely for intermediate values of lab
defect size. The transformed and weighted fit tend to
agree for the low values of lab defect size. However,
for large values of lab defect size, the weighted fit
tends to generate higher values for the predicted values
than does the transformed fit.

text:
This section covers **logarithmic transformations, Box-Cox transformations, and homogeneity of variance** to fix situations where your measurement data has uneven scatter or doesn't follow a straight-line relationship with your inputs—basically, when a simple model doesn't fit your data well because the noise gets bigger at higher values or the relationship is curved instead of linear. You might also search for this using terms like "stabilizing variance," "variance stabilization," "linearizing relationships," "making data more normal," "fixing non-constant error spread," or "transformation methods for improving model fit."

Someone might search for this concept using terms like "fitting a line to data," "understanding cause-and-effect relationships between two measurements," "predicting one value from another," or "validating measurement systems."

title: Initial Non-Linear Fit
text:
Nonlinear regression fitting requires choosing starting parameter values to avoid getting stuck at local optima instead of finding the best overall solution, a problem called local convergence that can be mitigated using prior data or educated guesses from the model's shape. Engineers troubleshooting why their curve-fitting software gives bad results, or trying to avoid "wrong answers that seem mathematically correct," should search for terms like initial parameter selection, starting values for nonlinear models, local minimum problems, or convergence sensitivity to understand why their first attempt might fail and how to pick better starting numbers.

Nonlinear models are fit with iterative methods that
require starting values. In some cases, inappropriate
starting values can result in parameter estimates
for the fit that converge to a local minimum or maximum
rather than the global minimum or maximum.
Some models are relatively insensitive to the choice
of starting values while others are extremely
sensitive.
If you have prior data sets that fit similar models,
these can often be used as a guide for determining
good starting values. We can also sometimes make
educated guesses from the functional form of the
model. For some models, there may be specific
methods for determining starting values. For
example, sinusoidal models that are commonly used
in time series are quite sensitive to good starting
values. The
beam deflection
case study
shows an example of obtaining starting
values for a sinusoidal model.
In the case where you do not know what good starting
values would be, one approach is to create a grid of
values for each of the parameters of the model and
compute some measure of goodness of fit, such as the
residual standard deviation, at each point on the grid.
The idea is to create a broad grid that encloses
reasonable values for the parameter. However, we
typically want to keep the number of grid points for
each parameter relatively small to keep the
computational burden down (particularly as the number
of parameters in the model increases). The idea is
to get in the right neighborhood, not to find the
optimal fit. We would pick the grid point that
corresponds to the smallest residual standard
deviation as the starting values.

title: Transformations to Improve Fit
text:
This page covers **homogeneity of variance** (also called **homoscedasticity**) and **variance-stabilizing transformations**, which solve the practical problem of making measurement variability consistent across different operating conditions—so you get reliable results whether your process is running at low or high settings. When your data shows that some conditions produce tight, consistent measurements while others are scattered and unpredictable, applying transformations like square root, logarithm, or reciprocal can "level the playing field" so your statistical tests work properly. People searching for this concept might also look for "constant variance," "unequal spread," "how to fix fanning pattern," "stabilize noise," or "make variability uniform across the range."

The first step is to try transformations of the
response variable that will result in homogeneous
variances. In practice, the square root, ln, and
reciprocal transformations often work well for
this purpose. We will try these first.
In examining these four plots, we are looking for the plot
that shows the most constant variability of the ultrasonic
response across values of metal distance. Although the scales
of these plots differ widely, which would seem to make comparisons
difficult, we are not comparing the absolute levesl of variability
between plots here. Instead we are comparing only how constant the
variation within each plot is for these four plots. The plot with
the most constant variation will indicate which transformation is best.
Based on constancy of the variation in the residuals, the square root
transformation is probably the best tranformation to use for this data.

title: Weighting to Improve Fit
text:
This section covers **variance stabilization** and **weighted least squares regression**, which solve the practical problem of unequal measurement variability across different operating conditions—when some test conditions naturally produce more scattered results than others, standard regression gives too much weight to the noisy data and produces misleading predictions. You might search for this concept using terms like "fixing non-constant error spread," "stabilizing scatter in data," "accounting for measurement noise that changes," "weighting data points by reliability," or "dealing with data that gets messier at certain ranges."

The following results were obtained for the fit
of ln(variances) against ln(means) for the replicate
groups.

title: Compare the Fits
text:
When fitting models to data, **residual standard deviation (RESSD)** and **goodness-of-fit comparison** help engineers decide which fitting method produces the most accurate predictions by measuring how far the model's guesses are from actual measurements. Instead of just looking at plots, comparing the prediction error numbers across different fitting approaches—whether unweighted, weighted, or using transformed data—lets you pick the method that makes the smallest mistakes. You might also search for this concept as "model accuracy comparison," "fitting error metrics," "prediction residuals," or "comparing different regression methods."

The first step in comparing the fits is to plot all three
sets of predicted values (in the original units) on the same
plot with the raw data.
This plot shows that all three fits generate comparable
predicted values. We can also compare the residual
standard deviations (RESSD) from the fits. The RESSD
for the transformed data is calculated after translating the
predicted values back to the original scale.
RESSD From Unweighted Fit = 3.361673
RESSD From Transformed Fit = 3.306732
RESSD From Weighted Fit = 3.392797
In this case, the RESSD is quite close for all three fits
(which is to be expected based on the plot).

title: Work This Example Yourself
text:
When your measurement data shows unequal scatter or spread at different levels (heteroscedasticity), a **square root transformation** on the response variable can stabilize the variance and make your predictions more reliable across the full range of operating conditions. This technique—also called **variance-stabilizing transformation** or **power transformation**—fixes the common problem where some parts of your data are noisier than others, which you might search for as "fixing unequal error spread," "making variance constant," or "eliminating fanning patterns in residuals."

1. The plots indicate that a square
root transformation on the dependent
variable (ultrasonic response) is a
good candidate model.
2. The plots indicate that no
transformation on the predictor
variable (metal distance) is
a good candidate model.
3. Carry out the fit on the transformed
data. The plot of the predicted
values overlaid with the data
indicates a good fit.
4. The 6-plot suggests that the model
assumptions, specifically homogeneous
variances for the errors, are
satisfied.
5. The detailed residual plot shows
more clearly that the homogeneous
variances assumption is now
satisfied.

title: Rational Function Models
text:
Rational function models use ratios of polynomials to fit data curves that have complicated shapes and behaviors, solving the practical problem of predicting system outputs when simple straight lines or curved polynomials don't bend and twist the right way to match real-world measurements. This approach produces smoother, less wiggly predictions than polynomial fitting alone, making it useful for engineers who need accurate forecasts without the overfitting problems that plague higher-degree polynomial models. Search synonyms: rational regression, ratio of polynomials, fractional polynomial models, non-polynomial curve fitting, Padé approximation.

Rational function models have the following advantages.
Rational function models have a moderately simple
form.
Rational function models are a closed family. As with
polynomial models, this means that rational function
models are not dependent on the underlying metric.
Rational function models can take on an extremely
wide range of shapes, accommodating a much wider range of
shapes than does the polynomial family.
Rational function models have better interpolatory
properties than polynomial models. Rational functions
are typically smoother and less oscillatory than
polynomial models.
Rational functions have excellent extrapolatory
powers. Rational functions can typically be tailored
to model the function not only within the domain of
the data, but also so as to be in agreement with
theoretical/asymptotic behavior outside the domain of
interest.
Rational function models have excellent asymptotic
properties. Rational functions can be either finite
or infinite for finite values, or finite or infinite
for infinite \(x\)
values. Thus, rational functions can easily be
incorporated into a rational function model.
Rational function models can often be used to model
complicated structure with a fairly low degree in both
the numerator and denominator. This in turn means
that fewer coefficients will be required compared to
the polynomial model.
Rational function models are moderately easy to handle
computationally. Although they are nonlinear models,
rational function models are a particularly easy
nonlinear models to fit.

title: Quadratic/Quadratic Rational Function Model
text:
This page demonstrates **nonlinear regression** and **rational function modeling** to fit curved relationships where the response variable depends on predictors in a ratio or fraction form, solving the practical engineering problem of capturing complex curved behavior in data that can't be described by simple straight-line or polynomial equations. Engineers and scientists searching for ways to model "curved data that doesn't fit a line," "fitting equations with variables in the denominator," or "ratio-based prediction models" would find this method useful for applications like chemical reaction rates, mechanical system responses, or other processes that follow fractional or rational patterns.

title: Cubic/Cubic Rational Function Model
text:
This page demonstrates **nonlinear regression** and **rational function modeling** to fit curved relationships in experimental data when simpler models fail. An engineer measuring how thermal expansion changes with temperature can use this cubic/cubic rational function to capture complex, non-linear behavior that a straight line or basic polynomial can't handle. Someone might search for this using terms like "fitting curved data," "nonlinear curve fitting," "rational polynomial models," "ratio of polynomials," or "piecewise behavior in data fitting."

Since the Q/Q model did not describe the data well, we
next fit a cubic/cubic (C/C) rational function
model.

title: Work This Example Yourself
text:
Process modeling through regression and curve fitting helps engineers build mathematical equations that describe how a system behaves based on measured data, so they can predict outputs for new inputs without running expensive experiments. This technique—also called fitting a model to data, finding the relationship between variables, or developing an empirical equation—lets you understand whether your process follows a straight line, polynomial curve, or more complex pattern.

This page allows you to repeat the analysis outlined in the case study
description on the previous page using
Dataplot
,
if you have
downloaded and
installed it
. Output from each
analysis step below will be displayed in one or more of the Dataplot windows.
The four main windows are the Output window, the Graphics window, the
Command History window and the Data Sheet window. Across the top of the main
windows there are menus for executing Dataplot commands. Across the bottom
is a command entry window where commands can be typed in.

title: Polynomial Functions
text:
Polynomial regression models can produce unreliable predictions outside your measured data range and create wild swings between data points, so engineers should use them only when fitting curves within known boundaries and consider alternatives like splines or exponential models for data that extends beyond your observations. Related searches: curve fitting problems, overfitting oscillations, model extrapolation failure, high-order polynomial dangers, data range limitations, prediction reliability outside measured range.

However, polynomial models also have the following limitations.
Polynomial models have poor interpolatory properties.
High degree polynomials are notorious for oscillations
between exact-fit values.
Polynomial models have poor extrapolatory properties.
Polynomials may provide good fits within the range of
data, but they will frequently deteriorate rapidly outside
the range of the data.
Polynomial models have poor asymptotic properties. By
their nature, polynomials have a finite response for finite \(x\)
values and have an infinite response if and only if the \(x\)
value is infinite. Thus polynomials may not model
asympototic phenomena very well.
Polynomial models have a shape/degree tradeoff. In order
to model data with a complicated structure, the degree of
the model must be high, indicating and the associated
number of parameters to be estimated will also be high.
This can result in highly unstable models.

title: Rational Functions
text:
Rational function models are mathematical curves created by dividing one polynomial by another, which engineers use when they need a smooth fitting curve that won't wiggle excessively between data points and can match a wider variety of real-world shapes than simple polynomial curves alone. If you're looking for this concept, you might search for "ratio of polynomials," "fraction-based curve fitting," "smooth interpolation without oscillation," or "flexible functional forms for process data."

Rational function models have the following advantages.
Rational function models have a moderately simple
form.
Rational function models are a closed family. As with
polynomial models, this means that rational function
models are not dependent on the underlying metric.
Rational function models can take on an extremely
wide range of shapes, accommodating a much wider range of
shapes than does the polynomial family.
Rational function models have better interpolatory
properties than polynomial models. Rational functions
are typically smoother and less oscillatory than
polynomial models.
Rational functions have excellent extrapolatory
powers. Rational functions can typically be tailored
to model the function not only within the domain of
the data, but also so as to be in agreement with
theoretical/asymptotic behavior outside the domain of
interest.
Rational function models have excellent asymptotic
properties. Rational functions can be either finite
or infinite for finite values, or finite or infinite
for infinite \(x\)
values. Thus, rational functions
can easily be incorporated into a rational function
model.
Rational function models can often be used to model
complicated structure with a fairly low degree in both
the numerator and denominator. This in turn means
that fewer coefficients will be required compared to
the polynomial model.
Rational function models are moderately easy to handle
computationally. Although they are nonlinear models,
rational function models are a particularly easy
nonlinear models to fit.

title: 6-Plot
text:
The 6-Plot technique visualizes residual diagnostics to verify that model errors are random, independent, normally distributed, and have constant variance—essentially checking whether your fit function actually captured the real relationship in your data or if it's missing something systematic. If residuals show patterns, trends, or unequal spread instead of random scatter, it means your model is incomplete or wrong. You might also search for this as "residual analysis," "error checking," "model validation," "goodness of fit assessment," or "assumption checking."

A model involving a response variable and a single independent
variable has the form:
\[ Y_{i} = f(X_{i}) + E_{i} \]
where
Y
is the response variable,
X
is the independent
variable,
f
is the linear or non-linear fit function, and
E
is the random component. For a good model, the
error component should behave like:
random drawings (i.e., independent);
from a fixed distribution;
with fixed location; and
with fixed variation.
In addition, for fitting models it is usually further assumed
that the fixed distribution is normal and the fixed location
is zero. For a good model the fixed variation
should be as small as possible. A necessary component
of fitting models is to verify these assumptions for the
error component and to assess whether the variation for
the error component is sufficiently small. The histogram,
lag plot, and normal probability plot are used to
verify the fixed distribution, location, and variation
assumptions on the error component. The plot
of the response variable and the predicted values versus
the independent variable is used to assess whether the
variation is sufficiently small. The plots of the
residuals versus the independent variable and the predicted
values is used to assess the independence assumption.
Assessing the validity and quality of the fit in terms of
the above assumptions is an absolutely vital part of
the model-fitting process. No fit should be considered
complete without an adequate model validation step.

title: Latin square and related designs
text:
Latin square designs and related blocking designs help experimenters test multiple potential sources of interference (called nuisance factors or blocking variables) without needing to run hundreds of experiments by organizing treatments in a grid pattern where each factor appears once in each row and column. If you're trying to compare treatments while controlling for things like different operators, machines, or time periods that might affect your results, you might search for "blocking designs," "factorial experiments with constraints," "designs that reduce experimental runs," or "controlling for multiple variables simultaneously" without realizing you need a Latin square. The tradeoff is that this method only works when your nuisance factors have the same number of levels as your main treatment comparison and assumes these factors don't interact with each other.

The advantages of Latin square designs are:
They handle the case when we have several nuisance factors and
we either cannot combine them into a single factor or we
wish to keep them separate.
They allow experiments with a relatively small number
of runs.
The disadvantages are:
The number of levels of each blocking variable must
equal the number of levels of the treatment factor.
The Latin square model assumes that there are no interactions
between the blocking variables or between the treatment variable
and the blocking variable.
Note that Latin square designs are equivalent to specific fractional
factorial designs (e.g., the 4x4 Latin square design is equivalent to a
4
3-1
fractional factorial
design).

title: Graeco-Latin square designs
text:
A Graeco-Latin square design is a blocked experimental method that lets you test multiple factors while controlling for two separate sources of unwanted variation (like different operators and different machines running at the same time). Instead of running hundreds of expensive tests, this design cuts your experiment down to just 25 runs while still letting you see which factors actually matter. You might also search for this using terms like "Latin square blocking," "factorial design with two nuisance factors," "orthogonal blocking for multiple factors," or "confounding control in experiments."

title: Hyper-Graeco-Latin square designs
text:
A Hyper-Graeco-Latin square design is an orthogonal experimental layout that lets you test multiple factors while blocking out the effects of nuisance variables, solving the real-world problem of running controlled experiments when you have several sources of unwanted variation (like different machines, operators, or time periods) that you need to account for without inflating your test size. If you're looking for ways to design an experiment with multiple blocking factors and treatment factors, or you need terms like "orthogonal Latin squares," "multi-factor blocking design," or "efficient factorial experiment with controls," this is the method that lets you squeeze maximum information from a manageable number of test runs.

title: Two-level full factorial designs
text:
A **two-level full factorial design** (also called a **2^k factorial experiment**) is a systematic method for testing how multiple factors—like pressure, speed, and force—affect a product when each factor is set to just a high or low value, allowing engineers to quickly figure out which settings matter most and how factors work together to influence the final result. Someone needing this approach might search for "testing multiple variables at once," "how to find which factors affect quality," "design of experiments for optimization," "screening test design," or "interaction effects between settings."

the greatest effect on product uniformity. Two replications were run at
each setting. A (full factorial) 2

calls for 8*2=16 runs.

title: Full factorial example
text:
**Randomization in factorial design** prevents hidden variables like temperature from sneaking into your results and making you draw wrong conclusions about which factors actually matter—basically, by mixing up the order you run experiments instead of doing them in a set pattern, you stop time-of-day or environmental changes from masking the true effects. Someone looking for this concept might search for "experimental design randomization," "how to avoid confounding variables," "blocking and randomization," or "why experiment order matters."

The more freely one can randomize experimental runs, the more insurance
one has against extraneous factors possibly affecting the results, and
hence perhaps wasting our experimental time and effort. For example,
consider the `Depth' column: the settings of Depth, in standard order,
follow a `four low, four high, four low, four high' pattern.
Suppose now that four settings are run in the day and four at night,
and that (unknown to the experimenter) ambient temperature in the
polishing shop affects Yield. We would run the experiment over two days
and two nights and conclude that Depth influenced Yield, when in fact
ambient temperature was the significant influence. So the moral is:
Randomize experimental runs as much as possible.

title: Blocking of full factorial designs
text:
Blocking of full factorial designs is a method for running 2-level factorial experiments while dividing them into separate groups to account for known disruptions or changes that occur during testing, such as switching to a new batch of materials halfway through—it lets you isolate the effects of what you're actually testing from the noise caused by predictable shifts in conditions. This solves the practical problem of experiments being compromised by unavoidable real-world interruptions: instead of throwing out data or accepting bad results, you structure your test plan to handle the disruption mathematically. Search for this concept using phrases like "handling disruptions in experiments," "isolating test effects from process changes," "accounting for batch changes in factorial tests," "dividing experiments into groups," or "managing nuisance factors in full factorial designs."

We often need to eliminate the influence of extraneous factors
when running an experiment. We do this by "blocking".
Previously, blocking was introduced when
randomized
block designs
were discussed. There we were concerned with one
factor in the presence of one of more nuisance factors. In this section
we look at a general approach that enables us to divide 2-level
factorial experiments into blocks.
For example, assume we anticipate predictable shifts will occur while
an experiment is being run. This might happen when one has to change to
a new batch of raw materials halfway through the experiment. The effect
of the change in raw materials is well known, and we want to eliminate
its influence on the subsequent data analysis.

title: A 23-1design (half of a 23)
text:
A **2³ factorial design** (also called a **half-factorial design** or **screening design**) lets you quickly test which of three process settings actually matter for your product quality by running the minimum number of experiments instead of trying every possible combination. This solves the real-world problem of figuring out what knobs to turn on your equipment without wasting time and materials running hundreds of test batches. People also search for this using terms like "factor screening," "main effects design," "minimum experiments needed," or "how to test multiple machine settings efficiently."

Example
: An engineering experiment calls for running three
factors, namely Pressure, Table speed, and Down force, each at a
'high' and a 'low' setting, on a production tool to determine
which has the greatest effect on product uniformity. Interaction
effects are considered negligible, but uniformity measurement error
requires that at least two separate runs (replications) be made at
each process setting. In addition, several 'standard setting' runs
(centerpoint runs) need to be made at regular intervals during the
experiment to monitor for process drift. As experimental time and
material are limited, no more than 15 runs can be planned.
A full factorial 2
3
design, replicated twice, calls for 8x2
= 16 runs, even without centerpoint runs, so this is not an option.
However a 2
3-1
design replicated twice requires only
4x2 = 8 runs, and then we would have 15-8 = 7 spare runs: 3 to 5 of
these spare runs can be used for centerpoint runs and the rest saved
for backup in case something goes wrong with any run. As long as we
are confident that the interactions are negligbly small (compared to
the main effects), and as long as complete replication is required,
then the above replicated 2
3-1
fractional factorial
design (with center points) is a very reasonable choice.
On the other hand, if interactions are potentially large (and if the
replication required could be set aside), then the usual 2
3
full factorial design (with center points) would serve as a good
design.

title: Constructing the 23-1half-fraction design
text:
A 2³⁻¹ fractional factorial design is a method that cuts your experimental runs in half by cleverly selecting which factor combinations to test, letting you estimate main effects with only four observations instead of eight. This solves the practical problem of reducing testing time and cost when you have three factors to investigate but can't afford to run every possible combination. You might also search for this concept using terms like "half-fraction design," "screening experiment," "reduced factorial," or "fractional replication."

This design gives the light-shaded corners of the box of
Figure 3.4.
Both 2
3-1
designs that we have generated are equally good, and both save half
the number of runs over the original 2
3

estimates
of the main
effects for the factors

from "low" to "high" for an effect), then the precision of the estimates

are
not quite as good as for the full 8-run factorial because we only have
four observations to construct the averages instead of eight; this is one
price we have to pay for using fewer runs.

title: Confounding (also called aliasing)
text:
**Defining relations and design generators** in fractional factorial experiments (2^(3-1) designs) let you figure out which experimental factors are mixed together so you can't tell their individual effects apart—solving the practical problem of running fewer tests while understanding which results you can actually trust. If you're looking for this concept, you might search for "confounding pattern," "aliasing in experiments," "factorial design aliases," or "what effects are tangled together in my screening study."

I=123 is called a
design generator
or a
generating relation
for this 2
3-1
design (the
dark-shaded corners of Figure 3.4). Since there is only one design
generator for this design, it is also the
defining relation
for
the design. Equally, I=-123 is the design generator (and defining
relation) for the light-shaded corners of Figure 3.4.
We call
I=123 the defining relation for the 2
3-1
design because with
it we can generate (by "multiplication") the complete confounding
pattern for the design
. That is, given I=123, we can generate the
set of {1=23, 2=13, 3=12, I=123}, which is the complete set of
aliases
, as they are called, for this 2
3-1
fractional
factorial design. With I=123, we can easily generate all the columns
of the half-fraction design 2
3-1
.

title: Fractional factorial design specifications and design resolution
text:
This page covers **fractional factorial design**, **design generators**, **defining relations**, and **design resolution**, which help engineers run fewer experiments while still understanding how multiple factors affect a product or process by strategically choosing which factor combinations to test instead of testing every possible combination. Someone looking for this concept might search for "how to reduce number of experiments needed," "confounded interactions," "screening design," "two-factor interactions," or "efficient experimental design with multiple variables."

Note
: There are other
\( 2_{V}^{8-3} \) fractional designs that can be derived starting with
different choices of design generators for the "6", "7" and "8" factor
columns. However, they are either equivalent (in terms of the number of
words of length of length of four) to the fraction with generators 6 = 345,
7 = 1245, 8 = 1235 (obtained by relabeling the factors), or they are
inferior to the fraction given because their defining relation contains
more words of length four (and therefore more confounded two-factor
interactions). For example, the
\( 2_{V}^{8-3} \) design with generators 6 = 12345, 7 = 135, and 8 = 245
has five length-four words in the defining relation (the defining relation
is I = 123456 = 1357 = 2458 = 2467 = 1368 = 123478 = 5678). As a result,
this design would confound more two factor-interactions (23 out of 28
possible two-factor interactions are confounded, leaving only "12", "14",
"23", "27" and "34" as estimable two-factor interactions).

title: Use of fractional factorial designs
text:
Fractional factorial designs allow engineers to test multiple variables at once using fewer experiments than a full factorial design, cutting testing time and costs while still identifying which factors actually matter. This solves the practical problem of screening out the few important process inputs from many less important ones without running prohibitively expensive full test matrices. Someone looking for this might search for: "how to test many variables cheaply," "screening design," "reduced factorial experiment," "efficient multi-factor testing," or "partial factorial DOE."

The basic purpose of a fractional factorial design is to economically
investigate cause-and-effect relationships of significance in a given
experimental setting. This does not differ in essence from the purpose
of any experimental design. However, because we are able to choose
fractions of a full design, and hence be more economical, we also have
to be aware that different factorial designs serve different purposes.
Broadly speaking, with designs of resolution three, and sometimes four,
we seek to screen out the few important main effects from the many
less important others. For this reason, these designs are often
termed main effects designs, or screening designs.
On the other hand, designs of resolution five, and higher, are used
for focusing on more than just main effects in an experimental situation.
These designs allow us to estimate interaction effects and such designs
are easily augmented to complete a second-order design - a design that
permits estimation of a full second-order (quadratic) model.

title: Summary tables of useful fractional factorial designs
text:
TABLE 3.17 provides fractional factorial designs using 2^(k-p) notation, which solves the practical problem of running fewer experiments while still identifying which factors and factor combinations affect your process output—saving time and cost when you can't afford to test every possible combination. This concept is also known as running a "fraction of a full factorial experiment," "screening design," "reduced experimental plan," or "partial factorial study" by engineers who need to test many variables without the expense of complete testing.

TABLE 3.17 catalogs these useful fractional factorial designs using
the notation previously described in
FIGURE
3.7.
Clicking on the
\( 2_{R}^{k-p} \)
specification for a given design provides details (courtesy of Dataplot
files) of the design generators, the defining relation, the confounding
structure (as far as main effects and two-level interactions are
concerned), and the design matrix.
The notation used follows our
previous labeling of factors with numbers, not letters.

title: Central Composite Designs (CCD)
text:
Central Composite Designs (CCC) are a type of response surface methodology that lets you map out how multiple process inputs affect your output by testing factor combinations at different levels, including extreme points, without requiring you to test every possible combination. Engineers use CCC when they need to find optimal settings for a process or product and want to understand how factors interact with each other while keeping the number of experiments manageable. You might also search for this concept as "circumscribed composite design," "star point design," "multi-factor optimization testing," or "response surface experiment planning."

TABLE 3.22: Central Composite Designs
Central Composite
Design Type
Terminology
Comments
Circumscribed
CCC
CCC designs are the original form of the central composite design.
The star points are at some distance
from
the center based on the properties desired for the design and the number
of factors in the design. The star points establish new extremes
for the low and high settings for all factors. Figure 5 illustrates
a CCC design. These designs have circular, spherical, or hyperspherical
symmetry and require 5 levels for each factor. Augmenting an existing
factorial or resolution V fractional factorial design with star points
can produce this design.
Inscribed
CCI
For those situations in which the limits specified for factor settings
are truly limits, the CCI design uses the factor settings as the star
points and creates a factorial or fractional factorial design within
those limits (in other words, a CCI design is a scaled down CCC design
with each factor level of the CCC design divided by
\( \alpha \) to generate the CCI design). This design also requires
5 levels of each factor.
Face Centered
CCF
In this design the star points are at the center of each face of the
factorial space, so
\( \alpha \) = ± 1. This variety requires 3 levels of each factor.
Augmenting an existing factorial or resolution V design with appropriate
star points can also produce this design.

title: Box-Behnken designs
text:
A Box-Behnken design is a response surface methodology that lets you test how multiple process factors work together using just three settings per factor, with test points placed at the edges and center of your operating space—solving the problem of mapping out how your outputs respond to input changes without needing as many expensive experiments as a full factorial design would require. If you're hunting for this concept, you might search for "three-level experimental design," "quadratic design for optimization," "edge-center design," or "alternative to central composite design."

The Box-Behnken design is an independent quadratic design in that it
does not contain an embedded factorial or fractional factorial design.
In this design the treatment combinations are at the midpoints of
edges of the process space and at the center. These designs are
rotatable (or near rotatable) and require 3 levels of each factor.
The designs have limited capability for orthogonal blocking compared
to the central composite designs.
Figure 3.22 illustrates a Box-Behnken design for three factors.

title: Comparisons of response surface designs
text:
Central Composite Design (CCC) enables engineers to build accurate prediction models for how process outputs respond to multiple input factors by combining factorial experiments with additional test points, solving the practical problem of needing to predict product quality or performance across the full operating range without running excessive expensive tests. If you're searching for this concept, you might look for "quadratic response surface," "second-order design," "building a mathematical model of a process," "optimization design," or "fitting a curved relationship between inputs and outputs."

Table 3.27 summarizes properties of the classical quadratic designs.
Use this table for broad guidelines when attempting to choose from
among available designs.
TABLE 3.27: Summary of Properties of Classical Response
Surface Designs
Design Type
Comment
CCC
CCC designs provide high quality predictions over the entire
design space, but require factor settings outside the range
of the factors in the factorial part.
Note:
When the
possibility of running a CCC design is recognized before starting
a factorial experiment, factor spacings can be reduced to ensure
that ±
for each
coded factor corresponds to feasible (reasonable) levels.
Requires 5 levels for each factor.
CCI
CCI designs use only points within the factor ranges originally
specified, but do not provide the same high quality prediction
over the entire space compared to the CCC.
Requires 5 levels of each factor.
CCF
CCF designs provide relatively high quality predictions over the
entire design space and do not require using points outside the
original factor range. However, they give poor precision for
estimating pure quadratic coefficients.
Requires 3 levels for each factor.
Box-Behnken
These designs require fewer treatment combinations than a central
composite design in cases involving 3 or 4 factors.
The Box-Behnken design is rotatable (or nearly so) but it contains
regions of poor prediction quality like the CCI. Its "missing
corners" may be useful when the experimenter should avoid
combined factor extremes. This property prevents a potential
loss of data in those cases.
Requires 3 levels for each factor.

title: Blocking a response surface design
text:
This page explains **central composite designs (CCD) with blocking** for five-factor response surface methodology, which solves the practical problem of running expensive or time-consuming experiments across multiple days or batches while keeping results comparable and free from day-to-day variation that could confuse your findings. Engineers and technicians looking for how to split a designed experiment into manageable chunks without wasting data should search for: "blocking in response surface designs," "running experiments in batches," "accounting for day-to-day differences in DOE," or "split-plot factorial experiments."

title: Mirror-Image foldover designs
chapter: link to the fractional design Table
text:
This page explains **alias structure** and **confounding** in **fractional factorial designs**, which solves the practical problem of figuring out which experimental effects get mixed together when you run fewer tests than a full experiment—so you know which results you can trust versus which ones might be hiding the true cause. Someone might search for this using terms like "confounded factors," "mixed-up effects," "which results are reliable in a small experiment," "factor aliasing," "how to interpret partial factorial designs," or "what does my data really tell me when I skip test combinations."

Using this defining relation, we can easily compute the alias structure
for the complete design, as shown previously in the
link to the fractional design Table
given
earlier
. For example, to figure out which
effects are aliased (confounded) with factor
X
1
we
multiply the defining relation by 1 to obtain:
1 = 24 = 35 = 1236 = 1347 = 1257 = 67 = 1456 = 237 = 12345 = 346
= 256 = 457 = 12467 = 13567 = 234567
In order to simplify matters, let us ignore all interactions with 3 or
more factors; we then have the following 2-factor alias pattern for
X
1

title: Alternative foldover designs
text:
This section covers **resolution III/IV factorial designs, foldover designs, and semifolding** — techniques for identifying which factors actually interact with each other when you've already run an initial screening experiment. When your first round of tests gives you good measurement precision but leaves you unsure about which factor pairs affect your output together, you can run a second, smaller batch of confirmation tests by flipping one factor's settings to separate out those interaction effects without needing a complete new experiment. Engineers might search for this using terms like "follow-up factorial experiments," "resolving confounded interactions," "factor aliasing," "two-factor interaction screening," or "efficient confirmation testing."

Reversing a single factor column to obtain de-aliased two-factor
interactions for that one factor works for any resolution III or IV
design. When used to follow-up a resolution IV design, there are
relatively few new effects to be estimated (as compared to
\( 2_{III}^{k-p} \) designs). When the original resolution IV fraction
provides sufficient precision, and the purpose of the follow-up runs is
simply to estimate all two-factor interactions for one factor, the
semifolding
option should be considered.

title: Design resolution: A measure of merit
text:
This page explains **fractional factorial design** (specifically a 2^8-3 resolution design) and **alias structure/confounding**, which solves the practical problem of running fewer experiments than a full factorial study while still being able to estimate the main effects you care about—letting engineers test 8 factors in just 32 runs instead of 256. Someone looking for this concept might search for "screening design," "reduced factorial experiment," "factor aliasing," "design with fewer runs," or "confounded effects in DOE."

FIGURE 3.6� Specifications for a 2 8-3 Design
(1)� Write down a full factorial for 8-3 = 5 factors; that is,
a 2 5 full factorial design. Such a design has 2 5 = 32 rows.� (2)� Add a sixth column to the design table for factor 6, using
6 = 345 (or 6 = -345) to manufacture it (i.e. using the column multiplication
we discussed above).� (3)� Do likewise for factor 7 and for factor 8, using the appropriate
generating relations given in Figure 3.6.� We note further that the generating relations, written in �I = ...�
form, for the 2 8-3 is� { I = � 3456; I = � 12457; I = � 12358 }. This collection of generating relations for a factorial design is called
a defining relation . There are three � words � in the defining
relation for the 2 8-3 design. The length of the shortest word
in the defining relation is called the resolution of the design. Resolution describes the degree to which estimated main effects are aliased (or confounded)
with estimated 2-level interactions, 3-level interactions, etc The length of the shortest word in the defining relation for the 2 8-3 design is four. This is written in Roman numeral script, and sub-scripted
as 2 IV 8-3 . Note that the 2 3-1 design has
one word: �I = �123� in its defining relation, and so has resolution
three; that is, we may write 2 III 3-1 .� Now Figure 3.6 may be completed by writing it as:� FIGURE 3.7� Specifications for a 2 8-3 , Showing Resolution
�IV� The design resolution tells us how badly the design is confounded. Previously,
in the 2 3-1 design, we saw the main effects were confounded
with two-factor interactions. However, main effects were not confounded
with other main effects. So, at worst, we have 3=12, or 2=13, etc., but
we do not have 1=1, or 2=2, etc. In fact, a resolution II design would
be pretty useless for any purpose whatsoever!� Similarly, in a resolution IV design, main effects are confounded with
at worst three-factor interactions. We can see, in Figure 3.7, that 6=345.
We also see that 36=45, 34=56, etc. (i.e. two-factor interactions are confounded
with certain other two-factor interactions) etc.; but we never see the
likes of� 2=13, or 5=34, (main effects confounded with two-factor
interactions).� The complete confounding pattern, for confounding of up to two-factor
interactions, arising from the design given in Figure 3.7 is� 34 = 56 = 78 35 = 46 36 = 45 37 = 48 38 = 47 57 = 68 Note that we have deliberately left out reference to three-factor interactions,
and higher than three-factor interactions, because as we have said they
are assumed negligible. (This may be a wrong assumption in some rare instances).� If we are interested in estimating up to two of the two-factor interactions
free of confounding with other two-factor interaction when running this
design, we would assign those factors of interest to columns 1 or column
2. This type of consideration is often key in choosing and setting up a
design.� This means that a resolution IV design is �better� than a resolution
III design because we have a less severe confounding pattern in the �IV�
than in the �III� situation; higher order interactions are usually assumed
to be much less significant than low-order interactions.� A higher resolution design for the same number of factors will, however,
require more runs and so it is �worse� than a lower-order design in that
sense.� Similarly, a resolution V design, main effects would be confounded with
at worst four-factor interactions, and two factor interactions would be
confounded with certain three-factor interactions.� Example : The 2 8-3 design is of resolution IV, and
requires 32 runs. The 2 8-2 design, also for eight factors, has
resolution V, but requires 64 runs.� The identifying relation for a design is not necessarily unique.

title: Design resolution: A measure of merit
text:
Factorial design resolution is a way to measure how well a fractional factorial experiment can separate the effects you're trying to measure from unwanted interference between factors, using generating relations and defining relations to determine which factor combinations get confounded together. This solves the practical problem of deciding whether a smaller, cheaper experiment will still give you clear answers about which factors matter, or if the cost savings will blur your results by mixing up the effects of different variables. Someone looking for this concept might search for "fractional factorial confounding," "design quality measure," "factorial experiment efficiency," "factor interaction separation," or "minimum experiment size that keeps results clean."

title: Design resolution: A measure of merit
text:
Design resolution is a rating system that tells you how mixed up your experimental results will be—specifically, which effects (main factors versus interactions between factors) will be impossible to tell apart from each other in a fractional factorial design. Engineers use this to decide if a smaller, cheaper experiment is worth doing or if the confounding is so bad that you can't trust the conclusions, with Resolution IV being acceptable for most practical purposes while Resolution II designs are essentially worthless. Alternative searches: "fractional factorial confounding," "how confounded is my design," "aliasing in experiments," "factorial design quality rating," "can I separate main effects from interactions."

Alternative ways to search for this: "confounding patterns in factorial designs," "aliasing structure," "fractional factorial design strategy," "main effect isolation," or "how to avoid confusing factor effects with interaction effects."

In practical terms, this solves the real-world problem of running fewer experiments while still being able to reliably figure out which factors matter most and how they work together, without confusion from unmeasured three-way or higher interactions. You might also search for this concept using terms like "confounding structure," "alias structure," "factorial design efficiency," "experimental design trade-offs," or "how to choose between Resolution III, IV, or V designs."

title: Critical values and p values
text:
This page explains **critical values** and **significance level (α)**, which help you decide whether to reject a null hypothesis by comparing your test statistic against a threshold—basically, it tells you what measurement result is extreme enough to mean something real is happening, not just random chance. Non-statisticians might search for this as "how do I know if my test results are significant," "what cutoff proves my hypothesis," "decision threshold for test results," or "when is a difference real versus coincidence."

Critical values for a test of hypothesis depend upon a test
statistic, which is specific to the type of test, and the
significance level, \(\alpha\),
which defines the sensitivity of the test. A value of \(\alpha\)
= 0.05
implies that the null hypothesis is rejected 5 % of the time when
it is in fact true. The choice of \(\alpha\)
is somewhat arbitrary, although in practice values of 0.1, 0.05, and
0.01 are common. Critical values are essentially cut-off values
that define regions where the test statistic is unlikely to lie;
for example, a region where the critical value is exceeded with
probability \(\alpha\)
if the null hypothesis is true. The null hypothesis is rejected
if the test statistic lies within this region which is often
referred to as the rejection region(s).
Critical values
for
specific tests of hypothesis are tabled in chapter 1.

title: DOE scatter plot
text:
The DOE scatter plot method identifies the most important factors and their optimal settings by examining whether data location shifts when factor levels change from low ("-") to high ("+"), with the factor showing the greatest separation (least overlap) ranked as most influential. This helps engineers quickly prioritize which process inputs have the biggest impact on output results and what dial settings work best, without getting bogged down in complex statistical models. People searching for this might look for: "factor importance ranking," "which input affects my output most," "comparing factor effects visually," "process sensitivity analysis," or "identifying critical process variables."

As discussed previously, the DOE scatter plot is
used to look for the following:
Most Important Factors;
Best Settings of the Most Important Factors;
Outliers.
Each of these will be discussed in turn.
Most Important Factors:
For each of the
k
factors, as we go from the "-" setting
to the "+" setting within the factor, is there a location shift
in the body of the data? If yes, then
Which factor has the biggest such data location shift (that
is, has least data overlap)? This defines the "most
important factor".
Which factor has the next biggest shift (that is, has next
least data overlap)? This defines the "second most important
factor".
Continue for the remaining factors.
In practice, the DOE scatter plot will typically only be able to
discriminate the most important factor (largest shift) and perhaps
the second most important factor (next largest shift). The degree
of overlap in remaining factors is frequently too large to
ascertain with certainty the ranking for other factors.
Best Settings for the Most Important Factors:
For each of the most important factors, which
setting ("-" or "+") yields the "best" response?
In order to answer this question, the engineer must first define "best".
This is done with respect to the overall project goal in
conjunction with the specific response variable under study. For
some experiments (e.g., maximizing the speed of a chip), "best"
means we are trying to maximize the response (speed). For other
experiments (e.g., semiconductor chip scrap), "best" means we are
trying to minimize the response (scrap). For yet other experiments
(e.g., designing a resistor) "best" means we are trying to hit a
specific target (the specified resistance). Thus the definition of
"best" is an engineering precursor to the determination of best
settings.
Suppose the analyst is attempting to maximize the response. In such a
case, the analyst would proceed as follows:
For factor 1, for what setting (- or +) is the body of the
data higher?
For factor 2, for what setting (- or +) is the body of the
data higher?
Continue for the remaining factors.
The resulting
k
-vector of best settings:
(x1best, x2best, ..., xkbest)
is thus theoretically obtained by looking at each factor
individually in the DOE scatter plot and choosing the setting
(- or +) that has the body of data closest to the desired
optimal (maximal, minimal, target) response.
As indicated earlier, the DOE scatter plot will typically be able to
estimate best settings for only the first few important factors.
Again, the degree of data overlap precludes ascertaining best
settings for the remaining factors. Other tools, such as the
DOE mean plot
, will do a better job of
determining such settings.
Outliers:
Do any data points stand apart from the bulk of the data? If so,
then such values are candidates for further investigation as
outliers. For multiple outliers, it is of interest to note if
all such anomalous data cluster at the same setting for any of
the various factors. If so, then such settings become candidates
for avoidance or inclusion, depending on the nature (bad or
good), of the outliers.

title: DOE mean plot
text:
A DOE mean plot ranks factors by their effect size—the amount the average response changes when switching each factor from its low ("-") setting to high ("+") setting—to identify which process inputs have the biggest impact on your output and what settings work best. This solves the practical problem of figuring out which knobs to turn first when trying to improve a product or process, and what position to set them to. Engineers might search for this using terms like "factor importance ranking," "main effects plot," "which variables matter most," or "comparing factor sensitivity."

From the DOE mean plot, we look for the following:
A ranked list of factors from most important to least important.
The best settings for each factor (on average).
Ranked List of Factors--Most Important to Least Important:
For each of the
k
factors, as we go from the "-" setting
to the "+" setting for the factor, is there a shift in location
of the average response?
If yes, we would like to identify the factor with the biggest
shift (the "most important factor"), the next biggest shift (the
"second most important factor"), and so on until all factors
are accounted for.
Since we are only plotting the means and each factor has identical
(-,+) = (-1,+1) coded factor settings, the above simplifies to
What factor has the steepest line? This is the
most important factor.
The next steepest line? This is the second most important
factor.
Continue for the remaining factors.
This ranking of factors based on local means is the most important
step in building the definitive ranked list of factors as required
in screening experiments.
Best Settings (on Average):
For each of the
k
factors, which setting (- or +) yields the
"best" response?
In order to answer this, the engineer must first define "best". This
is done with respect to the overall project goal in conjunction with
the specific response variable under study. For some experiments,
"best" means we are trying to maximize the response (e.g., maximizing
the speed of a chip). For other experiments, "best" means we are
trying to minimize the response (e.g., semiconductor chip scrap).
For yet other experiments, "best" means we are trying to hit a
specific target (e.g., designing a resistor to match a specified
resistance). Thus the definition of "best" is a precursor to the
determination of best settings.
For example, suppose the analyst is attempting to maximize
the response. In that case, the analyst would proceed as follows:
For factor 1, what setting (- or +) has the largest average
response?
For factor 2, what setting (- or +) has the largest average
response?
Continue for the remaining factors.
The resulting
k
-vector of best settings:
(x1best, x2best, ..., xkbest)
is in general obtained by looking at each factor individually in the
DOE mean plot and choosing that setting (- or +) that has an
average response closest to the desired optimal (maximal, minimal,
target) response.
This candidate for best settings is based on the averages. This
k
-vector of best settings should be similar to that obtained
from the
DOE scatter plot
, though the
DOE mean plot is easier to interpret.

title: Block plot
text:
Block plots are a graphical method for visualizing factorial experiment results to identify which process factors have the strongest and most stable effects across different operating conditions. This helps engineers quickly spot which input settings produce the most reliable improvements and which combinations of factors work best together, without needing to interpret complex statistical tables. You might also search for this using terms like "factor effect visualization," "robustness factor analysis," "interaction plot comparison," or "experimental design graphics."

Important factors (including 2-factor interactions): Look at each of the k block plots. Within a given block plot, Are the corresponding block heights 
 consistently large as we scan across the 
 within-plot robustness factor 
 settings--yes/no; and are the within-block 
 sign patterns (+ above -, or - above +) 
 consistent across all robustness factors 
 settings--yes/no? To facilitate intercomparisons, all block plots have the same
vertical axis scale. Across such block plots, Which plot has the consistently largest block heights,
 along with consistent arrangement of within-block +'s
 and -'s? This defines the "most important factor". Which plot has the consistently next-largest block heights,
 along with consistent arrangement of within-block 
 +'s and -'s? This defines the "second most important
 factor". Continue for the remaining factors. This scanning and comparing of the k block plots easily
leads to the identification of the most important factors. This
identification has the additional virtue over previous steps in
that it is robust. For a given important factor, the consistency
of block heights and sign arrangement across robustness factors
gives additional credence to the robust importance of that factor.
The factor is important (the change in the response will be large)
irrespective of what settings the robustness factors have. Having
such information is both important and comforting. Important Special Case; Large but Inconsistent: What happens if the block heights are large but not consistent?
Suppose, for example, a 2 3 factorial experiment is being
analyzed and the block plot focusing on factor X 1 is being
examined and interpreted so as to address the usual question of
whether factor X 1 is important. Let us consider in some detail how such a block plot might
appear. This X 1 block plot will have 2 3-1 = 4
combinations of the robustness factors X 2 and X 3 along
the horizontal axis in the following order: ( X 2 , X 3 ) = (+, +); ( X 2 , X 3 ) = (+, -); 
 ( X 2 , X 3 ) = (-, +); ( X 2 , X 3 ) = (-, -). If the block heights are consistently large (with "+" above "-" in
each block) over the four combinations of settings for X 2 and X 3 , as in ( X 2 , X 3 ) block height (= local X 1 effect) (+, +) 30 (+, -) 29 (-, +) 29 (-, -) 31 then from binomial considerations there is one chance in
2 4-1 = 1/8 \( \approx \) 12.5 % of the the four local X 1 effects having the same
sign (i.e., all positive or all negative). The usual statistical
cutoff of 5 % has not been achieved here, but the 12.5 % is suggestive.
Further, the consistency of the four X 1 effects
(all near 30) is evidence of a robustness of the X effect
over the settings of the other two factors. In summary, the
above suggests: Factor 1 is probably important (the issue of how large the
 effect has to be in order to be considered important will
 be discussed in more detail in a later section); and The estimated factor 1 effect is about 30 units. On the other hand, suppose the 4 block heights for
factor 1 vary in the following cyclic way: ( X 2 , X 3 ) block height (= local X 1 effect) (+, +) 30 (+, -) 20 (-, +) 30 (-, -) 20 then how is this to be interpreted? The key here to such interpretation is that the block plot is
telling us that the estimated X 1 effect is in fact at
least 20 units, but not consistent. The effect is
changing, but it is changing in a structured way. The "trick" 
is to scan the X 2 and X 3 settings and deduce what that
substructure is.

title: Block plot
text:
**Block plots and factorial interactions** help engineers spot when one factor's effect on output depends on the setting of another factor—like discovering that adjusting factor X₁ helps in some conditions but hurts in others depending on factor X₃. This technique solves the practical problem of identifying which factor combinations work best together rather than treating each adjustment independently. You might also search for this concept as "factor interactions," "combined effects analysis," "two-way interactions," or "when one knob affects how another knob works."

Doing so from the above table, we see that the
estimated X 1 effect is 30 for point 1 ( X 2 , X 3 ) = (+, +) and for point 3 ( X 2 , X 3 ) = (-, +) and then the estimated X 1 effect drops 10 units to 20 for point 2 ( X 2 , X 3 ) = (+, -) and for point 4 ( X 2 , X 3 ) = (-, -) We thus deduce that the estimated X 1 effect is 30 whenever X 3 = "+" 20 whenever X 3 = "-" When the factor X 1 effect is not consistent, but in fact changes
depending on the setting of factor X 3 , then definitionally that
is said to be an " X 1 * X 3 interaction". That is precisely
the case here, and so our conclusions would be: factor X 1 is probably important ; the estimated factor X 1 effect is 25
 (the average of 30, 20, 30, and 20); the X 1 * X 3 interaction is probably important; the estimated X 1 * X 3 interaction is about 10
 (the change in the factor X 1 effect as X 3 changes = 30 - 20 = 10); hence the X 1 * X 3 interaction is less 
 important than the X 1 effect. Note that we are using the term important in a qualitative sense here.
More precise determinations of importance in terms of statistical
or engineering significance are discussed in later sections. The block plot gives us the structure and the detail to allow
such conclusions to be drawn and to be understood. It is a
valuable adjunct to the previous analysis steps. Best settings: After identifying important factors, it is also of use to
determine the best settings for these factors. As usual, best
settings are determined for main effects only (since main
effects are all that the engineer can control). Best settings
for interactions are not done because the engineer has
no direct way of controlling them. In the block plot context, this determination of best factor
settings is done simply by noting which factor setting (+ or -)
within each block is closest to that which the engineer is
ultimately trying to achieve. In the defective springs case,
since the response variable is percent acceptable springs, we are clearly
trying to maximize (as opposed to minimize, or hit a target) the
response and the ideal optimum point is 100 %. Given this, we would
look at the block plot of a given important factor and note within
each block which factor setting (+ or -) yields a data value
closest to 100 % and then select that setting as the best for that
factor. From the defective springs block plots, we would thus conclude that the best setting for factor 1 is +; the best setting for factor 2 is -; the best setting for factor 3 cannot be easily determined.

title: Block plot
text:
Block plots are used in designed experiments to visually identify which process factors have the most consistent and largest effects on an output by comparing the height and pattern of blocks across different factor settings. This helps engineers quickly spot which knobs to turn to reliably improve results without getting lost in tables of numbers. You might also search for this concept using terms like "factor screening visualization," "main effects comparison," "robustness factor analysis," or "identifying critical process variables."

leads to the identification of the most important factors. This
identification has the additional virtue over previous steps in
that it is robust. For a given important factor, the consistency
of block heights and sign arrangement across robustness factors
gives additional credence to the robust importance of that factor.
The factor is important (the change in the response will be large)
irrespective of what settings the robustness factors have. Having

appear. This X 1 block plot will have 2 3-1 = 4

each block) over the four combinations of settings for X 2 and X 3 , as in ( X 2 , X 3 ) block height (= local X 1 effect) (+, +) 30 (+, -) 29 (-, +) 29 (-, -) 31 then from binomial considerations there is one chance in

sign (i.e., all positive or all negative). The usual statistical

Further, the consistency of the four X 1 effects
(all near 30) is evidence of a robustness of the X effect

 be discussed in more detail in a later section); and The estimated factor 1 effect is about 30 units. On the other hand, suppose the 4 block heights for
factor 1 vary in the following cyclic way: ( X 2 , X 3 ) block height (= local X 1 effect) (+, +) 30 (+, -) 20 (-, +) 30 (-, -) 20 then how is this to be interpreted? The key here to such interpretation is that the block plot is
telling us that the estimated X 1 effect is in fact at

title: Block plot
text:
The core concept is **interaction effects** (also called **factor interactions** or **X₁*X₃ interactions**), which describes how one factor's impact on the outcome changes depending on what level another factor is set to—solving the practical problem of understanding when adjusting one machine setting doesn't work the same way across all your operating conditions. Non-statisticians often search for this using phrases like "why does my fix work sometimes but not always," "dependent effects between factors," "when does changing one setting affect how another setting works," or "why aren't my results consistent across different configurations."

depending on the setting of factor X 3 , then definitionally that

the case here, and so our conclusions would be: factor X 1 is probably important ; the estimated factor X 1 effect is 25
 (the average of 30, 20, 30, and 20); the X 1 * X 3 interaction is probably important; the estimated X 1 * X 3 interaction is about 10
 (the change in the factor X 1 effect as X 3 changes = 30 - 20 = 10); hence the X 1 * X 3 interaction is less 
 important than the X 1 effect. Note that we are using the term important in a qualitative sense here.
More precise determinations of importance in terms of statistical
or engineering significance are discussed in later sections. The block plot gives us the structure and the detail to allow
such conclusions to be drawn and to be understood. It is a
valuable adjunct to the previous analysis steps. Best settings: After identifying important factors, it is also of use to
determine the best settings for these factors. As usual, best
settings are determined for main effects only (since main
effects are all that the engineer can control). Best settings
for interactions are not done because the engineer has
no direct way of controlling them. In the block plot context, this determination of best factor
settings is done simply by noting which factor setting (+ or -)
within each block is closest to that which the engineer is
ultimately trying to achieve. In the defective springs case,
since the response variable is percent acceptable springs, we are clearly
trying to maximize (as opposed to minimize, or hit a target) the
response and the ideal optimum point is 100 %. Given this, we would

each block which factor setting (+ or -) yields a data value
closest to 100 % and then select that setting as the best for that
factor. From the defective springs block plots, we would thus conclude that the best setting for factor 1 is +; the best setting for factor 2 is -; the best setting for factor 3 cannot be easily determined.
Which plot has the consistently next-largest block heights,
 along with consistent arrangement of within-block 
 +'s and -'s? This defines the "second most important
 factor". Continue for the remaining factors.
