In general, the range approach is quite satisfactory for sample sizes
up to around 10. For larger sample sizes, using subgroup standard
deviations is preferable. For small sample sizes, the relative
efficiency of using the range approach as opposed to using standard
deviations is shown in the following table.

title: Cusum Control Charts
section: Process or Product Monitoring and Control
text:
The CUSUM (cumulative sum control) chart with V-Mask is a statistical method for detecting small shifts in a process mean by plotting running totals of deviations from a target value, allowing manufacturers to spot when a process starts drifting out of control before it produces bad parts. Instead of just looking at individual measurements, this technique accumulates the differences over time so gradual changes become obvious—solving the real-world problem of catching process problems early when they're still small and fixable. Related searches might include: "cumulative sum chart," "detecting process drift," "sequential quality control," "tracking running totals for process monitoring," or "early warning system for process shifts."

An example will be used to illustrate the construction and application of a
V-
Mask. The 20 data points
324.925, 324.675, 324.725, 324.350, 325.350, 325.225, 324.125, 324.525,
325.225, 324.600, 324.625, 325.150, 328.325, 327.250, 327.825, 328.500,
326.675, 327.775, 326.875, 328.350
are each the average of samples of size 4 taken from a process that
has an estimated mean of 325. Based on process data, the process
standard deviation is 1.27 and therefore the sample means have
a standard deviation of \(1.27 / (4^{1/2}) = 0.635\).
We can design a
V-
Mask using \(h\) and \(k\)
or we can use an \(\alpha\) and \(\beta\)
design approach. For the latter approach we must specify
\(\alpha\):
the probability of a false alarm, i.e., concluding that a shift
in the process has occurred, while in fact it did not,
\(\beta\):
the probability of not detecting that a shift in the process
mean has, in fact, occurred, and
\(\delta\) (delta):
the amount of shift in the process mean
that we wish to detect, expressed as a multiple of the standard
deviation of the data points (which are the sample means).
Note: Technically, \(\alpha\) and \(\beta\)
are calculated in terms of one sequential trial where we monitor \(S_m\)
until we have either an out-of-control signal or \(S_m\)
returns to the starting point (and the monitoring begins, in effect, all over again).
The values of \(h\) and \(k\)
are related to \(\alpha\), \(\beta\), and \(\delta\)
based on the following equations (adapted from
Montgomery, 2000
).
$$ \begin{eqnarray}
k & = & \frac{\delta \sigma_{x}}{2} \\
\hspace{.2in} \\
d & = & \frac{2}{\delta^2}\mbox{ln } \left( \frac{1-\beta}{\alpha} \right) \\
\hspace{.2in} \\
h & = & d k
\end{eqnarray} $$
In our example we choose \(\alpha = 0.0027\)
(equivalent to the plus or minus 3 sigma criteria used in a standard
Shewhart chart), and \(\beta = 0.01\).
Finally, we decide we want to quickly detect a shift as large as
1 sigma, which sets \(\delta = 1\).

title: EWMA Control Charts
section: Process or Product Monitoring and Control
text:
EWMA (Exponentially Weighted Moving Average) control charts detect shifts in process mean by continuously updating a weighted average that gives more importance to recent measurements, solving the practical problem of catching small drifts or changes in manufacturing output faster than traditional control charts that treat all data points equally. Alternative search terms include "exponentially weighted moving average monitoring," "trend detection in process control," "sensitivity to process drift," "adaptive control limits," or "real-time process tracking."

The center line for the control chart is the target value
or \(\mbox{EWMA}_0\).
The control limits are:
$$ \begin{eqnarray}
UCL & = & \mbox{EWMA}_0 + k s_{\mbox{ewma}} \\
LCL & = & \mbox{EWMA}_0 - k s_{\mbox{ewma}} \, ,
\end{eqnarray} $$
where the factor \(k\)
is either set equal 3 or chosen using the
Lucas
and Saccucci (1990)
tables. The data are assumed to be
independent and these tables also assume a normal population.
As with all control procedures, the EWMA procedure depends on a
database of measurements that are truly representative of the
process. Once the mean value and standard deviation have been
calculated from this database, the process can enter the monitoring
stage, provided the process was in control when the data were
collected. If not, then the usual Phase 1 work would have to be
completed first.

title: Choosing a Sampling Plan: MIL Standard 105D
section: Process or Product Monitoring and Control
text:
**Attribute sampling plans** (also called **acceptance sampling by attributes**) help manufacturers decide whether to accept or reject batches of products based on inspecting just a sample rather than checking every single item, which saves time and cost while controlling the risk of shipping defective goods. Engineers might search for this under alternative names like "lot acceptance sampling," "go/no-go inspection procedures," "defect-based quality sampling," or "batch inspection tables."

Standard military sampling procedures for inspection by attributes were
developed during World War II. Army Ordnance tables and procedures were
generated in the early 1940's and these grew into the Army Service Forces
tables. At the end of the war, the Navy also worked on a set of tables.
In the meanwhile, the Statistical Research Group at Columbia University
performed research and outputted many outstanding results on attribute
sampling plans.
These three streams combined in 1950 into a standard called Mil.
Std. 105A. It has since been modified from time to time and issued
as 105B, 105C and 105D. Mil. Std. 105D was issued by the U.S. government
in 1963. It was adopted in 1971 by the American National Standards
Institute as ANSI Standard Z1.4 and in 1974 it was adopted (with minor
changes) by the International Organization for Standardization as
ISO Std. 2859. The latest revision is Mil. Std 105E and was issued
in 1989.
These three similar standards are continuously being updated and revised,
but the basic tables remain the same. Thus the discussion that follows
of the germane aspects of Mil. Std. 105E also applies to the other two
standards.

title:
Choosing a Sampling Plan with a given
OC Curve
section: Process or Product Monitoring and Control
text:
The **Operating Characteristic (OC) curve** and **acceptance sampling plan design** show how to pick the right sample size and acceptance number so that good batches get approved at the rate you want (producer's risk) and bad batches get rejected at the rate you want (consumer's risk). This solves the practical problem of deciding how many items to inspect from a shipment and what the pass/fail threshold should be, based on two quality levels you care about: an acceptable quality level (AQL) that you're willing to live with, and a rejectable quality level (LTPD) that's too bad to accept. Someone looking for this might search for: "sampling inspection rules," "lot acceptance criteria," "how many samples do I need to test," "incoming inspection plan," or "quality control acceptance threshold."

In order to design a sampling plan with a specified OC curve one
needs two designated points. Let us design a sampling plan such that
the probability of acceptance is \(1-\alpha\)
for lots with fraction defective \(p_1\)
and the probability of acceptance is \(\beta\)
for lots with fraction defective \(p_2\).
Typical choices for these
points are: \(p_1\)
is the
AQL
,
\(p_2\)
is the
LTPD
and \(\alpha, \, \beta\)
are the
Producer's
Risk (Type I error)
and
Consumer's Risk (Type II error)
, respectively.
If we are willing to assume that binomial sampling is valid, then the
sample size \(n\),
and the acceptance number \(c\)
are the solution to
$$ \begin{eqnarray}
1 - \alpha & = & \sum_{d=0}^c \frac{n!}{d!(n-d)!} p_1^d (1-p_1)^{n-d} \\
\beta & = & \sum_{d=0}^c \frac{n!}{d!(n-d)!} p_2^d (1-p_2)^{n-d} \, .
\end{eqnarray} $$
These two simultaneous equations are nonlinear so there is no simple,
direct solution. There are however a number of iterative techniques
available that give approximate solutions so that composition of a
computer program poses few problems.

title: Individuals Control Charts
section: Process or Product Monitoring and Control
text:
An **individuals control chart** (also called an I-chart or X-chart for single measurements) uses the average of moving ranges to set upper and lower control limits around individual data points, helping manufacturers detect when a process is drifting out of specification or behaving abnormally without needing to collect samples in batches. This solves the practical problem of monitoring product quality or process performance when you can only measure one item at a time, or when each measurement represents the whole process state, by automatically flagging when something goes wrong. Engineers and quality managers searching for this might also look for "single observation control chart," "individuals and moving range chart" (I-MR chart), "one-at-a-time process monitoring," or "detecting process shifts from individual measurements."

For the control chart for individual measurements, the lines plotted
are:
$$ \begin{eqnarray}
UCL & = & \bar{x} + 3\frac{\overline{MR}}{1.128} \\
\mbox{Center Line} & = & \bar{x} \\
LCL & = & \bar{x} - 3\frac{\overline{MR}}{1.128} \, ,
\end{eqnarray} $$
where \(\bar{x}\)
is the average of all the individuals and \(\overline{MR}\)
is the average of all the moving ranges of two observations. Keep in
mind that either or both averages may be replaced by a standard or
target, if available. (Note that 1.128 is the value of \(d_2\) for \(n = 2\).

title: Cusum Average Run Length
section: Process or Product Monitoring and Control
text:
The CUSUM chart's Average Run Length (ARL) determines how many samples you need to collect before the chart catches a real problem or triggers a false alarm—so you can tune the chart's sensitivity (k_s and h_s parameters) to balance catching defects quickly without bothering operators with too many false alerts. This solves the practical problem of deciding how strict to make your quality control monitoring: tighter settings catch problems faster but create more nuisance alarms, while looser settings reduce false alarms but let bad batches slip through longer. People looking for this concept might search for "how many parts until detection," "tuning control chart sensitivity," "process monitoring response time," "false alarm versus missed defect tradeoff," or "control chart alert delay."

The average run length (ARL) at a given quality level is the
average number of samples (subgroups) taken before an action
signal is given. The standardized parameters \(k_s\) and \(h_s\)
together with the sample size \(n\)
are usually selected to yield approximate ARLs \(L_0\) and \(L_1\)
at acceptable and rejectable quality levels \(\mu_0\) and \(\mu_1\)
respectively. We would like to see a high ARL, \(L_0\),
when the process is on target, (i.e. in control), and a low ARL, \(L_1\),
when the process mean shifts to an unsatisfactory level.
In order to determine the parameters of a CUSUM chart, the
acceptable and rejectable quality levels along with the desired
respective ARLs are usually specified. The design parameters
can then be obtained by a number of ways. Unfortunately, the
calculations of the ARL for CUSUM charts are quite involved.
There are several nomographs available from different sources
that can be utilized to find the ARLs when the standardized
\(h\) and \(k\)
are given. Some of the nomographs solve the
unpleasant integral equations that form the basis of the exact
solutions, using an approximation of Systems of Linear Algebraic
Equations (SLAE). This Handbook used a computer program
that furnished the required ARLs given the standardized
\(h\) and \(k\).
An example is given below.

title: Counts Control Charts
section: Process or Product Monitoring and Control
text:
This excerpt covers **counts control charts** and the distinction between **nonconforming units** (defective items) and **nonconformities** (defects), which helps quality engineers track whether entire products fail inspection versus counting the number of individual problems found on those products. If you're trying to set up a monitoring system to catch bad batches coming off a production line, or you need to decide whether to count "how many chips are bad" versus "how many problems exist across all chips," this teaches you the difference. Related searches: defect versus defective, quality control metrics, product inspection classification, nonconformity tracking, defect counting methods.

The literature differentiates between
defect
and
defective
, which is the same as differentiating between
nonconformity
and
nonconforming units
. This may sound
like splitting hairs, but in the interest of clarity let's try to
unravel this man-made mystery.
Consider a wafer with a number of chips on it. The wafer is referred
to as an "item of a product". The chip may be referred to as
"a specific point". There exist certain specifications for the
wafers. When a particular wafer (e.g., the item of the product) does
not meet at least one of the specifications, it is classified as a
nonconforming item.
Furthermore, each chip, (e.g., the
specific point) at which a specification is not met becomes a
defect
or
nonconformity.
So, a nonconforming or defective item contains at least one defect or
nonconformity. It should be pointed out that a wafer can contain
several defects but still be classified as conforming. For example,
the defects may be located at noncritical positions on the wafer. If,
on the other hand, the number of the so-called "unimportant" defects
becomes alarmingly large, an investigation of the production of these
wafers is warranted.
Control charts involving counts can be either for the
total
number
of nonconformities (defects) for the sample of inspected
units, or for the
average number
of defects per
inspection unit.

title: Proportions Control Charts
section: Process or Product Monitoring and Control
text:
A **p-chart** (proportion control chart) monitors the fraction or percentage of defective items coming off a production line to detect when your process quality is drifting out of control. Instead of measuring how bad each defect is, you simply count whether each item passes or fails inspection, then track that pass/fail rate over time to catch problems early. You might also search for this using terms like "defect rate tracking," "quality proportion monitoring," "nonconforming items chart," or "fraction defective control."

The proportion or fraction nonconforming (defective) in a
population is defined as the ratio of the number of nonconforming
items in the population to the total number of items in that
population. The item under consideration may have one or more
quality characteristics that are inspected simultaneously. If at
least one of the characteristics does not conform to standard, the
item is classified as nonconforming.
The fraction or proportion can be expressed as a decimal, or, when
multiplied by 100, as a percent. The underlying statistical
principles for a control chart for proportion nonconforming are
based on the binomial distribution.
Let us suppose that the production process operates in a stable
manner, such that the probability that a given unit will not conform
to specifications is \(p\).
Furthermore, we assume that
successive units produced are independent. Under these conditions,
each unit that is produced is a realization of a Bernoulli random
variable with parameter \(p\).
If a random sample of \(n\)
units of product is selected and if \(D\)
is the number of units that are nonconforming, then \(D\)
follows a
binomial distribution
with parameters \(n\) and \(p\)
so that

title: Hotelling Control Charts
section: Process or Product Monitoring and Control
text:
Hotelling T² distance is a multivariate control chart method that monitors multiple related measurements simultaneously by accounting for how those measurements naturally vary together, solving the real-world problem that traditional single-measurement control charts miss out-of-control situations because they ignore correlations between variables. Engineers and operators searching for ways to monitor process quality when they have several interdependent specifications—like temperature, pressure, and flow rate together—might look for "multivariate process control," "multi-variable quality monitoring," or "simultaneous monitoring of correlated measurements" without realizing it's the Hotelling method they need.

The Hotelling \(T^2\)
distance is a measure that
accounts for the covariance structure of a multivariate normal
distribution. It was proposed by Harold Hotelling in 1947
and is called Hotelling \(T^2\).
It may be thought
of as the multivariate counterpart of the Student's-
t
statistic.
The \(T^2\)
distance is a constant multiplied by a quadratic
form. This quadratic form is obtained by multiplying the following
three quantities.
The vector of deviations between the observations and
the mean, \( \bf{m} \),
which is expressed by \((\bf{X-m})'\),
the inverse of the covariance matrix, \(\bf{S}^{-1}\),
and the vector of deviations, \((\bf{X-m})\).
It should be mentioned that for independent variables, the
covariance matrix is a diagonal matrix and \(T^2\)
becomes proportional to the sum of squared standardized variables.
In general, the higher the \(T^2\)
value, the more
distant is the observation from the mean. The formula for
computing the \(T^2\)
is:
$$ T^2 = c( \bf{X-m'} ) \bf{S}^{-1} ( \bf{X-m} ) \, . $$
The constant \(c\)
is the sample size from which the covariance
matrix was estimated.

title: Principal Components Control Charts
section: Process or Product Monitoring and Control
text:
**Principal components control charts** use uncorrelated linear combinations of standardized process variables to monitor multiple measurements at once, letting operators watch just a handful of summary metrics instead of tracking dozens of individual readings. This solves the practical problem of data overload—instead of juggling 20 correlated measurements that mostly say the same thing, you boil them down to 1-3 independent numbers that capture what actually matters. Search alternatives: dimensionality reduction for process control, multivariate monitoring using PCA, condensing multiple process variables into fewer independent indicators, or variance-focused control charting.

Another way to analyze the data is to use
principal
components
. For each multivariate measurement (or observation),
the principal components are linear combinations of the standardized \(p\)
variables (to standardize subtract their respective targets
and divide by their standard deviations). The principal components
have two important advantages:
the new variables are uncorrelated (or almost), and
very often, a few (sometimes 1 or 2) principal components
may capture most of the variability in the data so that we
do not have to use all of the \(p\)
principal components for control.

title: Multivariate EWMA Charts
section: Process or Product Monitoring and Control
text:
Multivariate EWMA (Exponentially Weighted Moving Average) charts monitor multiple related quality measurements simultaneously to detect when a process starts drifting out of control, which is useful when you can't rely on checking each measurement separately because they're correlated with each other. This technique solves the problem of catching small, gradual shifts in process performance that single-variable control charts would miss, especially when variables influence each other. People might search for this using terms like "multi-variable process monitoring," "tracking correlated quality metrics," "multivariate control charts," or "detecting process changes across multiple measurements at once."

Here is an example of the application of an MEWMA control chart. To
faciltate comparison with existing literature, we used data from
Lowry et al. The data were simulated from a bivariate normal
distribution with unit variances and a correlation coefficient
of 0.5. The value for \(\lambda = 0.10\)
and the values for \(T_i^2\)
were obtained by the equation
given above. The covariance of the MEWMA vectors was obtained
by using the non-simplified equation. That means that for each
MEWMA control statistic, the computer computed a covariance matrix,
where \(i = 1, \, 2, \, \ldots, \, 10\).
The results of the computer routine
are:
*****************************************************
* Multi-Variate EWMA Control Chart *
*****************************************************
DATA SERIES MEWMA Vector MEWMA
1 2 1 2 STATISTIC
-1.190 0.590 -0.119 0.059 2.1886
0.120 0.900 -0.095 0.143 2.0697
-1.690 0.400 -0.255 0.169 4.8365
0.300 0.460 -0.199 0.198 3.4158
0.890 -0.750 -0.090 0.103 0.7089
0.820 0.980 0.001 0.191 0.9268
-0.300 2.280 -0.029 0.400 4.0018
0.630 1.750 0.037 0.535 6.1657
1.560 1.580 0.189 0.639 7.8554
1.460 3.050 0.316 0.880 14.4158
VEC XBAR MSE Lamda
1 .260 1.200 0.100
2 1.124 1.774 0.100
The
UCL
= 5.938 for \(\alpha = 0.05\).
Smaller choices of \(\alpha\)
are also used.

title: Hotelling'sTsquared
section: Process or Product Monitoring and Control
text:
Hotelling's T-squared is a multivariate statistical test that simultaneously checks whether the average values across multiple related measurements have shifted away from target specifications, solving the problem of monitoring whether a process or product is staying on track when you need to watch several quality characteristics at once instead of one at a time. Alternative names and search phrases include: multivariate process control, multivariate mean test, T² control chart, simultaneous monitoring of multiple variables, and multivariate out-of-control detection.

When \(T^2\)
is generalized to \(p\)
variables it becomes
$$ T^2 = n (\bar{{\bf x}} - {\bf \mu}_0) {\bf S}^{-1} (\bar{{\bf x}} - {\bf \mu}_0) \, , $$
with
$$
\bar{{\bf x}} = \left[ \begin{array}{c}
\bar{x}_1 \\
\bar{x}_2 \\
\vdots \\
\bar{x}_p
\end{array} \right]
\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,
{\bf \mu}_0 = \left[ \begin{array}{c}
\mu_1^0 \\
\mu_2^0 \\
\vdots \\
\mu_p^0
\end{array} \right]
\, . $$
\({\bf S}^{-1}\)
is the inverse of the sample variance-covariance matrix, \({\bf S}\), and \(n\)
is the sample size upon which each \(\bar{x}_i, \, i=1, \, 2, \, \ldots, \, p\),
is based. (The diagonal elements of \({\bf S}\)
are the variances and the off-diagonal elements are the
covariances for the \(p\)
variables. This is discussed further in
Section 6.5.4.3.1
.)

title: Single Moving Average
section: Process or Product Monitoring and Control
text:
The Single Moving Average is a smoothing technique that computes successive means of fixed-size subsets from sequential data to reduce noise and reveal underlying trends. This helps engineers identify real changes in a process by filtering out random fluctuations in measurements—like spotting an actual shift in supplier costs instead of getting distracted by day-to-day price bounces. You might also search for this using terms like "rolling average," "running mean," "sliding window averaging," or "trend smoothing."

An alternative way to summarize the past data is to compute the
mean of successive smaller sets of numbers of past data as follows.
Recall the set of numbers 9, 8, 9, 12, 9, 12, 11, 7, 13, 9, 11, 10
which were the dollar amount of
12 suppliers
selected at random. Let us set \(M\),
the size of the "smaller set" equal to 3. Then the average of the first 3 numbers is:
(9 + 8 + 9) / 3 = 8.667.
This is called "smoothing" (i.e., some form of averaging). This smoothing
process is continued by advancing one period and calculating the next average
of three numbers, dropping the first number.

title: Centered Moving Average
section: Process or Product Monitoring and Control
text:
Double Moving Averages for a Linear Trend Process is a forecasting technique that calculates a second moving average from an initial moving average to better predict future values when data shows a consistent upward or downward pattern over time. This solves the practical problem that a simple moving average fails to account for trends and produces inaccurate predictions when your process is steadily increasing or decreasing. Someone might also search for this using terms like "trend-adjusted forecasting," "moving average with slope correction," "double smoothing for trending data," or "forecasting methods that handle upward/downward drift."

Unfortunately, neither the mean of all data nor the moving average of
the most recent
M
values, when used as forecasts for the next
period, are able to cope with a significant trend.
There exists a variation on the MA procedure that often does a better
job of handling trend. It is called
Double Moving Averages for
a  Linear Trend Process
. It calculates a second moving average
from the original moving average, using the same value for
M
. As
soon as both single and double moving averages are available, a computer
routine uses these averages to compute a slope and intercept, and then
forecasts one or more periods ahead.

title: Single Exponential Smoothing
section: Process or Product Monitoring and Control
text:
Single Exponential Smoothing (also called Exponential Weighted Moving Average or EWMA) is a technique for filtering out random noise from a series of measurements by giving more weight to recent observations while gradually discounting older values. If you're monitoring a process and your sensor readings bounce around unpredictably, this method smooths out the jitter to reveal the true underlying trend so you can spot real problems versus normal measurement noise. You might also search for this under "exponential moving average," "data smoothing," "time series filtering," or "adaptive averaging for process control."

This smoothing scheme begins by setting \(S_2\) to \(y_1\),
where \(S_i\)
stands for smoothed observation or EWMA, and \(y\)
stands for the original observation.
The subscripts refer to the time periods, \(1, \, 2, \, \ldots, \, n\).
For the third period, \(S_3 = \alpha y_2 + (1-\alpha) S_2\);
and so on. There is no \(S_1\);
the smoothed series starts with the smoothed version of the second observation.
For any time period \(t\),
the smoothed value \(S_t\)
is found by computing
$$ S_t = \alpha y_{t-1} + (1-\alpha)S_{t-1} \,\,\,\,\,\,\, 0 < \alpha \le 1 \,\,\,\,\,\,\, t \ge 3 \, . $$
This is the
basic equation of exponential smoothing
and the
constant or parameter \(\alpha\)
is called the
smoothing constant
.
Note
: There is an alternative approach to exponential smoothing
that replaces \(y_{t-1}\)
in the basic equation with \(y_t\),
the current observation. That formulation,
due to Roberts (1959), is described in the section on
EWMA control charts
.
The formulation here follows Hunter (1986).

title: Forecasting with Single Exponential Smoothing
section: Process or Product Monitoring and Control
text:
Single exponential smoothing uses a weighted average of the most recent observation and the previous forecast to predict the next value, allowing you to make forecasts even when you don't have new data coming in. This solves the practical problem of smoothing out noisy measurements and projecting trends forward in real-time monitoring without waiting for fresh measurements. People searching for this concept might also look for "exponential moving average," "smoothing constant," "forecast decay," or "simple forecasting method."

The last data point in the previous example was 70 and its forecast
(smoothed value \(S\))
was 71.7. Since we do have the data point
and
the forecast available, we can calculate the next forecast
using the regular formula with \(\alpha = 0.1\) as
$$ \begin{eqnarray}
S_{t+1} & = & \alpha y_{orgin} + (1-\alpha) S_t \\
& = & 0.1(70) + 0.9(71.7) \\
& = & 71.5 \, .
\end{eqnarray} $$
But for the next forecast we have no data point (observation).
So now we compute:
$$ S_{t+2} = 0.1(70) + 0.9(71.5) = 71.35 \, . $$

title: Double Exponential Smoothing
section: Process or Product Monitoring and Control
text:
Double Exponential Smoothing (also called Holt's linear exponential smoothing or trend-adjusted exponential smoothing) fixes the problem where simple smoothing fails to track data that's consistently going up or down, by using two equations with smoothing constants α and γ to separately estimate the current level and the trend direction. Someone looking for this might search for "exponential smoothing with trend," "forecasting methods for trending data," "smoothing technique that handles upward or downward patterns," or "how to smooth data that keeps increasing or decreasing."

As was
previously
observed
, Single Smoothing does not excel in following the data when
there is a trend. This situation can be improved by the introduction
of a second equation with a second constant, \(\gamma\),
which must be chosen in conjunction with \(\alpha\).
Here are the two equations associated with Double Exponential Smoothing.
$$ \begin{eqnarray}
S_t & = & \alpha y_t + (1 - \alpha)(S_{t-1} + b_{t-1}) & & 0 \le \alpha \le 1 \\
& & \\
b_t & = & \gamma(S_t - S_{t-1}) + (1 - \gamma) b_{t-1} & & 0 \le \gamma \le 1
\end{eqnarray} $$
Note that the current value of the series is used to calculate its
smoothed value replacement in double exponential smoothing.

title: Forecasting with Double Exponential Smoothing(LASP)
section: Process or Product Monitoring and Control
text:
Double exponential smoothing (also called Holt's linear exponential smoothing) is used to forecast data that's trending up or down over time by tracking both the current level and the rate of change, which solves the practical problem of predicting future values when your measurements consistently increase or decrease rather than staying flat. Someone needing this technique might search for "forecasting with trend," "exponential smoothing for trending data," "smoothing method that handles slope," "linear trend forecasting," or "how to predict values that keep going up or down."

Consider once more the data set:
6.4,  5.6,  7.8,  8.8,  11,  11.6,
16.7,  15.3,  21.6,  22.4.
Now we will fit a double smoothing model with \(\alpha = 0.3623\)
and \(\gamma = 1.0\).
These are the estimates that result in the lowest possible MSE when
comparing the orignal series to one step ahead at a time forecasts
(since this version of double exponential smoothing uses the current
series value to calculate a smoothed value, the smoothed series cannot
be used to determine an \(\alpha\)
with minimum MSE). The chosen starting values are
\(S_1 = y_1 = 6.4\) and \(b_1 = ((y_2 - y_1) + (y_3 - y_2) + (y_4 - y_3))/3 = 0.8\).
For comparison's sake we also fit a single smoothing model with \(\alpha = 0.977\)
(this results in the lowest MSE for single exponential smoothing).
The MSE for double smoothing is 3.7024.
The MSE for single smoothing is 8.8867.

title: Triple Exponential Smoothing
section: Process or Product Monitoring and Control
text:
The Holt-Winters method (also called triple exponential smoothing) solves the problem of forecasting data that has both an upward or downward trend AND repeating seasonal patterns—like monthly sales that grow over time but spike every December. Alternative names you might search for include seasonal trend decomposition, multiplicative seasonality smoothing, or forecasting with trend and seasonality; engineers often look for "how to forecast data with repeating cycles and growth" or "handling seasonal patterns in trending data."

In this case double smoothing will not work. We now introduce a
third equation to take care of seasonality (sometimes called
periodicity). The resulting set of equations is called the
"Holt-Winters" (HW) method after the names of the inventors.
The basic equations for their method are given by:
$$ \begin{eqnarray}
S_t & = & \alpha \frac{y_t}{I_{t-L}} + (1-\alpha)(S_{t-1}+b_{t-1}) & \,\,\,\,\, & \mbox{OVERALL SMOOTHING} \\
& & & & \\
b_t & = & \gamma (S_t - S_{t-1}) + (1 - \gamma)b_{t-1} & & \mbox{TREND SMOOTHING} \\
& & & & \\
I_t & = & \beta \frac{y_t}{S_t} + (1 - \beta) I_{t-L} & & \mbox{SEASONAL SMOOTHING} \\
& & & & \\
F_{t+m} & = & (S_t + m b_t) I_{t-L+m} & & \mbox{FORECAST} \, ,
\end{eqnarray} $$
where
\(y\)
is the observation
\(S\)
is the smoothed observation
\(b\)
is the trend factor
\(I\)
is the seasonal index
\(F\)
is the forecast at
m
periods ahead
\(t\)
is an index denoting a time period
and \(\alpha\), \(\beta\), and \(\gamma\)
are constants that must be estimated in such a way that the MSE of the
error is minimized. This is best left to a good software package.

title: Example of Triple Exponential Smoothing
section: Process or Product Monitoring and Control
text:
This example demonstrates **triple exponential smoothing** (also called **Holt-Winters method**) for **trend and seasonality estimation** in time series data, which solves the practical problem of forecasting sales or production output when your data has both a consistent upward/downward pattern and repeating seasonal cycles throughout the year. Non-statisticians might search for this under terms like "forecasting with seasonal patterns," "accounting for trends and repeating cycles," "seasonal adjustment," "smoothing methods for data with ups and downs," or "predicting quarterly sales with growth and seasonal effects."

The data set consists of quarterly sales data. The season is
1 year and since there are 4 quarters per year, \(L=4\).
Using the formula we obtain:
$$ \begin{eqnarray}
b_1 & = & \frac{1}{4} \left[ \left( \frac{y5-y1}{4} \right) + \left( \frac{y6-y2}{4} \right)
+ \left( \frac{y7-y3}{4} \right) + \left( \frac{y8-y4}{4} \right) \right] \\
& & \\
& & \\
& = & \frac{1}{4} \left[ \left( \frac{382-362}{4} \right) + \left( \frac{409-385}{4} \right)
+ \left( \frac{498-432}{4} \right) + \left( \frac{387-341}{4} \right) \right] \\
& & \\
& & \\
& = & \frac{5 + 6 + 16.5 + 11.5}{4} = 9.75 \, .
\end{eqnarray} $$

title: Stationarity
section: Process or Product Monitoring and Control
text:
To make data with trends or patterns "behave normally" for analysis, you can use differencing (subtracting each value from the previous one) or remove long-term drift by fitting a curve and analyzing what's left over—techniques called stationarity transformation that let you work with time series data that isn't sitting around a stable average. Someone looking for this concept might search for "removing trends from time series," "making non-stationary data stationary," "detrending," "how to fix data that keeps going up," or "preparing time series for forecasting."

If the time series is not stationary, we can often transform it
to stationarity with one of the following techniques.
We can difference the data. That is, given the series \(Z_t\),
we create the new series
$$ Y_i = Z_i - Z_{i-1} \, . $$
The differenced data will contain one less point than the
original data. Although you can difference the data more
than once, one difference is usually sufficient.
If the data contain a trend, we can fit some type of
curve to the data and then model the residuals from that
fit. Since the purpose of the fit is to simply remove
long term trend, a simple fit, such as a straight
line, is typically used.
For non-constant variance, taking the logarithm or
square root of the series may stabilize the variance.
For negative data, you can add a suitable constant to make
all the data positive before applying the transformation.
This constant can then be subtracted from the model
to obtain predicted (i.e., the fitted) values and forecasts
for future points.
The above techniques are intended to generate series with constant
location and scale. Although seasonality also violates stationarity,
this is usually explicitly incorporated into the time series model.

title: Seasonality
section: Process or Product Monitoring and Control
text:
This page covers **seasonality detection** using graphical techniques including run sequence plots, seasonal subseries plots, box plots, and autocorrelation plots—methods that help engineers identify repeating patterns or cycles in production data so they can distinguish normal recurring fluctuations from actual process problems. If your measurements go up and down at regular intervals (like higher defect rates every Monday, or seasonal demand spikes), these plots reveal whether you're looking at predictable cycles versus real changes you need to fix. Search alternatives: "detecting repeating patterns in data," "finding cyclical trends," "identifying regular ups and downs in measurements," or "spotting recurring time-based patterns."

The following graphical techniques can be used to detect seasonality.
A
run sequence
plot
will often show seasonality.
A
seasonal subseries plot
is a
specialized technique for showing seasonality.
Multiple
box
plots
can be used as an alternative to the seasonal
subseries plot to detect seasonality.
The
autocorrelation
plot
can help identify seasonality.
Examples of each of these plots will be shown below.
The run sequence plot is a recommended first step for analyzing any
time series. Although seasonality can sometimes be indicated with
this plot, seasonality is shown more clearly by the seasonal
subseries plot or the box plot. The seasonal subseries plot does an
excellent job of showing both the seasonal differences (between
group patterns) and also the within-group patterns. The box plot
shows the seasonal difference (between group patterns) quite well,
but it does not show within group patterns. However, for large
data sets, the box plot is usually easier to read than the seasonal
subseries plot.
Both the seasonal subseries plot and the box plot assume that the
seasonal periods are known. In most cases, the analyst will in fact
know this. For example, for monthly data, the period is 12 since
there are 12 months in a year. However, if the period is not known,
the autocorrelation plot can help. If there is significant
seasonality, the autocorrelation plot should show spikes at lags
equal to the period. For example, for monthly data, if there is a
seasonality effect, we would expect to see significant peaks
at lag 12, 24, 36, and so on (although the intensity may decrease
the further out we go).

title: Common Approaches to Univariate Time Series
section: Process or Product Monitoring and Control
text:
The **moving average (MA) model** is a time series forecasting method that predicts future values by treating them as a linear combination of past random shocks or prediction errors, solving the practical problem of smoothing out noise and irregularities in data to make more stable, reliable predictions without needing to know what caused past variations. Someone looking for this technique might search for "error-based forecasting," "shock-based prediction," "smoothing random fluctuations," "lag of errors model," or "what happened last time affects what happens now."

Another common approach for modeling univariate time series models
is the moving average (MA) model:
$$ X_t = \mu + A_t - \theta_1 A_{t-1} - \theta_2 A_{t-2} - \cdots - \theta_q A_{t-q} \, $$
where \(X_t\)
is the time series, \(\mu\)
is the mean of the series, \(A_{t-i}\)
are white noise terms, and \(\theta_1, \, \ldots, \, \theta_q\)
are the parameters of the model.
The value of \(q\)
is called the order of the MA model.
That is, a moving average model is conceptually a
linear regression
of
the current value of the series against the white noise or random
shocks of one or more prior values of the series. The
random shocks at each point are assumed to come from the same
distribution, typically a normal distribution, with location at
zero and constant scale. The distinction in this model is that these
random shocks are propogated to future values of the time series.
Fitting the MA estimates is more complicated than with AR models
because the error terms are not observable. This means
that iterative non-linear fitting procedures need to be used in
place of linear least squares. MA models also have a less obvious
interpretation than AR models.
Sometimes the
ACF
and
PACF
will suggest that a MA model would be a
better model choice and sometimes both AR and MA terms should be used
in the same model (see
Section 6.4.4.5
).
Note, however, that the error terms
after
the model is fit
should be independent and follow the standard
assumptions
for a univariate process
.

title: Box-Jenkins Models
section: Process or Product Monitoring and Control
text:
Box-Jenkins models (ARIMA: AutoRegressive Integrated Moving Average) handle time series data that trends or shifts over time by applying differencing to make the data stable and predictable, solving the problem of forecasting measurements that don't stay at a constant level. If you're tracking a value that keeps going up or down instead of fluctuating around a fixed point, differencing removes that drift so your model can work correctly. You might also search for: "detrending time series," "making non-stationary data stationary," "seasonal forecasting," or "ARIMA differencing."

A couple of notes on this model.
The Box-Jenkins model assumes that the time series is
stationary
. Box and Jenkins
recommend differencing non-stationary series one or more times
to achieve stationarity. Doing so produces an ARIMA model,
with the "I" standing for "Integrated".
Some formulations transform the series by subtracting the
mean of the series from each data point. This yields a
series with a mean of zero. Whether you need to do this
or not is dependent on the software you use to estimate
the model.
Box-Jenkins models can be extended to include
seasonal
autoregressive and seasonal
moving average terms. Although this complicates the
notation and mathematics of the model, the underlying
concepts for seasonal autoregressive and seasonal moving
average terms are similar to the non-seasonal autoregressive
and moving average terms.
The most general Box-Jenkins model includes difference
operators, autoregressive terms, moving average terms,
seasonal difference operators, seasonal autoregressive
terms, and seasonal moving average terms. As with
modeling in general, however, only necessary terms should
be included in the model. Those interested in the
mathematical details can consult
Box, Jenkins and
Reisel (1994)
,
Chatfield (1996)
,
or
Brockwell and Davis
(2002)
.

title: Box-Jenkins Model Identification
section: Process or Product Monitoring and Control
text:
To identify how many past values affect your current measurement in an autoregressive model, you use the **autocorrelation function (ACF)** and **partial autocorrelation function (PACF)** — tools that show you the decay pattern of dependencies over time so you can determine the correct model order without guessing. Engineers and technicians use these plots to figure out "how far back do I need to look" when setting up a forecasting or control system, avoiding over-complicated or under-specified models. Other names for this same concept include lag analysis, correlation decay plots, determining AR model order, or identifying dependency depth.

Specifically, for an AR(1) process, the sample autocorrelation
function should have an exponentially decreasing appearance.
However, higher-order AR processes are often a mixture of
exponentially decreasing and damped sinusoidal components.
For higher-order autoregressive processes, the sample
autocorrelation needs to be supplemented with a partial
autocorrelation plot. The partial autocorrelation of an
AR(\(p\))
process becomes zero at lag \(p + 1\)
and greater, so we examine the sample partial autocorrelation
function to see if there is evidence of a departure from zero. This
is usually determined by placing a 95 % confidence interval on the
sample partial autocorrelation plot (most software programs that
generate sample autocorrelation plots will also plot this confidence
interval). If the software program does not generate
the confidence band, it is approximately \(\pm 2/\sqrt{N}\),
with \(N\)
denoting the sample size.

title: Box-Jenkins Model Diagnostics
section: Process or Product Monitoring and Control
text:
Box-Jenkins model diagnostics involves checking whether the residuals (leftover errors) from your time series forecast are white noise—meaning they're random, have no pattern, and don't violate assumptions—which tells you if your forecasting model is actually working properly or if you need to try a different approach. Engineers and analysts use this validation step to confirm their ARIMA or SARIMA model fits the data well before deploying it for predictions. Other names for this same process include residual analysis, error term validation, assumption checking for time series models, or verifying that forecast errors are random and unpatterned.

Model diagnostics for Box-Jenkins models is similar to model
validation for
non-linear
least squares fitting
.
That is, the error term \(A_t\)
is assumed to
follow the
assumptions for
a stationary univariate process
. The residuals should be white
noise (or independent when their distributions are normal) drawings
from a fixed distribution with a constant mean and variance. If
the Box-Jenkins model is a good model for the data, the residuals
should satisfy these assumptions.
If these assumptions are not satisfied, we need to fit a more
appropriate model. That is, we go back to the model identification
step and try to develop a better model. Hopefully the analysis of
the residuals can provide some clues as to a more appropriate model.

title: Example of Univariate Box-Jenkins Analysis
section: Process or Product Monitoring and Control
text:
This page demonstrates **autocorrelation function (ACF) analysis**, a technique that identifies patterns in sequential measurement data by measuring how strongly each data point relates to previous points at different time intervals. For engineers monitoring production or processes over time, ACF analysis helps you figure out what type of forecasting model will work best by revealing whether your data has repeating cycles, trends, or random noise. People also search for this concept using terms like "lag analysis," "time series correlation," "checking for patterns in historical data," or "deciding between ARIMA models."

We compute the
autocorrelation function
(ACF) of the data for the first 35 lags to determine the
type of model to fit to the data. We list the numeric results and
plot the ACF (along with 95 % confidence limits) versus the lag number.

title: Example of Multivariate Time Series Analysis
section: Process or Product Monitoring and Control
text:
This page demonstrates **Vector Autoregressive (VAR) or multivariate autoregressive modeling**, which solves the practical problem of predicting how two related process measurements (like gas flow rate and CO₂ output) affect each other over time so you can spot when the system starts behaving abnormally. Engineers and technicians might search for this using terms like "multivariate time series forecasting," "correlated sensor monitoring," "multiple related process variables," or "how one process output influences another."

The scalar form of the ARV(2) model is the following.
$$ \begin{eqnarray}
x_t & = & \phi_{1.11}x_{t-1} + \phi_{2.11}x_{t-2} +
\phi_{1.12}y_{t-1} + \phi_{2.12}y_{t-2} + a_{1t} \\
& & \\
y_t & = & \phi_{1.22}y_{t-1} + \phi_{2.22}y_{t-2} +
\phi_{1.21}x_{t-1} + \phi_{2.21}x_{t-2} + a_{2t}
\end{eqnarray} $$

corresponds to gas rate while the equation for \(y_t\)
corresponds to CO\(_2\)
concentration.

title: Mean Vector and Covariance Matrix
section: Process or Product Monitoring and Control
text:
This section covers **mean vector and covariance matrix** calculation, which helps you summarize the central tendency and spread of multiple related measurements so you can spot patterns in how your process variables move together and detect when something goes wrong. You might also search for this using plain terms like "average and variation of multiple measurements," "how variables relate to each other," "multivariate process statistics," "sample mean and variance-covariance matrix," or "summarizing groups of related data points."

The results are:
$$ {\bf \bar{x}} = \left[ \begin{array}{ccc}
4.10 & 2.08 & 0.604
\end{array} \right] $$
$$ {\bf S} = \left[ \begin{array}{ccc}
0.025 & 0.0075 & 0.00175 \\
0.0075 & 0.0070 & 0.00135 \\
0.00175 & 0.00135 & 0.00043
\end{array} \right] $$
where the mean vector contains the arithmetic averages of the three
variables and the (unbiased) variance-covariance matrix \({\bf S}\)
is calculated by
$$ S = \frac{1}{n-1} \sum_{i=1}^n (X_i - \bar{X})(X_i - \bar{X})' \, , $$
where \(n=5\)
for this example.
Thus, 0.025 is the variance of the length variable, 0.0075 is the
covariance between the length and the width variables, 0.00175 is the
covariance between the length and the height variables, 0.007 is the
variance of the width variable, 0.00135 is the covariance between the
width and height variables and 0.00043 is the variance of the height
variable.

title: Numerical Examples
section: Process or Product Monitoring and Control
text:
This page explains **quadratic forms** and **matrix multiplication**, showing how to combine a row vector, a square matrix, and a column vector to get a single number—a calculation engineers use to compute weighted sums of squared or cross-product terms in control charts and variance calculations. Someone looking for this concept might search for "weighted sum of squares," "scalar from matrix multiplication," "combining vectors and matrices into one number," or "computing variance-like metrics with matrices."

It is not possible to pre-multiply a matrix by a column vector,
nor to post-multiply a matrix by a row vector. The matrix product
\({\bf a}'{\bf Ba}\)
yields a scalar and is called a quadratic form. Note
that \({\bf B}\)
must be a square matrix if \({\bf a}'{\bf Ba}\)
is to conform
to multiplication. Here is an example of a quadratic form
$$
{\bf a}'{\bf Ba} =
\left[ \begin{array}{cc} 2 & 3 \end{array} \right]
\left[ \begin{array}{cc} 1 & 2 \\ 3 & 1 \end{array} \right]
\left[ \begin{array}{c} 2 \\ 3 \end{array} \right] =
\left[ \begin{array}{cc} 11 & 7 \end{array} \right]
\left[ \begin{array}{c} 2 \\ 3 \end{array} \right] = 43 \, .
$$

title: Determinant and Eigenstructure
section: Process or Product Monitoring and Control
text:
This page covers the **covariance matrix determinant and eigenstructure analysis**, which helps engineers detect when multiple related measurements are moving together in unexpected ways or when a process has lost its normal variability pattern. If you're looking for this concept but don't know the name, you might search for "correlation matrix determinant," "multivariate variation detection," "data spread measurement," "process capability for multiple variables," or "detecting hidden relationships between measurements."

Of great interest in statistics is the determinant of a square
symmetric matrix \({\bf D}\)
whose diagonal elements are sample
variances and whose off-diagonal elements are sample covariances.
Symmetry means that the matrix and its transpose are identical
(i.e., \({\bf A} = {\bf A}'\)).
An example is
$$ {\bf D} = \left[ \begin{array}{cccc}
s_1^2 & s_1 s_2 r_{12} & \cdots & s_1 s_p r_{1p} \\
s_2 s_1 r_{21} & s_2^2 & \cdots & s_2 s_p r_{2p} \\
\vdots & \vdots & & \vdots \\
s_p s_1 r_{p1} & s_p s_2 r_{p2} & \cdots & s_p^2
\end{array} \right] \, . $$
where \(s_1\) and \(s_2\)
are sample standard deviations and \(r_{ij}\)
is the sample correlation.
\({\bf D}\)
is the
sample variance-covariance matrix
for
observations of a multivariate vector of \(p\)
elements. The determinant of \({\bf D}\),
in this case, is sometimes called the
generalized variance.

title: The Multivariate Normal Distribution
section: Process or Product Monitoring and Control
text:
The **multivariate normal distribution** describes how multiple related measurements vary together, allowing you to model and monitor product quality when you're tracking several dimensions at once (like length, width, and weight together) rather than one at a time. This solves the practical problem of detecting when a process goes out of control in ways that a single measurement might miss, because it accounts for how changes in one variable relate to changes in others. Alternative search terms include: joint normal distribution, Gaussian distribution for multiple variables, correlated measurements model, or multivariate quality control.

A \(p\)-dimensional
vector of random variables,
$$ {\bf X} = X_1, \, X_2, \, \ldots, \, X_p \,\,\,\,\,\, -\infty < X_i < \infty, \,\, i = 1, \, \ldots, \, p \, , $$
is said to have a multivariate normal distribution if its
density function \(f({\bf X})\)
is of the form
$$ \begin{eqnarray}
f({\bf X}) & = & f(X_1, \, X_2, \, \ldots, \, X_p) \\
& = & \left( \frac{1}{2 \pi} \right)^{p / 2} |{\bf \Sigma}|^{-1/2} \mbox{exp}
\left[ -\frac{1}{2} ({\bf X} - {\bf m})' {\bf \Sigma}^{-1} ({\bf X} - {\bf m}) \right] \, ,
\end{eqnarray} $$
where \({\bf m} = (m_1, \, \ldots, \, m_p)\)
is the vector of means and \({\bf \Sigma}\)
is the variance-covariance matrix of the multivariate normal distribution.
The shortcut notation for this density is
$$ {\bf X} = \mbox{N}_p ({\bf m}, {\bf \Sigma}) \, . $$

title:
T2Chart for Subgroup Averages --
Phase I
section: Process or Product Monitoring and Control
text:
This page explains how to calculate a **variance-covariance matrix** and **sample variances** by averaging measurements across multiple subgroups in Phase I control charting, which solves the practical problem of understanding how different process measurements vary together and individually so you can set up baseline control limits before monitoring production. Engineers looking for this concept might search for "pooled variance," "within-subgroup variation," "combining variance estimates across samples," or "how to establish process baseline statistics for control charts."

The variances and covariances are similarly averaged over the subgroups.
Specifically, the \(s_{ij}\)
elements of the variance-covariance matrix \({\bf S}\)
are obtained as
$$ s_{ij} = \frac{1}{k} \sum_{l=1}^k s_{ijl} \, , $$
with \(s_{ijl}\) for \(i \ne j\)
denoting the sample covariance between variables \(X_i\) and \(X_j\)
for the \(l\)th
subgroup, and \(s_{ij}\) for \(i = j\)
denotes the sample variance of \(X_i\).
The variances \(s_{ijl}^2 (=s_{iil})\)
for subgroup \(l\)
and for variables \(i = 1, \, 2, \, \ldots, \, p\)
are computed as
$$ \frac{1}{n-1} \sum_{r=1}^n (x_{ilr} - \bar{x}_{il})^2 \, . $$
Similarly, the covariances \(s_{ijl}\)
between variables \(X_i\) and \(X_j\)
for subgroup \(l\)
are computed as
$$ \frac{1}{n-1} \sum_{r=1}^n (x_{ilr} - \bar{x}_{il})(x_{jlr} - \bar{x}_{jl}) \, . $$

title:
T2Chart for Subgroup
Averages -- Phase II
section: Process or Product Monitoring and Control
text:
This section explains **Hotelling's T² statistic for Phase II multivariate control charting**, which lets you monitor whether future batches or samples are staying on target when measuring multiple quality characteristics simultaneously. The practical problem it solves is detecting when a product's overall performance drifts out of spec across several related measurements at once—something a simple go/no-go check can't catch. Related searches: multivariate process control, multi-characteristic monitoring, detecting shifts in multiple variables, T-squared chart, statistical process control for multiple properties, comparing sample means to baseline performance.

To illustrate, assume that \(a\)
a
subgroups had been discarded (with possibly \(a = 0\))
so that \(k - a\)
subgroups are used in obtaining \({\bf S}_p\) and \(\bar{\bar{\bf x}}\).
We shall let these two values be represented by
\({\bf S}_p^\bullet\) and \(\bar{\bar{\bf x}}^\bullet\)
to distinguish them from the original values, \({\bf S}_p\) and \(\bar{\bar{\bf x}}\),
before any subgroups are deleted. Future values to be plotted
on the multivariate chart would then be obtained from
$$ n (\bar{{\bf x}}^{(future)} - \bar{\bar{{\bf x}}}^\bullet)' ({\bf S}_p^\bullet)^{-1}
(\bar{{\bf x}}^{(future)} - \bar{\bar{{\bf x}}}^\bullet) \, ,$$
with \(\bar{{\bf x}}^{(future)}\)
denoting an arbitrary vector containing the averages for the \(p\)
characteristics for a single subgroup obtained in the future. Each of
these future values would be plotted on the multivariate chart and
compared with

title: Chart for Individual Observations -- Phase I
section: Process or Product Monitoring and Control
text:
This section covers **multivariate control limits** for **Phase I monitoring** using the **beta distribution**, which solves the practical problem of detecting when multiple related measurements on a product or process are going out of spec—telling you when something is wrong with your system before defects pile up. Alternative search terms include "multivariate process control," "monitoring multiple variables at once," "control chart for several measurements together," "Tracy Young Mason limits," or "beta distribution control limits."

Each value of \(Q_j\)
is compared against control limits of
$$ \begin{eqnarray}
LCL & = & \left( \frac{(m-1)^2}{m} \right) B \left( 1-\frac{\alpha}{2}; \, \frac{p}{2}; \, \frac{m-p-1}{2} \right) \\
& & \\
UCL & = & \left( \frac{(m-1)^2}{m} \right) B \left( \frac{\alpha}{2}; \, \frac{p}{2}; \, \frac{m-p-1}{2} \right) \\
\end{eqnarray} $$
with \(B(\cdot)\)
denoting the
beta distribution
with
parameters \(p/2\) and \((m-p-1)/2\).
These limits are due to Tracy, Young and Mason (1992). Note that a
LCL
is stated, unlike the other multivariate control chart procedures
given in this section. Although interest will generally be centered
at the
UCL
, a value of \(Q\)
below the
LCL
should also be investigated, as this could signal
problems in data recording.

title: Hotelling'sT2Chart
section: Process or Product Monitoring and Control
text:
Hotelling's T² chart is a multivariate control chart that monitors multiple related quality measurements simultaneously to detect when a process goes out of control. Instead of watching each measurement separately, this method catches problems that only show up when you look at how the measurements work together, making it useful for catching process issues that individual charts might miss. Engineers might search for this using terms like "multivariate process control," "multiple quality characteristic monitoring," "T-squared control chart," or "simultaneous monitoring of correlated measurements."

Inspection of this figure suggests an out-of-control conditions at
sample numbers 1, 2, 9, 10, and 11 since these points exceed the
T-square for means
UCL
of 14.52. Fortunately, the process was
apparently back in control after the periods 9-12.
The problem may be caused by one or more univariates, and it could be
easier to remedy than when the covariance plays a role in the creation
of the undesired situation. To study the cause or causes of the problem
one should also contemplate the individual univariate control charts.�

title: Properties of Principal Components
section: Process or Product Monitoring and Control
text:
Principal component analysis (PCA) uses eigenvalues and eigenvectors of a correlation matrix to reduce the number of measurements you need to monitor without losing the important information in your data. If you're collecting data from many sensors or variables and want to simplify your monitoring system, PCA identifies which combinations of those measurements capture most of the variation. People also search for this using terms like "dimensionality reduction," "data compression," "feature extraction," "finding dominant patterns in data," or "simplifying multivariate monitoring."

In summary, here are the main steps to obtain the eigenstructure for
a correlation matrix.
Compute \({\bf R}\),
the correlation matrix of the original
data. \({\bf R}\)
is also the correlation matrix of the
standardized data.
Obtain the characteristic equation of \({\bf R}\)
which is a
polynomial of degree \(p\)
(the number of variables),
obtained from expanding the determinant of \(|{\bf R} - \lambda{\bf I}|=0\)
and solving for the roots \(\lambda_p\),
that is: \(\lambda_1, \, \lambda_2, \, \ldots, \, \lambda_p\).
Then solve for the columns of the \({\bf V}\)
matrix, (\({\bf v}_1, \, {\bf v}_2\, \, \ldots, \, {\bf v}_p\)).
The roots, \(\lambda_i\),
are called the
eigenvalue
s (or
latent values). The columns of \({\bf V}\)
are called the
eigenvectors
.

title: Numerical Example
section: Process or Product Monitoring and Control
text:
This page demonstrates **factor analysis** and the computation of the **factor loading matrix** (also called the **structure matrix**), which simplifies complex correlations among multiple measurements by identifying a smaller set of underlying patterns or hidden drivers—solving the practical problem of figuring out which variables move together and what's really causing the variation you're seeing in your process data. The factor loadings tell engineers how strongly each original measurement relates to these underlying factors, helping them reduce monitoring complexity and focus on what actually matters. Alternative searches: principal components, latent factors, dimensionality reduction, variable relationships, correlation structure, hidden variables, measurement patterns.

Now form the matrix \({\bf L}^{1/2}\),
which is a diagonal matrix whose elements are the
square roots of the eigenvalues of \({\bf R}\).
Then obtain \({\bf S}\),
the factor structure, using \({\bf S} = {\bf VL}^{1/2}\).
$$ \left[ \begin{array}{rrr}
0.64 & 0.38 & -0.66 \\
0.69 & 0.10 & 0.72 \\
-0.34 & 0.91 & 0.20
\end{array} \right]
\left[ \begin{array}{ccc}
1.33 & 0 & 0 \\
0 & 0.96 & 0 \\
0 & 0 & 0.55
\end{array} \right]
=
\left[ \begin{array}{rrr}
0.85 & 0.37 & -0.37 \\
0.91 & 0.10 & 0.40 \\
-0.45 & 0.88 & 0.11
\end{array} \right] $$
So, for example, 0.91 is the correlation between the second variable and the
first principal component.

title: Likelihood ratio tests
text:
**Likelihood ratio tests** compare simplified statistical models against more complex ones by checking if the extra parameters actually improve the fit to your data—helping engineers decide whether to use a simpler exponential model instead of a more complicated Weibull distribution, or to treat test cells as separate groups versus pooling them together. This concept is also known as **model comparison**, **nested model testing**, **chi-square goodness-of-fit testing**, or asking "do I really need all these parameters?" when fitting reliability data.

i) It is suspected that a type of data, typically modeled by
a Weibull distribution, can be fit adequately by an exponential model.
The exponential distribution is a special case of the Weibull, with the
shape parameter \(\gamma\)
set to 1. If we write the Weibull likelihood function for the data, the
exponential model likelihood function is obtained by setting \(\gamma\)
to 1, and the number of unknown parameters has been reduced from two to
one.
ii) Assume we have \(n\)
cells of data from an acceleration test,
with each cell having a different operating temperature. We assume a lognormal
population model applies in every cell. Without an acceleration model assumption,
the likelihood of the experimental data would be the product of the likelihoods
from each cell and there would be \(2n\)
unknown parameters (a different \(T_{50}\) and \(\sigma\)
for each cell). If we assume an
Arrhenius model
applies, the total number of parameters drops from \(2n\)
to just 3, the single common \(\sigma\)
and the Arrhenius \(A\) and \(\Delta H\)
parameters. This acceleration assumption "saves" \((2n-3)\)
parameters.
iii) We life test samples of product from two vendors. The product is
known to have a failure mechanism modeled by the Weibull distribution,
and we want to know whether there is a difference in reliability between
the vendors. The unrestricted likelihood of the data is the product of
the two likelihoods, with 4 unknown parameters (the shape and characteristic
life for each vendor population). If, however, we assume no difference
between vendors, the likelihood reduces to having only two unknown parameters
(the common shape and the common characteristic life). Two parameters are
"lost" by the assumption of "no difference".
Clearly, we could come up with many more examples like these three, for
which an important assumption can be restated as a reduction or restriction
on the number of parameters used to formulate the likelihood function of
the data. In all these cases, there is a simple and very useful way to
test whether the assumption is consistent with the data.
The Likelihood Ratio Test Procedure

title: Graphical Representation of the Data
section: Process or Product Monitoring and Control
text:
The 4-plot analysis combines run sequence plots, lag plots, and histograms to detect whether your process is stable and consistent over time or if something is causing it to drift and change. This helps manufacturers and engineers spot when a machine or process needs adjustment because the output quality or measurements are no longer staying in the same range. You might also search for "process stability monitoring," "detecting process shifts," "time-series stability checks," or "identifying when processes go out of control."

This
4-plot
shows the following.
The
run sequence
plot
(upper left) indicates that the location and scale are
not constant over time. This indicates that the three factors
do in fact have an effect of some kind.
The
lag plot
(upper
right) indicates that there is some mild autocorrelation in the
data. This is not unexpected as the data are grouped in a
logical order of the three factors (i.e., not randomly) and the
run sequence plot indicates that there are factor effects.
The
histogram
(lower left) shows that most of the data fall between 1 and 5,
with the center of the data at about 2.2.
Due to the non-constant location and scale and autocorrelation
in the data, distributional inferences from the
normal
probability plot
(lower right) are not meaningful.
The run sequence plot is shown at full size to show greater detail. In
addition, a numerical summary of the data is generated.

title: Subgroup Analysis
section: Process or Product Monitoring and Control
chapter:
equating mean squares (MS) to
expected mean squares (EMS).
text:
This page explains **nested random effects ANOVA** and **variance components analysis**, which helps you figure out how much of your measurement variation comes from different sources (like which cassette, which wafer within that cassette, or which measurement spot on the wafer) so you can identify where problems are actually occurring. Someone might search for this using terms like "breaking down sources of variation," "where does my process scatter come from," "multi-level variation analysis," "hierarchical ANOVA," or "apportioning variability to different factors."

If your software does not generate the variance components directly,
they can be computed from a standard analysis of variance output by
equating mean squares (MS) to
expected mean squares (EMS).
The sum of squares and mean squares
for a nested, random effects model are shown below.
Degrees of Sum of
Source Freedom Squares Mean Squares
-------------------- ---------- --------- ------------
Cassette 29 127.40293 4.3932
Wafer(Cassette) 60 25.52089 0.4253
Site(Cassette, Wafer) 360 63.17865 0.1755
The expected mean squares for cassette, wafer within cassette, and
site within cassette and wafer, along with their
associated mean squares, are the following.
4.3932 = (3*5)*Var(cassettes) + 5*Var(wafer) + Var(site)
0.4253 = 5*Var(wafer) + Var(site)
0.1755 = Var(site)
Solving these equations, we obtain the variance component estimates
0.2645, 0.04997, and 0.1755 for cassettes, wafers, and sites,
respectively.
All of the analyses in this section can be completed using
R code
.
The reader can download the data as a
text file
.

title: Shewhart Control Chart
section: Process or Product Monitoring and Control
text:
When monitoring processes with batch-to-batch variation, you need separate control charts using lot-to-lot variation instead of within-lot variation to avoid falsely detecting problems that are actually normal differences between batches. This approach—combining individuals/moving range charts with lot means charts—solves the practical problem of distinguishing between acceptable batch-to-batch differences and real process drift that requires corrective action. Related searches: "batch control charts," "lot variation analysis," "between-batch vs within-batch monitoring," "multi-level control charting," or "stratified process control."

A commonly applied solution is the first option; have multiple
charts on this process. When creating the control limits for the
lot means, care must be taken to use the lot-to-lot variation
instead of the within lot variation. The resulting control charts
are: the standard individuals/moving range charts (as seen
previously), and a control chart on the lot means that is
different from the previous lot means chart. This new chart uses
the lot-to-lot variation to calculate control limits instead of
the average within-lot standard deviation. The accompanying
standard deviation chart is the same as seen previously.

text:
This example teaches **control charts (moving mean, moving range, mean, and standard deviation charts)** and **variance components analysis**, which help manufacturing teams spot when a process is drifting out of spec and pinpoint whether problems are coming from equipment differences, location differences, or natural variation. Engineers use these charts to catch bad batches before shipping and to figure out which machines or production lines are causing most of the quality problems. You might also search for this under "process capability analysis," "SPC charts," "out-of-control points detection," "variance decomposition," or "identifying sources of variation in manufacturing."

1. The moving mean plot shows
a large number of out-of-
control points.
2. The moving range plot shows
a large number of out-of-
control points.
3. The mean control chart shows
a large number of out-of-
control points.
4. The sd control chart shows
no out-of-control points.
5. The mean control chart shows
a large number of out-of-
control points.
6. The sd control chart shows
no out-of-control points.
7. The analysis of variance and
components of variance
calculations show that
cassette to cassette
variation is 54% of the total
and site to site variation
is 36% of the total.
8. The mean control chart shows one
point that is on the boundary of
being out of control.
