title: Model Identification
section: Process or Product Monitoring and Control
text:
A **run sequence plot** and **stationarity analysis** help you check if your process data is stable and predictable over time by spotting trends, jumps, or repeating patterns that would make forecasts unreliable. If your data drifts or cycles, you can fix it through **differencing** (subtracting consecutive values) or **trend removal** before fitting a **Box-Jenkins (ARIMA) model** to predict future behavior. Search alternatives: "time series stability check," "detecting process drift," "removing trends from data," "seasonal pattern identification," or "data preprocessing for forecasting."

The first step in the analysis is to generate a
run sequence
plot
of the response variable. A run sequence plot can indicate
stationarity
(i.e., constant location and scale), the presence of outliers, and
seasonal patterns.
Non-stationarity can often be removed by differencing the data or
fitting some type of trend curve. We would then attempt to fit a
Box-Jenkins model to the differenced data or to the residuals after
fitting a trend curve.
Although Box-Jenkins models can estimate seasonal components, the
analyst needs to specify the seasonal period (for example, 12 for
monthly data). Seasonal components are common for economic time series.
They are less common for engineering and scientific data.

title: Model Estimation
section: Process or Product Monitoring and Control
text:
This page explains **autoregressive (AR) models** and **parameter estimation with confidence intervals**, which help engineers predict the next value in a time-series measurement by using the previous two measurements—solving the practical problem of forecasting what comes next in a process when past readings influence future ones. Someone looking for this concept might search for "predicting future values from past data," "how to use yesterday's numbers to forecast tomorrow," "time series forecasting," "auto-correlation modeling," or "building equations from historical patterns."

The following parameter estimates were computed for the AR(2)
model based on the differenced data.
Parameter Standard 95 % Confidence
Source Estimate Error Interval
------ --------- -------- ----------------
Intercept -0.0050 0.0119
AR1 -0.4064 0.0419 (-0.4884, -0.3243)
AR2 -0.1649 0.0419 (-0.2469, -0.0829)
Number of Observations: 558
Degrees of Freedom: 558 - 3 = 555
Residual Standard Deviation: 0.4423
Both AR parameters are significant since the
confidence intervals do not contain zero.
The model for the differenced data, \(Y_t\),
is an AR(2) model,
$$ Y_{t} = -0.4064 Y_{t-1} - 0.1649 Y_{t-2} - 0.0050 \, , $$
with \(\sigma = 0.4423\).
It is often more convenient to express the model in terms of the
original data, \(X_t\),
rather than the differenced data.
From the definition of the difference, \(Y_t = X_t - X_{t-1}\),
we can make the appropriate substitutions into the above equation,
$$ X_{t} - X_{t-1} = -0.4064 (X_{t-1} - X_{t-2}) - 0.1649 (X_{t-2} - X_{t-3}) - 0.0050 \, , $$
to arrive at the model in terms of the original series,
$$ X_{t} = 0.5936 X_{t-1} + 0.2415 X_{t-2} + 0.1649 X_{t-3} - 0.0050 \, . $$

title: Model Validation
section: Process or Product Monitoring and Control
text:
A **4-plot residual analysis** (combining run sequence, lag, histogram, and normal probability plots) checks whether a fitted model's predictions are trustworthy by verifying that the leftover errors behave randomly, don't repeat patterns, and follow a normal bell curve—telling you whether your model is actually capturing the real behavior or just getting lucky. Someone looking to validate that their regression model or process equation is working correctly might search for "checking if my model is good," "residual diagnostics," "model adequacy testing," "validating regression assumptions," or "do my model errors look random."

We can make the following conclusions based on the above 4-plot.
The
run sequence
plot
shows that the residuals do not violate the assumption
of constant location and scale. It also shows that most of the
residuals are in the range (-1, 1).
The
lag plot
indicates that the residuals are not autocorrelated at lag 1.
The
histogram
and
normal probability
plot
indicate that the normal distribution provides an
adequate fit for this model.
This 4-plot of the residuals indicates that the fitted model is
adequate for the data.

title: Work This Example Yourself
section: Process or Product Monitoring and Control
text:
Process monitoring and control helps manufacturers detect when their production is going off track so they can fix problems before making bad parts, using statistical methods to watch whether ongoing output stays within acceptable limits. This is also called quality control monitoring, real-time process checking, or production surveillance—terms used interchangeably when workers need to track if a machine or assembly line is still running right.

This page allows you to repeat the analysis outlined in the
case study description on the previous page using
Dataplot
. It is required that you have already
downloaded and installed
Dataplot and
configured your browser
. to run Dataplot. Output from each analysis
step below will be displayed in one or more of the Dataplot windows.
The four main windows are the Output Window, the Graphics window, the
Command History window, and the data sheet window. Across the top of
the main windows there are menus for executing Dataplot commands.
Across the bottom is a command entry window where commands can be typed
in.

title: Trend tests
text:
The Reverse Arrangement Test is a trend test that uses inter-arrival times (waiting periods between consecutive failures) to detect whether a system's reliability is getting better or worse over time. This helps maintenance engineers figure out if their equipment is improving, degrading, or staying stable by checking if failures are happening closer together or farther apart as time goes on. People might search for this under alternative names like "failure pattern analysis," "time between failures test," "reliability degradation detection," or "NHPP trend detection."

The Reverse Arrangement Test
Assume there are \(r\)
repairs during the observation period and
they occurred at system ages \(T_1, \, T_2, \, T_3, \, \ldots, \, T_r\).
(We set the start of the observation period to \(T = 0\)).
Let \(I_1 = T_1, \, I_2 = T_2 - T_1, \, I_3 = T_3 - T_2, \, \ldots, \, I_r = T_r - T_{r-1}\)
be the inter-arrival times for repairs (i.e., the sequence of waiting times
between failures). Assume the observation period ends at time \(T_{end} > T_r\).
Previously, we plotted this sequence of inter-arrival times to look
for evidence of trends. Now, we calculate how many instances we have of
a later inter-arrival time being strictly greater than an earlier inter-arrival
time. Each time that happens, we call it a
reversal
. If there are
a lot of reversals (more than are likely from pure chance with no trend),
we have significant evidence of an improvement trend. If there are too
few reversals we have significant evidence of degradation.
A formal definition of the reversal count and some properties of this
count are:
count a reversal every time \(I_j < I_k\)
for some \(j\) and \(k\) with \(j < k\)
this reversal count is the total number of reversals \(R\)
for \(r\)
repair times, the maximum possible number of reversals is \(r(r-1)/2\)
if there are no trends, on the average one would expect to have \(r(r-1)/4\)
reversals.
As a simple example, assume we have 5 repair times at system ages 22, 58,
71, 156 and 225, and the observation period ended at system age 300 . First
calculate the inter arrival times and obtain: 22, 36, 13, 85, 69. Next,
count reversals by "putting your finger" on the first inter-arrival time,
22, and counting how many later inter arrival times are greater than that.
In this case, there are 3. Continue by "moving your finger" to the second
time, 36, and counting how many later times are greater. There are exactly
2. Repeating this for the third and fourth inter-arrival times (with many
repairs, your finger gets very tired!) we obtain 2 and 0 reversals, respectively.
Adding 3 + 2 + 2 + 0 = 7, we see that \(R\) = 7.
The total possible
number of reversals is 5x4/2 = 10 and an "average" number is half this,
or 5.
In the example, we saw 7 reversals (2 more than average). Is this strong
evidence for an improvement trend? The following table allows us to answer
that at a 90 % or 95 % or 99 % confidence level - the higher the confidence,
the stronger the evidence of improvement (or the less likely that pure
chance alone produced the result).

title: Chi-square goodness-of-fit test
text:
The chi-square goodness-of-fit test compares actual observed counts in grouped data against what you'd expect if a particular distribution were true, helping you figure out whether your real-world measurements actually match the theoretical pattern you're testing. If you're an engineer wondering whether your product's failure times follow an exponential distribution, or whether defects are randomly scattered across batches, this test gives you a yes/no answer. You might also search for this as "comparing observed versus expected frequencies," "testing if data fits a distribution," "categorical data hypothesis test," or "goodness of fit analysis."

The test requires that the data first be grouped. The actual
number of observations in each group is compared to the expected
number of observations and the test statistic is calculated as a
function of this difference. The number of groups and how group
membership is defined will affect the power of the test (i.e., how
sensitive it is to detecting departures from the null hypothesis).
Power will not only be affected by the number of groups and
how they are defined, but by the sample size and shape of the null and
underlying (true) distributions. Despite the lack of a clear
"best method", some useful rules of thumb can be given.

title: Kolmogorov- Smirnov test
text:
The Kolmogorov-Smirnov test is a statistical method for comparing whether sample data matches a theoretical distribution or differs between two datasets, solving the practical problem of checking if your measurements fit an expected pattern or if two batches of products are genuinely different. Critical values for this test are hard to calculate by hand for real sample sizes, so engineers typically rely on statistical software or published tables (like Birnbaum 1952), and must apply a correction factor if they estimate distribution parameters from the same data being tested. Other names to search for include: goodness-of-fit test, distribution testing, comparing data to a reference distribution, or checking whether samples come from the same population.

Critical values associated with the test statistic, \(D\),
are difficult to compute for finite sample sizes, often requiring
Monte Carlo simulation. However, some general purpose statistical
software programs support
the Kolmogorov-Smirnov test at least for some of the more
common distributions. Tabled values can be found in
Birnbaum (1952)
. A correction factor can be
applied if the parameters of the distribution are estimated with
the same data that are being tested. See
D'Agostino and Stephens (1986)
for details.

title: Confidence interval approach
text:
This page explains **confidence intervals** (also called **margin of error bands** or **uncertainty ranges**) for estimating a population mean when you only have sample data and don't know the true standard deviation—solving the practical problem of determining the range of values where the true average likely falls based on your measurements. Engineers and quality professionals use this to decide whether their process average is acceptable or whether observed differences from a target are just random variation. Related searches: "confidence limits," "estimate the true mean," "how sure am I of my average," "range for the population mean," or "acceptable variation in measurements."

Tests of hypotheses that can be made from a single sample of data
were discussed on the
foregoing page
. As
with null hypotheses, confidence intervals can be two-sided or
one-sided, depending on the question at hand. The general form of
confidence intervals, for the three cases discussed earlier, where
the standard deviation is unknown are:
Two-sided confidence interval for \(\mu\):
$$ \bar{Y} + \frac{s}{\sqrt{N}} \, t_{\alpha/2, \, N-1} \, \le \, \mu \, \le \, \bar{Y} + \frac{s}{\sqrt{N}} \, t_{1-\alpha/2, \, N-1} \, , $$
Lower one-sided confidence interval for \(\mu\):
$$ \mu \ge \bar{Y} + \frac{s}{\sqrt{N}} \, t_{\alpha, \, N-1} \, , $$
Upper one-sided confidence interval for \(\mu\):
$$ \mu \le \bar{Y} + \frac{s}{\sqrt{N}} \, t_{1-\alpha, \, N-1} \, , $$
where \(t_{\alpha/2, \, N-1}\)
is the \(\alpha/2\)
critical value from the \(t\)
distribution with \(N\)
- 1 degrees of freedom and similarly for cases (2) and (3). Critical values can be found in the
t
table
in
Chapter 1.

title: Sample sizes required
text:
This page covers **hypothesis testing, sample size determination, and power analysis** for detecting a shift in the mean relative to the standard deviation. It solves the practical problem of figuring out how many parts or measurements you need to test to reliably catch when a process average has actually changed by a meaningful amount. People might search for this using terms like "how many samples do I need to test," "detecting process shifts," "statistical power," "significance level," or "minimum sample size for quality control."

The table below gives sample sizes for a two-sided test of hypothesis
that the mean is a given value, with the shift to be detected a
multiple of the standard deviation. For a one-sided test at

title: Confidence interval approach
chapter: tests of hypothesis on the previous page
text:
This page covers **confidence intervals for standard deviation** using the **chi-square distribution**, which solves the practical problem of determining a reliable range of values for how much variation or spread exists in your manufacturing process or product measurements. An engineer might search for this using terms like "finding the range of variability," "estimating process spread," "uncertainty in standard deviation," "precision limits," or "how much does my process vary."

Confidence intervals for the true standard deviation can be
constructed using the chi-square distribution. The \(100(1-\alpha)\) %
confidence intervals that correspond to the
tests of hypothesis on the previous page
are given by
Two-sided confidence interval for \(\sigma\)
$$ \frac{s\sqrt{N-1}}{\sqrt{ \chi^2_{1-\alpha/2, N-1} }} \le \sigma \le
\frac{s\sqrt{N-1}}{\sqrt{ \chi^2_{\alpha/2, N-1} }} \, ,$$
Lower one-sided confidence interval for \(\sigma\)
$$ \sigma \ge \frac{s\sqrt{N-1}}{\sqrt{ \chi^2_{1-\alpha, N-1} }} \, ,$$
Upper one-sided confidence interval for \(\sigma\)
$$ 0 \le \sigma \le \frac{s\sqrt{N-1}}{\sqrt{ \chi^2_{\alpha, N-1} }} \, .$$
For case (1), \(\chi_{\alpha/2}^2\)
is the \(\alpha/2\)
critical value from the chi-square distribution with \(N -1\)
degrees of freedom and similarly for cases (2) and (3). Critical
values can be found in the
chi-square table
in Chapter 1.

title: Sample sizes required
text:
This page covers **sample size determination** and **statistical power analysis** using the **chi-square distribution** and **non-centrality parameter**, which solves the practical problem of figuring out how many test samples or measurements you need to collect to reliably detect a real difference of a specified size between products or processes. Someone looking for this concept might search for "how many samples do I need," "minimum number of observations for a test," "sample size calculation," "detecting a meaningful change," or "statistical power and sample requirements."

If software is available to compute the roots (or zero values) of a
univariate function, then we can determine the sample size by finding
the roots of a function that calculates \(C_\nu\)
for a given value of \(\nu\).
The procedure is:
Define constants.
$$ \begin{eqnarray}
\alpha & = & 0.05 \\
\beta & = & 0.01 \\
\delta & = & 55 \\
\sigma_0^2 & = & 100 \\
R & = & 1 + \delta / \sigma_0^2
\end{eqnarray} $$
Create a function, \(C_\nu\).
$$ C_\nu = F(F^{-1}(\alpha, \, \nu)/R, \, \nu) - \beta $$
\(F(x, \nu)\)
returns the probability of a chi-square random
variable with \(\nu\)
degrees of freedom that is less than or equal to \(x\)
and \(F^{-1}(\alpha, \, \nu)\)
returns \(x\)
such that \(F(x, \, \nu) = \alpha\).
Find the value of \(\nu\)
for which the function, \(C_\nu\), is zero.
Using this procedure, \(C_\nu\) is zero when \(\nu\)
is 169.3. Therefore, the minimum sample size needed to
guarantee the risk level is \(N\)
= 170.
Alternatively, we can determine the sample size by simply printing computed
values of \(C_\nu\) for various values of \(\nu\).
Define constants.
$$ \begin{eqnarray}
\alpha & = & 0.05 \\
\delta & = & 55 \\
\sigma_0^2 & = & 100 \\
R & = & 1 + \delta / \sigma_0^2
\end{eqnarray} $$
Generate \(C_\nu\) for values of \(\nu\)
from 1 to 200.
$$ \beta_\nu = F^{-1}(\alpha, \,\nu) / R$$
$$ C_\nu = F(\beta_\nu, \, \nu) $$
The values of \(C_\nu\) generated for \(\nu\)
between 165 and 175 degrees of freedom are shown below.
\(\nu\)
\(\beta_\nu\)
\(C_\nu\)
165 126.4344 0.0114
166 127.1380 0.0110
167 127.8414 0.0107
168 128.5446 0.0104
169 129.2477 0.0101
170 129.9506 0.0098
171 130.6533 0.0095
172 131.3558 0.0092
173 132.0582 0.0090
174 132.7604 0.0087
175 133.4625 0.0085
The value of \(C_\nu\) closest to 0.01 is 0.0101, which is associated with
\(\nu\)
= 169 degrees of freedom. Therefore,
the minimum sample size needed to guarantee the risk level is \(N\)
= 170.
The calculations used in this section can be performed using
both
Dataplot code
and
R code
.

title: Confidence intervals
text:
This passage covers **confidence intervals for proportions** (also called binomial confidence intervals), which solves the practical problem of determining a range of plausible values for the true defect rate in a batch or production line based on a sample inspection—telling you not just that 20% of your sample was bad, but how confident you can be about what the actual defect rate really is. Engineers might also search for this using terms like "tolerance limits for defect rates," "uncertainty bounds for pass/fail data," "how many defects should I expect," or "statistical limits on percentage defective."

The equations above that determine \(p_L\) and \(p_U\)
can be solved using readily available functions.
Take as an example the situation where twenty units are sampled
from a continuous production line and four items are found to be
defective. The proportion defective is estimated to be \(\hat{p}\)
= 4/20 = 0.20.
The steps for calculating a 90 % confidence interval for the true
proportion defective, \(p\)
follow.
1. Initalize constants.
alpha = 0.10
Nd = 4
N = 20
2. Define a function for upper limit (fu) and a function
for the lower limit (fl).
fu =
F
(Nd,pu,20) - alpha/2
fl =
F
(Nd-1,pl,20) - (1-alpha/2)
F
is the cumulative density function for the
binominal distribution.
3. Find the value of pu that corresponds to fu = 0 and
the value of pl that corresponds to fl = 0 using software
to find the roots of a function.
The values of \(p_U\) and \(p_L\) for our example are:
pu = 0.401029
pl = 0.071354
Thus, a 90 % confidence interval for the proportion defective, \(p\),
is
(0.071, 0.400). Whether or not the interval is truly "exact" depends on
the software.
The calculations used in this example can be performed using
both
Dataplot code
and
R code
.

title: Approximate intervals that contain most of the population values
text:
The **Empirical Rule** and **Bienaymé-Chebyshev inequality** provide formulas for predicting how many measurements will fall within a certain distance (standard deviations) from the average, which helps engineers decide whether scattered data points are normal variation or signs of a real problem. Instead of counting every data point manually, you can quickly estimate how much of your product or process output should cluster around the target—for example, knowing that about 95% of parts should fall within ±2 standard deviations tells you when you've got too many outliers. People might search for this using terms like "how spread out is my data," "normal distribution percentages," "confidence intervals," "where do my measurements cluster," or "how many defects should I expect."

The Bienayme-Chebyshev rule is conservative because it applies to
any distribution. For a
normal
distribution, a higher

title: Percentiles
text:
# Percentiles

This page explains **percentiles** — the method for finding cutoff values that divide data into 100 equal parts, which engineers use to understand where a measurement falls compared to all other measurements (like finding the 95th percentile for worst-case performance specs). The handbook reviews nine different calculation approaches (R1-R9) developed by Hyndman and Fan, since statistical software implements percentiles differently and can give slightly different answers depending on which method is used. If you're searching for ways to calculate **quartiles, deciles, quantiles, ranking positions, or finding threshold values** from a dataset, this is the concept you need.

Hyndman and Fan (1996)
in an
American Statistician article evaluated nine different methods (we
will refer to these as R1 through R9) for computing percentiles relative
to six desirable properties. Their goal was to advocate a "standard"
definition for percentiles that would be implemented in statistical
software. Although this has not in fact happened, the article does
provide a useful summary and evaluation of various methods for
computing percentiles. Most statistical and spreadsheet software
use one of the methods described in Hyndman and Fan.
The method described above corresponds to method R6 of
Hyndman and Fan. This is the default method used by Dataplot.
The method advocated by Hyndman and Fan is R8. For the R8 method,
set \( p(N+(1/3)) + (1/3) \) and proceed as above. Note that any
p
≤ (2/3)/(
N
+(1/3)) will be set to the minimum value
and any
p
≥ (
N
-(1/3))/(
N
+(1/3)) will be set to
the maximum value. Both R and Dataplot can optionally use this method.
For the example given above, R8 gives 95.1972 (compared to 95.1981)
for the 90-th percentile.
Some software packages set \(1 + p(N-1)\) equal to \(k + d\) and
then proceed as above. This is method R7 of Hyndman and Fan. This
is the method used by Excel and is the default method for R (the R
quantile function can optionally use any of the nine methods
discussed in Hyndman and Fan). For the example given above, R7
gives 95.1957.
The R6, R7, and R8 methods give fairly similar, but not exactly
the same (particularly for small samples), results. For most
purposes, any of these three methods should be acceptable.
Another method of calculating percentiles (given in some elementary
textbooks) starts by calculating \(p N\). If that is not an
integer, round up to the next highest integer \(k\) and use \(Y_{[k]}\)
as the percentile estimate. If \(p N\) is an integer \(k\),
use \( 0.5 \left( Y_{[k]} + Y_{[k+1]} \right) \). One of R6, R7, or
R8 would typically be preferred to this method.

title: Tolerance intervals for a normal distribution
text:
This page covers **tolerance intervals for normal distributions** using the **non-central t-distribution method**, which solves the practical problem of determining the range of values that will contain a specified percentage of your product or process output with a given level of confidence—useful when you need to set specification limits or predict where 95% of future parts will fall. Engineers looking for this concept might search for "prediction intervals," "coverage intervals," "process capability ranges," "how to find the bounds that capture a percentage of my data," or "non-central t-distribution tolerance limits."

The value of \(k_1\)
can also be computed using the
inverse cumulative distribution function for the non-central \(t\)
distribution. This method may give more accurate
results for small values of \(N\).
The value of \(k_1\)
using the non-central \(t\)
distribution (using the same example as above) is:
$$ \begin{eqnarray}
\delta & = & z_{p} \sqrt{N} = 1.2816 \sqrt{43} = 8.4037 \\
& & \\
k_1 & = & \frac{ t_{\alpha, \, N-1, \, \delta} }{ \sqrt{N} }
= \frac{12.28834}{\sqrt{43}} = 1.8740 \, ,
\end{eqnarray} $$
where \(\delta\)
is the non-centrality parameter.
In this case, the difference between the two computations
is negligible (1.8752 versus 1.8740). However, the difference
becomes more pronounced as the value of \(N\)
gets smaller (in particular, for \(N \le\)
10). For example, if \(N\)
= 43 is replaced with \(N\)
= 6, the non-central \(t\)
method returns a value of 4.4111 for \(k_1\)
while the method based on the Natrella formulas returns a value
of 5.2808.
The disadvantage of the non-central \(t\)
method is that it depends on the inverse cumulative distribution
function for the non-central \(t\)
distribution. This function is not available in many statistical
and spreadsheet software programs, but it is available in Dataplot and R (see
Dataplot code
and
R code
).
In addition, the inverse of the non-central t function may lose
accuracy for large sample sizes. The Natrella formulas only depend on
the inverse cumulative distribution function for the normal distribution
(which is available in just about all statistical and spreadsheet
software programs). Unless you have small samples
(say \(N \le\)
10), the difference in the methods should not have much practical effect.

title: Tolerance intervals based on the largest and smallest observations
chapter:
upper critical value
of the chi-square distribution
text:
This page covers **tolerance intervals based on order statistics** (specifically, the range between minimum and maximum values), which solves the practical problem of determining how many samples you need to collect so that you can be confident the gap between your lowest and highest measurements will capture at least a certain percentage of your product or process output. Alternative search terms include "sample size for range-based coverage," "how many samples to guarantee a proportion of population falls between min and max," or "using smallest and largest observations to predict population spread."

Suppose we want to know how many measurements to make in order to guarantee
that the interval between the smallest and largest observations covers a
proportion \(p\)
of the population with probability \(\alpha\)
= 0.95. From the
table for the
upper critical value
of the chi-square distribution
, look under the column labeled 0.95 in
the row for 4 degrees of freedom. The value is found to be
\(\chi_{0.95, \, 4}^2\)
= 9.488 and calculations are shown below for \(p\)
equal to 0.90 and 0.99.
For \(p\) = 0.90, \(\alpha\) = 0.95;
$$ N \cong \frac{1}{4} \frac{(1+0.90)}{(1-0.90)} \, \chi^2_{0.95, 4}
+ \frac{1}{2} = 0.25(19)(9.488)+0.5 = 45.57 = 46 \, . $$
For \(p\) = 0.99, \(\alpha\) = 0.95;
$$ N \cong \frac{1}{4} \frac{(1+0.99)}{(1-0.99)} \, \chi^2_{0.95, 4}
+\frac{1}{2} = 0.25(199)(9.488)+0.5 = 472.5 = 473 \, . $$
These calculations demonstrate that requiring the tolerance interval to
cover a very large proportion of the population may lead to an unacceptably
large sample size.

Engineers and analysts use this when they want to know: "Is my manufacturing process really out of spec, or could this variation just be normal chance?" or "Does this sample actually come from a different population?" Alternative names include chi-squared test table, chi-square critical value reference, or tests for whether observed versus expected frequencies differ significantly.

title: Analysis of paired observations
text:
Paired observations (also called matched pairs or dependent samples) is a statistical method for comparing two measurements taken on the same items or subjects to check if they're giving different results. This solves the practical problem of figuring out whether two testing devices, processes, or methods are producing the same output on identical products—for example, testing the same part with two different gauges to see if they agree. Someone looking for this technique might search for "comparing two measurement devices," "testing if two methods give the same answer," "dependent samples analysis," or "before-and-after comparison."

Given two random samples,
$$ Y_1, \, \ldots, \, Y_N \,\,\,\,\, \mbox{ and } \,\,\,\,\,
Z_1, \, \ldots, \, Z_N $$
from two populations, the data are said to be paired if the \(i\)-th
measurement on the first sample is naturally paired with the \(i\)-th
measurement on the second sample. For example, if \(N\)
supposedly identical products are chosen from a production line, and
each one, in turn, is tested with first one measuring device and then
with a second measuring device, it is possible to decide whether the
measuring devices are compatible; i.e., whether there is a difference
between the two measurement systems. Similarly, if "before" and
"after" measurements are made with the same device on \(N\)
objects,
it is possible to decide if there is a difference between "before" and
"after"; for example, whether a cleaning process changes an important
characteristic of an object. Each "before" measurement is paired with
the corresponding "after" measurement, and the differences,
$$ d_i = Y_i - Z_i \,\,\,\,\, (i = 1, \, \ldots, \, N) \, , $$
are calculated.

title:
Upper Critical Values of the F
             Distribution
text:
The F-distribution table provides **upper critical values for one-sided F-tests** at significance levels α = 0.05, 0.10, and 0.01, which lets engineers determine whether to reject a null hypothesis by comparing a computed test statistic against a threshold value based on degrees of freedom. This solves the practical problem of deciding if two groups have significantly different variability or if one experimental condition produces more consistent results than another. Someone might also search for this using terms like "F-test critical values," "when to reject the null hypothesis in variance tests," "comparing group variances," or "F-distribution threshold tables."

This table contains the upper critical values of the
F distribution
. This table is used for
one-sided F tests at the
α
= 0.05, 0.10, and 0.01 levels.
More specifically, a test statistic is computed with
ν
1
and
ν
2
degrees of freedom,
and the result is compared to this table. For a one-sided test, the
null hypothesis is rejected when the test statistic is greater than the
tabled value. This is demonstrated with the graph of an F distribution
with
ν
1
= 10 and
ν
2
= 10. The
shaded area of the graph indicates the rejection region at the
α
significance level. Since this is a one-sided test, we
have
α
probability in the upper tail of exceeding the
critical value and zero in the lower tail. Because the F distribution is
asymmetric, a two-sided test requires a set of of tables (not included
here) that contain the rejection regions for both the lower and upper
tails.

title: Data Used for Chi-Square Test for the Variance
The ANOVA table and tests of hypotheses about means
chapter:
constructing confidence intervals
       around the difference of two means,
text:
When you run an ANOVA test and find that resistor readings differ across three operating temperatures, you know *something* is different, but you don't know which temperatures cause the differences or by how much—so you need post-hoc analysis techniques like confidence intervals, contrasts, or multiple comparisons to pinpoint the specific problem areas. These follow-up methods let you safely compare pairs of temperatures and estimate the actual size of differences without inflating your error rate. People looking for this also search for "pairwise comparisons," "post-hoc testing," "follow-up analysis after ANOVA," or "which groups are actually different."

The populations here are resistor readings while operating under the
three different temperatures. What we do
not
know at this
point is whether the three means are all different or which of the
three means is different from the other two, and by how much.
There are several techniques we might use to further analyze the
differences. These are:
constructing confidence intervals
around the difference of two means,
estimating combinations of factor levels
with confidence bounds
multiple comparisons of combinations of
factor levels tested simultaneously.

title: The one-way ANOVA model and assumptions
text:
One-way ANOVA (analysis of variance) is a statistical method for testing whether different treatment levels or experimental conditions produce significantly different results by decomposing total variation into treatment effects and random error. An engineer would use this when they need to determine if switching between, say, three different suppliers, machines, or process settings actually changes their product quality or performance, rather than just seeing random noise. Search alternatives: "comparing multiple groups," "testing if different conditions matter," "does changing settings affect output," or "which treatment is best."

The mathematical model that describes the relationship between
the response and treatment for the one-way ANOVA is given by
$$ Y_{ij} = \mu + \tau_i + \epsilon_{ij} \, , $$
where \(Y_{ij}\)
represents the \(j\)-th
observation (\(j = 1, \, 2, \, \ldots, \, n_i\))
on the \(i\)-th
treatment (\(i = 1, \, 2, \, \ldots, \, k\)
levels). So, \(Y_{23}\)
represents the third observation using level 2
of the factor. \(\mu\)
is the common effect for the whole experiment, \(\tau_i\)
represents the \(i\)-th
treatment effect, and \(\epsilon_{ij}\)
represents the random error present in the \(j\)-th
observation on the \(i\)-th
treatment.

title: Confidence intervals for the difference of treatment means
text:
This page covers **confidence intervals for the difference between treatment means**, a method that tells you the likely range of the true difference in average performance between two different process conditions or product designs. An engineer uses this to determine whether changes actually improve results—for instance, whether adjusting a manufacturing parameter really makes parts better—by calculating bounds around the observed difference with known statistical certainty. Related search terms include: "comparing two treatments," "difference between group averages," "margin of error for comparing processes," "statistical significance testing for two groups," or "how much better is option A than option B."

For the example, we have the following quantities for the
formula.
\(\bar{y}_3 = 8.56\)
\(\bar{y}_1 = 5.34\)
\(\sqrt{1.454(1/5 + 1/5)} = 0.763\)
\(t_{0.975, \, 12} = 2.179\)
Substituting these values yields (8.56 - 5.34) ±
2.179(0.763) or 3.22 ±
1.663.
That is, the confidence interval is (1.557, 4.883).

title: Assessing the response from any factor combination
text:
Contrasts and orthogonal contrasts are statistical tools for comparing specific combinations of treatment means in designed experiments, allowing you to break down complex factor effects into meaningful comparisons that directly answer your engineering questions about which conditions perform better. If you're trying to figure out how to fairly compare groups of test results—like comparing two product formulations against a control, or isolating the effect of one factor while accounting for others—you're looking for contrast analysis, also called planned comparisons, treatment comparisons, or linear combinations of means. Engineers and experimenters might also search for terms like "independent comparisons," "non-redundant factor effects," or "how to decompose treatment differences systematically."

Definitions
A contrast is a linear combination of two or more factor level means
with coefficients that sum to zero.
Two contrasts are orthogonal if the sum of the products of
corresponding coefficients (i.e., coefficients for the same means)
adds to zero.
Formally, the definition of a contrast is expressed below, using the
notation \(\mu_i\)
for the \(i\)-th
treatment mean:
$$ C = c_1 \mu_1 + c_2 \mu_2 + \cdots + c_j \mu_j + \cdots + c_k \mu_k \, , $$
where
$$ c_1 + c_2 + \cdots + c_j + \cdots + c_k = \sum_{j=1}^k c_j = 0 \, . $$
Simple contrasts include the case of the difference between two
factor means, such as \(\mu_1 - \mu_2\).
If one wishes to compare treatments 1 and 2 with treatment 3, one
way of expressing this is by: \(\mu_1 + \mu_2 - 2\mu_3\).
Note that
\(\mu_1 - \mu_2\)
has coefficients +1, -1.
\(\mu_1 + \mu_2 - 2\mu_3\)
has coefficients +1, +1, -2.
These coefficients sum to zero
.

title: Assessing the response from any factor combination
text:
This page explains **factor level mean estimation with confidence intervals** in one-way ANOVA, which solves the practical problem of determining not just the average result for a given setting or condition, but also how much uncertainty exists around that average—helping engineers decide if observed differences between process settings are real or just due to random variation. You might also search for this concept using terms like "confidence bounds for group means," "how to calculate error bars around treatment averages," "uncertainty ranges for factor levels," or "comparing average responses with confidence limits."

text:

Engineers and researchers searching for this concept might look for "comparing group averages," "estimated differences between treatments," "confidence bounds for mean comparisons," or "how to tell if specific factor combinations produce different results."

text:
This page covers **confidence intervals for linear combinations of factor means**, which solves the practical problem of determining a reliable range of values when you need to combine results from different experimental groups with different weights or costs assigned to each one. Engineers might search for this using terms like "weighted average of test results," "combining multiple factor effects with different importance," or "confidence bounds for a cost-adjusted outcome."

title: Assessing the response from any factor combination
text:
This page teaches **one-way ANOVA (Analysis of Variance)** and **confidence intervals for group means**, which helps engineers figure out whether differences between four treatment groups are real or just random variation by comparing how spread out each group's results are. If you're trying to decide whether different production settings, materials, or processes actually produce different outcomes, or if you want to establish a reliable range for what one group's true average performance should be, this is your method. You might also search for "comparing multiple groups," "testing if treatments are different," "mean comparison test," or "is my group average trustworthy."

title: Assessing the response from any factor combination
text:
Contrasts and orthogonal contrasts are statistical tools for comparing specific combinations of treatment means to see which factors or settings actually make a difference in your results. Instead of just looking at whether treatments differ overall, contrasts let you ask targeted questions like "does combining treatments 1 and 2 perform better than treatment 3?" or "are these two comparisons independent of each other?" You might also search for this concept using terms like "planned comparisons," "linear combinations of means," "independent factor comparisons," or "structured treatment comparisons."

A contrast is a linear combination of 2 or more factor level means
with coefficients that sum to zero. Two contrasts are orthogonal if the sum of the products of corresponding
coefficients (i.e. coefficients for the same means) adds to zero. Formally, the definition of contrast is expressed below, using the notation m i for the i -th treatment mean:� Simple contrasts includes the case of the difference between two factor
means, such as m 1 - m 2 .
If one wishes to compare treatments 1 and 2 with treatment 3, one way of
expressing this is by: m 1 + m 2 - 2 m 3. Note that� m 1 - m 2 has
coefficients +1, -1� m 1 + m 2 - 2 m 3� has coefficients
+1, +1, -2.� These coefficients sum to zero.� As an example of orthogonal contrasts note the three contrasts defined
by the table below, where the rows denote coefficients for the column treatment
means.� The following is true:� 1. The sum of the coefficients for each contrast is zero.� 2. The sum of the products of coefficients of each pair of contrasts�
is also 0 (orthogonality property).� 3. The first two contrasts are simply pair wise comparisons, the third
one involves all the treatments.� � As might be expected, contrasts are estimated by taking the same linear
combination of treatment mean estimates . In other words:� and� Note: These formulas hold for any linear combination of treatment

interval is� -.5 � 2.12(.5158) or� (-1.594, 0.594). Estimation of Linear Combinations� Sometimes we are interested in a linear combination of the factor level

title: Tukey's method
text:
Tukey's method provides simultaneous confidence intervals for all pairwise comparisons between group means while controlling the overall error rate across all comparisons at once, solving the problem of comparing multiple groups without inflating the false positive rate when making many tests together. This approach is also known as Tukey's Honest Significant Difference (HSD) test, honest significant difference procedure, or simultaneous pairwise comparisons, and engineers often search for it when they need to know which groups differ from each other after an ANOVA without doing individual t-tests that would give misleading results.

The Tukey confidence limits for all pairwise comparisons with
confidence coefficient of at least \(1 - \alpha\)
are:
$$ \bar{y}_{i \scriptsize{\, \bullet}} - \bar{y}_{j \scriptsize{\, \bullet}} \pm
\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \, q_{\alpha; \, r, \, N-r} \,\, \hat{\sigma}_\epsilon \, \sqrt{\frac{2}{n}}
\,\,\,\,\, i, \, j = 1, \, \ldots, \, r; \,\, i \ne j \, . $$
Notice that the point estimator and the estimated variance are the
same as those for a
single pairwise comparison
that was illustrated previously. The only
difference between the confidence limits for simultaneous
comparisons and those for a single comparison is the multiple of
the estimated standard deviation.
Also note that the sample sizes must be equal when using the
studentized range approach.

title: Scheffe's method
text:
Scheffé's method constructs simultaneous confidence intervals for multiple linear contrasts of group means while controlling the overall error rate across all comparisons. This solves the practical problem of comparing different weighted combinations of treatment group averages (like "is the average of groups 1 and 2 together different from group 3?") while guaranteeing that your confidence level applies to all your comparisons together, not just one at a time. Engineers and researchers might search for this using phrases like "multiple comparison method," "simultaneous confidence intervals," "family-wise error control," "contrast testing," or "post-hoc test for complex comparisons."

For a confidence coefficient of 95 percent and degrees of freedom
in the numerator of \(r\)
- 1 = 4 - 1 = 3, and in the denominator
of 20 - 4 = 16, we have:
$$ \sqrt{(r-1) F_{\alpha; \, r-1, \, N-r}} = \sqrt{3 \, F_{0.05; \, 3, \, 16}} = 3.12 \, . $$
The confidence limits for \(C_1\)
are -0.5 ± 3.12(0.5158) = -0.5 ± 1.608, and for \(C_2\)
they are 0.34 ± 1.608.
The desired simultaneous 95 percent confidence intervals are
$$ -2.108 \le C_1 \le 1.108 $$
$$ -1.268 \le C_2 \le 1.948 \, . $$

title: Bonferroni's method
text:
Bonferroni's method is a statistical technique that controls family-wise error rate when making multiple confidence interval estimates simultaneously, solving the problem that when you test or compare many things at once, your overall chance of making a wrong conclusion gets worse unless you adjust each individual test to be stricter. Engineers and scientists use this when they need to compare several product batches, process conditions, or design variations all at once and want to be confident their conclusions about all of them are correct together, not just individually. People also search for this concept as "simultaneous confidence intervals," "multiple comparisons correction," "Bonferroni correction," or "adjusting significance level for multiple tests."

In particular, if each \(A_i\)
is the event that a
calculated confidence interval for a particular linear combination
of treatments includes the true value of that combination, then the
left-hand side of the inequality is the probability that all the
confidence intervals simultaneously cover their respective true
values. The right-hand side is one minus the sum of the
probabilities of each of the intervals missing their true values.
Therefore, if simultaneous multiple interval estimates are desired
with an overall confidence coefficient \(1 - \alpha\),
one can construct each interval with confidence coefficient \((1 - \alpha/g)\),
and the Bonferroni inequality insures that the overall confidence
coefficient is at least \(1 - \alpha\).

title:
Comparing multiple proportions: The 



              Marascuillo procedure
text:
The Marascuillo procedure is a post-hoc test for comparing multiple proportions simultaneously while controlling overall error rates across all pairwise comparisons. This method helps engineers determine which production batches, process variants, or defect rates are genuinely different from each other when testing more than two groups at once, avoiding the problem of false alarms that occurs when running many separate tests. Someone looking for this concept might search for "comparing multiple percentages," "pairwise proportion comparisons," "post-hoc test for proportions," or "chi-square follow-up test for categorical data across groups."

For an overall level of significance of 0.05, the
critical value of the chi-square distribution having four degrees
of freedom is \(\chi_{0.95, \, 4}^2\)
= 9.488 and the square root of 9.488 is 3.080.
Calculating the 10 absolute differences and the 10 critical values
leads to the following summary table.
