title: Graphical Output and Interpretation
text:
When data points are not independent but instead follow a pattern (shown by a linear lag plot), the basic statistical model assuming each measurement is just a constant plus random noise breaks down—this matters because it means your data has memory or trends that simple averaging won't capture. This concept, called **autocorrelation** or **serial correlation**, requires detecting non-random patterns through **lag plots** and **run sequence plots** before you can trust standard statistical summaries like means and confidence intervals. Someone needing this might search for "data points not independent," "repeated measurements correlated," "time series pattern detection," "does my data have memory," or "why lag plot shows linear pattern."

The assumptions are addressed by the graphics shown above:
The
run sequence plot
(upper left) indicates a significant shift in location
around
x
=35.
The linear appearance in the
lag plot
(upper right) indicates a non-random pattern in the data.
Since the lag plot indicates significant non-randomness, we
do not make any interpretation of either the
histogram
(lower left) or the
normal probability plot
(lower right).
The serious violation of the non-randomness assumption means that
the univariate model
\( Y_{i} = C + E_{i} \)
is not valid. Given the linear appearance of the lag plot, the first
step might be to consider a model of the type
\( Y_{i} = A_0 + A_1*Y_{i-1} + E_{i} \)
However, in this case discussions with the scientist revealed that
non-randomness was entirely unexpected. An examination of the
experimental process revealed that the sampling rate for the
automatic data acquisition system was too fast. That is, the
equipment did not have sufficient time to reset before the next sample
started, resulting in the current measurement being contaminated
by the previous measurement. The solution was to rerun the experiment
allowing more time between samples.
Simple graphical techniques can be quite effective in revealing
unexpected results in the data. When this occurs, it is important
to investigate whether the unexpected result is due to problems in
the experiment and data collection or is indicative of
unexpected underlying structure in the data. This determination
cannot be made on the basis of statistics alone. The role of the
graphical and statistical analysis is to detect problems or unexpected
results in the data. Resolving the issues requires the knowledge of
the scientist or engineer.

title: Quantitative Output and Interpretation
text:
Autocorrelation analysis detects whether your data points are influenced by previous values (lag 1 autocorrelation being the primary metric), which tells you if measurements are truly independent or if past results are affecting current ones—a critical check before applying standard statistical methods that assume independence. Someone looking for this concept might search for "checking if data points depend on each other," "detecting patterns or memory in time series," "testing whether measurements are independent," or "lag correlation analysis."

There are many ways in which data can be non-random. However,
most common forms of non-randomness can be detected with a
few simple tests. The lag plot in the 4-plot in the previous
seciton is a simple graphical technique.
One check is an autocorrelation plot that shows the
autocorrelations
for various lags. Confidence bands can be plotted at the 95 % and
99 % confidence levels. Points outside this band indicate statistically
significant values (lag 0 is always 1).
The lag 1 autocorrelation, which is generally the one of most interest,
is 0.93. The critical values at the 5 % level are -0.277 and 0.277.
This indicates that the lag 1 autocorrelation is statistically
significant, so there is strong evidence of non-randomness.
A common test for randomness is the
runs test
.
H
0
: the sequence was produced in a random manner
H
a
: the sequence was not produced in a random manner
Test statistic:
Z
= -5.3246
Significance level:
α
= 0.05
Critical value:
Z
1-
α
/2
= 1.96
Critical region: Reject H
0
if |
Z
| > 1.96
Because the test statistic is outside of the critical region, we
reject the null hypothesis and conclude that the data are not random.

title: Work This Example Yourself
text:
This page teaches **Exploratory Data Analysis (EDA)**, a foundational approach for getting to know your data before formal statistical testing, which helps engineers spot patterns, outliers, and data quality issues upfront so you don't build models on bad information. People looking for this concept might search for "data visualization," "understanding your dataset," "data inspection," "preliminary data examination," or "checking data before analysis."

This page allows you to repeat the analysis outlined in the
case study description on the previous page using
Dataplot
. It is required that you have already
downloaded and installed
Dataplot and
configured your browser
. to run Dataplot. Output from each analysis
step below will be displayed in one or more of the Dataplot windows.
The four main windows are the Output window, the Graphics window, the
Command History window, and the data sheet window. Across the top of
the main windows there are menus for executing Dataplot commands.
Across the bottom is a command entry window where commands can be typed
in.

title: Graphical Output and Interpretation
text:
When checking whether your measurement data meets the basic requirements for statistical analysis, use **run sequence plots, lag plots, histograms, and normal probability plots** to spot real-world problems like trends over time, data points that depend on previous values, and non-bell-shaped distributions that could make your calculations unreliable. These graphics help you catch issues like "measurements creeping up gradually," "yesterday's reading affects today's," or "my data doesn't look normal" before you apply standard statistical methods. You might also search for these under: **assumption checking, diagnostic plots, data validation charts, autocorrelation detection, or preliminary data screening**.

The assumptions are addressed by the graphics shown above:
The
run sequence plot
(upper left) indicates significant shifts
in both location and variation. Specifically, the location
is increasing with time. The variability seems greater
in the first and last third of the data than it does in the
middle third.
The
lag plot
(upper right) shows a significant non-random
pattern in the data. Specifically, the strong linear
appearance of this plot is indicative of a model that
relates
Y
t
to
Y
t-1
.
The distributional plots, the
histogram
(lower left) and the
normal probability plot
(lower right), are not interpreted
since the randomness assumption is so clearly violated.
The serious violation of the non-randomness assumption means that
the univariate model
\( Y_{i} = C + E_{i} \)
is not valid. Given the linear appearance of the lag plot, the
first step might be to consider a model of the type
\( Y_{i} = A_0 + A_1*Y_{i-1} + E_{i} \)
However, discussions with the scientist revealed the following:
the drift with respect to location was expected.
the non-constant variability was not expected.
The scientist examined the data collection device and determined
that the non-constant variation was a seasonal effect. The
high variability data in the first and last thirds was collected
in winter while the more stable middle third was collected in the
summer. The seasonal effect was determined to be caused by
the amount of humidity affecting the measurement equipment. In this
case, the solution was to modify the test equipment to be less
sensitive to enviromental factors.
Simple graphical techniques can be quite effective in revealing
unexpected results in the data. When this occurs, it is important
to investigate whether the unexpected result is due to problems in
the experiment and data collection, or is it in fact indicative of an
unexpected underlying structure in the data. This determination
cannot be made on the basis of statistics alone. The role of the
graphical and statistical analysis is to detect problems or unexpected
results in the data. Resolving the issues requires the knowledge of
the scientist or engineer.

title: Quantitative Output and Interpretation
text:
Autocorrelation plots and lag plots detect whether your data points are randomly scattered or if they're influenced by previous values—a critical check because many statistical methods assume independence, and this tells you if your measurements are actually dependent on each other over time. If you're seeing patterns where each data point looks suspiciously similar to the one before it (high lag 1 autocorrelation like 0.97), your data isn't truly random and you may need different analysis methods. Search also for: serial correlation, time series dependency, data independence testing, or "are my measurements correlated with themselves."

There are many ways in which data can be non-random. However,
most common forms of non-randomness can be detected with a
few simple tests. The lag plot in the 4-plot in the previous
section is a simple graphical technique.
One check is an autocorrelation plot that shows the
autocorrelations
for various lags. Confidence bands can be plotted at the 95 % and
99 % confidence levels. Points outside this band indicate statistically
significant values (lag 0 is always 1).
The lag 1 autocorrelation, which is generally the one of greatest
interest, is 0.97. The critical values at the 5 % significance level are
-0.062 and 0.062. This indicates that the lag 1 autocorrelation is
statistically significant, so there is strong evidence of
non-randomness.
A common test for
randomness
is the runs test.
H
0
: the sequence was produced in a random manner
H
a
: the sequence was not produced in a random manner
Test statistic:
Z
= -30.5629
Significance level:
α
= 0.05
Critical value:
Z
1-
α
/2
= 1.96
Critical region: Reject H
0
if |
Z
| > 1.96
Because the test statistic is outside of the critical region, we
reject the null hypothesis and conclude that the data are not random.

title: Work This Example Yourself
chapter:
1. Generate a table of summary
      statistics.
text:
This NIST guidance covers univariate exploratory data analysis including summary statistics, confidence intervals, linear regression for trend detection, Levene's test for variance stability, autocorrelation analysis, and runs tests—practical tools for understanding whether your measurement data is stable, drifting over time, or randomly scattered. A non-statistician engineer would use these techniques to verify that their process output isn't changing unexpectedly, that variation isn't growing, and that readings aren't influenced by time-related patterns. Someone might also search for "data stability checks," "process drift detection," "checking if measurements are random," "variance consistency testing," or "baseline characterization of measurement systems."

4. Generate summary statistics, quantitative
analysis, and print a univariate report.
1. Generate a table of summary
statistics.
2. Generate the sample mean, a confidence
interval for the population mean, and
compute a linear fit to detect drift in
location.
3. Generate the sample standard deviation,
a confidence interval for the population
standard deviation, and detect drift in
variation by dividing the data into
quarters and computing Levene's test for
equal standard deviations.
4. Check for randomness by generating an
autocorrelation plot and a runs test.
5. Print a univariate report (this assumes
steps 2 thru 5 have already been run).

title: Graphical Output and Interpretation
text:
This section covers **run sequence plots, lag plots, histograms, and normal probability plots**—four graphical tools used to verify that your data meets the basic requirements before applying standard statistical tests. In practical terms, these charts let you quickly check whether your measurements are stable over time, randomly scattered without patterns, reasonably bell-shaped, and free from weird outliers that could mess up your analysis. You might also search for these concepts as "data validation graphics," "assumption checking charts," "distribution diagnostics," or "preliminary data screening plots."

The assumptions are addressed by the graphics shown above:
The
run sequence plot
(upper left) indicates that the data do not
have any significant shifts in location or scale over time.
The
lag plot
(upper right)
does not indicate any non-random pattern in the data.
The
histogram
(lower
left) shows that the data are reasonably symmetric, there does
not appear to be significant outliers in the tails, and it
seems reasonable to assume that the data are from approximately
a normal distribution.
The
normal probability
plot
(lower right) verifies that an assumption of normality
is in fact reasonable.

title: Quantitative Output and Interpretation
text:
This page covers **autocorrelation** and **lag plots**, statistical techniques for detecting whether data points are independent or whether each measurement is influenced by previous measurements. If you're collecting sensor readings, measurements over time, or test results in sequence, these methods help you spot if your data has hidden patterns or dependencies that could invalidate your analysis—essentially checking whether your observations are truly random or if there's "memory" built into the sequence. You might also search for this concept as "serial correlation," "data independence testing," "time series correlation," or "detecting non-random patterns in sequential data."

There are many ways in which data can be non-random. However,
most common forms of non-randomness can be detected with a
few simple tests. The lag plot in the previous section is a
simple graphical technique.
Another check is an autocorrelation plot that shows the
autocorrelations
for various lags. Confidence bands can be plotted at the 95 % and
99 % confidence levels. Points outside this band indicate statistically
significant values (lag 0 is always 1).
The lag 1 autocorrelation, which is generally the one of greatest
interest, is 0.281. The critical values at the 5 % significance level
are -0.140 and 0.140. This indicates that the lag 1 autocorrelation is
statistically significant, so there is evidence of non-randomness.
A common test for randomness is the
runs test
.
H
0
: the sequence was produced in a random manner
H
a
: the sequence was not produced in a random manner
Test statistic:
Z
= -3.2306
Significance level:
α
= 0.05
Critical value:
Z
1-
α
/2
= 1.96
Critical region: Reject H
0
if |
Z
| > 1.96
The value of the test statistic is less than -1.96, so we reject the
null hypothesis at the 0.05 significant level and conclude that the
data are not random.
Although the autocorrelation plot and the runs test indicate some
mild non-randomness, the violation of the randomness assumption is
not serious enough to warrant developing a more
sophisticated model. It is common in practice that some of the
assumptions are mildly violated and it is a judgement call as to
whether or not the violations are serious enough to warrant
developing a more sophisticated model for the data.

title: Work This Example Yourself
chapter:
1. Generate a table of summary
      statistics.
text:
This NIST guidance covers univariate exploratory data analysis including summary statistics, confidence intervals, linear trend detection, Bartlett's test for equal variances, autocorrelation plots, runs tests, and normal probability plots—essentially the complete toolkit for understanding a single stream of measurement data. It solves the practical problem of engineers needing to verify that their collected measurements are reliable, stable, and behaving predictably before using them for decision-making, by checking whether the data drifts over time, has consistent spread, shows random behavior, and follows expected patterns. Someone might search for these techniques using phrases like "checking if my data is good," "detecting if measurements are drifting," "testing for measurement consistency," "validating data quality," or "is my dataset trustworthy."

4. Generate summary statistics, quantitative
analysis, and print a univariate report.
1. Generate a table of summary
statistics.
2. Generate the mean, a confidence
interval for the mean, and compute
a linear fit to detect drift in
location.
3. Generate the standard deviation, a
confidence interval for the standard
deviation, and detect drift in variation
by dividing the data into quarters and
computing Bartlett's test for equal
standard deviations.
4. Check for randomness by generating an
autocorrelation plot and a runs test.
5. Check for normality by computing the
normal probability plot correlation
coefficient.
6. Check for outliers using Grubbs' test.
7. Print a univariate report (this assumes
steps 2 thru 6 have already been run).

title: Background and Data
text:
This page covers probabilistic modeling and probability distribution fitting for reliability analysis—specifically, how to pick the right mathematical model to predict when materials or structures will break under real-world conditions. An engineer dealing with equipment failures or product lifetime might search for "failure prediction models," "how to choose a distribution for failure times," "predicting when things break," or "modeling material life span" to find this content. The practical value is that once you have the correct probability model, you can make realistic estimates about product durability and design safety margins instead of guessing.

The goal of this case study is to select a probabilistic model,
from among several reasonable alternatives, to describe the
dispersion of the resulting measured values of life-length.
The original study, in the field of statistical reliability
analysis, was concerned with the prediction of failure times of a
material subjected to a load varying in time. It was well-known
that a structure designed to withstand a particular static load
may fail sooner than expected under a dynamic load.
If a realistic model for the probability distribution of lifetime
can be found, then it can be used to estimate the time by which a
part or structure needs to be replaced to guarantee that the
probability of failure does not exceed some maximum acceptable
value, for example 0.1 %, while it is in service.
The chapter of this eHandbook that is concerned with the
assessment of product reliability
contains additional material on statistical methods used in
reliability analysis. This case study is meant to complement that
chapter by showing the use of graphical and other techniques in
the model selection stage of such analysis.
When there is no cogent reason to adopt a particular model, or
when none of the models under consideration seems adequate for
the purpose, one may opt for a non-parametric statistical method,
for example to produce tolerance bounds or confidence
intervals.
A non-parametric method does not rely on the assumption that the
data are like a sample from a particular probability distribution
that is fully specified up to the values of some adjustable
parameters. For example, the Gaussian probability distribution is
a parametric model with two adjustable parameters.
The price to be paid when using non-parametric methods is loss of
efficiency, meaning that they may require more data for statistical
inference than a parametric counterpart would, if applicable.
For example, non-parametric confidence intervals for model parameters
may be considerably wider
than what a confidence interval would need to be if the
underlying distribution could be identified correctly. Such
identification is what we will attempt in this case study.
It should be noted --- a point that we will stress later in the
development of this case study --- that the very exercise of
selecting a model often contributes substantially to the
uncertainty of the conclusions derived after the selection has
been made.

title: Graphical Output and Interpretation
text:
Exploratory Data Analysis (EDA) uses simple graphical tools like histograms, boxplots, and dot-charts to quickly spot where your data sits, how spread out it is, and whether you have weird outliers or unusual clumps that might indicate measurement problems or interesting patterns. Instead of staring at raw numbers, these visual methods let engineers and analysts instantly see the shape and range of test results—like understanding at a glance that your fatigue measurements span from 350,000 to 2,500,000 cycles with some suspicious values sticking out. You might also search for this concept as "data visualization for quality control," "spotting outliers and trends visually," "non-parametric density estimation," "data distribution plots," or "preliminary data inspection methods."

Simple diagrams can be very informative about location, spread,
and to detect possibly anomalous data values or particular patterns
(clustering, for example). These include dot-charts, boxplots,
and histograms. Since building an effective histogram requires
that a choice be made of bin size, and this choice can be
influential, one may wish to examine a non-parametric estimate of
the underlying probability density.
These several plots variously show that the measurements range
from a value slightly greater than 350,000 to slightly less than
2,500,000 cycles. The boxplot suggests that the largest
measured value may be an outlier.
A recommended first step is to check consistency between the data
and what is to be expected if the data were a sample from a
particular probability distribution. Knowledge about the
underlying properties of materials and of relevant industrial
processes typically offer clues as to the models that should be
entertained. Graphical diagnostic techniques can be very useful
at this exploratory stage: foremost among these, for univariate
data, is the quantile-quantile plot, or QQ-plot
(Wilk and Gnanadesikan, 1968)
.
Each data point is represented by one point in the QQ-plot. The
ordinate of each of these points is one data value; if this data
value happens to be the
k
th order statistic in the sample (that
is, the
k
th largest value), then the corresponding abscissa is
the "typical" value that the
k
th largest value should have in
a sample of the same size as the data, drawn from a particular
distribution. If
F
denotes the cumulative probability
distribution function of interest, and the sample comprises
n
values, then
F
-1
[(
k
- 1/2) / (
n
+ 1/2)]
is a reasonable choice for that "typical" value, because it is an
approximation to the median of the
k
th order statistic in a
sample of size
n
from this distribution.
The following figure shows a QQ-plot of our data relative to the
Gaussian (or, normal) probability distribution. If the data
matched expectations perfectly, then the points would all fall on
a straight line.
In practice, one needs to gauge whether the deviations from such
perfect alignment are commensurate with the natural variability
associated with sampling. This can easily be done by examining
how variable QQ-plots of samples from the target distribution may
be.
The following figure shows, superimposed on the QQ-plot of the
data, the QQ-plots of 99 samples of the same size as the data,
drawn from a Gaussian distribution with the same mean and
standard deviation as the data.
The fact that the cloud of QQ-plots corresponding to 99 samples
from the Gaussian distribution effectively covers the QQ-plot for
the data, suggests that the chances are better than 1 in 100 that
our data are inconsistent with the Gaussian model.
This proves nothing, of course, because even the rarest of events
may happen. However, it is commonly taken to be indicative of an
acceptable fit for general purposes. In any case, one may
naturally wonder if an alternative model might not provide an
even better fit.
Knowing the provenance of the data, that they portray strength of
a material, strongly suggests that one may like to examine
alternative models, because in many studies of reliability
non-Gaussian models tend to be more appropriate than Gaussian
models.

The Information Technology Laboratory (ITL) is one of NIST’s six research laboratories. ITL focuses on IT measurements, testing, and standards, and is a globally recognized and trusted source of high-quality, independent, and unbiased research and data. ITL’s mission, to cultivate trust in information technology (IT) and metrology, is accomplished using its world-class measurement and testing facilities and encompassing a wide range of areas of computer science, mathematics, statistics, and systems engineering.
This non-regulatory role, along with ITL’s deep technical expertise in all fields of information technology, helps increase trust in IT worldwide.

title: Example of control chart for precision
text:
This example demonstrates **control charts for standard deviations** and **pooled variance estimation** applied to measurement system monitoring, solving the practical problem of tracking whether your measurement equipment's repeatability (how consistently it gives the same answer) stays stable over time or is drifting out of acceptable bounds. Engineers and technicians looking for this concept might search for "monitoring measurement precision over time," "tracking equipment consistency with control limits," "detecting when measurement equipment needs recalibration," or "statistical process control for measurement systems."

The standard deviations over time and many calibrations are tracked
and monitored using a
control chart for standard
deviations.
The database and control limits are updated on a
yearly or bi-yearly basis and standard deviations for each calibration
run in the next cycle are compared with the control limits. In this
case, the standard deviations from 117 calibrations between 1975 and
1985 were pooled to obtain a repeatability standard deviation with
v
= 3*117 = 351 degrees of freedom, and the
control limits
were computed
at the 1 % significance level.

title: Left-right (constant instrument) bias
text:
Left-right balanced designs are randomization schemes that eliminate instrument bias by systematically alternating the position or order of measurements, solving the problem where a measuring device consistently reads higher or lower depending on which side an artifact is placed on or measured first. Engineers and technicians might also search for this using plain terms like "alternating measurement order," "counterbalancing measurement positions," "eliminating operator bias from left-right placement," or "removing systematic errors from measurement sequence" when trying to ensure fair comparison of reference standards in the same controlled environment.

This type of scheme is called left-right balanced and the principle is
extended to create a
catalog of left-right balanced
designs
for intercomparing reference standards among themselves.
These designs are appropriate
ONLY
for comparing reference
standards in the same environment, or enclosure, and are not appropriate
for comparing, say, across standard voltage cells in two boxes.
Left-right balanced design for a group of 3
artifacts
Left-right balanced design for a group of 4
artifacts
Left-right balanced design for a group of 5
artifacts
Left-right balanced design for a group of 6
artifacts

text:
This page covers **balance design**, **orthogonal arrays**, and **variance component estimation** for a 1-1-1 weighing scheme used in metrology. It solves the practical problem of figuring out how to arrange repeated weight measurements across different days so you can separate out measurement noise from day-to-day drift, and then calculate multiplier factors to convert raw measurement scatter into reliable estimates of short-term and long-term repeatability. Someone looking for this might search for "weighing design," "measurement uncertainty breakdown," "how to separate random error from systematic drift," "calibration design for repeated measurements," or "factors for combining measurement variability."

title: Repeatability and level-2 standard deviations
text:
This section explains how to calculate **repeatability standard deviation** and **residual standard deviation** from calibration measurement data using the degrees of freedom formula ν = n - m + 1, which tells you how much confidence to put in your instrument's precision measurement. Engineers use this to figure out the built-in variability of their measurement equipment—the "noise floor" that shows up even when measuring the same thing multiple times. You might also search for "instrument precision," "measurement uncertainty from calibration," "residual error from design analysis," or "how repeatable is my instrument."

The repeatability standard deviation of the instrument
can be computed in two ways.
It can be computed as the residual standard deviation from the
design and should be available as output from any software
package that reduces data from calibration designs. The matrix
equations for this computation are shown in the section on
solutions to calibration designs
. The
standard deviation has degrees of freedom
$$ \nu = n - m + 1 $$
for
n
difference measurements and
m
items.
Typically the degrees of freedom are very small. For two
differences measurements on a reference standard and test item,
the degrees of freedom is \( \nu = 1 \).

title: Type A evaluations for calibration designs
text:
This section covers **Type A uncertainty evaluation** and **calibration design models** that distinguish between **random measurement error** and **systematic effects** in comparing test items against reference standards. In practice, this helps engineers figure out whether their measurement differences are just due to instrument wobble (Model 1) or whether there are other consistent sources of variation like temperature or operator effects that also need to be accounted for (Model 2). You might search for this using terms like "comparing measurements to a standard," "instrument precision vs. accuracy in calibration," "measurement uncertainty sources," or "reference standard comparison models."

To contrast the simple model with the more
complicated model, a measurement of the difference between
X,
the test item, with unknown and yet to be determined value,
X*,
and a reference standard,
R
, with known value,
R*
, and
the reverse measurement are shown below.
Model (1) takes into account only instrument imprecision so that:
(1)
\begin{eqnarray}
Y_1 = X - R + error_1 \\
Y_2 = R - X + error_2
\end{eqnarray}
with the error terms random errors that come from the imprecision
of the measuring instrument.
Model (2) allows for both instrument imprecision and level-2 effects
such that:
(2)
\begin{eqnarray}
Y_1 = (X + \Delta_X) - (R + \Delta_R) + error_1 \\
Y_2 = (R + \Delta_R) - (X + \Delta_X) + error_2
\end{eqnarray}
where the delta terms explain small changes in the values of the
artifacts that occur over time. For both models, the value of the test
item is estimated as
$$ \widehat{Test} = X^* = \frac{1}{2} (Y_1 - Y_2) + R^* $$

title: Combination of repeatability and level-2 standard deviations
text:
This section covers **combination of repeatability and level-2 standard deviations** using **matrix equations** and **design catalogs** to solve the practical problem of figuring out how much total measurement uncertainty to expect when you're running the same test items multiple times across different setups or days—combining the variation you get from running things over and over (repeatability) with the variation from differences between your measurement systems or conditions (level-2 variation). You might search for this as "combining multiple sources of measurement error," "total measurement uncertainty from repeated tests," "pooling within-run and between-run variation," or "estimating overall measurement reliability across different test conditions."

Tables for estimating standard deviations for all test items are
reported along with the solutions for all designs in the
catalog
. The
use of the
tables
for estimating the standard deviations for test items is
illustrated for the 1,1,1,1 design.
Matrix
equations
can be used for deriving estimates for designs that are
not in the catalog.
The check standard for each design is either an additional test item in
the design, other than the test items that are submitted for
calibration, or it is a construction, such as the difference between two
reference standards as estimated by the design.

title: Calculation of standard deviations for 1,1,1,1 design
text:
This page covers **standard deviation decomposition** and **variance partitioning** for measurement systems, solving the practical problem of figuring out how much of your measurement variation comes from day-to-day changes versus repeated measurements of the same item on the same day. Engineers looking for this might search for "breaking down measurement uncertainty," "separating short-term and long-term variation," "repeatability versus reproducibility," or "how to account for different sources of measurement error."

The steps in computing the standard deviation for a test item are:
Compute the
repeatability standard
deviation
from historical data.
Compute the
standard deviation of
the check standard
from historical data.
Locate the factors,
K
1
and
K
2
,
for the check standard.
Compute the between-day variance (using the unifying equation for
the check standard). For this example,
$$
\begin{array}{l}
{\large s}_2^2 = \left\{ \left( \sqrt{0.5} \, {\large s}_1 \right)^2 + \left( \sqrt{2} \, {\large s}_{days} \right)^2 \right\}
= \left\{ \frac{1}{2} {\large s}_1^2 + 2 {\large s}_{days}^2 \right\} \\
\begin{array}{l}
{\small implies} \\
\,\,\, \Longrightarrow
\end{array} \\
{\large s}_{days}^2 = \frac{1}{2} \left\{ {\large s}_2^2 - \frac{1}{2}{\large s}_1^2 \right\} \,\, .
\end{array}
$$
If this variance estimate is negative, set
\( {\large s}_{days} = 0 \).
(This is possible and indicates that there is no
contribution to uncertainty from day-to-day effects.)
Locate the factors,
K
1
and
K
2
,
for the test items, and compute the standard deviations using the
unifying equation. For this example,
$$ {\large s}_{X_1} = \sqrt{\frac{3}{8} {\large s}_1^2 + \frac{3}{2} {\large s}_{days}^2}
= \sqrt{\frac{3}{8} {\large s}_1^2 + \frac{3}{2} \cdot \frac{1}{2} \left\{ {\large s}_2^2 - \frac{1}{2}{\large s}_1^2 \right\} }
= \sqrt{\frac{3}{4} {\large s}_2^2} $$
and
$$ {\large s}_{X_2} = {\large s}_{X_1} \,\, .$$

title: Type B uncertainty
text:
**Type B uncertainty** quantifies how much doubt or error comes from a reference standard's documented specifications when you're calibrating test equipment, letting you account for the imperfection of your "known good" standard rather than treating it as perfect. Engineers call this "uncertainty from the reference standard," "calibration standard uncertainty," or "restraint uncertainty," and it's computed by dividing the reported uncertainty value by the coverage factor k to get the standard deviation that feeds into your overall measurement uncertainty budget.

The reference standard is assumed to have known value,
R*,
for
the purpose of solving the calibration design. For the purpose of
computing a standard uncertainty, it has a
type B uncertainty
that contributes
to the uncertainty of the test item.
The value of
R*
comes from a higher-level calibration laboratory
or process, and its value is usually reported along with its uncertainty,
U.
If the laboratory also reports the
k
factor for
computing
U,
then the standard deviation of the restraint is
$$ {\large s}_{R^*} = \frac{U}{k} \, .$$
If
k
is not reported, then a conservative way of proceeding is
to assume
k
= 2.

title: Expanded uncertainties
text:
This section covers **measurement uncertainty propagation, variance decomposition, and degrees of freedom estimation** in designed experiments, solving the practical problem of figuring out how confident you should be in your test results when you've broken down measurement variation into multiple sources (like day-to-day drift versus instrument noise). Engineers looking for this concept might search for "how to combine different sources of measurement error," "calculating confidence in repeated measurements," "uncertainty from multiple error sources," or "degrees of freedom with nested measurement variation."

For the
1,1,1,1 design
,
the standard deviation of the test items can be rewritten by
substituting in the equation
$$ {\large s}_{X_1} = {\large s}_{X_2} =
\sqrt{\frac{3}{8}{\large s}_1^2 + \frac{3}{2} {\large s}_{days}^2 } =
\sqrt{\frac{3}{8}{\large s}_1^2 + \frac{3}{2} \left\{ \frac{1}{2} {\large s}_2^2 - \frac{1}{4} {\large s}_1^2 \right\} } =
\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} {\large s}_2 $$
so that the degrees of freedom depends only on the degrees of freedom
in the standard deviation of the check standard. This device may not
work satisfactorily for all designs.

title: Single-trace roundness design
text:
This section covers **least-squares circle fitting** and **Fourier harmonic estimation**, which solves the practical problem of finding the best-fitting perfect circle through roundness measurement data to separate true geometric shape from measurement noise. Engineers might search for this under terms like "best-fit circle calculation," "roundness error analysis," "spindle runout measurement," "circular form fitting," or "finding the center and radius from roundness traces."

For this purpose, a single trace covering exactly 360� is made of the
workpiece and measurements
\( Y_i \)
at angles \( \theta_i \)
of the distance between the center of the spindle and the trace, are
made at
$$ \theta_i \{ i=1, \, \ldots, \, N\} $$
equally spaced angles. A least-squares circle fit to the data gives
the following estimators of the parameters of the circle.
\begin{array} \(
\widehat{R} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} Y_i \\
\widehat{a} = \frac{2}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} Y_i \, \mbox{cos}(\theta_i) \\
\widehat{b} = \frac{2}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} Y_i \, \mbox{sin}(\theta_i) \\
\end{array}

title: Multiple-trace roundness designs
text:
This handbook section covers **least-squares estimation** and **constraint-based parameter fitting** for separating spindle runout from part roundness errors in precision measurement. It solves the practical problem of isolating true part geometry from machine imperfections by mathematically filtering out spindle wobble and centering errors that contaminate roundness measurements. Engineers searching for this might look for "removing spindle runout," "separating machine error from part error," "compensating for spindle drift in roundness testing," or "least-squares roundness fitting with constraints."

The estimation of the individual parameters is obtained as a
least-squares solution that requires six restraints which essentially
guarantee that the sum of the vertical and horizontal deviations of
the spindle from the center of the LSC are zero. The expressions for
the estimators are as follows:
\begin{array} \(
\widehat{\alpha}_i = \sum_{j=1}^n \sum_{k=1}^n t_{k-i+j+n} \, Z_{kj} \\
\widehat{\beta}_i = \sum_{j=1}^n \sum_{k=1}^n t_{k-i+1+n} \, Z_{kj} \\
\widehat{R}_j = \sum_{k=1}^n Z_{kj} \\
\widehat{a}_j = \frac{2}{n} \sum_{k=1}^n Z_{kj} \, \mbox{cos} (\theta_k) \\
\widehat{b}_j = \frac{2}{n} \sum_{k=1}^n Z_{kj} \, \mbox{sin} (\theta_k) \,\, , \\
\end{array}
where
\begin{array} \(
t_m = \frac{n-3}{n^2} ; \,\, m=1 \\
t_m = -\frac{1}{n^2} \left( 1 + 2 \mbox{cos} (\theta_m) \right) ; \,\, 2 \le m \le n \,\, .
\end{array}
Finally, the standard deviations of the profile estimators are given by:
$$ {\large s}_{\alpha_i} = {\large s}_{\beta_i} = \frac{\sqrt{n-3}}{n} {\large s} \, ; \,\, (i = 1, \, \ldots, \, n) \, . $$

title: Difference between 2 wiring configurations
text:
This example demonstrates **measurement system validation** through **comparison of means** and **random variation assessment** to verify that different wiring configurations produce equivalent results on the same instrument. In practical terms, it shows how to check whether swapping equipment setups or probe connections actually changes your measurement readings, or if any differences you see are just normal noise from the measuring process itself. Engineers might search for this using terms like "checking if equipment changes matter," "validating measurement repeatability across configurations," "detecting systematic bias between setups," or "proving two methods give the same answer."

Differences between measurements in configurations A and B, made on the
same day, are plotted over six days for each wafer. The two graphs
represent two runs separated by approximately two months time. The
dotted line in the center is the zero line. The pattern of data points
scatters fairly randomly above and below the zero line -- indicating no
difference between configurations for probe #2362. The conclusion
applies to probe #2362 and cannot be extended to all probes of this
type.

This page explains **repeatability standard deviation** and **pooled standard deviation**, which help engineers understand how much their measurement instrument naturally varies when taking multiple readings of the same part under identical conditions. Instead of just looking at one day's variation, pooling data across all six days gives you a more reliable estimate of your instrument's true short-term precision—answering the practical question: "How consistent is my measurement system?" People searching for this concept might also look for "within-day variation," "short-term measurement scatter," "instrument precision assessment," or "gage repeatability."

title: Dataplot macros
text:
This macro demonstrates Shewhart control charts with 2-sigma control limits, a fundamental quality control technique for monitoring whether a measurement process stays within expected bounds over time. It solves the practical problem of detecting when your equipment or process starts drifting out of normal operation—in this case tracking electrical resistance measurements from a crystal wafer probe—so you can catch problems before they ruin your product. Engineers might also search for this under names like "process control chart," "statistical process control," "trend monitoring," "control limits," or "out-of-control detection."

reset data
reset plot control
reset i/o
dimension 30 columns
skip 50
read mpc62.dat crystal wafer mo day hour min op hum probe temp y sw df
skip 0
let time = mo +(day-1)/31.
let avg = mean y
let sprocess = standard deviation y
let ucl = avg + 2*sprocess
let lcl = avg - 2*sprocess
print avg
print sprocess
print ucl lcl
title Shewhart control chart
characters O blank blank blank
lines blank dashed solid dashed
y1label ohm.cm
x1label Time in days
x2label Check standard 137 with probe 2362
x3label 2-sigma control limits
let ybar = y - y + avg
let lc1 = y - y + lcl
let lc2 = y - y + ucl
plot y lc1 ybar lc2 vs time

title: Dataplot macros
text:
This macro demonstrates **bias estimation and residual analysis** by computing differences between observed measurements and group-level means, allowing engineers to identify systematic errors or offsets in their measurement system across different runs and wafers. Someone looking to "find and remove consistent measurement errors," "check if my equipment reads high or low by batch," or "separate true variation from systematic drift" would need this same technique, which is also called **measurement bias quantification**, **within-group residuals**, or **systematic error detection**.

reset data
reset plot control
reset i/o
dimension 30 columns
label size 3
set read format f1.0,f6.0,f8.0,32x,f10.4,f10.4
skip 50
read mpc61.dat run wafer probe y sr
skip 0
set read format
.
cross tabulate mean y run wafer
retain run wafer probe y sr subset probe 2362
skip 1
read dpst1f.dat runid wafid ybar
let ngroups = size ybar
skip 0
.
let m3 = y - y
feedback off
loop for k = 1 1 ngroups
let runa = runid(k)
let wafera = wafid(k)
let ytemp = ybar(k)
let m3 = ytemp subset run = runa subset wafer = wafera
end of loop
feedback on
.
let d = y - m3
let bias1 = average d subset run 1
let bias2 = average d subset run 2
.
mean plot d wafer subset run 1
let b1 = yplot
let wafer1 = xplot
mean plot d wafer subset run 2
let b2 = yplot
retain b1 b2 wafer1 subset tagplot = 1
.
extend b1 b2
let sd = standard deviation b1
let sdcorr = sd/(10**(1/2))
let correct = -(bias1+bias2)/2.
. correction for probe #2362, standard dev, and standard dev of corr
print correct sd sdcorr
. end of calculations

title: One-Way Value-Splitting
text:
This excerpt covers **F-test for equal means and ANOVA (analysis of variance)**, which solves the practical problem of determining whether differences you see between machines are real and significant or just random noise in your production data. Engineers looking for this concept might search for "how to tell if machines are different," "comparing machine performance statistically," "testing if production equipment affects quality," or "machine effect analysis."

The last step is to calculate the F-value and perform the test of equal
level means. The F- value is just the level mean square divided by the
residual mean square. In this case the F-value=4.86. If we look
in an F-table for 4 and 20 degrees of freedom at 95% confidence, we see
that the critical value is 2.87, which means that we have a significant
result and that there is thus evidence of a strong machine effect. By
looking at the level-effect overlay we see that this is driven by
machines 2 and 4.

title: Two-way Crossed Value-Splitting Example
text:
This example demonstrates **two-way ANOVA with interaction effects and residual analysis** to understand how multiple factors (machines and coolants) independently and jointly affect a manufacturing output (pin diameter). It solves the practical problem of figuring out which machines and coolants produce the most consistent and accurate part sizes, and whether certain machine-coolant combinations work better or worse together than you'd expect. Someone might search for this using terms like "comparing two factors in manufacturing," "machine and material interaction effects," "why do some combinations work better together," or "checking if machine choice and coolant choice affect each other."

By looking at the table of residuals, we see that the residuals for
coolant B tend to be a little higher than for coolant A. This implies
that there may be more variability in diameter when we use coolant B.
From the effects table above, we see that machines 2 and 5 produce
smaller pin diameters than the other machines. There is also a very
slight coolant effect but the machine effect is larger. Finally, there
also appears to be slight interaction effects. For instance, machines 1
and 2 had smaller diameters with coolant A but the opposite was true
for machines 3,4 and 5.

title: Two-Way Nested Value-Splitting Example
text:
This example demonstrates **nested design** (also called **hierarchical design**) where operators are nested within machines, allowing engineers to separate how much variation comes from different machines versus how much comes from the different people running them. The practical problem it solves is figuring out whether you should invest in better equipment or better training—by measuring pin diameters from each operator on each lathe, you can tell if some machines are naturally inconsistent or if some operators are less skilled regardless of which machine they use. People looking for this concept might search for "how to split variation between equipment and people," "which factor causes more problems—the machine or the operator," or "comparing sources of defects in manufacturing."

The data table below contains data collected from five different

with shift instead of operator, the layout would be crossed. The measurement
is the diameter of a turned pin.
