title: Quadratic Polynomial
Linear / Quadratic Rational Function
text:
This section covers **rational functions with linear numerators and quadratic denominators**, showing how to find where the function crosses zero (roots) and where it becomes undefined (vertical asymptotes) using the quadratic formula. Engineers use this to model situations where a response variable depends on another variable in a way that has natural limits or breakpoints—like finding operating points where a system behaves differently, or predicting where a process breaks down. Practical synonyms include: finding zeros of rational expressions, locating singularities or poles in transfer functions, asymptotic behavior analysis, or determining critical points where a process response is undefined or changes character.

Horizontal asymptote at:
\( \displaystyle y = 0 \)
and vertical asymptotes at:
\( \displaystyle x = \frac{-\beta_2 \pm \sqrt{\beta_2^2 - 4\beta_3}} {2\beta_3} \)
There will be 0, 1, or 2 real solutions to this equation
corresponding to whether
\( \displaystyle \beta_2^2 - 4\beta_3 \)
is negative, zero, or positive.

title: Quadratic / Linear Rational Function
text:
This page covers **range of rational functions** and **function extrema** for quadratic-over-linear models, which solves the practical problem of determining what output values are actually possible when you fit a curved relationship between two variables—helping engineers know the bounds of predictions their model can produce. Someone looking for this concept might search for "rational function range," "output limits of curve fitting," "where does my fitted equation go," "bounds on model predictions," or "feasible region for nonlinear regression."

\( \displaystyle
\left\{
\begin{array}{lll}
(-\infty, \, \infty) & \mbox{for} & \beta_2^2-(\beta_1-\beta_0\beta_3)\beta_2\beta_3 \leq 0 \\
& & \\
(-\infty, \, f_{max}] \, \cup \, [f_{min}, \, \infty) & \mbox{for} & \beta_2^2-(\beta_1-\beta_0\beta_3)\beta_2\beta_3 > 0
\end{array}
\right.
\)
with
\( \displaystyle f_{min} = \max \left[
f \left( \frac{-\beta_2-\sqrt{\beta_2^2-\beta_0\beta_3}}{\beta_2\beta_3} \right), \
f \left( \frac{-\beta_2+\sqrt{\beta_2^2-\beta_0\beta_3}}{\beta_2\beta_3} \right)
\right] \)
and
\( \displaystyle f_{max} = \min \left[
f \left( \frac{-\beta_2-\sqrt{\beta_2^2-\beta_0\beta_3}}{\beta_2\beta_3} \right), \
f \left( \frac{-\beta_2+\sqrt{\beta_2^2-\beta_0\beta_3}}{\beta_2\beta_3} \right)
\right] \)

title: Quadratic / Quadratic Rational Function
text:
This NIST handbook section covers **rational function modeling** and **asymptotic behavior analysis** for fitting curved relationships in experimental data using quadratic polynomials in both the numerator and denominator. Engineers use this when they need to model physical systems that level off or have forbidden operating zones—like chemical reaction rates that plateau, pressure-flow relationships with constraints, or sensor responses that break down at certain points. Someone looking for this technique might search for "asymptote fitting," "rational curve fitting," "S-curve modeling," "systems with limits or barriers," or "how to fit data that has flat regions and forbidden values."

Horizontal asymptotes at:
\( \displaystyle y = \frac{\beta_2}{\beta_4} \)
and vertical asymptotes at:
\( \displaystyle x = \frac{-\beta_3 \pm \sqrt{\beta_3^2 - 4\beta_4}} {2\beta_4} \)
There will be 0, 1, or 2 real solutions to this equation
corresponding to whether
\( \displaystyle \beta_3^2 - 4\beta_4 \)
is negative, zero, or positive.

title: Cubic / Quadratic Rational Function
text:
This page covers **rational function modeling** and **singularities/poles of rational functions**, showing how to find the vertical asymptotes (undefined points) where a cubic polynomial divided by a quadratic polynomial breaks down—solving the practical engineering problem of identifying where your fitted model equation will blow up or become unreliable. Someone looking for this concept might search for "where does my rational function equation break," "vertical asymptotes in curve fitting," "poles in rational models," "undefined points in fitted equations," or "singularities in ratio-of-polynomials regression."

\( \displaystyle (-\infty, \infty) \)
with undefined points at
\( \displaystyle x = \frac{-\beta_4 \pm \sqrt{\beta_4^2 - 4\beta_5}} {2\beta_5} \)
There will be 0, 1, or 2 real solutions to this equation
corresponding to whether
\( \displaystyle \beta_4^2 - 4\beta_5 \)
is negative, zero, or positive.

title: Linear / Cubic Rational Function
text:
This handbook section covers **rational function modeling** and **asymptotic behavior analysis** for fitting curved relationships between inputs and outputs when your data shows a horizontal baseline and potential discontinuities. It helps engineers find where a model equation breaks down or becomes undefined (the vertical asymptotes), which is crucial for understanding the valid operating range of a process—think of it as identifying danger zones or limits where your mathematical model stops working. Someone might search for "where does my equation blow up," "finding discontinuities in a model," "polynomial roots," "undefined points in a formula," or "valid range for a process model."

Horizontal asymptote at:
\( \displaystyle y = 0 \)
and vertical asymptotes at the roots of
\( \displaystyle 1 + \beta_2x + \beta_3x^2 + \beta_4x^3 \)
There will be 1, 2, or 3 roots, depending on the particular values
of the parameters. Explicit
solutions for the roots of a cubic polynomial are complicated and
are not given here. Many mathematical and statistical software
programs can determine the roots of a polynomial equation numerically,
and it is recommended that you use one of these programs if you need
to know where these roots occur.

title: Quadratic / Cubic Rational Function
text:
This page covers **rational function modeling** with quadratic numerators and cubic denominators, which helps engineers fit curved relationships between variables when data shows asymptotic behavior or breaks down at certain operating points. Engineers use this model when they have measurements that approach zero at extreme conditions but become undefined or spike sharply near certain input values (the vertical asymptotes). Someone might search for this using terms like "fitting curves with poles," "ratio of polynomials," "asymptotic models," "nonlinear curve fitting with singularities," or "modeling data with flat and steep regions."

Horizontal asymptote at:
\( \displaystyle y=0 \)
and vertical asymptotes at the roots of
\( \displaystyle 1 + \beta_3x + \beta_4x^2 + \beta_5x^3 \)
There will be 1, 2, or 3 roots, depending on the particular values
of the parameters. Explicit
solutions for the roots of a cubic polynomial are complicated and
are not given here. Many mathematical and statistical software
programs can determine the roots of a polynomial equation numerically,
and it is recommended that you use one of these programs if you need
to know where these roots occur.

title: Motivation: What is the Form of the Model?
text:
This section covers **linear regression modeling with interaction terms**, which solves the practical problem of figuring out the right mathematical equation to predict how an output responds to multiple input factors—including cases where two factors work together in ways that pure addition can't capture. If you're looking for this concept, you might search for "how to build a prediction model," "main effects and interactions," "regression equation form," "combining factor effects," or "two-way factor interactions."

From the above discussion, we thus note and recommend a form
of the model that consists of an additive constant plus a linear
combination of main effects and interactions. What then is the
specific form for the linear combination?
The following are the full models for various values of
k
.
The selected final model will be a subset of the full model.
For the
k
= 1 factor case:
\( Y = f(X_{1}) + \epsilon = c + B_{1}X_{1} + \epsilon \)
For the
k
= 2 factor case:
\( \begin{array}{lcl}
Y & = & f(X_{1},X_{2}) + \epsilon \\
& = & c + B_{1}X_{1} + B_{2}X_{2} + B_{12}X_{1}X_{2} +
\epsilon
\end{array}
\)
For the
k
= 3 factor case:
\( \begin{array}{lcl}
Y & = & f(X_{1},X_{2},X_{3}) + \epsilon \\
& = & c + B_{1}X_{1} + B_{2}X_{2} + B_{3}X_{3} + \\
& & B_{12}X_{1}X_{2} + B_{13}X_{1}X_{3} +
B_{23}X_{2}X_{3} + \\
& & B_{123}X_{1}X_{2}X_{3} + \epsilon
\end{array}
\)
and for the general
k
case:
\( \begin{array}{lcl}
Y & = & f(X_{1},X_{2}, \ldots , X_{k}) + \epsilon \\
& = & c + \mbox{linear combination of all main} \\
& & \mbox{effects and all interactions} + \epsilon
\end{array}
\)
Note that the model equations shown above include coefficients that
represent the change in
Y
for a one-unit change in
X
i
. To obtain an effect estimate, which represents a
two-unit change in
X
i
if the levels of
X
i
are +1 and -1, simply multiply the coefficient by
two.

title: How do you execute the design?
text:
This guidance covers **experimental design** and **process control** principles that prevent your test results from being ruined by measurement errors, operator mistakes, or unexpected changes happening mid-experiment—basically, it keeps your data clean and trustworthy so you can actually tell if your process improvement worked. If you're looking for this information, you might search for "how to run a reliable test," "controlling variables during experiments," "preventing test contamination," "experimental execution checklist," or "ensuring measurement validity."

Important practical considerations
in planning and running experiments are
Check performance of gauges/measurement devices first
Keep the experiment as simple as possible
Get the runs over with as quickly as possible
Check that all planned runs are feasible
Watch out for process drifts and shifts during the run
Avoid unplanned changes (e.g. swap operators at half time)
Allow some time (and back-up material) for unexpected events
Obtain buy-in from all parties involved
Maintain effective ownership of each step in the experimental plan
Preserve all the raw data�do not keep only summary averages !
Record everything that happens
Reset equipment to its original state after the experiment.

title: Statistical significance
text:
To determine whether an observed difference in a process is real or just random noise, you need to estimate the standard deviation of that difference—and this handbook covers three methods to do it, with the most practical being replication (running the experiment multiple times) since you usually don't know the true variation beforehand. Someone looking for this concept might search for "how to calculate confidence intervals for process changes," "methods to estimate variation in experiments," "how many times should I repeat a test," or "how to know if my improvement is statistically significant."

The key in the above approach is to determine an estimate for
sd
(
effect
). Three statistical approaches are common:
Prior knowledge about
σ
:
If
σ
is known, we can compute
sd
(
effect
)
from the above expression and make use of a conservative
(normal-based) 95% confidence interval by drawing the line at
\( \mbox{2 sd(effect)} = 2 \left( \frac{2 \sigma}{\sqrt{n}}
\right) \)
This method is rarely used in practice because
σ
is rarely known.
Replication in the experimental design:
Replication will allow
σ&
to be estimated from the data without depending on the
correctness of a deterministic model. This is a real
benefit. On the other hand, the downside of such replication
is that it increases the number of runs, time, and expense of
the experiment. If replication can be afforded, this
method should be used. In such a case, the analyst separates
important from unimportant terms by drawing the line at
\( t \mbox{*sd(effect)} = t \mbox{*} \left(
\frac{2\hat{\sigma}}{\sqrt{n}} \right) \)
with
t
denoting the 97.5 percent point from the
appropriate
Student's-
t
distribution
.
Assume 3-factor interactions and higher are zero:
This approach "assumes away" all 3-factor interactions and higher
and uses the data pertaining to these interactions to estimate
σ
. Specifically,
\( \hat{\sigma} = \sqrt{\frac{\mbox{SSQ}}{h}} \)
with
h
denoting the number of 3-factor interactions and
higher, and SSQ is the sum of squares for these higher-order
effects. The analyst separates important from unimportant
effects by drawing the line at
\( t \mbox{*sd(effect)} = t \mbox{*}
\left( \frac{2 \hat{\sigma}}{\sqrt{n}} \right) \)
with
t
denoting the 97.5 percent point from the
appropriate (with
h
degrees of freedom)
Student's-
t
distribution
.
This method warrants caution:
it involves an untestable assumption (that such
interactions = 0);
it can result in an estimate for
sd
(
effect
)
based on few terms (even a single term); and
it is virtually unusable for highly-fractionated
designs (since high-order interactions are not
directly estimable).

title: Engineering significance
text:
**Engineering significance** (also called **practical significance** or **substantive significance**) addresses the problem of determining when a real-world change in your measurements actually matters enough to act on it—distinguishing between statistically detectable changes and changes big enough to justify the cost and effort of implementation. Engineers and scientists need a predetermined threshold or **minimum detectable effect** (a specific number in their measurement units) to decide upfront what size improvement they would actually care about before collecting any data. Related search terms include: "practical vs statistical significance," "effect size," "minimum important difference," "meaningful change threshold," "engineering judgment in statistics," and "decision rule for process improvement."

This approach requires preliminary, data-free thinking on the part
of the analyst as to how big (= what number?) an effect (any effect)
must be before the analyst would "care" as an engineer/scientist? In
other words, in the units of the response variable, how much would the
response variable have to change consistently before the analyst would
say "that's a big enough change for me from an engineering
point of view"? An engineering number, a cutoff value, is needed here.
This value is non-statistical; thie value must emanate
from the engineer's head.
If upon reflection the analyst does
not
have such a value in
mind, this "engineering significance" approach would be set aside. From
experience, it has been found that the engineering soul-searching that
goes into evoking such a cutoff value is frequently useful and should
be part of the decision process, independent of statistical
considerations, of separating the effects into
important/unimportant categories.

title: Pattern significance
text:
The **effects plot** and **Pareto principle** for factor screening help engineers quickly identify which process inputs actually matter versus which ones don't by spotting the "kink" where bar heights drop sharply—solving the practical problem of knowing which variables to focus on tuning and which to ignore. You might also search for this using terms like "main effects plot interpretation," "vital few versus trivial many factors," "screening design analysis," or "which factors should I optimize."

The |effects| plot has a characteristic horizontally-elongated
L
-shaped pattern. The vertical arm of the
L
consists
of important factors. The horizontal arm is comprised of unimportant
factors. If a factor is important, the bar height will be large and
succeeding bar heights may drop off considerably (perhaps by 50 %)--such
factors make up the left arm of the
L
. On the other hand, if a
factor is not important, its bar height will tend to be small and
near-zero--such factors make up the bottom arm of the
L
. It is
of interest to note where the kink is in the
L
. Factors to the
left of that kink are arguably declared important while factors at the
kink point and to the right of it are declared unimportant.

title: Motivation: What is a Model?
text:
A **fitted model** uses factor settings (X₁ to Xₖ) to make predictions that should match observed response values (Y), with **residuals** (deviations/errors) measuring how far off those predictions are—this lets engineers figure out if their mathematical equation actually works for real-world data instead of just guessing. You might search for this concept using terms like "model prediction accuracy," "how close are my predictions to actual measurements," "difference between predicted and actual values," "model fit quality," or "checking if my equation works."

For given settings of the factors
X
1
to
X
k
, a fitted model will yield predicted values. For
each (and every) setting of the
X
i
's, a
"perfect-fit" model is one in which the predicted values are identical
to the observed responses
Y
at these
X
i
's.
In other words, a perfect-fit model would yield a vector of predicted
values identical to the observed vector of response values. For these
same
X
i
's, a "good-fitting" model is one that yields
predicted values "acceptably near", but not necessarily identical to,
the observed responses
Y
.
The residuals (= deviations = error) of a model are the vector of
differences (Y - \( \small \hat{Y} \))
between the responses and the predicted values from the model. For
a perfect-fit model, the vector of residuals would be all zeros. For a
good-fitting model, the vector of residuals will be acceptably
(from an engineering point of view) close to zero.

title:
Motivation: How do we Construct a Goodness-of-fit
Metric for a Model?
text:
The residual standard deviation (also called root mean square error, RMS error, or standard error of the estimate) measures how far off your model's predictions typically are from the actual data—essentially telling you whether your fitted equation does a good job predicting real-world values. Engineers use this metric to pick between competing models by comparing which one has smaller prediction errors, making it a straightforward "smaller is better" scorecard. If you're looking for ways to measure model fit, goodness-of-fit statistics, prediction accuracy, or how well an equation matches your test data, this is the metric you need.

Our final metric, which is used directly in inferential statistics,
is the residual standard deviation
\[ s_{res} = \sqrt{ \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n}{r_{i}^{2}}} {n-p}} \]
with
p
denoting the number of fitted coefficients in the
model. This statistic is the standard deviation of the residuals
from a given model. The smaller is this residual standard deviation,
the better fitting is the model. We shall use the residual standard
deviation as our metric of choice for evaluating and comparing
various proposed models.

title: Motivation: How do we Construct a Good Model?
text:
The **trivial model** (or **baseline model** / **null model**) establishes that when no factors or interactions improve predictions, the best estimate is simply the **sample mean** (average of all observations). This solves the practical problem of knowing what your prediction performance should beat—if your fancy model with all the bells and whistles can't do better than just always guessing the average value, then those factors aren't actually helping you. Someone might search for this as "comparing against the average," "what's the simplest possible model," "baseline prediction," "mean prediction," or "null hypothesis model."

The simplest such proposed model is
\( Y = c + \epsilon \)
that is, the response
Y
= a constant + random error. This
trivial model says that all of the factors (and interactions) are in
fact worthless for prediction and so the best-fit model is one
that consists of a simple horizontal straight line through the body
of the data. The least squares estimate for this constant
c
in
the above model is the sample mean \( \bar{Y} \).
The prediction equation for this model is thus
\( \hat{Y} = \bar{Y} \)
The predicted values \( \small \hat{Y} \)
for this fitted trivial model are thus given by a vector consisting of the
same value (namely \( \bar{Y} \))
throughout. The residual vector for this model will thus simplify to
simple deviations from the mean:
\( Y - \bar{Y} \)
Since the number of fitted coefficients in this model is 1 (namely the
constant
c
), the residual standard deviation is the following:
\( s_{res} = \sqrt{\frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n}{(Y_{i} - \bar{Y})^2}}{n - 1}} \)
which is of course the familiar, commonly employed sample standard
deviation. If the residual standard deviation for this trivial model
were "small enough", then we could terminate the model-building process
right there with no further inclusion of terms. In practice, however,
this trivial model does
not
yield a residual standard
deviation that is small enough (because the common value \( \bar{Y} \)
will not be close enough to some of the raw responses
Y
) and so the model must be augmented--but how?

title:
Motivation: How do we Know When to Stop Adding
Terms?
text:
The **hockey stick curve** and **residual standard deviation** concepts address the practical problem of knowing when you've added enough variables or terms to your model—when adding more won't meaningfully improve your results anymore, so you can stop fine-tuning and move forward. This helps engineers avoid wasting time on tiny improvements while also preventing overfitting that makes models unreliable on new data. You might also search for this concept as "model complexity trade-off," "diminishing returns in model building," "when to stop adding predictors," or "identifying the elbow point in model performance."

Proceeding left to right, as we add more terms to the model, the
cumulative residual standard deviation "curve" will typically decrease.
At the beginning (on the left), as we add large-effect terms, the
decrease from one residual standard deviation to the next residual
standard deviation will be large. The incremental improvement
(decrease) then tends to drop off slightly. At some point the
incremental improvement will typically slacken off considerably.
Appearance-wise, it is thus very typical for such a curve to have a
"hockey stick" appearance:
starting with a series of large decrements between successive
residual standard deviations; then
hitting an elbow; then
having a series of gradual decrements thereafter.

title: Motivation: What are the Advantages of the Linear Combinatoric Model?
text:
The **linear combinatoric model** and **coded factor variables** solve the problem of making regression coefficients comparable and stable regardless of whether you measure temperature in Fahrenheit or Celsius, or time in minutes versus hours—by converting raw measurements like "300-350 degrees" into standardized (-1, +1) scales so the numbers don't change when someone switches units or measurement systems. Engineers often call this **factor coding**, **variable scaling**, **normalization**, or **standardizing your inputs** to avoid coefficients that are either huge or tiny depending on arbitrary measurement choices. People searching for this concept might also look for **coded variables**, **centered and scaled factors**, **unit-independent regression**, or **why my coefficients changed when I switched to Celsius**.

To illustrate in detail the above latter point, suppose the (-1, +1)
factor
X
1
is really a coding of temperature
T
with the original
temperature ranging from 300 to 350 degrees and the (-1, +1) factor
X
2
is really a coding of time
t
with the original time
ranging from 20 to 30 minutes. Given that, a linear model in the
original temperature
T
and time
t
would yield coefficients whose
magnitude depends on the magnitude of
T
(300 to 350) and
t
(20 to
30), and whose value would change if we decided to change the units of
T
(e.g., from Fahrenheit degrees to Celsius degrees) and
t
(e.g., from minutes to seconds). All of this is avoided by carrying
out the fit not in the original units for
T
(300,350) and
t
(20, 30), but in the coded units of
X
1
(-1, +1) and
X
2
(-1, +1). The
resulting coefficients are unit-invariant, and thus the coefficient
magnitudes reflect the true contribution of the factors and
interactions without regard to the unit of measurement.

title:
Motivation: How do we use the Model to Generate
Predicted Values?
text:
When fitting data perfectly to all points using 2^k coefficients, the model suffers from **overfitting** and **lack of parsimony**, which means you're memorizing noise in your data rather than capturing the true underlying pattern, causing predictions on new data to perform poorly even though your original fit looks perfect. Engineers dealing with **design of experiments (DOE)**, **factorial designs**, or **model selection** face this tradeoff between **goodness of fit** and **model simplicity**—you can match your test data exactly but end up with an unnecessarily complex model that doesn't generalize. Related concepts include **degrees of freedom**, **variance inflation**, **bias-variance tradeoff**, **model complexity**, **unnecessary variables**, and **reducing the number of parameters** in your prediction equation.

What price is paid for this perfect fit? One price is that the variance
of \( \scriptsize \hat{Y} \)
is increased unnecessarily. In addition, we have a non-parsimonious
model. We must compute and carry the average and the coefficients of
all main effects and all interactions. Including the average, there
will in general be 2
k
coefficients
to fully describe the fitting of the
n
= 2
k
points. This is very much akin to the
Y
=
f
(
X
)
polynomial fitting of
n
distinct points. It is well known that
this may be done "perfectly" by fitting a polynomial of degree
n
-1. It is comforting to know that such perfection is
mathematically attainable, but in practice do we want to do this all
the time or even anytime? The answer is generally "no" for two reasons:
Noise: It is very common that the response data
Y
has
noise (= error) in it. Do we want to go out of our way to fit
such noise? Or do we want our model to filter out the noise and
just fit the "signal"? For the latter, fewer coefficients may
be in order, in the same spirit that we may forego a
perfect-fitting (but jagged) 11-th degree polynomial to 12 data
points, and opt out instead for an imperfect (but smoother) 3rd
degree polynomial fit to the 12 points.
Parsimony: For full factorial designs, to fit the
n
=
2
k
points we would need to compute
2
k
coefficients. We gain information by noting
the magnitude and sign of such coefficients, but numerically we
have
n
data values
Y
as input and
n
coefficients
B
as output, and so no numerical reduction
has been achieved. We have simply used one set of
n
numbers (the data) to obtain another set of
n
numbers (the
coefficients). Not all of these coefficients will be equally
important. At times that importance becomes clouded by the sheer
volume of the
n
= 2
k
coefficients.
Parsimony suggests that our result should be simpler and
more focused than our
n
starting points. Hence fewer
retained coefficients are called for.
The net result is that in practice we almost always give up the
perfect, but unwieldy, model for an imperfect, but parsimonious,
model.

title:
Motivation: How do we Use the Model Beyond the Data
Domain?
text:
This section covers **interpolation and extrapolation**—the statistical practices of making predictions within versus beyond your observed data range—which helps engineers know when they can safely use a model to estimate values they haven't measured yet versus when they're taking a risky guess. Non-statisticians might search for this concept using terms like "predicting between data points," "forecasting outside your range," "how far can I trust my model," "safe prediction zones," or "when does a model break down."

Modeling and prediction allow us to go beyond the data to gain
additional insights, but they must be done with great caution.
Interpolation is generally safer than extrapolation, but
mis-prediction, error, and misinterpretation are liable to occur
in either case.
The analyst should definitely perform the model-building process and
enjoy the ability to predict elsewhere, but the analyst must always be
prepared to validate the interpolated and extrapolated predictions by
collection of additional real, confirmatory data. The general
empirical model that we recommend knows "nothing" about the
engineering, physics, or chemistry surrounding your particular
measurement problem, and although the model is the best generic model
available, it must nonetheless be confirmed by additional data. Such
additional data can be obtained pre-experimentally or
post-experimentally. If done pre-experimentally, a recommended
procedure for checking the validity of the fitted model is to augment
the usual 2
k
or 2
k
-
p
designs
with additional points at the center of the design. This is discussed
in the next section
.

title:
Motivation: What is the Best Confirmation Point for
Interpolation?
text:
The **confirmatory run** and **center point validation** are core techniques for assessing **model interpolation reliability** in designed experiments. This solves the practical problem of knowing whether your fitted equation can be trusted to make predictions anywhere in the middle of your experimental region, not just at the tested corners—by running one extra test at the center and checking if the prediction matches reality. People also search for this concept as "validation experiment," "confirmation point check," "model verification," or "center point confirmation test."

The importance of the confirmatory run cannot be overstated. If the
confirmatory run at the center point yields a data value of, say,
Y
= 5.1, since the predicted value at the center is 5 and
we know the model is perfect at the corner points, that would give the
analyst a greater confidence that the quality of the fitted model may
extend over the entire interior (interpolation) domain. On the other
hand, if the confirmatory run yielded a center point data value quite
different (e.g.,
Y
= 7.5) from the center point predicted value
of 5, then that would prompt the analyst to
not
trust the fitted
model even for interpolation purposes. Hence when our factors are
continuous, a single confirmatory run at the center point helps
immensely in assessing the range of trust for our model.

title: Model Identification for the CO2Concentrations Data
section: Process or Product Monitoring and Control
text:
Partial autocorrelation analysis and seasonal ARIMA modeling help engineers identify the right type of forecasting pattern to use when monitoring data that repeats in cycles—like CO2 levels that spike at the same time each year—by showing which past measurements actually influence future values and filtering out noise so you pick the simplest model that works. Instead of guessing whether to use AR(2), seasonal AR(12), or differencing, this method lets you objectively decide which combination best captures both the short-term patterns and the yearly repeating behavior in your data. Related searches: "how to pick between AR and ARIMA models," "detecting seasonality in time series," "lag analysis for forecasting," "removing trend and seasonal effects before modeling," or "choosing seasonal versus non-seasonal differencing."

The partial autocorrelation plot suggests that an AR(2) model might
be appropriate since the partial autocorrelation becomes zero after
the second lag. The lag 12 is also significant, indicating some
remaining seasonality.
In summary, our intial attempt would be to fit an AR(2) model with a
seasonal AR(12) term on the data with a linear trend line removed.
We could try the model both with and without seasonal differencing
applied. Model validation should be performed before accepting this
as a final model.

title: Box-Ljung Test
section: Process or Product Monitoring and Control
text:
The Box-Ljung test is a statistical hypothesis test that checks whether the leftover errors from a fitted model are random or contain hidden patterns—solving the practical problem of deciding whether your model has captured all the important signals in your data or if something's still being missed. If you're looking for ways to validate that your forecasting model or control chart is working properly, or you need to test for autocorrelation in residuals, you might search for "residual autocorrelation test," "model adequacy check," "goodness-of-fit for time series," or "testing if errors are independent."

In general, the Box-Ljung test is defined as:
\(H_0\):
The model does not exhibit lack of fit.
\(H_a\):
The model exhibits lack of fit.
Test Statistic:
Given a time series \(Y\)
of length \(n\),
the test statistic is defined as:
$$ Q = n(n+2)\sum_{k=1}^{m} \, \frac{\hat{r}^2_k}{n-k} $$
where \(\hat{r}_k\)
is the estimated autocorrelation of the series at lag \(k\),
and \(m\)
is the number of lags being tested.
Significance Level:
\(\alpha\)
Critical Region:
The Box-Ljung test rejects the null hypothesis (indicating that the model has
significant lack of fit) if
$$ Q > \chi_{1-\alpha, \, h}^2 $$
where \(\chi_{1-\alpha, \, h}^2\)
is the chi-square distribution table value with \(h\)
degrees of freedom and significance level \(\alpha\).
Because the test is applied to residuals, the degrees of
freedom must account for the estimated model parameters so that
\(h = m-p-q\),
where \(p\) and \(q\)
indicate the number of parameters from the ARMA(\(p,q\))
model fit to the data.

The Information Technology Laboratory (ITL) is one of NIST’s six research laboratories. ITL focuses on IT measurements, testing, and standards, and is a globally recognized and trusted source of high-quality, independent, and unbiased research and data. ITL’s mission, to cultivate trust in information technology (IT) and metrology, is accomplished using its world-class measurement and testing facilities and encompassing a wide range of areas of computer science, mathematics, statistics, and systems engineering.
This non-regulatory role, along with ITL’s deep technical expertise in all fields of information technology, helps increase trust in IT worldwide.

title: One-way ANOVA overview
text:
One-way ANOVA partitions the total sum of squares (SS) into components by splitting the corrected sum of squares, which equals the raw sum of squares minus the correction term for the mean, allowing you to break down overall variation in your data into meaningful pieces. This solves the practical problem of figuring out how much of the differences you see between multiple product batches or process conditions is real variation versus just random noise. People looking for this concept might search for "how to split up variation between groups," "breaking down total variation in ANOVA," "comparing multiple samples or treatments," or "what causes differences between my process runs."

The numerator part is called the
sum of squares
of deviations
from the mean, and the denominator is called the
degrees of freedom
.
The variance, after some algebra, can be rewritten as:
$$ s^2 = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^n y_i^2 - \frac{1}{n} \, \left( \sum_{i=1}^n y_i \right)^2}{n-1} \, . $$
The first term in the numerator is called the "
raw sum of squares
"
and the second term is called the "
correction term for the mean
".
Another name for the numerator is the "
corrected sum of squares
",
and this is usually abbreviated by
Total \(SS\) or \(SS(Total)\)
.
The \(SS\) in a one-way ANOVA can be split into two components, called the
"sum of squares of treatments
" and
"sum of squares of error
",
abbreviated as \(SST\) and \(SSE\), respectively.

title:
Choosing a Sampling Plan with a given
OC Curve
section: Process or Product Monitoring and Control
text:
Average Outgoing Quality (AOQ) measures the long-run defect rate in products that leave an inspection station after applying acceptance sampling with 100% inspection of rejected lots—it tells you what level of bad parts you'll actually ship to customers when you inspect some lots completely and let others through based on a sample. Someone searching for this might look for "defect rate after inspection," "quality level leaving the plant," "effective defect percentage with sampling plans," or "what percentage of defects make it to the customer."

Average Outgoing Quality (\(AOQ\))
We can also calculate the
\(AOQ\)
for a \((n,c)\)
sampling plan, provided rejected lots are 100 % inspected
and defectives are replaced with good parts.
Assume all lots come in with exactly \(p_0\)
proportion of defectives. After screening a rejected lot, the final fraction defectives
will be zero for that lot. However, accepted lots have fraction defective \(p_0\).
Therefore, the outgoing lots from the inspection stations
are a mixture of lots with fractions defective \(p_0\)
and 0. Assuming the lot size is \(N\),
we have
$$ AOQ = \frac{p_a p(N-n)}{N} \, . $$
For example, let \(N=10,000\), \(n = 52\), \(c=3\) and \(p\),
the quality of incoming lots
,
= 0.03. Now at
\(p=0.03\), we glean from the OC curve table that \(p_a=0.930\) and
$$ AOQ = \frac{0.930(0.03)(10,000-52)}{10,000} = 0.00278\, . $$
Setting \(p = 0.01, \, 0.02, \, \ldots, \, 0.12\),
we can generate the following table.
$$ \begin{eqnarray}
AOQ & \,\,\,\,\, & P_d \\
0.0010 & \,\,\,\,\, & 0.01 \\
0.0196 & \,\,\,\,\, & 0.02 \\
0.0278 & \,\,\,\,\, & 0.03 \\
0.0338 & \,\,\,\,\, & 0.04 \\
0.0369 & \,\,\,\,\, & 0.05 \\
0.0372 & \,\,\,\,\, & 0.06 \\
0.0351 & \,\,\,\,\, & 0.07 \\
0.0315 & \,\,\,\,\, & 0.08 \\
0.0270 & \,\,\,\,\, & 0.09 \\
0.0223 & \,\,\,\,\, & 0.10 \\
0.0178 & \,\,\,\,\, & 0.11 \\
0.0138 & \,\,\,\,\, & 0.12
\end{eqnarray} $$
A plot of the \(AOQ\) versus \(p\)
is given below.
From examining this curve we observe that when the incoming quality
is very good (very small fraction of defectives coming in) then the outgoing
quality is also very good (very small fraction of defectives going out)
When the incoming lot quality is very bad, most of the lots are rejected
and then inspected. The "duds" are eliminated or replaced by good ones,
so that the quality of the outgoing lots, the \(AOQ\), becomes very good.
In between these extremes, the \(AOQ\) rises, reaches a maximum, and
then drops.
The maximum ordinate on the \(AOQ\) curve represents the worst possible
quality that results from the rectifying inspection program. It is called
the
average outgoing quality limit,
(
\(AOQL\)
).
From the table we see that the \(AOQL = 0.0372\) at \(p = 0.06\)
for the above example.
One final remark: if \(N \gg n\),
then \(AOQ \approx p_a p\).

title: Models and calculations for the two-way ANOVA
text:
Two-way ANOVA (analysis of variance) with balanced factorial design lets you test how two different factors and their combination affect your product or process results by breaking down variation into separate pieces you can compare. Instead of running experiments one factor at a time, this method lets you change two things simultaneously while keeping equal numbers of test runs in each combination, then figure out which factor (or both together) is actually causing the differences you see. People also call this "two-factor ANOVA," "factorial experiment analysis," "interaction testing," or "multifactor comparison."

b

title: Left-right balanced design for 4 standard cells
text:
This page demonstrates **balanced experimental design** and **left-right bias correction** for comparing four standard reference cells by systematically alternating their positions (+ and - placements) across 12 measurements to cancel out directional measurement errors. An engineer trying to verify if four calibration standards are truly equivalent will use this design pattern to ensure that any systematic drift or positional bias in their measurement system doesn't skew the results, giving them confidence that differences detected are real and not artifacts of how the cells were arranged. You might also search for "counterbalanced measurement design," "order effect elimination," "systematic error removal in calibration," or "reference standard comparison method."

title: Left-right balanced design for 5 standard cells
text:
This page describes **balanced incomplete block design (BIBD)** and **left-right bias correction** for comparing multiple measurement standards, solving the practical problem of how to fairly test 5 reference cells against each other using only 10 measurements without always measuring every possible pair. Engineers looking for this concept might search for "counterbalanced measurement design," "eliminating instrument bias from comparisons," "systematic testing plan to remove left-right effects," or "how to compare multiple standards efficiently without bias."
